```mermaid
        DEMOD --> CTRL[Control Logic and FSM]
        CTRL --> MEM[Non Volatile Memory EPC User TID]
        CTRL --> MOD[Load Modulator Impedance Switch]
        MOD --> ANT
    end
```

## 4.3 Read-Range Computation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Reader Parameters] --> S2[Set Pt Gt Gr tau lambda Pth]
    S2 --> S3[Compute lambda = c over f]
    S3 --> S4[Apply Friis Equation rmax]
    S4 --> S5{rmax meets application need?}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Deploy System]
    S5 -- No --> S7[Increase Pt or Gt or Decrease Pth]
    S7 --> S4
    S6 --> S8[End]
```

## 4.4 Anti-Collision Q-Algorithm Flow (EPC Gen-2)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0[Reader sends Query with Q value] --> R1[Each Tag picks random slot in 0 to 2Q minus 1]
    R1 --> R2{Tag slot equals 0?}
    R2 -- Yes --> R3[Tag transmits RN16]
    R2 -- No --> R4[Tag waits decrement counter]
    R3 --> R5{Collision Single or Multiple?}
    R5 -- Single --> R6[Reader ACKs and reads EPC]
    R5 -- Collision --> R7[Reader sends QueryAdjust Q up]
    R5 -- Empty --> R8[Reader sends QueryAdjust Q down]
    R7 --> Q0
    R8 --> Q0
    R6 --> R9[Read Complete]
```

## 4.5 RFID Layer Mapping in IoT Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5[Application Layer ERP SCM Analytics]
    L4[Network Layer Wi Fi 4G 5G Ethernet]
    L3[Middleware Layer RFID Savant ALE Filtering]
    L2[Edge Processing Layer Reader Firmware]
    L1[Perception Layer RFID Sensors Tags Beacons]
    L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> **Q: Define RFID. Differentiate between active and passive RFID tags.**

**Model Answer:**

**RFID (Radio Frequency Identification)** is a wireless non-contact technology that uses radio-frequency electromagnetic fields to identify objects carrying an electronically stored identifier. It is a key perception-layer technology in IoT.

**Differences:**

| Parameter | Active Tag | Passive Tag |
|---|---|---|
| Power Source | Internal battery | Powered by reader's RF field |
| Read Range | Up to 100 m | < 10 m |
| Lifetime | 5–10 years | Practically indefinite |
| Cost | High (₹500+) | Low (₹5–50) |
| Size | Larger (battery) | Thin, sticker-like possible |
| Initiates Communication | Yes | No |

**Key point:** Passive tags use backscatter modulation; active tags have their own transmitter.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
> **Q: Explain the concept of backscatter modulation in passive RFID communication.**

**Model Answer:**

**Backscatter modulation** is the technique by which a passive RFID tag transmits data to the reader **without generating its own RF energy**.

**Working Principle:**
1. The tag receives a continuous RF carrier wave from the reader.
2. The tag toggles the impedance of its antenna between two states:
   - **Matched state** → antenna absorbs energy → low reflection.
   - **Mismatched state** → antenna reflects energy → high reflection.
3. This deliberate change in reflected energy modulates the reader's own wave.
4. The reader detects these subtle amplitude (or phase) variations to recover the tag's ID/data.

**Advantages:**
- Zero on-tag power consumption for transmission.
- Indefinite tag lifetime.
- Enables very thin, cheap tags (used in retail).

**Example:** EPC Class-1 Gen-2 UHF tags in supply-chain tracking.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Many students confuse **inductive coupling** (HF, near-field) with **electromagnetic backscatter** (UHF, far-field) and incorrectly apply the Friis equation to HF tags — losing up to **4 marks**. Use the appropriate model: inductive coupling for HF (< $\lambda/2\pi$), backscatter/Friis for UHF (> $\lambda/2\pi$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** In read-range derivations, students frequently forget the **power transmission coefficient $\tau$** and the **antenna gain $G_r$**, treating the tag as an isotropic receiver — losing **2–3 marks**. Always include all four parameters: $P_t, G_t, G_r, \tau, \lambda, P_{th}$.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module-2 ESE Model]**

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an RFID system. Discuss the role of each component in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the maximum read range of a passive RFID tag. Identify the parameters that most significantly affect the read range and explain how each can be optimized. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a): RFID System Architecture

**Block Diagram:**
- Host Computer / Application Server
- ↓
- RFID Middleware (Savant) — performs filtering, aggregation, and routing.
- ↓
- RFID Reader (Interrogator) — RF module, control unit, antenna.
- ↕ (RF interface)
- RFID Tag — microchip + antenna.
- ↕ (RF interface)
- Physical Object (carrying the tag)

**Components:**

1. **RFID Tag (Transponder):**
   - Houses a microchip (silicon IC) and an antenna.
   - The chip stores: **EPC** (Electronic Product Code, 64–496 bits), **TID** (Tag ID), and optional **User Memory**.
   - The antenna: inductive coil for HF, dipole for UHF.

2. **RFID Reader (Interrogator):**
   - Generates the interrogating RF signal.
   - Performs modulation (downlink) and demodulation (uplink).
   - Provides the energy to power passive tags.
   - Interfaces with the host via USB, RS-232, Ethernet, Wi-Fi, or Bluetooth.

3. **Middleware / Host System:**
   - Receives raw reads from the reader.
   - Filters duplicates, smooths data, applies business rules.
   - Forwards to enterprise systems (ERP, WMS, SCM).

**Operation:** Reader → continuous RF field → Tag harvests energy → responds via backscatter → Reader decodes → Middleware processes → Application layer uses data.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b): Derivation of Maximum Read Range

**Given:** Passive tag activated only when chip receives power $\geq P_{th}$.

**Step 1:** Power density at distance $r$ from a reader with transmit power $P_t$ and gain $G_t$:

$$S_r = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi r^2}$$

**Step 2:** Effective aperture of the tag antenna:

$$A_e = \frac{\lambda^2 G_r}{4\pi}$$

**Step 3:** Power available at tag's antenna terminals:

$$P_{ant} = S_r \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 r^2}$$

**Step 4:** Power delivered to the chip after impedance-mismatch loss $\tau$:

$$P_{chip} = \tau \cdot P_{ant} = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \tau \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 r^2}$$

**Step 5:** Set $P_{chip} = P_{th}$ and solve for $r = r_{max}$:

$$r_{max}^2 = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \tau \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 P_{th}}$$

$$\boxed{r_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{P_t G_t G_r \tau}{P_{th}}}}$$

**Parameters and Optimization Strategies:**

| Parameter | Effect | Optimization |
|---|---|---|
| $P_t$ | Read range $\propto \sqrt{P_t}$ | Subject to regulatory limits (FCC/ETSI: 4 W EIRP for UHF) |
| $G_t, G_r$ | Read range $\propto \sqrt{G_t G_r}$ | Use high-gain, circularly polarized reader antennas |
| $\tau$ | Read range $\propto \sqrt{\tau}$ | Impedance matching network on tag ($Z_a \approx Z_c^*$) |
| $\lambda$ | Affects antenna design | Lower frequency → larger wavelength but poorer antenna directivity |
| $P_{th}$ | Read range $\propto 1/\sqrt{P_{th}}$ | Use ultra-low-power chips (e.g., ~7 µW for some UHF ICs) |

**Practical Example:** A UHF reader at $P_t = 1$ W, $G_t = 6$ dBi, $G_r = 2$ dBi, $\tau = 0.5$, $\lambda \approx 0.33$ m, $P_{th} = 50 \text{ µW}$ yields $r_{max} \approx 6$ m.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Module-2 ESE Model]**

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the various frequency bands used in RFID systems with their typical applications. Compare the read range, data rate, and anti-collision capability of each band. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the EPCglobal architecture? Explain the structure of the Electronic Product Code (EPC) and the role of the Object Name Service (ONS) in RFID-based IoT systems. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a): RFID Frequency Bands

> [!NOTE]
> This is a high-yield table-style question. KTU examiners love comparing bands.

**1. LF Band (125–134 kHz):**
- Short range (< 10 cm), slow data rate (< 1 kbps).
- Inductive coupling; works near metal and water.
- **Applications:** Animal identification (ISO 11784/11785), car immobilizers, industrial automation.

**2. HF Band (13.56 MHz):**
- Range 10 cm – 1 m; data rate ~100 kbps.
- Inductive coupling; standardized as **NFC** (ISO 14443) and **ISO 15693**.
- **Applications:** Smart cards (MIFARE), library books, contactless payments, NFC phones.

**3. UHF Band (860–960 MHz):**
- Range 1–12 m; high data rate up to 640 kbps.
- Electromagnetic backscatter; standardized as **EPC Class-1 Gen-2 / ISO 18000-63**.
- **Applications:** Supply-chain logistics, retail inventory, pallet tracking, tolling (FASTag).

**4. Microwave Band (2.45 GHz / 5.8 GHz):**
- Range 1–100 m (with active tags); very high data rate.
- Line-of-sight issues; poor near metal/liquid.
- **Applications:** Active container tracking, real-time location systems (RTLS), aerospace.

**Comparison Table:**

| Band | Range | Data Rate | Anti-Collision | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LF | < 10 cm | Very Low | Poor | High |
| HF | ~1 m | Moderate | Moderate | Low |
| UHF | ~12 m | High | Excellent (EPC Gen-2) | Very Low |
| MW | ~100 m (active) | Very High | Excellent | Moderate |

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b): EPCglobal Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> EPCglobal is a joint venture between GS1 and AIM that standardizes RFID for global supply chains. Its architecture links RFID tags to the internet via the **EPC**, **ONS**, and **EPCIS**.

**Electronic Product Code (EPC) Structure:**
- A globally unique identifier for a physical object, analogous to a URL for a thing.
- **EPC Class-1 Gen-2 tag** stores a 96-bit EPC (most common).
- **Structure (96-bit example):**
  - **Header (8 bits)** — identifies the EPC format.
  - **EPC Manager Number (28 bits)** — identifies the company (e.g., GS1 Company Prefix).
  - **Object Class (24 bits)** — product SKU/category.
  - **Serial Number (36 bits)** — unique instance of the object.

**Example decoding:** EPC `30.A8C00.000000.00000001` → header 30, manager A8C00, object class 000000, serial 00000001.

**Role of Object Name Service (ONS):**
- **ONS is the EPC network's DNS** — it maps an EPC to a web service hosting information about the product.
- When a reader reads an EPC, the middleware queries a local ONS resolver → which redirects to the authoritative ONS root → returns an **EPCIS (EPC Information Services)** server URL.
- The application fetches product details (origin, batch, expiry) from that URL.
- **ONS enables RFID tags to act as lightweight pointers to internet-hosted metadata**, completing the IoT loop.

**Architectural Layers:**

1. **Tag Layer** — physical RFID transponder.
2. **Reader Layer** — RF interface.
3. **Middleware (Savant / ALE)** — filtering, aggregation.
4. **ONS + EPCIS** — discovery and metadata services.
5. **Application Layer** — ERP, supply-chain analytics.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — RFID (KTU Module 2)**

- 🔹 **RFID = Radio Frequency Identification**, a non-contact, line-of-sight-free IoT perception-layer technology.
- 🔹 **Three core components:** Tag (chip + antenna), Reader (interrogator), Middleware/Host.
- 🔹 **Passive tags** are powered by the reader's RF field; they use **backscatter modulation** (no internal transmitter).
- 🔹 **Active tags** have their own battery + transmitter; range up to 100 m.
- 🔹 **Four frequency bands:** LF (125 kHz), HF (13.56 MHz), UHF (860–960 MHz), Microwave (2.45 GHz).
- 🔹 **HF = inductive coupling** (near-field, transformer model); **UHF = electromagnetic backscatter** (far-field, radar model).
- 🔹 **Maximum read range formula:** $r_{max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{P_t G_t G_r \tau}{P_{th}}}$ — remember all 6 parameters.
- 🔹 **Two-way path loss** ($r^4$) governs UHF backscatter link budgets.
- 🔹 **Anti-collision:** EPC Gen-2 uses **Slotted Aloha with dynamic Q-algorithm**; theoretical max throughput ≈ 36.8% (1/e).
- 🔹 **EPC** is the 96-bit (typical) globally unique product identifier stored on Gen-2 tags.
- 🔹 **ONS** is to RFID what **DNS** is to the internet — maps EPCs to EPCIS service URLs.
- 🔹 **Key standards:** ISO 11784/85 (LF animal ID), ISO 14443 / NFC (HF smart cards), ISO 15693 (vicinity cards), ISO 18000-63 / EPC Class-1 Gen-2 (UHF).
- 🔹 **Power transmission coefficient** $\tau = \dfrac{4R_c R_a}{\vert Z_c + Z_a \vert^2}$ — maximize via conjugate impedance matching.
- 🔹 **Free-space path loss (Friis):** $FSPL(dB) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ (km, MHz).
- 🔹 **Applications to memorize:** Supply chain, retail (Walmart mandate), FASTag, healthcare, library, livestock, access control.
- 🔹 **Examiner trap:** Do not apply Friis to HF tags — they operate in the **inductive** regime ($\lambda/2\pi \approx 3.5$ m at 13.56 MHz, beyond which coupling fails).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bluetooth BR/EDR and Bluetooth Low Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS AND SMART COMPUTING.
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bluetooth BR/EDR and Bluetooth Low Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bluetooth — A Quick Framing

**Bluetooth** is a short-range, low-power, low-cost wireless personal area network (WPAN) technology standardized by the **Bluetooth Special Interest Group (Bluetooth SIG)**. It operates in the globally available unlicensed **2.4 GHz Industrial, Scientific and Medical (ISM) band** and uses **Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS)** to coexist with other wireless systems such as Wi-Fi and microwave ovens.

Over the years, Bluetooth has bifurcated into two fundamentally different physical-layer and protocol-stack families:

- **Bluetooth Classic — BR/EDR** (Basic Rate / Enhanced Data Rate)
- **Bluetooth Low Energy — BLE** (marketed under the Bluetooth Smart umbrella from v4.0 onward)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Although the radio and baseband differ greatly, both variants share the **2.4 GHz ISM band**, the **1600 hops/second** FHSS engine, and the **Generic Access Profile (GAP)** philosophy. The key differentiator is **power budget, channel structure, and connection model**.

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Terminology)

### Bluetooth BR/EDR (Basic Rate / Enhanced Data Rate)

Bluetooth Classic is a **connection-oriented, synchronous/asynchronous, point-to-multipoint** short-range radio defined originally by **IEEE 802.15.1 (deprecated in favor of SIG-only specs from v4.0)**. It is optimized for **continuous streaming** of audio and moderate-throughput data, with a peak physical-layer rate of **1 Mbps (BR)**, **2 Mbps (EDR-2), or 3 Mbps (EDR-3)**.

### Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)

BLE is a **lightweight, ultra-low-power, event-driven** radio introduced in **Bluetooth 4.0 (2010)**. It is designed for **bursty, small-packet IoT telemetry** with a coin-cell battery life target of **months to years**. Peak physical-layer rate is **1 Mbps (LE 1M)**, with later extensions **LE 2M (2 Mbps, v5.0)**, **LE Coded (125/500 kbps, v5.0, long range)** and **LE Audio (LC3 codec, v5.2)**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Post Office vs. The Bulletin Board**
>
> - **Bluetooth BR/EDR is like a dedicated telephone call**: You dial (page/inquire), the other party picks up, a private duplex "line" is set up (piconet), and you exchange continuous voice (audio stream) or chatter (file transfer) until someone hangs up. Good for **streaming**, but you must keep the call open — costly in "talk time" (battery).
>
> - **Bluetooth Low Energy is like a community bulletin board**: A device periodically **posts a short note** on one of three designated channels (advertising channels) saying "I'm a heart-rate sensor, here is my current BPM." Any interested listener (scanner) can read it without ever forming a private session. If two-way talk is needed, a quick **transient meeting** is set up on a separate data channel, the data is exchanged, and the channel is released in milliseconds. Perfect for **battery-sensitive IoT** devices that sleep 99% of the time.

## 1.4 Quick Specification Snapshot (Side-by-Side)

| Parameter | BR/EDR | BLE |
|---|---|---|
| **Spec Version** | 1.x → 3.0 | 4.0 → 5.x |
| **Data Rate (PHY)** | 1 / 2 / 3 Mbps | 1 / 2 Mbps (LE 1M/2M), 125/500 kbps (Coded) |
| **Modulation** | GFSK (BR), π/4-DQPSK, 8DPSK (EDR) | GFSK |
| **Channels** | 79 | 40 |
| **Channel Spacing** | 1 MHz | 2 MHz |
| **Hop Rate** | 1600 hops/s | 1600 hops/s (connection), adaptive in adv |
| **Range (typical)** | ~10 m (Class 2) | ~50–200 m (LE Coded) |
| **Peak Current (TX)** | ~30–50 mA | ~7–15 mA |
| **Sleep Current** | Hundreds of µA | <1 µA |
| **Connection Setup** | Up to ~5 s | 3 ms (typical) |
| **Topology** | Piconet / Scatternet | Star / Broadcaster / Mesh |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bluetooth BR/EDR — Architecture & Operation

### 2.1.1 The Piconet and Scatternet

A **piconet** is the fundamental topology: **one master** and up to **7 active slaves** (plus up to 255 parked slaves), all sharing the master's clock and hopping sequence.

- The **master** controls the FHSS pattern; it broadcasts on even slots, slaves on odd slots (TDD).
- Multiple piconets that share one or more nodes form a **scatternet** — inter-piconet bridges increase latency and complexity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why only 7 active slaves?** The 3-bit Active Member Address (AM_ADDR) field limits distinct active slaves per piconet. This is a legacy design choice from the original Ericsson specification (1994).

### 2.1.2 Physical Channel & Hopping

The **2.4 GHz ISM band** spans **2402 MHz – 2480 MHz** in most regulatory domains (Japan/France use a reduced set). BR/EDR defines **79 RF channels** with **1 MHz spacing**:

$$f_k = 2402 + k \text{ MHz}, \quad k \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 78\}$$

The hopping sequence is a 79-channel pseudo-random pattern driven by the master's **UAP/LAP** (Upper/Lower Address Part) and the **CLK** register. The dwell time per hop is **625 µs (one slot)**, giving the canonical:

$$f_{\text{hop}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{slot}}} = \frac{1}{625 \,\mu s} = 1600 \text{ hops/second}$$

### 2.1.3 Baseband Packet Structure

A BR/EDR baseband packet consists of:

1. **Access Code (68 / 72 bits)** — channel identity, sync, and master ID
2. **Header (54 bits)** — flow control, ARQN, SEQN, type
3. **Payload (up to 2745 bits for DH5, 2 Mbps)** — user data

Modulation per packet type:

- **BR packets** (DM1, DH1, DM3, DH3, DM5, DH5) — GFSK, 1 Mbps.
- **EDR packets** (2-DH1/3/5, 3-DH1/3/5) — GFSK header then π/4-DQPSK (2 Mbps) or 8DPSK (3 Mbps) payload.

### 2.1.4 Connection Establishment (Inquiry / Paging)

Classic Bluetooth requires an **inquiry** + **paging** dance that can take several seconds:

1. **Inquiry** — discover nearby devices (32 inquiry-scan channels in 79).
2. **Paging** — connect to a known device (32 page-scan channels).
3. **Master/Slave switch** — roles may swap for power optimization.
4. **LMP / L2CAP / SDP / RFCOMM / OBEX / SPP** stack layers come online.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall — Don't Confuse Inquiry with Paging.** Inquiry is "who is out there?" Paging is "you, specifically, connect to me now." They use different channel sub-sets and FHS packets.

### 2.1.5 Throughput & Link Budget

The maximum *application-layer* throughput is much lower than the PHY rate because of:

- **Baseband overhead** (~30–50% of slot time for DH5).
- **L2CAP segmentation, RFCOMM framing, SDP queries**.
- **AFH (Adaptive Frequency Hopping)** to avoid Wi-Fi interference (mandatory in v1.2+).

A practical rule-of-thumb for v2.0 + EDR:

$$R_{\text{app}} \approx 0.6 \times R_{\text{PHY}}$$

So a 3-Mbps EDR link yields ~**1.8 Mbps** of real throughput, while 1-Mbps BR yields ~**700 kbps**.

---

## 2.2 Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) — Architecture & Operation

### 2.2.1 Channel Structure — The 40-Channel Engine

BLE divides the 2.4 GHz band into **40 RF channels**, each **2 MHz wide**, indexed 0–39:

$$f_k = 2402 + 2k \text{ MHz}, \quad k \in \{0, 1, \dots, 39\}$$

These are partitioned into:

- **3 Advertising Channels** (37, 38, 39) — used for broadcast, discovery, and connection initiation. Centered on the **Wi-Fi-resilient frequencies** (2402, 2426, 2480 MHz) so that 20-MHz-wide Wi-Fi channels do not always clobber them.
- **37 Data Channels** (0–36, excluding 37/38/39) — used for connected isochronous traffic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why only 3 advertising channels?** Coexistence with Wi-Fi: choosing frequencies that straddle Wi-Fi channel boundaries maximizes the chance an advertising packet is heard in a noisy environment.

### 2.2.2 Two Connectionless "Discovery" Modes

1. **Advertising** — Peripheral/Broadcaster transmits on one or more of the three advertising channels. Types include:
   - **ADV_IND** — connectable undirected (general discoverable).
   - **ADV_DIRECT_IND** — connectable directed, fast-connect, used for reconnection.
   - **ADV_NONCONN_IND** — broadcast only (e.g., iBeacon / Eddystone).
   - **ADV_SCAN_IND** — accepts scan requests for more data.
   - **Extended Advertising** (v5.0) — chained packets, off-channel chaining, 255-byte data.

2. **Scanning** — Central/Observer listens. Two flavors:
   - **Passive scan** — just hears advertising packets.
   - **Active scan** — sends SCAN_REQ on the same channel; advertiser replies with SCAN_RSP (additional 31 bytes).

Advertising interval:

$$T_{\text{adv}} \in [20 \text{ ms},\ 10240 \text{ ms}] \quad \text{(leg.); } \pm \text{random } 0\text{–}10 \text{ ms}$$

### 2.2.3 Connection Setup — The 3-Connection-Message Dance

Connection setup uses **three messages** on a single advertising channel:

1. **CONNECT_IND** (from advertiser) — contains the next hop channel, hop increment, sleep-clock-accuracy, and a CRC init.
2. **First Connection Event** — peripheral sends; central replies.
3. **Subsequent Connection Events** — periodic data exchange.

The total latency is:

$$T_{\text{conn\_setup}} \approx 1.25 \text{ ms (link-layer)} + T_{\text{adv}} + T_{\text{scan}} \approx 3 \text{ ms typical}$$

### 2.2.4 Connection Events & Interval

Once connected, both peers agree on:

- **Connection Interval (CI)** — 7.5 ms to 4 s.
- **Slave Latency (SL)** — 0 to 499 (number of CI the slave may skip).
- **Supervision Timeout (STO)** — 100 ms to 32 s.

Data rate is governed by:

$$R_{\text{eff}} = \frac{N_{\text{payload\_bytes}} \times 8}{CI_{\text{sec}}} \quad \text{where} \quad N_{\text{payload}} \in [27, 244] \text{ bytes (LE 1M)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slave Latency is the single most powerful power-saving knob in BLE.** A heart-rate monitor that reports 1 reading/s with SL = 99 and CI = 1 s actually transmits **once every 100 s**, giving years of battery life on a CR2032.

### 2.2.5 GATT / ATT — The Data Model

After the link layer, BLE defines:

- **ATT (Attribute Protocol)** — a thin client-server L2CAP-based protocol with **attributes** of the form:

  $$\text{Attribute} = \big\{\, \text{Handle},\, \text{UUID},\, \text{Value},\, \text{Permissions} \,\big\}$$

- **GATT (Generic Attribute Profile)** — groups attributes into **Services** and **Characteristics**. Standardized profiles (Heart Rate, Battery, Environmental Sensing) make BLE interoperable.

Three roles:

- **GATT Client** — reads/writes/initiates (almost always the central).
- **GATT Server** — holds the data table (almost always the peripheral).
- **Roles in GAP** — Broadcaster, Observer, Peripheral, Central (the 4 GAP roles of BLE).

### 2.2.6 Modulation & PHY Layers

All BLE PHYs use **GFSK (Gaussian Frequency-Shift Keying)** with modulation index:

$$h = 0.5 \quad \text{(LE 1M / 2M / Coded PHYs)}$$

The frequency deviation is therefore:

$$\Delta f = \frac{h \times R_{\text{sym}}}{2} = \frac{0.5 \times 1\,\text{Msps}}{2} = 250 \text{ kHz}$$

For **LE Coded (v5.0)**, an additional **Forward Error Correction (FEC)** layer is applied:

- **S = 2 (500 kbps)** — convolutional code, simple repetition.
- **S = 8 (125 kbps)** — longer pattern with error correction; ~+10 dB link-budget gain.

### 2.2.7 Power Budget Comparison

Energy to send one packet is approximated as:

$$E_{\text{pkt}} = I_{\text{tx}} \cdot V_{\text{batt}} \cdot T_{\text{tx}} + I_{\text{standby}} \cdot V_{\text{batt}} \cdot T_{\text{standby}}$$

For a CR2032 (3 V, 220 mAh):

| Mode | I_avg | Battery Life |
|---|---|---|
| BR/EDR Audio (SCO) | 25 mA | ~9 hours |
| BR/EDR Data (DH1) | 12 mA | ~18 hours |
| BLE Adv 1 s interval | 15 µA | ~1.5 years |
| BLE Conn 1 s, SL=99 | 1 µA | ~10 years |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All high-yield formulas and constants for KTU 2024 examinations on this topic.**

| # | Concept | Equation / Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BR/EDR channel spacing | $\Delta f_{ch} = 1$ | MHz |
| 2 | BR/EDR channels | $N_{ch} = 79$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | BR/EDR hop rate | $f_{hop} = 1/T_{slot} = 1600$ | hops/s |
| 4 | Slot duration | $T_{slot} = 625$ | µs |
| 5 | BR PHY rate | $R_{BR} = 1$ | Mbps (GFSK) |
| 6 | EDR-2 PHY rate | $R_{EDR2} = 2$ | Mbps (π/4-DQPSK) |
| 7 | EDR-3 PHY rate | $R_{EDR3} = 3$ | Mbps (8DPSK) |
| 8 | Max active slaves / piconet | $N_{slave} = 7$ | from 3-bit AM_ADDR |
| 9 | Max parked slaves | $N_{parked} = 255$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | BLE channels | $N_{ch}^{BLE} = 40$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | BLE channel spacing | $\Delta f_{ch}^{BLE} = 2$ | MHz |
| 12 | BLE advertising channels | $N_{adv} = 3$ | index 37, 38, 39 |
| 13 | BLE data channels | $N_{data} = 37$ | index 0–36 |
| 14 | LE 1M PHY rate | $R_{1M} = 1$ | Mbps |
| 15 | LE 2M PHY rate (v5.0) | $R_{2M} = 2$ | Mbps |
| 16 | LE Coded PHY S=2 | $R_{S2} = 500$ | kbps |
| 17 | LE Coded PHY S=8 | $R_{S8} = 125$ | kbps |
| 18 | GFSK modulation index | $h = 0.5$ | dimensionless |
| 19 | Frequency deviation | $\Delta f = 250$ | kHz |
| 20 | Conn. Interval range | $CI \in [7.5, 4000]$ | ms |
| 21 | Slave Latency range | $SL \in [0, 499]$ | dimensionless |
| 22 | Superv. Timeout range | $STO \in [100, 32000]$ | ms |
| 23 | Max L2CAP MTU (LE) | $MTU_{max} = 517$ | bytes |
| 24 | Effective conn. data rate | $R_{eff} = \frac{N_{pay} \cdot 8}{CI}$ | bits/s |
| 25 | Connection setup latency | $T_{cs} \approx 3$ | ms (typical) |
| 26 | Air-time (1 adv pkt LE 1M) | $T_{air} = 376 \, \mu s$ | µs |
| 27 | Max BLE adv payload | $L_{adv} = 37$ | bytes (legacy), 255 (ext) |
| 28 | Tx power class 1 | $P_{tx} \le 20$ | dBm |
| 29 | Tx power class 2 | $P_{tx} \le 4$ | dBm (most IoT) |
| 30 | Tx power class 3 | $P_{tx} \le 0$ | dBm |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BR/EDR** is irreplaceable for **A2DP audio streaming** (TWS earbuds, car hands-free), **HID (keyboards, mice, gamepads)**, and **SPP-based legacy industrial links** (RS-232 replacement). Its **continuous isochronous channel** and **robust frequency hopping** survive noisy environments.
- **BLE** is the **de-facto wireless fabric of IoT**: **iBeacon/Eddystone** indoor location, **wearable health** (heart rate, SpO₂, temperature), **smart-home** (door locks, light bulbs), **automotive** (TPMS, keyless entry, C-V2X companions), **industrial** sensor beacons, and the underlying PHY/MAC of **Bluetooth Mesh** (lighting, asset tracking, building automation).

> [!NOTE]
> The **Bluetooth Mesh** specification (2017) uses the **BLE advertising channels** to flood messages and is a critical enabler for **large-scale building and industrial IoT**, often examined at the conceptual level in KTU.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Hop Time and Slot Duration

> **Goal:** Show that the BR/EDR slot duration of **625 µs** is a direct consequence of the 1-MHz channel spacing and the 1600-hop/second rate.

**Step 1 — State the design constraint.**

BR/EDR requires a hop rate of exactly 1600 hops/second, defined by the Bluetooth SIG specification. The time spent on a single RF channel is called the **dwell time** and equals one **slot** of the TDD frame.

**Step 2 — Set up the relationship.**

$$f_{\text{hop}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{slot}}} \implies T_{\text{slot}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{hop}}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerically.**

$$T_{\text{slot}} = \frac{1}{1600 \text{ s}^{-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute and convert units.**

$$T_{\text{slot}} = 0.000625 \text{ s} = 625 \,\mu s$$

**Step 5 — Derive the slot count per second.**

$$\text{Slots per second} = \frac{1 \text{ s}}{625 \,\mu s} = 1600 \text{ slots/s} \quad \checkmark$$

> **Conclusion:** The 625-µs slot is the canonical TDD quantum of Bluetooth Classic; the same hop rate is *inherited* by BLE for connected traffic.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Frequency Deviation of a GFSK Transmitter

> **Goal:** Given a symbol rate of 1 Msps and a modulation index $h = 0.5$, derive the **250 kHz** frequency deviation used by LE 1M PHY.

**Step 1 — Definition of the GFSK modulation index.**

For binary GFSK the modulation index is:

$$h = \frac{2 \, \Delta f}{R_{sym}}$$

where $R_{sym}$ is the symbol rate and $\Delta f$ is the peak frequency deviation of the underlying FSK tone.

**Step 2 — Rearrange for the deviation.**

$$\Delta f = \frac{h \times R_{sym}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $h = 0.5$ and $R_{sym} = 1\,\text{Msps}$.**

$$\Delta f = \frac{0.5 \times 1 \times 10^{6}}{2} \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate.**

$$\Delta f = \frac{500 \times 10^{3}}{2} = 250 \times 10^{3} \text{ Hz} = 250 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 5 — Verify against channel spacing.**

The BLE channel spacing is 2 MHz; with $h = 0.5$ the **occupied bandwidth** is:

$$BW_{occ} \approx 2 \,(\Delta f + R_{sym}/2) = 2 \times (250 + 500) = 1500 \text{ kHz} \le 2 \text{ MHz} \quad \checkmark$$

> **Conclusion:** The 250-kHz deviation is the optimum that confines the spectrum within the 2-MHz channel while preserving orthogonality.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Effective BLE Throughput

> **Goal:** Compute the application-layer throughput when a BLE peripheral reports 20 bytes of sensor data every connection interval $CI = 100 \text{ ms}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

$$N_{\text{pay}} = 20 \text{ bytes}, \quad CI = 100 \text{ ms}, \quad R_{\text{PHY}} = 1 \text{ Mbps (LE 1M)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the throughput formula.**

$$R_{\text{eff}} = \frac{N_{\text{pay}} \times 8}{CI}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and convert CI to seconds.**

$$R_{\text{eff}} = \frac{20 \times 8}{0.100} = \frac{160}{0.100}$$

**Step 4 — Compute.**

$$R_{\text{eff}} = 1600 \text{ bps} = 1.6 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the raw PHY capacity.**

$$\eta = \frac{R_{\text{eff}}}{R_{\text{PHY}}} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{3}}{1 \times 10^{6}} = 0.0016 = 0.16\%$$

> **Conclusion:** BLE is **not** designed for streaming — its strength is *energy-per-bit*, not bulk throughput. A 1.6-kbps application on a 1-Mbps PHY is typical and *intentional* in IoT design.

---

## 3.4 Derivation D — Energy Saved by Slave Latency

> **Goal:** Show that slave latency $SL = 99$ with $CI = 1 \text{ s}$ reduces wake-ups from 1 Hz to 0.01 Hz.

**Step 1 — Effective wake-up period.**

$$T_{\text{wake}} = CI \times (SL + 1)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$T_{\text{wake}} = 1 \text{ s} \times (99 + 1) = 100 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Effective average data-report rate.**

$$f_{\text{report}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{wake}}} = \frac{1}{100 \text{ s}} = 0.01 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Duty cycle.**

$$D = \frac{1}{SL + 1} = \frac{1}{100} = 1\%$$

**Step 5 — Energy saving (assuming identical per-event energy).**

$$\frac{E_{\text{with\_SL}}}{E_{\text{no\_SL}}} = 1\% \implies 99\% \text{ energy reduction} \quad \checkmark$$

> **Conclusion:** Slave Latency is the **single most important BLE power-saving lever** for periodic telemetry.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — BR/EDR vs. BLE

| Dimension | BR/EDR | BLE | IoT Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power | mA continuous | µA average | BLE enables coin-cell IoT |
| Latency | 100 ms+ connect | 3 ms connect | BLE → real-time control loops |
| Topology | Piconet (8 nodes) | Star (theoretical 2¹⁶), Mesh via flood | BR/EDR → small PAN; BLE → building-scale |
| Data model | L2CAP, RFCOMM, OBEX | ATT/GATT | BLE → standardized IoT profiles |
| Range | 10 m typical | 200 m (LE Coded) | BLE → smart agriculture, asset tracking |
| Throughput | 1–3 Mbps | 0.125–2 Mbps | BR/EDR → audio; BLE → telemetry |
| Spectrum use | 79 × 1 MHz | 40 × 2 MHz | BR/EDR denser; BLE sparser + robust |
| Setup | Inquiry + Page | Advertise + Connect (1 message) | BLE → faster, lower energy |
| Voice | SCO/eSCO with mSBC | LE Audio (LC3, v5.2) | Industry shift to LE Audio |
| Modulation | GFSK, π/4-DQPSK, 8DPSK | GFSK (all PHYs) | BR/EDR higher spectral efficiency |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bluetooth BR/EDR — Piconet + Scatternet Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    masterA[Master A<br/>BR EDR piconet 1]
    slaveA1[Slave 1<br/>AM ADDR 001]
    slaveA2[Slave 2<br/>AM ADDR 010]
    slaveA3[Slave 3<br/>AM ADDR 011]
    masterB[Master B<br/>BR EDR piconet 2]
    slaveB1[Slave 1<br/>AM ADDR 001]
    slaveB2[Slave 2<br/>AM ADDR 010]
    bridge[Bridge Node<br/>acts in two piconets]

    masterA --- slaveA1
    masterA --- slaveA2
    masterA --- slaveA3
    masterB --- slaveB1
    masterB --- slaveB2
    masterA --- bridge
    masterB --- bridge

    classDef master fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef slave fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef bridge fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#B22222,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class masterA,masterB master
    class slaveA1,slaveA2,slaveA3,slaveB1,slaveB2 slave
    class bridge bridge
```

> **Reading guide:** Two piconets (gold = master, blue = slaves) share one **bridge** node (pink) to form a scatternet. The bridge participates in *both* hopping sequences, which is why scatternets are rare in production deployments — they add jitter and complexity.

---

## 4.2 Bluetooth Low Energy — GAP Roles, Advertising, and Connection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Peripheral<br/>GAP Broadcaster + Advertiser] -->|ADV IND on ch 37 38 39| B[Central<br/>GAP Observer + Scanner]
    B -->|CONN REQ| A
    A -->|Connection event on data channel| C[GATT Server<br/>Services and Characteristics]
    B -->|Read Write Notify Indicate| C
    C -->|Notify| B

    subgraph ChannelMap
    ch37[Channel 37<br/>2402 MHz]
    ch38[Channel 38<br/>2426 MHz]
    ch39[Channel 39<br/>2480 MHz]
    ch0[Channels 0 to 36<br/>data only]
    end

    A -. uses .-> ch37
    A -. uses .-> ch38
    A -. uses .-> ch39
    A -. after connect .-> ch0

    classDef peripheral fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef central fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gatt fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef channel fill:#FFEFD5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class A peripheral
    class B central
    class C gatt
    class ch37,ch38,ch39,ch0 channel
```

> **Reading guide:** The peripheral **advertises** on the three **primary advertising channels** (cream nodes). The central **scans**, then sends a **CONNECT_IND** that triggers a frequency-hop on the **37 data channels**. Data is exchanged via **GATT** (purple).

---

## 4.3 Protocol Stack Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph BR_EDR_Stack
    BRApp[Application<br/>A2DP HFP HID SPP OBEX]
    BRRFCOMM[RFCOMM]
    BRL2CAP[L2CAP]
    BRLMP[LMP]
    BRBB[Baseband]
    BRRF[Radio 79 ch 1 MHz]
    BRApp --> BRRFCOMM --> BRL2CAP --> BRLMP --> BRBB --> BRRF
    end

    subgraph BLE_Stack
    BLEApp[Application<br/>GATT Profiles]
    BLEGATT[GATT]
    BLEATT[ATT]
    BLEL2CAP[L2CAP LE]
    BLELL[Link Layer]
    BLERF[Radio 40 ch 2 MHz]
    BLEApp --> BLEGATT --> BLEATT --> BLEL2CAP --> BLELL --> BLERF
    end

    classDef classic fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ble fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class BRApp,BRRFCOMM,BRL2CAP,BRLMP,BRBB,BRRF classic
    class BLEApp,BLEGATT,BLEATT,BLEL2CAP,BLELL,BLERF ble
```

> **Reading guide:** BR/EDR (left, beige) carries rich profile layer like **RFCOMM/OBEX**; BLE (right, blue) collapses everything into the **GATT/ATT** attribute table. The PHY and L2CAP layers remain the only direct peers between the two stacks.

---

## 4.4 BLE Connection Event Timing Diagram

```mermaid
gantt
    title BLE Connection Events with Slave Latency SL equals 3
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section Central TX then RX
    Central TX to peripheral    :a1, 0, 1ms
    Peripheral TX to central    :a2, 1, 1ms
    section Connection Interval 1 s
    CI 0    :active, ci0, 2, 1
    CI 1    :crit, ci1, 1002, 1
    CI 2    :crit, ci2, 2002, 1
    CI 3    :crit, ci3, 3002, 1
    CI 4    :active, ci4, 4002, 1
    section Slave skip
    Slave skips  :skip, 1002, 3000
```

> **Reading guide:** With **$SL = 3$** the slave may **skip 3 connection intervals** between each listen window. Effective report rate: once every $4 \times CI$.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a BLE SoC (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    APP[Application CPU<br/>ARM Cortex M4]
    RADIO[Radio<br/>2.4 GHz GFSK]
    PHY[PHY Layer]
    BB[Baseband Link Layer]
    HCI[HCI Optional]
    HOST[Host Stack<br/>L2CAP ATT GATT GAP SM]
    PROFILES[Profiles<br/>Heart Rate Battery]
    APP_CTRL[Profile Logic]
    SENSOR[I2C SPI GPIO ADC]
    SENSOR_DATA[Sensor<br/>Temp HR IMU]
    BAT[Coin Cell<br/>CR2032]
    PMU[PMU DC DC LDO]
    ANT[Antenna 2.4 GHz]

    SENSOR_DATA --> SENSOR --> APP_CTRL
    APP_CTRL --> PROFILES --> HOST --> BB --> PHY --> RADIO --> ANT
    BB -->|events| APP
    APP -->|commands| BB
    BAT --> PMU --> APP
    BAT --> PMU --> RADIO
    PMU --> PHY
    PMU --> BB
    PMU --> HOST

    classDef app fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef radio fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#B22222,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef host fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef power fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class APP,APP_CTRL,PROFILES app
    class RADIO,PHY,BB,HCI radio
    class HOST,SENSOR,SENSOR_DATA host
    class BAT,PMU,ANT power
```

> **Reading guide:** The PMU (grey) is critical — it powers **all blocks with sequenced voltage rails** to enable deep sleep between connection events. The sensor (blue) feeds the application (yellow), which feeds the host stack, which drives the radio (pink) via the baseband/PHY.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Pattern (PCCGT602 ESE):** Part A — 3 marks × 5 questions (compulsory). Part B — 14 marks × 3 modules (one full question per module, internal choice between Question A and Question B). Each Part-B question typically has **two 7-mark sub-parts**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
Differentiate between Bluetooth BR/EDR and BLE in terms of **physical channels, modulation, and connection setup time**.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | BR/EDR | BLE |
|---|---|---|
| Physical channels | 79 × 1 MHz | 40 × 2 MHz |
| Modulation | GFSK (BR) + π/4-DQPSK + 8DPSK (EDR) | GFSK only |
| Connection setup | Inquiry + Page (≈ 5 s) | Advertise + Connect (≈ 3 ms) |
| Advertising ch. | None | 3 fixed (37/38/39) |
| Hop rate | 1600 hop/s | 1600 hop/s (conn.) |
| Typical data rate | 1/2/3 Mbps | 1/2 Mbps + 500/125 kbps (Coded) |

The connection setup for BLE is **~3 orders of magnitude faster** because it uses a single-message (CONNECT_IND) handshake on the advertising channels, whereas BR/EDR requires separate inquiry and paging sub-states with their own hopping patterns. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**
Define **Slave Latency** in BLE. How does it affect power consumption?

**Model Answer:**
**Slave Latency (SL)** is the number of connection events a peripheral may **skip** without listening, while still maintaining a logical link.

- **Range:** 0 to 499
- **Effective period:** $T_{eff} = CI \times (SL + 1)$
- **Power impact:** The radio stays in sleep mode during the skipped intervals; the average current drain drops roughly as $1/(SL + 1)$.
- **Example:** $SL = 99$, $CI = 1 \text{ s} \Rightarrow T_{eff} = 100 \text{ s} \Rightarrow$ 99% energy reduction.

This is the primary low-power lever of BLE. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice A / B)

### Question A (14 marks) **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023, Model QP]**

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the Bluetooth BR/EDR piconet architecture. Discuss the role of the master, the maximum number of active slaves, and the formation of a scatternet.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Piconet definition:** A piconet is the basic unit of a Bluetooth Classic network, comprising **one master** and **up to 7 active slaves** sharing the master's clock and FHSS sequence.
2. **Master's role:** The master:
   - Defines the **79-channel hopping pattern** (driven by its UAP/LAP and the CLK register).
   - **Polls** slaves in TDD slots (625 µs each).
   - Manages **paging, inquiry, and connection state**.
3. **Active slave count:** Limited to 7 because the **Active Member Address (AM_ADDR)** is a 3-bit field. An additional 255 slaves can be **parked** (held in low-power state, addressable).
4. **Scatternet formation:** When a node belongs to two piconets, it acts as a bridge. **Inter-piconet communication** must be time-multiplexed because the bridge has only one radio.
5. **Diagram:** (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram).
6. **Limitations:** Scatternets add **clock drift, scheduling overhead, and asymmetric throughput**. They are rarely deployed in practice.

> **Valuation note:** Drawing is worth **2 marks**; the rest is the description.

**(b) Explain the physical channel, modulation, and baseband packet format of Bluetooth EDR. Compare BR and EDR-3 data rates.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Physical channel:** 2.402–2.480 GHz, **79 channels** with **1 MHz spacing**, FHSS at 1600 hops/s.
2. **Modulation:**
   - **BR packets** use **GFSK** with $h = 0.3$–0.35, $R = 1$ Mbps.
   - **EDR packets** retain a GFSK **access code + header** (for backward compatibility) and switch to π/4-DQPSK (**EDR-2, 2 Mbps**) or 8DPSK (**EDR-3, 3 Mbps**) for the **synchronous payload**.
3. **Packet format:** Access code (68/72 b) → Header (54 b) → Payload. EDR adds a **guard time** and **synchronization sequence** between header and payload.
4. **Data-rate comparison:**

$$R_{BR} = 1 \text{ Mbps}, \quad R_{EDR2} = 2 \text{ Mbps}, \quad R_{EDR3} = 3 \text{ Mbps}$$

5. **Why EDR was introduced:** Higher-order modulations are spectrally more efficient — the same 1-MHz slot can carry 2× or 3× the data without expanding bandwidth.
6. **Real-world use:** EDR-3 powers **A2DP stereo audio** in TWS earbuds and **fast object-exchange (OBEX)** transfers.

> **Valuation note:** Mentioning the **mixed-modulation trick** (GFSK header + DPSK payload) is worth 1 mark and is a common KTU differentiator.

---

### Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023, Model QP]**

**(a) Explain the BLE channel structure. Why are only 3 channels used for advertising? Describe the BLE connection setup procedure.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **40 channels** indexed 0–39, each 2 MHz wide, spanning 2.402–2.480 GHz:

$$f_k = 2402 + 2k \text{ MHz}$$

2. **Partitioning:**
   - **3 advertising channels** at indices **37, 38, 39** → 2402, 2426, 2480 MHz.
   - **37 data channels** at indices 0–36.
3. **Why exactly 3 advertising channels?**
   - **Coexistence:** Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n/ac occupies 20-/40-/80-/160-MHz blocks. The 3 BLE channels are **deliberately placed at the edges and middle of the 2.4 GHz band**, maximizing the chance that *at least one* advertising channel falls in a Wi-Fi gap.
   - **Power:** Listening on 40 channels is expensive; scanning 3 is cheap and fast.
4. **Connection setup procedure (3 messages):**
   - **Step 1 — Advertise:** Peripheral sends ADV_IND on 1+ of the 3 adv channels at $T_{adv} \in [20, 10240]$ ms.
   - **Step 2 — Scan:** Central listens (passive) or sends SCAN_REQ (active) → gets SCAN_RSP.
   - **Step 3 — Connect:** Central sends **CONNECT_IND** carrying the **hop increment (hopInc), channel map, CRC init, sleep clock accuracy, and the next anchor point**.
   - **Step 4 — First connection event:** Both sides hop to the first data channel; first packet exchange happens ~1.25 ms later.
   - **Total latency:** ~**3 ms** typical, vs. ~5 s for BR/EDR.
5. **Post-connect state machine:** Connection parameters (CI, SL, STO) are negotiated; the link is now a periodic, channel-hopping duplex.

**(b) With a clear diagram, describe the BLE GATT data model. Explain services, characteristics, descriptors, and the role of the GATT client and server.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **GATT = Generic Attribute Profile**, defined on top of the **Attribute Protocol (ATT)**.
2. **Attribute = {Handle, UUID, Value, Permissions}** — a flat table stored on the **GATT server**.
3. **Hierarchy:**

   ```mermaid
   graph TD
   PROFILE[Profile e.g. Heart Rate] --> SERVICE[Service UUID 0x180D Heart Rate]
   PROFILE --> BAT[Service UUID 0x180F Battery]
   SERVICE --> CH1[Char HR Measurement UUID 0x2A37 Notify]
   SERVICE --> CH2[Char Body Sensor Location UUID 0x2A38 Read]
   CH1 --> DESC1[Descriptor CCCD UUID 0x2902]
   CH1 --> DESC2[Descriptor User Description UUID 0x2901]
   BAT --> BCH[Char Battery Level UUID 0x2A19 Read Notify]
   ```

4. **Term definitions:**
   - **Service** — a collection of related characteristics. Primary (0x2800) or Secondary (0x2801).
   - **Characteristic** — a typed data value with declared **properties** (Read, Write, WriteWithoutResponse, Notify, Indicate, Broadcast).
   - **Descriptor** — metadata about a characteristic (CCCD enables Notify, User Description gives a human label).
5. **Roles:**
   - **GATT Server** — holds the attribute table; almost always the **peripheral** (sensor, light bulb, watch).
   - **GATT Client** — reads/writes/subscribes; almost always the **central** (phone, gateway).
6. **Operations:** *Read Request, Read Response, Write Request/Response, Notification (server-initiated, no ack), Indication (server-initiated, with ack)*.
7. **Standardized profiles** (Bluetooth SIG-adopted): Heart Rate, Battery Service, Environmental Sensing, Glucose, Health Thermometer, etc. — ensure multi-vendor interoperability.

> **Valuation note:** Drawing the service/characteristic hierarchy in any form is worth **2 marks**; the rest is the descriptor of operations.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on Bluetooth BR/EDR vs. BLE questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating inquiry and paging** in BR/EDR — these are *different* sub-states with different channel maps. 1 mark lost if the answer does not distinguish them.
> 2. **Forgetting the GFSK header in EDR packets** — EDR is *not* pure π/4-DQPSK/8DPSK; the access code and header are still GFSK for backward compatibility. 1 mark lost.
> 3. **Stating "BLE has 79 channels"** — Wrong. BLE has exactly **40 channels (2 MHz spacing)**. This is a recurring 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Drawing the wrong number of advertising channels** — BLE uses **3** (ch 37, 38, 39), not 4, not 7. The Wi-Fi coexistence *reason* must be stated to get full marks.
> 5. **Ignoring Slave Latency range** — the range is 0–499, **not** 0–255. A common error.
> 6. **Saying BR/EDR uses 8DPSK for everything** — only the EDR payload uses 8DPSK; the access code and header remain GFSK.
> 7. **Mixing up GATT and GAP** — GATT is the **data** profile (services/characteristics); GAP is the **role + discovery + connection** layer. The 4 GAP roles are Broadcaster, Observer, Peripheral, Central.
> 8. **Omitting the 3-Mbps BR/EDR air-time vs. payload distinction** — The PHY rate is *not* the application throughput.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **BR/EDR** = "Classic" Bluetooth, **79 × 1 MHz channels**, 1/2/3 Mbps, GFSK + π/4-DQPSK + 8DPSK, 1600 hops/s, 625-µs slots.
- ✅ **BLE** = "Smart" Bluetooth (v4.0+), **40 × 2 MHz channels**, 1/2 Mbps + 500/125 kbps (Coded), **GFSK only**, 3 advertising channels (37/38/39) + 37 data channels.
- ✅ **Piconet**: 1 master + up to 7 active slaves + 255 parked; **Scatternet** = multiple piconets sharing a bridge node.
- ✅ **BLE roles** — GAP: Broadcaster, Observer, Peripheral, Central.
- ✅ **GATT data model** — Attributes (Handle, UUID, Value, Permissions) → Services → Characteristics → Descriptors.
- ✅ **Connection setup** — BR/EDR ≈ 5 s (Inquiry + Page); BLE ≈ 3 ms (Advertise + Connect on a single message).
- ✅ **Power levers of BLE** — Slave Latency (0–499), Connection Interval (7.5 ms–4 s), Advertising Interval, TX Power.
- ✅ **LE Coded PHY** — $S = 2$ → 500 kbps; $S = 8$ → 125 kbps with **+10 dB link-budget gain** (long range).
- ✅ **LE 2M PHY** (v5.0) → 2 Mbps at same 1 mW / no range penalty.
- ✅ **Modulation index** $h = 0.5$, frequency deviation $\Delta f = 250$ kHz for LE 1M/2M.
- ✅ **Max L2CAP MTU (LE)** = 517 bytes; max legacy adv payload = 37 bytes (255 bytes for extended advertising).
- ✅ **Hop dwell** = 1 / 1600 s = **625 µs** for both BR/EDR and BLE connection state.
- ✅ **Bluetooth Mesh** (2017) is built on top of **BLE advertising channels**; not the same as BR/EDR scatternet.
- ✅ **LE Audio** (v5.2) uses LC3 codec, isochronous channels, and **replaces** the BR/EDR SCO/eSCO voice path in next-generation devices.
- ✅ **Use-case mapping:** BR/EDR → audio, HID, SPP; BLE → beacons, wearables, smart home, industrial telemetry, automotive.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ZigBee IP/ZigBee SE 2.0
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS AND SMART COMPUTING.
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** ZigBee IP/ZigBee SE 2.0

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 ZigBee IP — Formal Definition

**ZigBee IP** is the first open, **IPv6-based** wireless mesh networking standard built specifically for low-power, low-data-rate, and lossy networks (LLNs). It is a complete standards-based networking stack that combines the **IEEE 802.15.4** physical and MAC layers with the **IETF 6LoWPAN** adaptation layer, the **RPL** (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks), and the **UDP/TCP** transport protocols, all running natively over **IPv6**.

In simpler KTU-syllabus terminology:
> ZigBee IP is essentially a *"Thread-like"* IoT networking stack that lets every sensor node have a **global IPv6 address** and talk directly to the Internet, while still forming a self-healing, low-power mesh.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key fact for the KTU board:** ZigBee IP ≠ ZigBee. ZigBee (PRO) is a **closed, application-layer** profile that uses 802.15.4 and proprietary mesh routing. **ZigBee IP** is the **next-generation, IP-native** evolution that opens the door to **direct Internet integration** of every node.

### 1.2 ZigBee Smart Energy (SE) 2.0 — Formal Definition

**ZigBee Smart Energy 2.0** (often written as **ZigBee SEP 2** or **SE 2.0**) is the **application-layer standard** that runs *on top of* ZigBee IP, dedicated to **smart energy / smart metering** use cases. It defines the commands, clusters, and procedures used by utility meters, in-home displays, smart appliances, energy management systems, and demand-response servers to communicate securely.

> [!NOTE]
> **SE 1.x** ran on top of the proprietary ZigBee PRO stack. **SE 2.0** was redesigned to run on top of the **IP stack** (ZigBee IP / IP-suite), giving utilities a single converged IP-based infrastructure for AMI, HAN, DR, and the broader IoT.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **post-office system**:
- The **letter (data)** is your energy reading.
- The **house address (IPv6 address)** lets any letter reach any house on Earth without translation.
- The **postal van route (RPL mesh routing)** figures out the cheapest, shortest path through neighbouring vans.
- The **postal department regulation (SE 2.0)** decides the *format* of the letter, *who* is allowed to send it, and *how* billing/DR notices are written.

So: **ZigBee IP = the postal road network for IoT**, and **ZigBee SE 2.0 = the official format of energy letters** exchanged on that network.

### 1.4 High-Yield Tagline for the Board

> *"ZigBee IP provides the **transport**; ZigBee SE 2.0 provides the **application language** for smart energy."*

### 1.5 Why this topic matters in KTU Examinations

| Reason | Board Impact |
|---|---|
| Direct coverage under *Module 2 – IoT Connectivity Protocols* | Frequently asked 7/14 mark questions |
| Connects 6LoWPAN, RPL, and 802.15.4 (already taught) | Tests integration ability |
| Industry relevance: deployed globally by utilities | Examiner loves real-world examples |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of ZigBee IP

ZigBee IP is **not a single protocol**; it is a **stack** of standards. From bottom to top:

1. **Physical / MAC Layer — IEEE 802.15.4**
   - Operates in **2.4 GHz** ISM band (16 channels), with optional sub-GHz variants in 868/915 MHz.
   - Data rates: **250 kbps** at 2.4 GHz, **20/40 kbps** in sub-GHz bands.
   - Provides **CSMA/CA**, **ACKs**, and **frame security** at the link layer.

2. **Adaptation Layer — 6LoWPAN (RFC 4944, RFC 6282)**
   - Compresses full **128-bit IPv6 headers** down to a few bytes using **IPHC** (IP Header Compression).
   - Performs **fragmentation & reassembly** because 802.15.4 MAC frames are limited to **127 bytes**.
   - Provides **Mesh-Addressing** for 802.15.4 networks.

3. **Network Layer — IPv6 + RPL (RFC 6550)**
   - Every node has a **unique 128-bit IPv6 address** (often derived from its 64-bit EUI-64).
   - **RPL** builds a **DODAG** (Destination Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph) rooted at a border router.
   - Uses **Objective Functions (OF)** like **OF0** (min-hop) and **MRHOF** (ETX-based) to choose the best path.
   - Two control messages: **DIO** (DODAG Information Object), **DAO** (Destination Advertisement Object).

4. **Transport Layer — UDP (and TCP where needed)**
   - UDP is preferred because it is **lightweight** and supports low-power sleeping nodes.
   - **CoAP** (Constrained Application Protocol, RFC 7252) typically runs over UDP.

5. **Application Layer**
   - **CoAP** for RESTful request/response.
   - **DTLS** (Datagram TLS) for end-to-end security.
   - **ZigBee Cluster Library (ZCL)** style attributes/commands carried over CoAP.
   - In the case of **SE 2.0**, the application layer is **SEP 2**, which is essentially a set of RESTful resources and a specific cluster model.

## 2.2 ZigBee SE 2.0 — Functional Building Blocks

The Smart Energy Profile 2.0 standardises the following **functional blocks** (this is the most important table for the 14-mark answers):

| Functional Block | Purpose | Typical Device |
|---|---|---|
| **Metering** | Periodic & on-demand reading of energy/gas/water usage | Smart Meter (ESI) |
| **In-Home Display (IHD)** | Shows consumption, price, messages | Display unit |
| **Pricing** | Publishes tariffs (flat, TOU, CPP) | Utility head-end |
| **Demand Response / Load Control (DRLC)** | Sends peak/off-peak signals to shift load | Smart appliance, thermostat |
| **Billing** | Retrieves historical bills & statements | Customer portal |
| **Prepayment** | Top-up energy credit, emergency credit | Prepay meter |
| **Text Messaging** | Utility → consumer notifications | IHD, app |
| **Energy Management** | Optimises consumption based on price & comfort | EMS, home gateway |
| **Distributed Energy Resources (DER)** | Manages solar, batteries, EV charging | Inverter, EVSE |

## 2.3 Security Architecture of ZigBee IP / SE 2.0

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Security is the **single most asked** sub-topic in the board paper for SE 2.0.

ZigBee IP mandates the following:
- **Link Layer (802.15.4)**: AES-128-CCM with **128-bit MIC** using 802.15.4 security suite.
- **Adaptation Layer**: 6LoWPAN hop-by-hop encryption (optional).
- **Network Layer**: RPL security with **pre-installed or enrolment keys**.
- **Transport/Application**: **DTLS** with **ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography)**, typically **secp256r1** for key exchange and **AES-128-CCM** for confidentiality.
- **Application (SE 2.0)**: Adds **role-based access (RBAC)** with **PKI certificates** issued by the utility.

Authentication is performed using **X.509 v3 certificates** and a **Certificate Trust Center** run by the utility or its delegate.

## 2.4 Differences: ZigBee PRO vs. ZigBee IP

| Parameter | ZigBee PRO (legacy) | ZigBee IP |
|---|---|---|
| Network Layer | ZigBee NWK (proprietary) | IPv6 / RPL |
| Addressing | 16-bit NWK address | 128-bit IPv6 |
| Routing | AODV-style mesh | RPL DODAG |
| Application | ZigBee Cluster Library over APS | CoAP / ZCL over UDP |
| Internet integration | Requires gateway | Native |
| Security | ZigBee Trust Center | DTLS + ECC + PKI |
| Optimised for | Closed, isolated HANs | Open, IP-based IoT |

## 2.5 Differences: SE 1.x vs. SE 2.0

| Parameter | SE 1.x (ZigBee) | SE 2.0 (IP) |
|---|---|---|
| Underlying stack | ZigBee PRO | ZigBee IP / IP stack |
| Transport | APS / NWK | CoAP / UDP |
| Security | ZigBee Trust Center | DTLS, X.509, ECC |
| Architecture | Closed HAN | Open, IP-native |
| Cert authority | Single trust centre | Distributed PKI |
| Functionality | Metering, DRLC, Pricing | All of SE 1.x + DER + Prepay + Text + Energy Mgmt |
| Reachability | Within HAN | Beyond HAN to cloud |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Numerical & Memory)

> [!NOTE]
> Most ZigBee IP/SE 2.0 questions are **descriptive**, but a few numerics are asked based on 802.15.4 framing.

| Quantity | Formula / Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 802.15.4 MAC payload max | $127 - 25 = 102\ \text{bytes}$ | minus MAC header, FCS |
| After 6LoWPAN compression | effective IPv6 header $\approx 6\text{–}8\ \text{bytes}$ | IPHC compression |
| Max fragments | $\left\lceil \dfrac{\text{IPv6 MTU (1280 B)}}{\text{link fragment size}} \right\rceil$ | from RFC 4944 |
| IPv6 address size | $128\ \text{bits}$ | native |
| 6LoWPAN link-local prefix | $\text{FE80::}/10$ | RFC 4291 |
| Data rate @ 2.4 GHz | $250\ \text{kbps}$ | O-QPSK modulation |
| Radio channels @ 2.4 GHz | $16$ | 5 MHz spacing |
| CoAP message overhead | $\approx 4\ \text{bytes}$ | TKL field |
| Energy per data byte | $\propto \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 8}{R}\ \text{J}$ | $R$ = bit-rate |

> No `|` characters are used inside table cells; the delimiter has been replaced with $\vert$ (rendered as \vert) to preserve Markdown table integrity.

## 2.7 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Smart Meters** in households (UK SMETS2, US AMI deployments) use ZigBee IP + SE 2.0 to expose consumption to in-home displays and the utility.
- **Microgrids and DER** use SE 2.0 to coordinate rooftop solar, battery storage, and EV chargers.
- **Home Energy Management Systems (HEMS)** talk directly to the cloud over IPv6 without a proprietary gateway.
- It bridges the gap between **utility-grade reliability** and **consumer-grade IP networking**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Logical Derivation: Why ZigBee IP was created (the *Why* behind the *What*)

The KTU board often asks: *"Why did ZigBee introduce an IP-based stack when ZigBee PRO was already working?"*

We will derive the answer step by step.

### Step 1 — Identify the limitation of ZigBee PRO
ZigBee PRO uses a **16-bit NWK address** and a **proprietary APS layer**. Therefore:
- A ZigBee PRO node **cannot be reached directly from the Internet**.
- It must pass through a **gateway** that translates between ZigBee semantics and IP semantics.
- This gateway becomes a **single point of failure** and a **bottleneck**.

### Step 2 — Identify the cost of this limitation
For utility-scale deployments, the gateway model is **expensive**:
- Each HAN needs a dedicated gateway (ZigBee <-> IP bridge).
- Firmware updates, security patches, and protocol upgrades must be coordinated through this gateway.
- Custom API translations delay product launches.

### Step 3 — Adopt the IP paradigm
The IoT industry converged on **IPv6** (RFC 8200) because:
- It is **ubiquitous** and supported by every laptop, smartphone, router, and cloud service.
- It provides **unlimited addressing** ($2^{128}$ addresses).
- It enables **end-to-end security** with IPsec/TLS/DTLS.

### Step 4 — Wrap IPv6 for 802.15.4
But 802.15.4 frames are too small to carry a full IPv6 packet (40-byte header). Hence **6LoWPAN (RFC 4944 / 6282)** was created to compress the IPv6 header.

### Step 5 — Pick a routing protocol for lossy links
Traditional Internet routing (OSPF, BGP) is too heavy for sleepy, lossy 802.15.4 links. Hence **RPL (RFC 6550)** was designed specifically for **LLNs** (Low-power and Lossy Networks).

### Step 6 — Standardise an application profile for energy
Now that the IP transport exists, a **standardised energy application profile** is needed — this is **ZigBee Smart Energy Profile 2.0 (SEP 2)**.

> **Final conclusion (board-ready line):**
> *"ZigBee IP was created to remove the proprietary gateway, enable native IPv6 connectivity for every low-power device, and provide a standards-based mesh; SE 2.0 was then layered on top to define the application semantics for smart energy."*

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Frame Construction in ZigBee IP

When an SE 2.0 application on Node A sends a "Report Consumption" message to the utility server, the packet undergoes the following encapsulation:

| Layer | Header / Field | Typical Size (bytes) | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| Application (SE 2.0 XML/EXI) | Payload | 50 – 200 | "Report Consumption" |
| CoAP | Type, Code, MID, Token | 4 | RESTful mapping |
| DTLS | Record, MAC | 13 + 8 | Confidentiality + Integrity |
| UDP | src, dst port, length, cksum | 8 | Multiplexing |
| IPv6 | 128-bit src, dst addresses | 40 (→ 6–8 with IPHC) | Logical addressing |
| 6LoWPAN | Mesh addr, frag hdr | 4 – 16 | Compression + fragmentation |
| 802.15.4 MAC | Frame ctrl, PAN, seq, addr | 9 – 25 | Local addressing, ACK |
| 802.15.4 PHY | Preamble, SFD, length | 6 | Radio transmission |

### Step-by-step frame walkthrough
1. **SE 2.0 application** in the meter constructs an *EXI-encoded* (Efficient XML Interchange) "ReportConsumption" message containing kWh readings.
2. The message is handed to **CoAP** as a POST to a URI such as `coap://[utility-ipv6]/edev/1/usagepoint/2/reading`.
3. **DTLS** encrypts the CoAP datagram using the pre-shared key (or mutual auth with X.509).
4. **UDP** wraps the DTLS record using the well-known CoAP port **5683** (or 5684 for DTLS).
5. **IPv6** attaches source and destination addresses. The destination is the utility's border router.
6. **6LoWPAN** compresses the 40-byte IPv6 header to 6–8 bytes using IPHC and tags the message with a **mesh** address.
7. **802.15.4 MAC** transmits the frame over the 2.4 GHz radio.
8. Each **mesh hop** in the RPL DODAG re-encapsulates with 6LoWPAN until the packet reaches the **Border Router**.
9. The Border Router hands the packet to the **utility's IP backhaul**, which forwards the IPv6 packet normally to the energy server.
10. The server decrypts with DTLS, parses the CoAP POST, and updates the consumer's account.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison: SE 1.x vs. SE 2.0 (Derivation Logic)

To derive *why* SE 2.0 was redesigned rather than patched:

- **Functional growth**: SE 1.x lacked native DER, prepayment, text messaging and energy management blocks → needed new functional blocks.
- **Architectural pressure**: Utility networks were becoming IP-based (TCP/IP, IPv4/v6) → bridging two stacks increased cost.
- **Security evolution**: SE 1.x's 128-bit symmetric-key trust centre was insufficient against modern attacks → ECC + PKI is mandatory in SE 2.0.
- **Cloud readiness**: SE 2.0's RESTful (CoAP) model maps directly to HTTP-based cloud APIs.

Therefore: **SE 2.0 = SE 1.x functionality + IP-native transport + modern PKI + cloud mapping**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical: 6LoWPAN Fragmentation

> **Q:** A 1280-byte IPv6 packet must be carried over 802.15.4 with 81 bytes of payload per fragment. How many 6LoWPAN fragments are produced?

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{frags}} &= \left\lceil \frac{L_{\text{IPv6}}}{L_{\text{frag}}} \right\rvert_{\text{ceil}} \\[4pt]
&= \left\lceil \frac{1280}{81} \right\rceil \\[4pt]
&= \lceil 15.80\ldots \rceil \\[4pt]
&= 16\ \text{fragments}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The first fragment carries the **compressed IPv6 header**; subsequent fragments carry **fragment headers** with offset increments of 81 bytes.

This confirms that 6LoWPAN's fragmentation extends IPv6's 1280-byte MTU gracefully across 802.15.4's tiny 81-byte link-MTU.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ZigBee IP Protocol Stack (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP["SE 2.0 APPLICATION LAYER\nMetering Pricing DRLC DER Text Billing"]
    COAP["COAP RFC 7252\nRESTful request response"]
    DTLS["DTLS RFC 6347\nECC secp256r1 AES 128 CCM"]
    UDP["UDP RFC 768\nPorts 5683 and 5684"]
    IPV6["IPV6 RFC 8200\n128 bit addressing"]
    LOW["6LoWPAN RFC 4944 6282\nHeader compression Fragmentation"]
    MAC["IEEE 802.15.4 MAC\nCSMA CA ACK Frame security"]
    PHY["IEEE 802.15.4 PHY\n2.4 GHz 250 kbps 16 channels"]
    RADIO["RADIO AIR INTERFACE"]

    APP --> COAP --> DTLS --> UDP --> IPV6 --> LOW --> MAC --> PHY --> RADIO
```

## 4.2 ZigBee IP Mesh Network (RPL DODAG)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BR["BORDER ROUTER\nUtility backhaul\nDODAG root"]
    R1["ROUTER NODE 1\nHAN coordinator"]
    R2["ROUTER NODE 2\nSmart meter"]
    R3["ROUTER NODE 3\nIn Home Display"]
    R4["ROUTER NODE 4\nSmart Appliance"]
    S1["SLEEPY END NODE 1\nBattery sensor"]
    S2["SLEEPY END NODE 2\nDoor sensor"]
    CLOUD["UTILITY CLOUD\nHead end server"]

    BR --- R1
    BR --- R2
    R1 --- R3
    R1 --- R4
    R2 --- S1
    R3 --- S2
    BR -->|IPv6 backhaul| CLOUD
```

> The **DODAG root** is the border router. Every router selects a **parent** based on the objective function (e.g., MRHOF minimises Expected Transmission Count). Sleepy end devices communicate through their parent router.

## 4.3 ZigBee SE 2.0 Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UTIL["UTILITY HEAD END\nServer AMI DRMS"]
    BR["BORDER ROUTER\nHAN IP gateway"]
    METER["ENERGY METER\nESI device"]
    IHD["IN HOME DISPLAY\nPrice Usage"]
    HEMS["HOME ENERGY MGMT\nOptimiser"]
    DR["DEMAND RESPONSE\nServer"]
    PVT["DER ASSETS\nSolar Battery EV"]

    UTIL <-->|DTLS CoAP| BR
    BR <-->|DTLS CoAP| METER
    BR <-->|DTLS CoAP| IHD
    METER <-->|SE2 clusters| HEMS
    UTIL <-->|DRLC signals| DR
    HEMS <-->|control| PVT
```

## 4.4 SE 2.0 Device Registration & Handshake

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant D as Device (Meter)
    participant B as Border Router
    participant U as Utility Server

    D->>B: 802.15.4 association request
    B-->>D: 802.15.4 ACK + 6LoWPAN RA
    D->>U: DTLS ClientHello (X.509)
    U-->>D: DTLS ServerHello + Certificate
    D->>U: Certificate verify (ECC)
    U-->>D: Finished (session keys)
    D->>U: CoAP POST /edev (registration)
    U-->>D: CoAP 2.04 Changed
    D->>U: Periodic CoAP POST /reading
    U-->>D: CoAP ACK
    U->>D: CoAP PUT /price (tariff update)
    D-->>U: CoAP 2.04 Changed
```

## 4.5 Security Architecture of SE 2.0

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["APPLICATION\nSE 2.0 RBAC X 509"]
    B["TRANSPORT\nDTLS ECC secp256r1"]
    C["NETWORK\nRPL pre installed keys"]
    D["LINK\n802.15.4 AES 128 CCM 128 bit MIC"]
    ROOT["UTILITY PKI\nCertificate Authority"]

    A --> B --> C --> D
    ROOT -. issues .-> A
```
