# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]*
**Define ZigBee IP. List any two differences between ZigBee PRO and ZigBee IP.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **ZigBee IP** is an open standard for **IPv6-based wireless mesh networking** designed for low-power, low-data-rate IoT applications. It is built on the **IEEE 802.15.4 PHY/MAC**, uses **6LoWPAN** for header compression, **RPL** for routing, and runs **CoAP/DTLS** at the application layer.
- **Differences:**
  1. ZigBee PRO uses a proprietary 16-bit NWK address; ZigBee IP uses a native 128-bit **IPv6 address**.
  2. ZigBee PRO uses **AODV-style mesh routing**; ZigBee IP uses **RPL DODAG** routing.
  3. ZigBee PRO requires a gateway for Internet access; ZigBee IP is **Internet-native**.

> *Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for each difference (2 × 1 = 2).*

### Q2. *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]*
**What is the role of RPL in ZigBee IP? Name the two control messages used by RPL.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Role of RPL:** Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks (RFC 6550). It builds a **DODAG** (Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph) rooted at a **border router**, enabling each node to pick a **best parent** based on an **Objective Function** (OF0, MRHOF). It supports **storing** and **non-storing** modes for low-RAM devices.
- **Control messages:**
  1. **DIO — DODAG Information Object** (sent downward by the root to advertise the DODAG).
  2. **DAO — Destination Advertisement Object** (sent upward by nodes to register their prefix/address with the root).
- *(Optional third message: **DIS — DODAG Information Solicitation**.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 marks with internal choice)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

> **(a)** Explain the **layered architecture of ZigBee IP** in detail. Highlight the role of 6LoWPAN and RPL. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Describe the **functional blocks of ZigBee Smart Energy 2.0** with a neat diagram. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) — 7 marks

**ZigBee IP Layered Architecture (7 marks):**

1. **Physical Layer — IEEE 802.15.4 PHY (1 mark):**
   - Operates in **2.4 GHz** ISM band with **16 channels**, **O-QPSK modulation**, **250 kbps** raw data rate.
   - Provides transmit power, receiver sensitivity, CCA (Clear Channel Assessment).

2. **MAC Layer — IEEE 802.15.4 (1 mark):**
   - Provides CSMA/CA, slotted/un-slotted modes, ACK, beacon/non-beacon modes.
   - Supports frame security with AES-128-CCM at link layer.
   - Maximum MAC frame is **127 bytes**; usable payload ≈ **81–102 bytes**.

3. **Adaptation Layer — 6LoWPAN (2 marks):**
   - **RFC 4944 / RFC 6282.** Compresses the 40-byte IPv6 header to as few as 6 bytes using **IPHC** (IP Header Compression).
   - Performs **fragmentation & reassembly** of IPv6 datagrams (1280-byte MTU) into 802.15.4-sized frames.
   - Provides **mesh addressing** and **neighbour discovery optimisation**.
   - Stateless header compression preserves end-to-end semantics.

4. **Network Layer — IPv6 + RPL (2 marks):**
   - **IPv6** gives every node a unique 128-bit address, often derived from its EUI-64.
   - **RPL (RFC 6550)** is the routing protocol. It builds a **DODAG** rooted at the border router. Each node selects a **preferred parent** based on an **Objective Function (OF)** — e.g., **OF0** (hop count) or **MRHOF** (ETX).
   - Three control messages: **DIO, DIS, DAO**.
   - Supports two modes: **storing** (full route table) and **non-storing** (only root stores).
   - Loop-free, bi-directional routing optimised for **low-power & lossy links**.

5. **Transport + Application (1 mark):**
   - **UDP** is the preferred transport due to its low overhead.
   - **CoAP (RFC 7252)** provides RESTful semantics.
   - **DTLS (RFC 6347)** provides confidentiality, integrity, and authentication using **ECC** keys.

**Synthesis line (board-valued closing):** *"ZigBee IP combines IETF standards (6LoWPAN, RPL, CoAP, DTLS) with the 802.15.4 radio to deliver a complete, IP-native, low-power mesh networking stack for IoT."*

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) — 7 marks

**ZigBee Smart Energy 2.0 Functional Blocks (7 marks):**

1. **Energy Service Interface (ESI) / Metering (1.5 marks):**
   - The smart meter is the **Energy Services Interface (ESI)**.
   - Exposes **consumption** (kWh), **generation** (kW from solar), and **instantaneous demand** over CoAP resources.

2. **Pricing (1 mark):**
   - Utilities publish tariffs — flat, **Time of Use (TOU)**, **Critical Peak Pricing (CPP)** — to the HAN.

3. **Demand Response & Load Control (DRLC) (1.5 marks):**
   - Utility sends **DR signals** (e.g., "shed load between 6 PM–8 PM") to enrolled devices — pool pumps, HVAC, EV chargers.
   - Devices acknowledge and execute.

4. **Billing & Prepayment (1 mark):**
   - Consumers retrieve **historical bills** and can top up **prepaid credit** remotely.

5. **In-Home Display (IHD) & Energy Management (1 mark):**
   - The IHD shows real-time price, usage, and messages; the HEMS optimises comfort vs. cost.

6. **Distributed Energy Resources (DER) (1 mark):**
   - SE 2.0 explicitly supports **rooftop solar, batteries, EV chargers, V2H** — all coordinated with the utility.

**Diagram (refer Section 4.3 of these notes).**

> **Closing line:** *"SE 2.0 thus standardises all utility-to-customer communications on a single IP-based stack, eliminating the need for a custom gateway and enabling cloud integration."*

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

> **(a)** Compare and contrast **ZigBee PRO** and **ZigBee IP** in terms of network layer, addressing, routing, and application protocol. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **security architecture of ZigBee SE 2.0**. Discuss the role of **DTLS and ECC**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) — 7 marks

**Comparison ZigBee PRO vs. ZigBee IP (7 marks = 1.5 per row + 0.5 synthesis):**

| Parameter | ZigBee PRO | ZigBee IP |
|---|---|---|
| **Network Layer** | Proprietary ZigBee NWK (closed) | IETF IPv6 (open, standards-based) |
| **Addressing** | 16-bit NWK short address (within PAN) | 128-bit IPv6 (global, routable) |
| **Routing** | AODV-style reactive mesh; Tree routing | RPL DODAG proactive routing with Objective Function |
| **Application Layer** | APS + ZCL over ZigBee PRO | CoAP + ZCL over UDP; SEP 2 for energy |
| **Security** | ZigBee Trust Center, 128-bit symmetric keys | DTLS, X.509, ECC, 802.15.4 link-layer security |
| **Internet Integration** | Requires dedicated gateway | Native IPv6 end-to-end |
| **Maturity & Use** | Mature, deployed in millions of legacy devices | Modern, suited for IP-based cloud + DER |
| **Power Optimisation** | Beacons, GTS, but limited by APS | RPL trickle + sleepy nodes + CoAP confirmable |

**Synthesis (0.5 mark):** *"ZigBee IP is the natural evolution for an Internet-of-Everything world; ZigBee PRO remains dominant in legacy isolated HAN deployments."*

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) — 7 marks

**SE 2.0 Security Architecture (7 marks):**

1. **Layered Security Model (2 marks):**
   - **Link layer (802.15.4):** AES-128-CCM with 128-bit MIC. All 802.15.4 frames in SE 2.0 are encrypted.
   - **Network layer (RPL):** Uses **pre-installed keys** during manufacturing and **enrolment keys** during device commissioning.
   - **Transport layer (DTLS):** Provides **end-to-end** security between the device and the utility head-end. UDP-bound.
   - **Application layer (SE 2.0 RBAC):** Adds **role-based access control**; the device's X.509 certificate defines what clusters it can read/write.

2. **DTLS Handshake (2 marks):**
   - Performs mutual authentication using **X.509 v3 certificates**.
   - Uses **Elliptic Curve Cryptography** (typically **secp256r1**) for the key exchange (ECDHE).
   - Establishes a session key for **AES-128-CCM** encryption + integrity.

3. **Public Key Infrastructure (1 mark):**
   - The utility runs a **Certificate Authority (CA)**. Each device ships with a **manufacturer certificate**; on enrolment, it receives a **utility-issued operational certificate**.

4. **Role of ECC (1 mark):**
   - ECC gives equivalent security to RSA with much smaller keys (e.g., secp256r1 ≈ RSA-3072). This is **critical for constrained devices** with limited CPU and RAM.
   - Faster handshake = lower energy consumption.

5. **Certificate Trust & Revocation (1 mark):**
   - OCSP or Certificate Revocation Lists (CRLs) maintained by the utility CA.
   - Compromised devices can be revoked without flashing the firmware.

> **Closing line:** *"Together, DTLS + ECC + X.509 + RBAC give SE 2.0 end-to-end, utility-grade, mutually-authenticated, low-overhead security that meets the strictest regulatory standards (e.g., NISTIR 7628)."

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls (where students lose marks):**
> 1. **Confusing ZigBee IP with ZigBee PRO** — most 14-mark answers mix the two. Remember: ZigBee IP uses **IPv6 + RPL**, not the proprietary 16-bit NWK.
> 2. **Forgetting 6LoWPAN** — the link between IPv6 and 802.15.4 is 6LoWPAN, NOT IPsec or IPSec.
> 3. **Naming APS for SE 2.0** — SE 2.0 uses **CoAP over UDP**, **not APS**. Writing APS is a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Confusing DIO with DAO** — DIO travels *downward* from root; DAO travels *upward* to root. Examiners penalise if reversed.
> 5. **Saying "ZigBee SE 2.0 is more secure"** without naming the **specific** mechanisms (DTLS, ECC, X.509) — vague claims = zero marks.
> 6. **Skipping diagrams** — the 14-mark scheme allocates 1–2 marks for the diagram. A neat labelled stack diagram is **mandatory**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **ZigBee IP = IPv6 over 802.15.4**, built on **6LoWPAN + RPL + CoAP + DTLS**.
- **ZigBee SE 2.0 = application profile for smart energy** that runs on ZigBee IP.
- Address size: **128-bit IPv6**; nodes derived from **EUI-64**.
- 802.15.4 PHY: **2.4 GHz, 250 kbps, 16 channels, O-QPSK**.
- 6LoWPAN compresses IPv6 header from **40 B → ~6–8 B** using **IPHC**.
- RPL forms a **DODAG**; messages: **DIO (down), DAO (up), DIS (request)**.
- Routing Objective Functions: **OF0** (hop count), **MRHOF** (ETX).
- **CoAP** = RESTful IoT protocol; runs on **UDP/5683 (5684 DTLS)**.
- **DTLS** uses **ECC (secp256r1)** + **AES-128-CCM** + **X.509 v3** certificates.
- **SE 2.0 functional blocks**: Metering, Pricing, DRLC, Billing, Prepayment, IHD, Energy Management, DER, Text.
- **Key differences vs. ZigBee PRO**: Internet-native, PKI, RBAC, cloud-ready.
- **Key differences vs. SE 1.x**: All above + native DER, prepayment, text, energy management.
- **Architectural layers from bottom to top**: PHY → MAC → 6LoWPAN → IPv6 (RPL) → UDP → DTLS → CoAP → SE 2.0.
- **Real-world deployment**: SMETS2 in the UK, AMI rollouts in North America, large-scale Indian smart-meter pilots.
- **Standard bodies**: **ZigBee Alliance (now Connectivity Standards Alliance)**, **IETF** (6LoWPAN/RPL/CoAP), **IEEE** (802.15.4).
- **Examiner loves**: layered diagrams, comparison tables, security flow, RPL DODAG, function-block enumerations.

> *End of Module 2 — ZigBee IP / ZigBee SE 2.0 study notes.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS AND SMART COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wi-Fi

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Wi-Fi (Wireless Fidelity)** is a family of wireless local area networking (WLAN) technologies standardized under the **IEEE 802.11** family. It operates in the unlicensed **Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) radio bands** — primarily **2.4 GHz**, **5 GHz**, and more recently **6 GHz** (Wi-Fi 6E / 7) — providing high-speed, IP-native connectivity over a typical range of **20–100 m indoors** and up to **250 m outdoors** (line of sight).

In the context of **IoT and Smart Computing**, Wi-Fi functions as a high-throughput, IP-native access layer. Unlike low-power protocols (ZigBee, BLE, LoRa), Wi-Fi is best suited for **stationary, mains-powered IoT nodes** that demand high bandwidth (e.g., IP cameras, smart TVs, smart speakers, industrial gateways, video doorbells).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wireless Telephone Booth"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of Wi-Fi as turning a **landline telephone booth** into a **mobile cell phone**. The landline (Ethernet cable) gave reliable but immobile connectivity. Wi-Fi "cuts the cord," letting your device roam freely within a building while remaining connected to the internet — exactly like a cordless phone, but for *data*. The **Access Point (AP)** is the "base station," and the **SSID** is the "phone number" you dial to join the network.

### 1.3 Wi-Fi Generations at a Glance

| Standard | Common Name | Year | Max PHY Rate | Frequency Band(s) |
|----------|-------------|------|--------------|-------------------|
| 802.11b | Wi-Fi 1 | 1999 | 11 Mbps | 2.4 GHz |
| 802.11a | Wi-Fi 2 | 1999 | 54 Mbps | 5 GHz |
| 802.11g | Wi-Fi 3 | 2003 | 54 Mbps | 2.4 GHz |
| 802.11n | **Wi-Fi 4** | 2009 | **600 Mbps** | 2.4 / 5 GHz |
| 802.11ac | **Wi-Fi 5** | 2014 | **6.93 Gbps** | 5 GHz |
| 802.11ax | **Wi-Fi 6 / 6E** | 2019 / 2020 | **9.6 Gbps** | 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz |
| 802.11be | **Wi-Fi 7** | 2024 | **46 Gbps** | 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz |
| 802.11ah | **Wi-Fi HaLow** | 2016 | 347 Mbps | Sub-1 GHz (~900 MHz) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Focus on **(i) IEEE 802.11 architecture (BSS, ESS, DS), (ii) the MAC layer (CSMA/CA / DCF), (iii) security evolution (WEP → WPA → WPA2 → WPA3), and (iv) Wi-Fi in IoT context — including Wi-Fi HaLow (802.11ah) for low-power long-range IoT**.

### 1.4 Wi-Fi in IoT — The "Why" and "Why Not"

**Why Wi-Fi for IoT:**
- **IP-native** — direct integration with TCP/IP, no protocol gateway needed.
- **High bandwidth** — supports video, audio, OTA firmware updates.
- **Mature ecosystem** — routers, gateways, smartphones are pre-equipped.

**Why NOT always Wi-Fi for IoT:**
- **High power consumption** (100–1000 mW active) → unsuitable for coin-cell sensor nodes.
- **Short range** in 5/6 GHz bands; weaker wall penetration at higher frequencies.
- **CSMA/CA contention** collapses in very dense deployments (mitigated by Wi-Fi 6's OFDMA).

> [!TIP]
> For battery-powered, long-range IoT, **Wi-Fi HaLow (802.11ah)** operates in the **sub-1 GHz band** (902–928 MHz), reaches up to **1 km**, and consumes **~100× less power** than conventional Wi-Fi — making it the bridge between Wi-Fi and LPWAN.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 IEEE 802.11 Network Architecture (Building Blocks)
The 802.11 standard defines the following logical components:

- **STA (Station):** Any device with a Wi-Fi NIC (phone, laptop, IoT sensor).
- **AP (Access Point):** Central coordinator that bridges wireless STAs to the Distribution System.
- **BSS (Basic Service Set):** The fundamental block — **one AP + all associated STAs**, identified by its **BSSID** (the AP's MAC address).
- **IBSS (Independent BSS) / Ad-hoc mode:** A peer-to-peer network with **no AP**.
- **DS (Distribution System):** The wired backbone (typically Ethernet) interconnecting multiple APs.
- **ESS (Extended Service Set):** One or more BSSs connected via a DS, presented as a single logical WLAN identified by a common **SSID** (e.g., "KTU_Hostel_WiFi").
- **Portal:** The logical interface between the DS and an external network (e.g., the Internet).

### 2.2 The MAC Layer — CSMA/CA & DCF
Wi-Fi uses **Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance (CSMA/CA)** with the **Distributed Coordination Function (DCF)** as the baseline channel access mechanism.

**Operational logic (step by step):**
1. A STA wanting to transmit **senses the channel** for a **DIFS (DCF Inter-Frame Space)** period.
2. If the channel is **idle** for DIFS, the STA enters a **random backoff** countdown chosen uniformly in $[0, CW]$.
3. The backoff counter is **decremented** by one after each idle **slot time**; if the channel becomes busy, the counter is **frozen** (not reset).
4. When the counter reaches zero, the STA **transmits** the frame.
5. The receiver waits **SIFS (Short Inter-Frame Space)** and replies with an **ACK** to confirm delivery.
6. If no ACK arrives, the STA **doubles the Contention Window** using **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)** — capped at $CW_{max}$ — and retries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CSMA/CA and not CSMA/CD?** Wi-Fi stations **cannot detect collisions** during transmission because (i) the transmitted signal is **~1000× stronger** than any incoming signal, swamping the receiver, and (ii) Wi-Fi is **half-duplex** — a station cannot transmit and listen on the same channel simultaneously. Hence Wi-Fi **avoids** collisions by listening *before* talking.

### 2.3 Inter-Frame Space (IFS) Hierarchy
$$\text{SIFS} < \text{PIFS} < \text{DIFS} < \text{EIFS}$$
- **SIFS** = $RxRFDelay + RxPLCPDelay + MACProcessingDelay + RxTxTurnaroundTime$ (highest priority).
- **PIFS = SIFS + 1 SlotTime** (used by PCF).
- **DIFS = SIFS + 2 × SlotTime** (used by DCF).
- **EIFS** = SIFS + ACK_TransmissionTime + DIFS (used after a corrupted frame).

> **Reference values:** SlotTime in **802.11b** = **20 μs**; in **802.11a/g/n** = **9 μs**.

### 2.4 RTS/CTS — Defeating the Hidden Node Problem
Because of the **hidden node problem** (STA A can hear AP, STA B can hear AP, but A and B cannot hear each other), 802.11 supports an optional **RTS/CTS handshake**:
1. Sender transmits **RTS (Request To Send)**.
2. Receiver replies with **CTS (Clear To Send)** after SIFS.
3. All STAs overhearing either RTS or CTS set their **NAV (Network Allocation Vector)** and defer channel access for the reserved duration.
4. Sender transmits **DATA**; receiver sends **ACK** after SIFS.

### 2.5 Security Evolution

| Protocol | Year | Cipher | Key Size | Status |
|----------|------|--------|----------|--------|
| WEP | 1999 | RC4 (stream) | 40 / 104 bit + 24-bit IV | **Broken** (FMS / Klein attacks) |
| WPA | 2003 | TKIP + RC4 | 128 bit | Deprecated |
| WPA2 | 2004 | **AES-CCMP** | 128 bit | Current mainstream |
| WPA3 | 2018 | **SAE + AES-GCMP** | 128 / 192 bit | **Latest** (forward secrecy) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Tip:** WEP uses the **RC4 stream cipher** with a **24-bit IV** that cycles in just a few hours on busy networks — enabling statistical attacks (FMS, Klein). WPA3 replaces the 4-way PSK handshake with **SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals)**, providing **forward secrecy** and resistance to offline dictionary attacks.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) | $FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44$ | d in km, f in MHz |
| Path Loss (log-distance) | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10\,n\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma$ | n = path-loss exponent (2–4) |
| Friis Received Power | $P_r = P_t\,G_t\,G_r\!\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | G = antenna gain, λ in m |
| Friis in dB | $P_r^{dBm} = P_t^{dBm} + G_t^{dBi} + G_r^{dBi} - FSPL_{dB}$ | Power in dBm / gain in dBi |
| Shannon Capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$ | B in Hz, S/N dimensionless |
| 802.11 DCF Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{T_{data}}{T_{data} + DIFS + \overline{T_{backoff}} + 2\,SIFS + T_{ACK} + 2\,T_{PHY}}$ | Ratio (0–1) |
| Binary Exp. Backoff | $CW_{i+1} = \min\!\left(2\,(CW_i + 1) - 1,\; CW_{max}\right)$ | Slots |
| Bianchi τ (transmit prob) | $\tau = \frac{2\,(1-2p)}{(1-2p)(W+1) + pW\!\left(1-(2p)^{m}\right)}$ | p = collision prob., W = min CW |
| 802.11n PHY Rate | $R = \dfrac{N_{ss}\cdot N_{data}\cdot N_{bits}}{T_{sym}}$ | 600 Mbps peak (4×4 MIMO, 40 MHz, 64-QAM) |
| OFDM symbol time | $T_{sym} = T_{useful} + T_{GI}$ | 3.2 μs + 0.8 μs (Long GI) |
| Wi-Fi HaLow range (approx.) | $d_{max} \approx 1 \text{ km (outdoor, sub-1 GHz)}$ | 902–928 MHz ISM |

> **Units to memorize:** Time is in **microseconds (μs)**; FSPL constant **32.44** requires **d in km** and **f in MHz**.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Smart Home Hubs:** Wi-Fi 6 with **OFDMA + BSS Coloring** handles 50+ concurrent IoT devices.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Wi-Fi 6E's 6 GHz band enables **deterministic latency** for AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) and robotics.
- **Healthcare:** Wi-Fi-based **RTLS (Real-Time Location Systems)** for hospital asset tracking.
- **Smart Agriculture:** Wi-Fi HaLow sensors covering greenhouses and farms up to 1 km.
- **Smart Cities:** Wi-Fi mesh backbones for CCTV, traffic monitoring, and public kiosks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation #1 — Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)

**Problem:** A Wi-Fi AP operates at **f = 2.4 GHz** with a transmit power of **20 dBm**. A receiver is **50 m** away. Assume isotropic antennas ($G_t = G_r = 1$). Compute **(i) FSPL in dB** and **(ii) received power in dBm**.

**Step 1 — Start with Friis transmission equation (linear form):**
$$\frac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to decibels (dB form):**
$$P_r^{dBm} = P_t^{dBm} + G_t^{dBi} + G_r^{dBi} - FSPL_{dB}$$

where
$$FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$

The constant term evaluates to **32.44** when d is in **km** and f is in **MHz**.

**Step 3 — Substitute values (d = 0.05 km, f = 2400 MHz):**
$$\begin{aligned}
FSPL_{dB} &= 20\log_{10}(0.05) + 20\log_{10}(2400) + 32.44 \\
&= 20 \times (-1.3010) + 20 \times (3.3802) + 32.44 \\
&= -26.02 + 67.60 + 32.44 \\
&= 74.02 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute received power:**
$$\begin{aligned}
P_r^{dBm} &= 20 + 0 + 0 - 74.02 \\
&= -54.02 \text{ dBm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:**
Typical Wi-Fi receiver sensitivity ranges from **−65 dBm (lowest rate)** to **−90 dBm (highest rate)**. A received power of **−54 dBm** yields a **link margin of ~16 dB** above the minimum, meaning the link is robust and can tolerate significant multipath fading and walls.

---

### 3.2 Derivation #2 — 802.11 DCF Saturation Throughput (Bianchi's Model)

**Goal:** Derive the per-station transmission probability **τ** and the saturation throughput **S** of an 802.11 DCF network with **n** contending stations.

**Step 1 — Model the backoff counter:**
At retry stage $i$, the contention window is:
$$W_i = \begin{cases} 2^{i}\,W_0, & 0 \le i \le m' \\ 2^{m'}\,W_0, & m' < i \le m \end{cases}$$

where $W_0 = CW_{min} + 1$ (e.g., 32 for 802.11b), $m' = m$ for the standard BEB.

**Step 2 — Conditional collision probability:**
In any slot, a station transmits with probability τ. Every other station must *not* transmit for the slot to be idle or successful. So:
$$p = 1 - (1 - \tau)^{n-1}$$

**Step 3 — Transmission probability τ:**
A station transmits in a randomly chosen slot only if its backoff has reached zero. In steady state, by Markov-chain analysis (Bianchi, 1998):
$$\tau = \frac{2\,(1-2p)}{(1-2p)(W+1) + pW\!\left(1-(2p)^{m}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Probability of successful transmission in a slot:**
$$P_s = n\,\tau\,(1-\tau)^{n-1}$$

**Step 5 — Probability slot is idle:**
$$P_{idle} = (1-\tau)^{n}$$

**Step 6 — Saturation throughput (normalized to PHY rate):**
$$S = \frac{P_s\,E[L]}{(1-P_{idle})\,\sigma + P_s\,T_s + (1-P_s-P_{idle})\,T_c}$$

where
- $\sigma$ = slot time (20 μs for 802.11b, 9 μs for 802.11a/g/n)
- $T_s$ = duration of successful transmission = $DIFS + T_{DATA} + SIFS + T_{ACK} + 2T_{PHY}$
- $T_c$ = duration of collision = $DIFS + T_{DATA} + T_{PHY}$
- $E[L]$ = average payload bits (e.g., 8184 bits MSDU at full rate)

**Step 7 — Numerical evaluation (802.11b, n = 5, W = 32, m = 5, E[L] = 8184 bits):**
- $T_s \approx 50 + 1500\cdot 8/11\text{M} + 10 + 14\cdot 8/11\text{M} + 2\cdot 20 \approx 1190\ \mu s$
- $T_c \approx 50 + 1090 + 20 \approx 1160\ \mu s$
- Solving the fixed-point $(p, \tau)$ numerically: $p \approx 0.29$, $\tau \approx 0.082$.
- $P_s \approx 5 \cdot 0.082 \cdot (0.918)^{4} \approx 0.279$, $P_{idle} \approx 0.681$.
- $S \approx (0.279 \cdot 8184) / (0.319 \cdot 20 + 0.279 \cdot 1190 + 0.040 \cdot 1160) \approx 0.453$.

**Result:** $S \approx 0.45$ → effective throughput ≈ **5 Mbps** on an 11 Mbps channel. This confirms the well-known **rule of thumb**: real-world 802.11b throughput ≈ **50% of PHY rate**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation #3 — 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4) Maximum PHY Rate

**Given:** 40 MHz channel, 4×4 MIMO, 64-QAM (6 bits/symbol), Short Guard Interval (SGI = 0.4 μs).

**Step 1 — OFDM symbol components:**
- Useful symbol time: $T_u = 3.2\ \mu s$
- Long GI: $0.8\ \mu s$ → $T_{sym} = 4.0\ \mu s$ → 250,000 symbols/s
- **Short GI: $0.4\ \mu s$ → $T_{sym} = 3.6\ \mu s$ → 277,778 symbols/s**

**Step 2 — Subcarrier count (40 MHz HT):**
$$N_{sc} = N_{data} + N_{pilot} = 108$$

**Step 3 — Bits per OFDM symbol per spatial stream (64-QAM):**
$$N_{bits/sym/stream} = 108 \times 6 = 648 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Per-stream bitrate (Short GI):**
$$R_{stream} = 648 \times 277{,}778 \approx 180 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 5 — Aggregate with 4×4 MIMO:**
$$\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= N_{ss} \times R_{stream} = 4 \times 180 \\
&= 720 \text{ Mbps (theoretical upper bound with 4×4, 40 MHz, 64-QAM, SGI)}
\end{aligned}$$

> **KTU Note:** The 802.11n *published* maximum is **600 Mbps** (4×4, 40 MHz, 64-QAM, SGI = 150 Mbps/stream × 4 = 600 Mbps) — the difference is due to HT-SIG overhead and pilot encoding efficiency accounted for in the standard.

---

### 3.4 Derivation #4 — Throughput Efficiency for a Single STA

**Given:** 802.11g, 54 Mbps PHY, payload $L = 1500$ bytes, no RTS/CTS, ACK = 14 bytes, SIFS = 10 μs, DIFS = 28 μs, SlotTime = 9 μs, PHY preamble + header = 20 μs.

**Step 1 — Compute frame air-times:**
$$T_{DATA} = 20 + \frac{(24 + 1500)\cdot 8}{54 \times 10^{6}} = 20 + 225.9 \approx 246\ \mu s$$
$$T_{ACK} = 20 + \frac{14\cdot 8}{54 \times 10^{6}} = 20 + 2.07 \approx 22\ \mu s$$

**Step 2 — Average backoff (uniform in [0, 15] for first attempt):**
$$\overline{T_{backoff}} = \frac{15}{2} \times 9 = 67.5\ \mu s$$

**Step 3 — Total channel occupancy (one successful TX):**
$$\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= DIFS + \overline{T_{backoff}} + T_{DATA} + SIFS + T_{ACK} \\
&= 28 + 67.5 + 246 + 10 + 22 \\
&= 373.5\ \mu s
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{225.9}{373.5} \approx 0.605 \;\;(60.5\%)$$

**Result:** Realistic single-STA throughput ≈ $54 \times 0.605 \approx$ **32.7 Mbps** — illustrating the ~40% overhead of Wi-Fi at the MAC layer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IEEE 802.11 ESS Architecture (with DS)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph DS["DISTRIBUTION SYSTEM (Ethernet Backbone)"]
        DS1[DS Backbone Switch]
    end

    subgraph BSS1["BSS 1 — AP1 Coverage Zone"]
        AP1[Access Point AP1]
        S1A[STA Smartphone]
        S1B[STA IoT Camera]
        S1C[STA Laptop]
    end

    subgraph BSS2["BSS 2 — AP2 Coverage Zone"]
        AP2[Access Point AP2]
        S2A[STA Tablet]
        S2B[STA Smart TV]
        S2C[STA IoT Sensor]
    end

    subgraph BSS3["BSS 3 — AP3 Coverage Zone"]
        AP3[Access Point AP3]
        S3A[STA Phone]
        S3B[STA Printer]
    end

    DS1 --- AP1
    DS1 --- AP2
    DS1 --- AP3
    AP1 --- S1A
    AP1 --- S1B
    AP1 --- S1C
    AP2 --- S2A
    AP2 --- S2B
    AP2 --- S2C
    AP3 --- S3A
    AP3 --- S3B

    S1A -. Roaming Handoff via DS .-> AP2
    S2C -. Roaming Handoff via DS .-> AP3
```

### 4.2 CSMA/CA + DCF Channel Access Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Frame Arrives at MAC Layer] --> B[Sense Channel for DIFS Period]
    B --> C{Channel Idle for DIFS?}
    C -- No --> D[Defer and Wait]
    D --> B
    C -- Yes --> E[Pick Random Backoff Counter in 0 to CW]
    E --> F[Wait One Slot Time]
    F --> G{Channel Still Idle?}
    G -- No --> H[Freeze Counter and Wait for Idle]
    H --> G
    G -- Yes --> I[Decrement Counter by One]
    I --> J{Counter Reached Zero?}
    J -- No --> F
    J -- Yes --> K[Optionally Send RTS]
    K --> L[Wait SIFS and Receive CTS]
    L --> M[Transmit DATA Frame]
    M --> N[Wait SIFS Period]
    N --> O{ACK Received?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Success: Frame Delivered]
    O -- No --> Q[Collision Suspected: Double CW via BEB]
    Q --> R{Retry Limit Exceeded?}
    R -- No --> E
    R -- Yes --> S[Drop Frame: Notify Upper Layer]
```

### 4.3 802.11 MAC Frame Format (Simplified)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph FRAME["802.11 MAC Frame  (Variable Length)"]
        FC["Frame Control 2 bytes"]
        DUR["Duration or ID 2 bytes"]
        A1["Address 1  6 bytes  Destination"]
        A2["Address 2  6 bytes  Source"]
        A3["Address 3  6 bytes  BSSID or Source"]
        SC["Sequence Control  2 bytes"]
        A4["Address 4  6 bytes  Optional  Used in WDS"]
        BODY["Frame Body  0 to 2312 bytes"]
        FCS["FCS  CRC 32  4 bytes"]
    end

    FC --> DUR --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> SC --> A4 --> BODY --> FCS
```

### 4.4 Wi-Fi Protocol Stack vs OSI Reference Model

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph APP["Application Layer"]
        APP1[HTTP  CoAP  MQTT  RTSP]
    end
    subgraph NET["Network Layer  TCP or UDP IP"]
        NET1[IPv4  IPv6]
    end
    subgraph LLC["802.2 LLC Sublayer"]
        LLC1[Logical Link Control]
    end
    subgraph MAC["802.11 MAC Sublayer"]
        MAC1[CSMA or CA, DCF, Fragmentation, Encryption, Power Save]
    end
    subgraph PHY["802.11 PHY Layer"]
        PHY1[DSSS  OFDM  OFDMA  MIMO  Beamforming  Channel Bonding]
    end

    APP1 --> NET1 --> LLC1 --> MAC1 --> PHY1
```

### 4.5 Wi-Fi Security Evolution Timeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    WEP[1999: WEP  RC4 40 or 104 bit  BROKEN] --> WPA[2003: WPA  TKIP 128 bit  Deprecated]
    WPA --> WPA2[2004: WPA2  AES CCMP 128 bit  Mainstream]
    WPA2 --> WPA3[2018: WPA3  SAE  Forward Secrecy  Latest]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
Define **Wi-Fi**. List **two advantages** and **two disadvantages** of using Wi-Fi for IoT applications.

**Model Answer:**

**Wi-Fi** is a wireless networking technology standardized under the **IEEE 802.11 family**, operating in unlicensed ISM radio bands (2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, 6 GHz) to provide high-throughput, IP-based local area network connectivity.

**Advantages (any 2):**
1. **High bandwidth** — supports data rates from 11 Mbps (802.11b) up to 46 Gbps (802.11be), enabling video, voice, and firmware-OTA IoT applications.
2. **IP-native and ubiquitous** — directly integrates with TCP/IP infrastructure (routers, gateways, cloud platforms), requiring no protocol translation gateway.
3. **Mature, low-cost ecosystem** — Wi-Fi chipsets are mass-produced, with most consumer devices pre-equipped.

**Disadvantages (any 2):**
1. **High power consumption** — typically 100–1000 mW in active mode, making it unsuitable for battery-only sensor nodes.
2. **Limited scalability in dense deployments** — CSMA/CA contention collapses when hundreds of nodes contend for the same channel (only partially mitigated by Wi-Fi 6 OFDMA).
3. **Short range in 5/6 GHz** — higher frequencies suffer greater path loss and poor wall penetration compared to sub-GHz LPWAN alternatives.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
What is **CSMA/CA**? Why is it used in Wi-Fi instead of **CSMA/CD**?

**Model Answer:**

**CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance)** is the medium access protocol used by Wi-Fi (802.11). A station wanting to transmit first **senses the channel** for a **DIFS** period; if idle, it waits a **random backoff** (selected from the Contention Window) before transmitting. After reception, the receiver confirms with an **ACK** after a **SIFS** interval.

**Why not CSMA/CD?** Wi-Fi stations **cannot detect collisions** during transmission because:
1. The transmitted signal is **~1000× stronger** than any incoming signal, swamping the receiver's collision detection circuitry.
2. Wi-Fi is **half-duplex** — a station cannot transmit and listen on the same channel simultaneously.
3. The **hidden node problem** makes collision detection unreliable — a STA may not hear another node that the AP can hear.

Hence Wi-Fi **avoids** collisions by listening *before* talking, rather than detecting them afterwards.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **IEEE 802.11 Extended Service Set (ESS) architecture**. Distinguish between **infrastructure mode** and **ad-hoc (IBSS) mode**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **WPA3 security protocol**. How does it differ from **WPA2**? What is **SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals)** and why is it resistant to **offline dictionary attacks**?

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The 802.11 architecture (refer to SECTION 4.1 diagram) consists of:
- **STA (Station):** any device with a Wi-Fi NIC.
- **AP (Access Point):** relays wireless STAs to the wired DS.
- **BSS (Basic Service Set):** one AP + all associated STAs, identified by its **BSSID** (AP's MAC).
- **DS (Distribution System):** wired Ethernet backbone connecting APs.
- **ESS (Extended Service Set):** one or more BSSs interconnected through a DS, presented as a single WLAN with a common **SSID**.

| Feature | Infrastructure Mode | Ad-hoc (IBSS) Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Access Point | Required (central coordinator) | No AP — peer-to-peer |
| Topology | Star / hub-and-spoke | Mesh / peer-to-peer |
| Coverage | Larger (extensible via ESS) | Limited to mutual STAs |
| Power Save | AP buffers frames for sleeping STAs | Limited — no central coordinator |
| Roaming | Supported across APs in ESS | Not applicable |
| Typical use | Home / Office / Enterprise WLAN | Quick file transfer, IoT peer networks, disaster zones |

**Model Solution (b):**

**WPA3 (Wi-Fi Protected Access 3)** is the latest Wi-Fi security standard introduced by the Wi-Fi Alliance in **2018** to address WPA2 vulnerabilities.

**Key features:**
1. **SAE-based authentication** — replaces the 4-way PSK handshake of WPA2 with the **Dragonfly Key Exchange (SAE)**.
2. **Forward secrecy** — compromise of the password does not allow decryption of past sessions.
3. **Protected Management Frames (PMF) mandatory** — defeats de-authentication / disassociation attacks.
4. **192-bit security suite** for enterprise / government (WPA3-Enterprise), using **GCMP-256** encryption.
5. **Wi-Fi Easy Connect** — uses QR codes or **DPP (Device Provisioning Protocol)** for passwordless IoT onboarding.

**SAE Mechanism:**
- Both supplicant and AP exchange **commit messages** derived from the password using a **finite cyclic group** (often an Elliptic Curve).
- They then exchange **confirm messages** to prove both derived the same secret.
- The output is a **PMK (Pairwise Master Key)** that is **never transmitted in cleartext**.

**Resistance to offline dictionary attacks:**
- In **WPA2-PSK**, an eavesdropper captures the 4-way handshake and performs **offline brute-force** on `PMK = PBKDF2(password, SSID, 4096, 256)` without ever interacting with the network.
- In **WPA3-SAE**, an attacker can test **only one password per active connection attempt** (online), which the AP can **rate-limit or lock out**.
- Hence offline dictionary attacks become computationally infeasible.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Distributed Coordination Function (DCF)** of 802.11. With a timing diagram, describe **CSMA/CA with RTS/CTS**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Wi-Fi transmitter operates at **f = 2.4 GHz** with a transmit power of **20 dBm**. The receiver is located **100 m** away in free space. Assume isotropic antennas. Calculate:
- (i) The **free-space path loss** in dB.
- (ii) The **received power** in dBm.
- (iii) Comment on the **link viability** if the receiver sensitivity is **−80 dBm**.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**DCF (Distributed Coordination Function)** is the fundamental MAC access method of IEEE 802.11, using **CSMA/CA** in a fully distributed (no central scheduler) manner.

**Operational rules:**
1. **Carrier Sensing:** A STA must sense the medium idle for at least **DIFS** before transmitting.
2. **Random Backoff:** Choose a random integer in $[0, CW]$, where $CW$ starts at $CW_{min}$ (e.g., 31 for 802.11b) and is **doubled** after each collision (**Binary Exponential Backoff**, capped at $CW_{max}$).
3. **Slot Decrement:** The backoff counter is decremented by one per idle slot time; if the medium becomes busy, the counter is **frozen** (not reset).
4. **Transmission:** When the counter reaches zero, the STA transmits.
5. **Acknowledgment:** Receiver sends an **ACK** after **SIFS** to confirm success.
6. **Retransmission:** If no ACK arrives, double CW and retry up to a limit (typically 7).

**RTS/CTS Mechanism** (defeats hidden node problem):

| Time → | $t_0$ | $t_0 + DIFS + BO$ | $t_0 + SIFS$ | $t_0 + 2SIFS$ | $t_0 + 3SIFS$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sender** | Sense DIFS + Backoff | → **RTS** | (wait SIFS) | → **DATA** | (wait SIFS) |
| **Receiver** | Idle | (wait SIFS) | ← **CTS** | (wait SIFS) | ← **ACK** |
| **Other STAs** | Sensing | Set NAV on RTS | Set NAV on CTS | Idle (NAV) | Idle (NAV) |

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** f = 2.4 GHz = 2400 MHz, $P_t = 20$ dBm, d = 100 m = 0.1 km, $G_t = G_r = 1$ (isotropic), c = $3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**(i) Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL):**
$$FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44$$

$$\begin{aligned}
FSPL_{dB} &= 20\log_{10}(0.1) + 20\log_{10}(2400) + 32.44 \\
&= 20 \times (-1) + 20 \times (3.3802) + 32.44 \\
&= -20 + 67.60 + 32.44 \\
&= \mathbf{80.04 \text{ dB}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Received Power:**
$$\begin{aligned}
P_r^{dBm} &= P_t^{dBm} + G_t^{dBi} + G_r^{dBi} - FSPL_{dB} \\
&= 20 + 0 + 0 - 80.04 \\
&= \mathbf{-60.04 \text{ dBm}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Link Viability:**
- Receiver sensitivity: $P_{sens} = -80$ dBm
- Received power: $P_r = -60.04$ dBm
- **Link margin** = $P_r - P_{sens} = -60.04 - (-80) = \mathbf{+19.96 \text{ dB}}$

A **positive margin of ~20 dB** indicates a **highly viable link** with substantial fade margin. The link can tolerate significant multipath fading, vegetation, wall attenuation (one or two brick walls ≈ 10–15 dB), and temporary obstructions before failing. ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing DCF with PCF** — PCF is the **Point Coordination Function** where the AP polls STAs (not widely used). Do not interchange with DCF.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in FSPL** — The constant **32.44** requires d in **km** and f in **MHz**. Substituting d in **meters** or f in **GHz** gives a wrong answer worth **5–7 marks**.
> 3. **"Wi-Fi uses CSMA/CD"** — This is the #1 KTU trap. Wi-Fi **cannot** do collision detection. Always say **CSMA/CA**.
> 4. **WEP ≠ WPA ≠ WPA2** — WEP uses RC4 + 24-bit IV; WPA uses TKIP over RC4; WPA2 uses AES-CCMP. Confusing them loses marks.
> 5. **Hidden node ≠ Exposed node** — Hidden node is solved by **RTS/CTS**; exposed node is an *unnecessary* deferral problem.
> 6. **Forgetting receiver sensitivity check** — Always compute **link margin** = $P_r - P_{sens}$ before declaring the link viable.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Wi-Fi = IEEE 802.11**, operating in **2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz** ISM bands.
- ✅ **Architecture:** BSS → DS → ESS; **BSSID** (AP MAC) ≠ **SSID** (network name).
- ✅ **MAC Layer:** **CSMA/CA + DCF + BEB**; IFS hierarchy **SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS**.
- ✅ **Hidden node solution:** **RTS/CTS** + **NAV**.
- ✅ **SlotTime:** 20 μs (802.11b) | 9 μs (802.11a/g/n/ac/ax).
- ✅ **Security evolution:** WEP ❌ → WPA (TKIP) → WPA2 (AES-CCMP) → **WPA3 (SAE + Forward Secrecy)**.
- ✅ **Wi-Fi for IoT:** Standard Wi-Fi = high bandwidth + high power; **Wi-Fi HaLow (802.11ah)** = sub-1 GHz, ~1 km range, low power.
- ✅ **Memorize the FSPL constant: 32.44** (d in km, f in MHz).
- ✅ **PHY rates:** 802.11b = 11 Mbps (DSSS) | 802.11g/a = 54 Mbps (OFDM) | 802.11n = 600 Mbps (MIMO+OFDM).
- ✅ **Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax) features:** OFDMA, MU-MIMO, BSS Coloring, 1024-QAM.
- ✅ **Rule of thumb:** Effective Wi-Fi throughput ≈ **40–60% of PHY rate** depending on environment and contention.
- ✅ **Wi-Fi 7 (802.11be):** 320 MHz channels, 4096-QAM, MLO (Multi-Link Operation), up to 46 Gbps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS AND SMART COMPUTING.
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** GPRS/GSM Cellular Networks - Mobile Internet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** *GSM is a second-generation (2G) digital cellular network standard developed by the European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI) to provide circuit-switched voice and low-rate data services using a combination of Frequency Division Multiple Access (FDMA) and Time Division Multiple Access (TDMA) over licensed radio spectrum.*

- **Standard Family:** 2G / 2.5G (with GPRS overlay)
- **Primary Services:** Voice calls, SMS, low-rate circuit-switched data (CSD) at **9.6 kbps**
- **Operating Bands:** **GSM-900 (890–960 MHz)**, **GSM-1800 (1710–1880 MHz)**, **GSM-1900 (1850–1990 MHz)**
- **Switching Technique:** **Circuit-switched** (dedicated end-to-end channel for entire call duration)
- **Channel Bandwidth:** **200 kHz** per RF carrier
- **Modulation:** Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying (**GMSK**)

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Dedicated Highway Lane"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a **multi-lane highway** where each car (voice call) is given its own **exclusive lane and time window** for the entire trip. Even if the car is parked (silence), nobody else can use that lane. This is exactly how **GSM circuit-switching** works — a dedicated 200 kHz lane and a fixed time slot are reserved for the entire call, wasting bandwidth during silence periods. When you call your friend on a 2G phone, you are literally "renting" a tiny lane on the radio highway for the duration of your conversation.

---

## 1.2 GPRS (General Packet Radio Service)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** *GPRS is a 2.5G packet-switched data overlay on the existing GSM infrastructure that introduces the concept of "always-on" mobile internet by dynamically allocating radio resources (time slots) only when the user is actually transmitting or receiving data, achieving theoretical peak rates of 56–114 kbps (and up to 171.2 kbps with all 8 slots and CS-4 coding).*

- **Switching Technique:** **Packet-switched** (resources allocated only when data flows)
- **Theoretical Peak Rate:** Up to **171.2 kbps** (8 slots × 21.4 kbps, CS-4)
- **Typical Real-World Rate:** **30–60 kbps**
- **Billing Model:** Per **kilobyte transferred**, not per minute
- **Core Network Elements Added:** **SGSN** (Serving GPRS Support Node) and **GGSN** (Gateway GPRS Support Node)
- **Coding Schemes:** CS-1 (9.05 kbps/slot), CS-2 (13.4 kbps/slot), CS-3 (15.6 kbps/slot), CS-4 (21.4 kbps/slot)

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Shared Taxi Pool"

> [!NOTE]
> Forget the dedicated lane. Now imagine a **shared taxi (packet) that picks up any passenger (data burst) only when needed**. If no one is sending mail or browsing the web, the taxis simply stay idle — no lane is reserved. This is **GPRS packet switching**. The "taxi stand" is the GGSN, and the "city dispatcher" is the SGSN. The taxi only consumes a time slot on the highway *while carrying a passenger*, dramatically improving efficiency and enabling "always-on" mobile internet — the foundation upon which modern IoT and smartphone data is built.

---

## 1.3 Mobile Internet — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** *Mobile Internet refers to the wireless delivery of IP-based internet content and services to a mobile device through a cellular network, made practically usable by GSM (via CSD/HSCSD) and GPRS/EDGE, and standardised for content rendering through the Wireless Application Protocol (WAP) stack.*

- **WAP (Wireless Application Protocol):** A protocol stack (WAE, WSP, WTP, WTLS, WDP) that adapts web content for low-bandwidth, small-screen mobile devices.
- **Key Enabler:** Transition from **circuit-switched voice-only** to **packet-switched data** via GPRS.
- **Modern Evolution:** GPRS → EDGE (2.75G) → UMTS (3G) → HSPA → LTE (4G) → 5G NR.

### Intuitive Picture of the Evolution

> [!NOTE]
> Think of mobile internet as the journey from **sending letters by horse-cart (GSM/SMS)** to **streaming movies via fiber-optic equivalents (5G)**. **GSM** = horse-cart (voice only, narrow pipes). **GPRS** = the first postal trucks (always-on parcels over the same road). **EDGE** = upgraded trucks. **3G/4G/5G** = highways and expressways. IoT devices that need to send a few kilobytes per hour still happily ride the "GPRS truck" today because of its enormous coverage and ultra-low cost per bit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 GSM Architecture — Layered Breakdown

The GSM system is decomposed into three principal subsystems:

| Subsystem | Components | Function |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **MS** (Mobile Station) | ME (Mobile Equipment) + SIM card | User endpoint; SIM holds IMSI, Ki, authentication |
| **BSS** (Base Station Subsystem) | BTS + BSC | BTS = radio transceiver; BSC = controls multiple BTS, handovers |
| **NSS** (Network Switching Subsystem) | MSC, HLR, VLR, AUC, EIR | Switching, subscriber location, security |

### Functional Roles of Network Elements

- **HLR (Home Location Register):** Central database of every subscriber belonging to the network. Stores IMSI, MSISDN, current VLR address, service profile.
- **VLR (Visitor Location Register):** Temporary database for subscribers currently roaming in its area. Avoids frequent HLR queries.
- **AUC (Authentication Center):** Stores the secret key **Ki** and generates triplets (RAND, SRES, Kc) for authentication & ciphering.
- **EIR (Equipment Identity Register):** Blacklists stolen/ faulty handsets using IMEI.
- **MSC (Mobile Switching Center):** Core PSTN gateway; handles call setup, routing, handover between BSCs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "HAVE ME"** → **H**LR, **A**UC, **V**LR, **E**IR, **M**SC, **E**IR (yes, EIR twice on purpose — call it the *security squad*).

## 2.2 GSM Radio Interface — Channel Structure (The "Why" and "How")

### Why FDMA + TDMA?

Pure FDMA would need **hundreds of filters per cell** (expensive). Pure TDMA on a single carrier would force all users to share one frequency (severe interference). **GSM ingeniously combines them:** the 25 MHz band is split into **124 carriers of 200 kHz** each, and each carrier is further time-shared among **8 users** using TDMA. This is a sweet spot between hardware cost and spectral efficiency.

### GSM Frame Hierarchy (from smallest to largest)

| Hierarchy Level | Duration | Composition | Purpose |
|-----------------|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Burst Period** | 0.577 ms (576.92 µs) | 156.25 bit periods | One physical transmission unit |
| **TDMA Frame** | 4.615 ms | 8 time slots | One rotation of all 8 users |
| **Multiframe (Traffic)** | 120 ms | 26 frames | TCH + SACCH |
| **Multiframe (Control)** | 235.4 ms | 51 frames | BCCH, CCCH, SDCCH |
| **Superframe** | 6.12 s | 26 × 51 = 1326 frames | One complete cycle |
| **Hyperframe** | 3 h 28 m 53 s | 2048 superframes | Encryption & paging cycle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The TDMA frame of **4.615 ms** divided into **8 time slots** means each user gets to transmit for **577 µs** every 4.615 ms. This is the heartbeat of GSM.

## 2.3 GPRS Architecture — What Is Added on Top of GSM?

GPRS introduces two new core-network elements while reusing the existing GSM radio access (BTS, BSC):

| New Element | Full Form | Function |
|-------------|-----------|----------|
| **SGSN** | Serving GPRS Support Node | Equivalent of MSC for packets; mobility management, session management, authentication, packet routing within the same PLMN |
| **GGSN** | Gateway GPRS Support Node | Gateway to external IP networks (Internet, X.25); assigns IP addresses; the "edge router" of the mobile network |
| **PCU** | Packet Control Unit | Added at BSC; routes packet data between BTS and SGSN |

### GPRS Logical Architecture Flow

> **MS → BTS → BSC (with PCU) → SGSN → GGSN → Internet**

- The **GTP (GPRS Tunneling Protocol)** is used to carry user IP packets between SGSN and GGSN inside the operator's backbone.
- A **PDP Context** is established between MS and GGSN to allocate an IP address and QoS profile.

## 2.4 GPRS Channel Allocation — "When Does a Slot Become GPRS?"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> GPRS uses the same **physical channels** as GSM but introduces the concept of a **PDCH (Packet Data Channel)**. Channels are allocated **dynamically**:
> 1. A cell advertises **PRACH** (Packet Random Access Channel) and **PCCCH** (Packet Common Control Channels) in the broadcast.
> 2. MS sends a **Packet Channel Request** on PRACH.
> 3. The network (PCU) assigns a **PDCH** — a free time slot that the cell decides to lend to GPRS traffic.
> 4. If all slots are busy with voice calls, **GPRS waits** — this is why GPRS throughput drops in congested cells.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Symbol / Formula | Value / Unit | Used To Compute |
|---|-----------|------------------|--------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Carrier bandwidth | $\Delta f$ | **200 kHz** | Channel count |
| 2 | Number of carriers (GSM-900 UL) | $N_c = \dfrac{890.2 - 880}{0.2} + 1$ | **51 carriers** | Spectrum planning |
| 3 | Number of carriers (GSM-900 DL) | $N_c = \dfrac{935 - 925}{0.2}$ | **49 carriers** | (with duplex gap) |
| 4 | Total duplex pairs (GSM-900) | $N_{duplex}$ | **124** | Cell capacity |
| 5 | Time slots per TDMA frame | $N_{TS}$ | **8** | User multiplexing |
| 6 | Frame duration | $T_f$ | **4.615 ms** | Burst timing |
| 7 | Bit duration | $T_b = \dfrac{T_f}{156.25 \text{ bits} \times N_{TS}/8}$ | **3.692 µs** | Modulation rate |
| 8 | Gross bit rate per carrier | $R_b = \dfrac{1}{T_b} = \dfrac{156.25}{T_f}$ | **270.833 kbps** | Per-carrier capacity |
| 9 | Data rate per user (CSD) | $R_{CSD} = R_b \times \dfrac{1}{8} \times \eta_{coding}$ | **9.6 kbps** | Voice-grade data |
| 10 | GPRS slot rate (CS-1) | $R_{CS1}$ | **9.05 kbps** | Robust coding |
| 11 | GPRS slot rate (CS-2) | $R_{CS2}$ | **13.4 kbps** | Balanced |
| 12 | GPRS slot rate (CS-3) | $R_{CS3}$ | **15.6 kbps** | Higher throughput |
| 13 | GPRS slot rate (CS-4) | $R_{CS4}$ | **21.4 kbps** | No FEC — clean cells only |
| 14 | GPRS peak (8 slots, CS-4) | $R_{GPRS,max} = 8 \times R_{CS4}$ | **171.2 kbps** | Theoretical max |
| 15 | GPRS typical | $R_{GPRS,typ}$ | **30–60 kbps** | Real-world |
| 16 | Reuse cluster size (typical 4/12) | $K$ | **4 or 12** | Co-channel interference |
| 17 | Frequency reuse distance | $D = R \sqrt{3K}$ | km | Cell planning |
| 18 | Erlangs / cell (GoS 2%) | $A_{erl}$ | From Erlang B table | Traffic engineering |
| 19 | Cell capacity (channels) | $C = N_{TS} \times N_{TRX}$ | e.g., 24 (3 TRX) | Users per cell |
| 20 | Hyperframe count | $2048 \times 26 \times 51$ | **2,715,648 TDMA frames** | Encryption cycle |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** Formulas 1–8 are essential for any question on GSM spectral efficiency. Formulas 10–15 are the heart of every GPRS data-rate problem. Formula 19 directly answers "how many simultaneous voice users can one cell serve?" — a favourite 7-mark question in KTU exams.

## 2.6 Why GPRS Was the True Birth of "Mobile Internet"

Before GPRS, GSM could only offer **Circuit-Switched Data (CSD)** at 9.6 kbps — a connection that took **30 seconds to establish** and **billed by the minute** even if no data flowed. GPRS flipped this by introducing:

1. **Always-on PDP context** — connection is established once, stays for hours/days.
2. **Per-kilobyte billing** — economic for low-bandwidth IoT.
3. **IP-native** — the GGSN directly assigns an IP address; the MS is on the Internet.
4. **Variable-rate** — 1 to 8 time slots allocated dynamically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering impact:** This is exactly why **millions of IoT devices (smart meters, vehicle trackers, asset monitors)** still use **GPRS modems (e.g., SIM800, SIM900, BG96)** in 2024–2026. Coverage is global, modules cost \$3, and a 100-byte sensor reading costs fractions of a cent. **5G will eventually replace it, but GPRS remains the workhorse of LPWAN-style IoT today.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bit Duration and Gross Bit Rate of GSM

### Given

- TDMA frame duration: $T_f = 4.615 \text{ ms}$
- Number of time slots per frame: $N_{TS} = 8$
- Number of bit periods per time slot: $156.25$
- A GSM burst occupies one time slot and contains 156.25 bit periods (148 data bits + 8.25 training sequence bits, rounded for the slot).

### Goal

Derive the **bit duration** $T_b$ and **gross bit rate** $R_b$ of a single RF carrier.

### Step 1 — Express the frame duration in terms of bit periods

$$
T_f = N_{TS} \times 156.25 \times T_b
$$

> *Explanation:* The total frame time equals the number of slots (8) times the bits per slot (156.25) times the duration of one bit.

### Step 2 — Substitute known values

$$
4.615 \times 10^{-3} = 8 \times 156.25 \times T_b
$$

> *Explanation:* We now have one equation with one unknown ($T_b$).

### Step 3 — Solve for $T_b$

$$
T_b = \dfrac{4.615 \times 10^{-3}}{8 \times 156.25}
$$

$$
T_b = \dfrac{4.615 \times 10^{-3}}{1250}
$$

$$
T_b = 3.692 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 3.692 \text{ µs}
$$

> *Conclusion:* Each bit in GSM occupies **3.692 microseconds** of time on the air.

### Step 4 — Compute the gross bit rate

$$
R_b = \dfrac{1}{T_b} = \dfrac{1}{3.692 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
R_b = 270.833 \times 10^{3} \text{ bps} = 270.833 \text{ kbps}
$$

> *Conclusion:* Each 200 kHz GSM carrier delivers **270.833 kbps** of gross (un-coded) bit rate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Net Data Rate of GSM Circuit-Switched Data (CSD)

### Given

- Gross bit rate per carrier: $R_b = 270.833$ kbps
- 1 user per time slot (out of 8)
- Channel coding rate (convolutional + block): $\eta_{coding} = \dfrac{1}{3.16}$  *(i.e., roughly 1/3 FEC efficiency, leaving about 0.316 of the gross rate as user data)*

### Goal

Derive the user-visible data rate of one CSD channel.

### Step 1 — Per-slot bit rate (before coding)

$$
R_{slot} = \dfrac{R_b}{N_{TS}} = \dfrac{270.833}{8} = 33.854 \text{ kbps}
$$

> *Explanation:* Eight users equally share the carrier, so one slot gets one-eighth.

### Step 2 — Apply coding efficiency

$$
R_{user} = R_{slot} \times \eta_{coding}
$$

$$
R_{user} = 33.854 \times \dfrac{1}{3.16}
$$

$$
R_{user} \approx 10.71 \text{ kbps} \text{ (raw) }
$$

### Step 3 — Account for framing overhead (SACCH, idle, etc.)

In practice, after subtracting the Slow Associated Control CHannel (SACCH) and the 26-multiframe structure, the **net user rate** is fixed by GSM spec at:

$$
R_{CSD} = 9.6 \text{ kbps}
$$

> *Conclusion:* The famous **9.6 kbps** of GSM CSD.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — GPRS Peak and Typical Rates

### Given

- Coding schemes and their per-slot rates:

$$
R_{CS1} = 9.05 \text{ kbps}, \quad R_{CS2} = 13.4 \text{ kbps}, \quad R_{CS3} = 15.6 \text{ kbps}, \quad R_{CS4} = 21.4 \text{ kbps}
$$

- Maximum time slots assignable to a single MS: $N_{TS,max} = 8$ (one entire frame)

### Goal

Find theoretical peak, and the realistic rate assuming 4 slots + CS-2.

### Step 1 — Theoretical peak (8 slots, CS-4)

$$
R_{GPRS,max} = N_{TS,max} \times R_{CS4}
$$

$$
R_{GPRS,max} = 8 \times 21.4 = 171.2 \text{ kbps}
$$

> *Conclusion:* **Theoretical maximum GPRS = 171.2 kbps.** This is the number you will quote in exams.

### Step 2 — Realistic peak (4 slots, CS-2)

Many networks limit MS to **4 slots uplink / 4 slots downlink** in early GPRS, and CS-2 is the most commonly used scheme.

$$
R_{GPRS,real} = 4 \times 13.4 = 53.6 \text{ kbps}
$$

> *Conclusion:* This is the **typical end-user speed** one quotes in KTU answers.

### Step 3 — Time to download a 100 KB IoT packet

$$
T = \dfrac{\text{Packet Size}}{R_{GPRS,real}} = \dfrac{100 \times 1024 \times 8 \text{ bits}}{53.6 \times 10^{3} \text{ bps}}
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{819{,}200}{53{,}600} \approx 15.28 \text{ s}
$$

> *Engineering insight:* This is why GPRS is suitable for **small, infrequent IoT packets** (smart-meter readings, GPS pings) but unsuitable for firmware updates or video.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Number of GSM Carriers and Reuse Pattern

### Step 1 — Compute carriers in GSM-900 uplink

$$
N_{c,UL} = \dfrac{890.2 - 880.2}{0.2} + 1 = \dfrac{10}{0.2} + 1 = 50 + 1 = 51
$$

> *Explanation:* Subtract the lower edge (880.2 MHz) from the upper edge (890.2 MHz), divide by 200 kHz spacing, add 1 to include both endpoints.

### Step 2 — Downlink carriers (935.2 – 944.8)

$$
N_{c,DL} = \dfrac{944.8 - 935.2}{0.2} + 1 = \dfrac{9.6}{0.2} + 1 = 48 + 1 = 49
$$

### Step 3 — Total duplex pairs

$$
N_{duplex} = N_{c,UL} + N_{c,DL} = 51 + 49 + 24 = 124
$$

> *(The "24" comes from the **extension band** 880–915 / 925–960 used in some regions.)*  Total carriers used in the famous KTU textbook figure: **124**.

### Step 4 — Cells in a 4/12 reuse cluster

If the operator has $C = 124$ carriers and a cluster size $K = 4$:

$$
\text{Carriers per cell} = \dfrac{124}{4} = 31
$$

$$
\text{Time slots per cell} = 31 \times 8 = 248
$$

> *Engineering insight:* Roughly **248 simultaneous voice users** per cell in GSM-900, *if every carrier is fully loaded*. Real engineering reserves capacity for handover and signalling, so the practical figure is **~200 Erlangs** (Erlang B at 2% GoS).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — GSM vs GPRS vs EDGE

| Feature | GSM (2G) | GPRS (2.5G) | EDGE (2.75G) |
|---------|----------|-------------|--------------|
| **Switching** | Circuit | Packet | Packet |
| **Modulation** | GMSK | GMSK | 8-PSK |
| **Symbol rate** | 270.833 ksym/s | 270.833 ksym/s | 270.833 ksym/s |
| **Bits/symbol** | 1 | 1 | 3 |
| **Per-slot peak** | 9.6 kbps | 21.4 kbps (CS-4) | **59.2 kbps** (MCS-9) |
| **Theoretical max (8 slots)** | 76.8 kbps | 171.2 kbps | **473.6 kbps** |
| **Latency** | High (call setup) | Medium (PDP ctx) | Medium |
| **Always-on** | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Billing** | Per minute | Per KB | Per KB |
| **IoT use today** | Legacy SMS | **Workhorse** | M2M upgrades |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EDGE (Enhanced Data rates for GSM Evolution)** changes the modulation from GMSK to **8-PSK**, packing 3 bits per symbol, which **triples** the per-slot rate. The formula becomes $R_{EDGE,max} = 8 \times 59.2 = 473.6$ kbps.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GSM Network Architecture — End-to-End Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MS["Mobile Station"]
        ME["ME - Mobile Equipment"]
        SIM["SIM Card - IMSI, Ki"]
    end

    subgraph BSS["Base Station Subsystem"]
        BTS["BTS - Base Transceiver Station"]
        BSC["BSC - Base Station Controller"]
    end

    subgraph NSS["Network Switching Subsystem"]
        MSC["MSC - Mobile Switching Center"]
        HLR["HLR - Home Location Register"]
        VLR["VLR - Visitor Location Register"]
        AUC["AUC - Authentication Center"]
        EIR["EIR - Equipment Identity Register"]
    end

    PSTN["PSTN / External Networks"]

    ME -- Um radio interface --> BTS
    BTS -- Abis interface --> BSC
    BSC -- A interface --> MSC
    MSC <--> HLR
    MSC <--> VLR
    MSC <--> AUC
    MSC <--> EIR
    MSC <--> PSTN
    SIM -. logical link .-> ME
```

> *Reading the diagram:* A call from your phone travels **ME → Um-air → BTS → Abis → BSC → A → MSC → PSTN**. The HLR/VLR/AUC/EIR are the "memory and security" of the network, sitting beside the MSC.

---

## 4.2 GPRS Architecture — Extension on Top of GSM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MS2["MS - Mobile Station"]
    BTS2["BTS"]
    BSC2["BSC with PCU"]
    SGSN["SGSN - Serving GPRS Support Node"]
    GGSN["GGSN - Gateway GPRS Support Node"]
    INTERNET["Internet / IP Network"]
    HLR2["HLR / AUC - shared with GSM"]
    OTHER["Other PLMN - SGSN of roaming network"]

    MS2 -- radio burst --> BTS2
    BTS2 --> BSC2
    BSC2 -- Gb interface --> SGSN
    SGSN -- Gn interface --> GGSN
    SGSN -- Gr interface --> HLR2
    SGSN -- Gn GTP tunnel --> OTHER
    GGSN -- Gi interface --> INTERNET
```

> *Reading the diagram:* The **PCU inside the BSC** splits packet traffic away from voice. The **SGSN** handles mobility and security for packets. The **GGSN** is the "IP edge router" that finally hands the data to the public Internet. The **Gn interface** between SGSN and GGSN uses **GTP (GPRS Tunnelling Protocol)**.

---

## 4.3 GSM/GPRS Frame Hierarchy — The Timing Ladder

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Burst Period - 0.577 ms / 156.25 bit periods"]
    B["TDMA Frame - 4.615 ms / 8 time slots"]
    C["Traffic Multiframe - 120 ms / 26 frames"]
    D["Control Multiframe - 235.4 ms / 51 frames"]
    E["Superframe - 6.12 s / 1326 frames"]
    F["Hyperframe - 3 h 28 m 53 s / 2,715,648 frames"]

    A -- x8 --> B
    B -- x26 --> C
    B -- x51 --> D
    C -- combine with D --> E
    E -- x2048 --> F
```

> *Reading the diagram:* Every "level up" is built by concatenating the smaller unit. The hyperframe of ~3.5 hours is the **encryption and frequency-hopping cycle** — a critical KTU fact.

---

## 4.4 GPRS Channel Allocation — Dynamic Slot Borrowing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C0["Carrier Frequency 1 - 200 kHz"]
    S0["Slot 0 - Voice TCH"]
    S1["Slot 1 - Voice TCH"]
    S2["Slot 2 - PDCH - GPRS packet"]
    S3["Slot 3 - PDCH - GPRS packet"]
    S4["Slot 4 - PDCH - GPRS packet"]
    S5["Slot 5 - idle or SDCCH"]
    S6["Slot 6 - PDCH - GPRS packet"]
    S7["Slot 7 - Voice TCH"]

    C0 --- S0
    C0 --- S1
    C0 --- S2
    C0 --- S3
    C0 --- S4
    C0 --- S5
    C0 --- S6
    C0 --- S7
```

> *Engineering insight:* Notice that **GPRS and voice share the same 8 time slots dynamically**. A slot is *not* permanently assigned to GPRS — the network lends it out via the PCU whenever data appears. This is the heart of "always-on" mobile internet.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> Explain the role of **HLR, VLR, and AUC** in a GSM network. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The three registers form the "memory and security brain" of the GSM core network.
> - **HLR (Home Location Register):** A permanent database that stores every permanent subscriber of the home network. It contains the **IMSI, MSISDN, current VLR address, and service profile** of each user. It is queried when a subscriber moves to a new VLR area.
> - **VLR (Visitor Location Register):** A temporary, location-specific database. When a subscriber enters a new MSC area, a copy of relevant HLR data is cached in the VLR so that local calls can be switched without repeated HLR lookups, reducing signalling load.
> - **AUC (Authentication Center):** Stores the secret **128-bit Ki** of every SIM. It generates the **authentication triplet (RAND, SRES, Kc)** that authenticates the SIM and produces the session ciphering key Kc, ensuring call privacy and preventing cloning.

*(Word count ~120 — appropriate for a 3-mark answer.)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> Differentiate between **GSM and GPRS** in terms of switching technique, data rate, and billing. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | GSM | GPRS |
|-----------|-----|------|
| **Switching** | Circuit-switched | Packet-switched |
| **Data Rate** | 9.6 kbps (CSD) | 56–114 kbps typical, 171.2 kbps peak |
| **Billing** | Per minute of airtime | Per kilobyte of data transferred |
| **Always-on** | No (dial-up style) | Yes (PDP context) |
| **Resource Use** | Reserved for whole call | Shared, allocated on demand |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse **GPRS coding schemes** (CS-1 to CS-4) with **GSM channels** (TCH, SDCCH, SACCH). Examiners deduct 2 marks if you interchange them.
> 2. **Always show units** (kbps, MHz, ms). Marks are awarded for "270.833 **kbps**", not "270.833".
> 3. In GPRS rate calculations, **explicitly state the assumption** (e.g., "assuming CS-2 and 4 time slots"). Marks are lost when students simply write "GPRS = 114 kbps" without justification.
> 4. Do **not** call GPRS "3G". It is officially **2.5G**. EDGE is **2.75G**.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a GSM network**. List the functions of **MSC, HLR, VLR, and AUC**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain the **GPRS architecture** and the role of **SGSN and GGSN**. Compute the **theoretical peak and typical data rate** of GPRS. *(7 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**(1) GSM Architecture Diagram (3 marks)**

The GSM network is divided into three subsystems:

1. **Mobile Station (MS):** Comprises the Mobile Equipment (ME) and the SIM card. The ME is the physical handset; the SIM stores the **IMSI** (International Mobile Subscriber Identity), the **Ki** (secret key), and the user's service profile.

2. **Base Station Subsystem (BSS):** Consists of the **BTS** (Base Transceiver Station) — the radio tower with antennas and transceivers covering a cell — and the **BSC** (Base Station Controller) — which manages multiple BTS units, handles radio resource allocation, frequency hopping, and inter-cell handovers.

3. **Network Switching Subsystem (NSS):** The "brain" of GSM. Contains the MSC, HLR, VLR, AUC, and EIR. It interfaces with the PSTN for actual call delivery.

**(2) Functions of Key Elements (4 marks — 1 mark each)**

- **MSC (Mobile Switching Center):** Central switching node. Routes voice calls between BSCs and the PSTN. Performs the **SS7 signalling** for call setup, location registration, and handovers. Acts as the **Gateway MSC (GMSC)** for incoming calls from external networks.
- **HLR (Home Location Register):** Permanent database of all subscribers belonging to the home network. Holds IMSI, MSISDN, current VLR pointer, and service subscription. Queried during location update.
- **VLR (Visitor Location Register):** Temporary database for subscribers currently in its MSC area. Stores a copy of relevant HLR data so that local switching is fast and HLR traffic is minimised.
- **AUC (Authentication Center):** Stores the 128-bit secret key **Ki** for each SIM. Generates **authentication triplets** (RAND, SRES, Kc) and the **ciphering key Kc** for A5/1 encryption. Protects against SIM cloning and eavesdropping.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**(1) GPRS Architecture (3 marks)**

GPRS is a **packet-switched overlay** on the existing GSM infrastructure. The radio side (BTS, BSC) is reused, with a **Packet Control Unit (PCU)** added inside the BSC to route packet data. Two new core elements are introduced:

- **SGSN (Serving GPRS Support Node):** MSC-equivalent for packets. Handles **mobility management (GMM)**, **session management (SM)**, authentication (with HLR/AUC), and tunnelling of user IP packets to the GGSN within the same PLMN.
- **GGSN (Gateway GPRS Support Node):** The IP edge router of the mobile operator. Allocates **dynamic or static IP addresses** to MS via the PDP context, encapsulates user data using **GTP (GPRS Tunnelling Protocol)**, and routes packets to the public Internet via the **Gi interface**.

The **PDP (Packet Data Protocol) Context** is the logical connection between MS and GGSN — once established, the MS is "always on" the Internet.

**(2) Theoretical Peak Rate Calculation (2 marks)**

$$
R_{GPRS,peak} = N_{TS,max} \times R_{CS4} = 8 \times 21.4 = \mathbf{171.2 \text{ kbps}}
$$

**(3) Typical Real-World Rate (2 marks)**

In deployed networks, the MS is typically granted **4 slots** and the cell uses **CS-2** (balance between robustness and throughput):

$$
R_{GPRS,typ} = 4 \times 13.4 = \mathbf{53.6 \text{ kbps}}
$$

Hence, the **practical range is 30–60 kbps**, often quoted in KTU answers as the user-experienced throughput.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

> **(a)** Explain the **GSM TDMA frame structure** with a neat sketch. Compute the **bit duration, gross bit rate per carrier, and the CSD user data rate**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** With reference to **GPRS**, explain the **four coding schemes (CS-1 to CS-4)**. Why does **CS-4 give a higher rate but lower robustness**? *(7 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**(1) GSM TDMA Frame Structure (3 marks)**

A GSM carrier is divided in **time** into repeating **TDMA frames** of duration **4.615 ms**. Each frame contains **8 time slots (TS 0–7)**. Each time slot carries a **burst** of **156.25 bit periods** (≈ 148 data bits + 8.25 training sequence + 3 tail + guard). A burst lasts **0.577 ms**.

Multiple frames are grouped into:
- **Traffic multiframe:** 26 frames = **120 ms** (carries TCH + SACCH).
- **Control multiframe:** 51 frames = **235.4 ms** (carries BCCH, CCCH, SDCCH).
- **Superframe:** 26 × 51 = 1326 frames = **6.12 s**.
- **Hyperframe:** 2048 superframes = **2,715,648 TDMA frames = 3 h 28 m 53 s** (the encryption and frequency-hopping cycle).

**(2) Bit Duration (1 mark)**

$$
T_b = \dfrac{T_f}{N_{TS} \times 156.25} = \dfrac{4.615 \times 10^{-3}}{8 \times 156.25} = 3.692 \text{ µs}
$$

**(3) Gross Bit Rate per Carrier (1 mark)**

$$
R_b = \dfrac{1}{T_b} = 270.833 \text{ kbps}
$$

**(4) CSD User Data Rate (2 marks)**

After FEC coding (rate 1/3.16) and subtracting SACCH/framing overhead:

$$
R_{CSD} = \dfrac{R_b}{8} \times \eta_{coding} \approx 9.6 \text{ kbps}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**(1) The Four GPRS Coding Schemes (4 marks)**

| Scheme | Per-slot Rate | Code Rate | Robustness | Use Case |
|--------|---------------|-----------|------------|----------|
| **CS-1** | 9.05 kbps | 1/2 | **Highest** | Poor signal / cell edge |
| **CS-2** | 13.4 kbps | ≈ 2/3 | High | Typical urban |
| **CS-3** | 15.6 kbps | ≈ 3/4 | Medium | Good signal |
| **CS-4** | 21.4 kbps | **1 (no FEC)** | **Lowest** | Excellent signal only |

**(2) Why CS-4 Is Faster but Less Robust (3 marks)**

- **Higher rate:** CS-4 has **no convolutional Forward Error Correction (FEC)**. Every transmitted bit is a *user* bit. With no redundancy bits to transmit, the useful bit rate per slot rises from 9.05 kbps (CS-1) to **21.4 kbps** (CS-4) — a 2.4× increase.
- **Lower robustness:** Without FEC, any bit corrupted by noise or interference *stays* corrupted. There is no error-correcting redundancy to recover it, so the **Block Error Rate (BLER)** rises sharply. CS-4 can only be used when the **C/I (carrier-to-interference ratio) is high** — typically only at the cell centre in a clean radio environment. At the cell edge, the network automatically **falls back** to CS-1 to maintain connectivity at the cost of speed.
- This is why GPRS adapts the coding scheme **per block** based on instantaneous channel quality — a process called **Link Adaptation**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — print this before the exam!**

- **GSM** = **2G**, circuit-switched, **GMSK** modulation, **200 kHz** carrier, **8 time slots/frame**, **4.615 ms** frame, **270.833 kbps** gross, **9.6 kbps** user (CSD).
- **GPRS** = **2.5G**, packet-switched, **always-on**, per-KB billing. Adds **SGSN** and **GGSN** to core, **PCU** to BSC.
- **GPRS peak (CS-4, 8 slots)** = **171.2 kbps**. Typical (CS-2, 4 slots) = **53.6 kbps**.
- **EDGE (2.75G)** uses **8-PSK** (3 bits/symbol) → per-slot rate **59.2 kbps**, peak **473.6 kbps**.
- **HAVE ME** = **H**LR, **A**UC, **V**LR, **E**IR, **M**SC — the GSM NSS memory/security squad.
- **SIM** holds **IMSI + Ki**. **AUC** generates authentication **triplets (RAND, SRES, Kc)**.
- **Hyperframe = 3 h 28 m 53 s** = encryption & frequency-hopping cycle (2048 superframes × 1326 frames).
- **Carrier spacing 200 kHz**, GSM-900 has **124 duplex pairs**.
- **GPRS coding schemes** adapt per radio block: CS-1 (robust) → CS-4 (fast, weak signal kills it).
- **WAP** is the protocol stack for rendering web content on 2G/2.5G devices (WAE, WSP, WTP, WTLS, WDP).
- **Mobile Internet** truly began with **GPRS** because it introduced IP-native, always-on, packet-billed connectivity to GSM.
- **IoT context:** GPRS modems (SIM800, SIM900, BG96) still dominate **low-bandwidth, low-cost, high-coverage** M2M applications (smart meters, vehicle tracking, asset monitoring) — your viva question.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonics:**
> - **"8 slots, 4.6 ms"** → TDMA frame of GSM.
> - **"1+1+1+3"** → GMSK (1 bit) vs 8-PSK (3 bits).
> - **"SGSN inside, GGSN outside"** → SGSN = inside PLMN; GGSN = edge to Internet.
> - **"CS-4 = no FEC = no mercy"** → fastest, but only in clean cells.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wired Communication - Serial Peripheral Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS AND SMART COMPUTING.
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wired Communication - Serial Peripheral Interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Serial Peripheral Interface (SPI)** is a synchronous, full-duplex, master-slave based **serial communication protocol** developed by Motorola in the mid-1980s. It is used for short-distance, high-speed communication between a **master controller** (e.g., microcontroller) and one or more **peripheral slave devices** (e.g., sensors, SD cards, displays, ADCs/DACs). SPI operates using a shared **clock signal (SCLK)** generated by the master, making it a *clock-synchronous* bus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCGT602 – Module 2):** Under the topic *Wired Communication*, SPI is a mandatory sub-topic. Examiners frequently test the four SPI modes (CPOL & CPHA), the pin configuration (MOSI, MISO, SCLK, SS/CS), and the master-slave data exchange mechanism.

## 1.2 The Four SPI Signal Lines

| Line | Full Name | Direction | Function |
|------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **MOSI** | Master Out, Slave In | Master → Slave | Carries data from master to slave |
| **MISO** | Master In, Slave Out | Slave → Master | Carries data from slave to master |
| **SCLK** | Serial Clock | Master → Slave | Synchronization clock generated by master |
| **SS/CS** | Slave Select / Chip Select | Master → Slave | Active-low line to select a specific slave |

> [!NOTE]
> **GND (Common Ground)** is mandatory. Although not part of the "4-wire SPI" count, both master and slave **must share a common ground reference** for the voltage levels to be interpreted correctly.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The School Assembly Line"

Imagine a **head chef (master)** coordinating multiple kitchen assistants (slaves) in a restaurant:
- The head chef blows a **whistle (SCLK)** at regular intervals to keep everyone synchronized.
- The chef hands recipe cards down the **outgoing conveyor belt (MOSI)**.
- The assistants report back through the **incoming conveyor belt (MISO)**.
- To get a particular assistant's attention, the chef presses a **specific call button (SS/CS)** — only that assistant responds.

This perfectly mirrors SPI: data flows both ways *simultaneously* (full-duplex), the master controls timing via a shared clock, and only the selected slave participates in the conversation.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **Full-Duplex Communication**: Data transfer happens in **both directions simultaneously**.
- **Clock Speed Range**: From **100 kHz** (low-speed peripherals) up to **> 50 MHz** (high-speed sensors, displays).
- **No Formal Maximum Word Length**: SPI is **not limited to 8-bit words**; word sizes of 8, 12, or 16 bits are common.
- **Push-Pull Drivers**: SPI uses strong **push-pull CMOS drivers** (unlike I²C's open-drain), enabling higher speeds.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — How SPI Works Step-by-Step

1. **Master Initializes the Bus**: The master configures the clock frequency, polarity (CPOL), and phase (CPHA).
2. **Slave Selection**: The master pulls the **SS/CS line of the target slave LOW** (active-low logic). Other slaves ignore the bus.
3. **Clock Generation**: The master generates the **SCLK** signal. Each clock cycle corresponds to one bit transfer.
4. **Bit-Shifting Mechanism**: Both master and slave maintain an internal **shift register** (typically 8-bit). On each clock edge, **one bit is shifted out** on MOSI/MISO and **one bit is shifted in**.
5. **Data Exchange (Simultaneous)**: While the master sends a byte on MOSI, the slave simultaneously sends a byte on MISO. **After 8 clock pulses, both registers have effectively exchanged their contents.**
6. **Deselection**: The master raises SS/CS **HIGH** to release the slave.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Circular Shift Register" View**: Many KTU examiners award full marks if you draw SPI as **two shift registers connected as a ring**. This single image captures MOSI + MISO + SCLK in one diagram.

## 2.2 SPI Data Transmission Timing — CPOL and CPHA

The behavior of SCLK and the moment data is sampled is controlled by two configuration bits:

### 2.2.1 Clock Polarity (CPOL)

- **CPOL = 0**: SCLK is **LOW** in the idle state. The leading edge is a **rising edge**.
- **CPOL = 1**: SCLK is **HIGH** in the idle state. The leading edge is a **falling edge**.

### 2.2.2 Clock Phase (CPHA)

- **CPHA = 0**: Data is **sampled on the leading (first) edge** and changed on the trailing edge.
- **CPHA = 1**: Data is **sampled on the trailing (second) edge** and changed on the leading edge.

### 2.2.2 The Four SPI Modes (High-Yield for KTU!)

| Mode | CPOL | CPHA | Idle Clock | Sampling Edge | Shifting Edge |
|:----:|:----:|:----:|:----------:|:-------------:|:-------------:|
| **Mode 0** | 0 | 0 | LOW | Rising edge | Falling edge |
| **Mode 1** | 0 | 1 | LOW | Falling edge | Rising edge |
| **Mode 2** | 1 | 0 | HIGH | Falling edge | Rising edge |
| **Mode 3** | 1 | 1 | HIGH | Rising edge | Falling edge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mode 0 is the most common** configuration in IoT sensors (e.g., SD cards default to Mode 0). Examiners love asking: *"What is the default SPI mode of an SD card?"* — answer: **Mode 0 (CPOL=0, CPHA=0)**.

## 2.3 Multi-Slave Configurations

### 2.3.1 Independent Slave Configuration
- Each slave has its **own dedicated SS/CS line**.
