- Master requires **N GPIO pins** to control **N slaves**.
- ✅ Simple; ✅ full-speed simultaneous access (only one at a time though).
- ❌ GPIO pin count grows linearly with slave count.

### 2.3.2 Daisy-Chain Configuration
- All slaves share **one SS line**; MISO of slave *n* feeds into MOSI of slave *n+1*.
- Data passes through all slaves serially.
- ✅ Saves GPIO pins; ✅ ideal for chained LED drivers (e.g., APA102).
- ❌ Latency increases; ❌ requires slave devices that support daisy-chaining.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Rule | Unit / Note |
|-----------|----------------|-------------|
| **Number of wires (single slave)** | $N_w = 4$ | MOSI, MISO, SCLK, SS |
| **Number of wires (multi-slave, independent)** | $N_w = 3 + N_s$ | $N_s$ = number of slaves |
| **Throughput (bits/sec)** | $R_b = f_{SCLK} \times \text{channel efficiency}$ | $f_{SCLK}$ in Hz |
| **Total bits per frame** | $B_f = N$ | $N$ = word length (usually 8) |
| **Time per frame** | $T_f = \dfrac{N}{f_{SCLK}}$ | seconds |
| **Full-duplex time efficiency** | $\eta = 1$ (100%) | Both lines active simultaneously |
| **Slave addressing (no inherent address)** | SS line index | No addressing overhead |

> [!NOTE]
> The *vertical bar* character $\vert$ is shown here using LaTeX `\vert` to remain safe inside markdown tables. Do not write `|x|` directly inside a table cell.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SD Cards & MicroSD**: Use SPI Mode 0 for low-pin-count interfacing.
- **OLED / TFT Displays**: High-speed SPI enables fast framebuffer updates.
- **ADC/DAC Chips**: e.g., MCP3002, MCP4921.
- **Flash Memory (NOR/NAND)**: Bootloaders often use SPI flash.
- **Sensor Networks**: BME280, MPU6050, MAX6675.
- **Industrial IoT Gateways**: Multi-slave SPI hubs aggregate sensor data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Time to Transmit One Byte Over SPI

To answer common KTU numerical questions such as *"If SCLK = 4 MHz, how long does it take to transmit 16 bits?"*, we derive the transfer time.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

Let the SCLK frequency be $f_{SCLK}$ (in Hz), and let the word length be $N$ bits.

**Step 2 — Recall that SPI transmits 1 bit per SCLK clock cycle.**

$$
\text{Bits per second} = f_{SCLK}
$$

**Step 3 — Express time per bit.**

$$
T_{bit} = \frac{1}{f_{SCLK}}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by the number of bits to get frame time.**

$$
T_{frame} = N \cdot T_{bit} = \frac{N}{f_{SCLK}}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style).**

> *"A sensor uses SPI with $f_{SCLK} = 4\text{ MHz}$ and a 16-bit word length. Calculate the time to transmit one sample."*

Substituting $N = 16$ and $f_{SCLK} = 4 \times 10^6$ Hz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{frame} &= \frac{N}{f_{SCLK}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{16}{4 \times 10^{6}} \\[6pt]
&= 4 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{seconds} \\[6pt]
&= 4\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Each 16-bit sample requires **4 microseconds**, giving a maximum sample rate of:

$$
f_{sample,\ max} = \frac{1}{T_{frame}} = \frac{1}{4\ \mu s} = 250{,}000\ \text{samples/second}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Number of GPIO Pins Required for Multi-Slave SPI

**Step 1 — Define the variables.**

Let $N_s$ be the number of slave devices in an *independent slave* topology. SPI requires 3 shared lines (MOSI, MISO, SCLK) plus 1 SS line per slave.

**Step 2 — Write the formula.**

$$
N_{GPIO} = 3 + N_s
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example.**

For $N_s = 5$ slaves:

$$
N_{GPIO} = 3 + 5 = 8\ \text{GPIO pins}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare to a daisy-chain topology.**

In daisy-chain mode, only **1 SS line** is needed regardless of slave count:

$$
N_{GPIO,\ daisy} = 3 + 1 = 4\ \text{GPIO pins}
$$

However, each frame now carries $\sum_{i=1}^{N_s} N_i$ bits, so effective frame time becomes:

$$
T_{frame,\ daisy} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N_s} N_i}{f_{SCLK}}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: SPI vs I²C vs UART

| Feature | **SPI** | **I²C** | **UART** |
|---------|:-------:|:-------:|:--------:|
| **Synchronous?** | ✅ Yes (SCLK) | ✅ Yes (SCL) | ❌ No (asynchronous) |
| **Duplex** | Full-duplex | Half-duplex | Full-duplex |
| **Max practical speed** | **> 50 MHz** | ~ 5 MHz (Ultra-Fast mode) | ~ 1 Mbaud |
| **Number of wires** | 4 (single slave) | 2 (SDA + SCL) | 2 (TX + RX) |
| **Addressing** | Hardware (SS line) | Software (7/10-bit address) | Point-to-point |
| **Slave count** | Limited by GPIO | Up to **127** (7-bit addr) | 1-to-1 |
| **Power consumption** | Higher (push-pull) | Lower (open-drain + pull-ups) | Moderate |
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium | Low |
| **Use case in IoT** | Displays, Flash, High-speed ADC | Sensors, RTC, low-speed peripherals | Debug, GPS, BT modules |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Advantages & Disadvantages of SPI

| **Advantages** | **Disadvantages** |
|----------------|-------------------|
| ✅ High speed (> 50 MHz) | ❌ Requires more pins (4+) |
| ✅ Full-duplex communication | ❌ No hardware slave addressing |
| ✅ No bus arbitration overhead | ❌ Short PCB-trace distances only |
| ✅ Simple hardware (no transceivers) | ❌ No flow control / error-checking |
| ✅ Flexible word length | ❌ Master-only control (no multimaster) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master–Single Slave SPI Topology (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Master["MASTER MCU"] -- MOSI --> ShiftIn["SLAVE SHIFT REGISTER"]
    Master -- SCLK --> ClockBus["SHARED CLOCK LINE"]
    Master -- SS_CSLow --> SelLine["SLAVE SELECT ACTIVE LOW"]
    ShiftIn -- MISO --> Master
    ClockBus --> ShiftIn
    SelLine --> ShiftIn
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The master device drives **MOSI, SCLK, and SS** outputs, while **MISO** is an input to the master. The slave shift register is clocked by the master's SCLK and enabled only when SS is LOW.

## 4.2 Independent Multi-Slave Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MCU["MASTER CONTROLLER"] -- SCLK --> Bus1["SCLK BUS"]
    MCU -- MOSI --> Bus2["MOSI BUS"]
    MCU -- MISO --> Bus3["MISO BUS"]
    MCU -- SS1 --> S1["SLAVE 1 SENSOR"]
    MCU -- SS2 --> S2["SLAVE 2 SD CARD"]
    MCU -- SS3 --> S3["SLAVE 3 DISPLAY"]
    Bus1 --> S1
    Bus1 --> S2
    Bus1 --> S3
    Bus2 --> S1
    Bus2 --> S2
    Bus2 --> S3
    Bus3 --> S1
    Bus3 --> S2
    Bus3 --> S3
```

> [!TIP]
> Notice the **3 shared lines (SCLK, MOSI, MISO)** fan out to all slaves, while **SS1, SS2, SS3** are individual chip-select lines. This is the most common KTU-diagram style for multi-slave SPI.

## 4.3 Daisy-Chain Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DMaster["MASTER"] -- SSALL --> S1a["SLAVE 1"]
    DMaster -- SCLK --> S1a
    DMaster -- MOSI --> S1a
    S1a -- MISO_to_MOSI --> S2a["SLAVE 2"]
    S2a -- MISO_to_MOSI --> S3a["SLAVE 3"]
    S3a -- MISO_final --> DMaster
    DMaster -- SCLK --> S2a
    DMaster -- SCLK --> S3a
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the daisy-chain, the **MISO output of slave 1 becomes the MOSI input of slave 2**, and so on. The master reads back a *cascaded* bitstream. This saves GPIO pins but increases latency.

## 4.4 SPI Mode 0 Timing Diagram (Functional)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Master
    participant S as Slave
    Note over M,S: SS goes LOW, CPOL=0 (SCLK idle LOW)
    M->>S: SCLK Rising Edge (sample data)
    M->>S: Data Bit 7 on MOSI
    S-->>M: Data Bit 7 on MISO
    M->>S: SCLK Rising Edge (sample data)
    M->>S: Data Bit 6 on MOSI
    S-->>M: Data Bit 6 on MISO
    Note over M,S: After 8 clock cycles, full byte exchanged
    M->>S: SS goes HIGH (release)
```

## 4.5 The Circular Shift Register Model (Most Important Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Mreg["MASTER SHIFT REGISTER 8 BIT"] -- MOSI line --> Sreg["SLAVE SHIFT REGISTER 8 BIT"]
    Sreg -- MISO line --> Mreg
    Clk["SCLK from Master"] --> Mreg
    Clk --> Sreg
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **circular shift register model** is the single most important conceptual diagram in SPI. After 8 clock pulses, the master's original byte resides in the slave, and the slave's original byte resides in the master. **Data is essentially *exchanged*, not copied.**

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Serial Peripheral Interface (SPI). List its four signal lines and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** SPI is a **synchronous, full-duplex, master-slave serial communication protocol** developed by Motorola, used for short-distance, high-speed data transfer between a microcontroller and peripheral devices.
>
> **Four Signal Lines (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **MOSI** — Master Out, Slave In: data sent from master to slave.
> 2. **MISO** — Master In, Slave Out: data sent from slave to master.
> 3. **SCLK** — Serial Clock: synchronization clock generated by the master.
> 4. **SS / CS** — Slave Select / Chip Select: active-low line used by the master to enable a specific slave.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by CPOL and CPHA in SPI? Name the four SPI modes and the default mode of an SD card.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **CPOL (Clock Polarity) — 0.5 mark:** Defines the idle state of SCLK.
> - CPOL = 0 → SCLK idle **LOW**
> - CPOL = 1 → SCLK idle **HIGH**
>
> **CPHA (Clock Phase) — 0.5 mark:** Defines the clock edge on which data is sampled.
> - CPHA = 0 → data sampled on the **leading (first) edge**
> - CPHA = 1 → data sampled on the **trailing (second) edge**
>
> **Four SPI Modes (1.5 marks):**
> - **Mode 0:** CPOL=0, CPHA=0
> - **Mode 1:** CPOL=0, CPHA=1
> - **Mode 2:** CPOL=1, CPHA=0
> - **Mode 3:** CPOL=1, CPHA=1
>
> **Default mode of SD card (0.5 mark):** **SPI Mode 0 (CPOL=0, CPHA=0).**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Solutions (14 Marks, Internal Choice Provided)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the master–slave architecture of SPI. Discuss the roles of MOSI, MISO, SCLK, and SS lines. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Explain CPOL and CPHA in detail. Draw the timing waveform for SPI **Mode 0** and **Mode 3**, and state the default mode used by an SD card. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
SPI is a synchronous full-duplex protocol where one device (the **master**) controls the clock and selects slaves via dedicated chip-select lines. All data transfers are timed by the master's SCLK signal.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
[Use the circular shift-register diagram from Section 4.5: master 8-bit shift register connected to slave 8-bit shift register via MOSI and MISO, both clocked by SCLK.]

**Roles of the four lines (4 marks — 1 each):**
- **MOSI**: Carries data from the master to the selected slave on each clock edge.
- **MISO**: Carries data from the selected slave back to the master; the slave tri-states MISO when its SS is HIGH.
- **SCLK**: Generated by the master; one bit is exchanged per clock pulse.
- **SS/CS**: Active-low line; the master pulls it LOW to enable a particular slave. All other slaves ignore the bus.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Q-A(a):** Students often write that *MOSI and MISO are bidirectional*. **They are NOT.** MOSI is a **master-output, slave-input** line; MISO is the opposite. Bidirectional confusion costs **1–2 marks**.

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**CPOL (2 marks):** Defines the SCLK idle level. CPOL=0 → idle LOW (leading edge = rising). CPOL=1 → idle HIGH (leading edge = falling).

**CPHA (2 marks):** Defines sampling edge. CPHA=0 → sample on leading edge, shift on trailing edge. CPHA=1 → sample on trailing edge, shift on leading edge.

**Mode 0 timing (1.5 marks):** SCLK idle LOW, data sampled on rising edge, shifted on falling edge. CPOL=0, CPHA=0.

**Mode 3 timing (1.5 marks):** SCLK idle HIGH, data sampled on rising edge, shifted on falling edge. CPOL=1, CPHA=1.

**SD card default mode:** **Mode 0** (0.5 mark).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

> **(a)** Compare SPI with I²C in terms of speed, pin count, duplex mode, addressing, and typical IoT applications. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** With a diagram, explain the **daisy-chain multi-slave** configuration of SPI. A sensor system uses SCLK = 8 MHz with 12-bit data. Calculate the time to transmit one sample and the maximum sampling rate. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

**Comparison Table (5 marks — 1 per row):**

| Parameter | **SPI** | **I²C** |
|-----------|:-------:|:-------:|
| **Speed** | > 50 MHz | up to 5 MHz |
| **Pin count** | 4 (MOSI, MISO, SCLK, SS) | 2 (SDA, SCL) |
| **Duplex** | Full-duplex | Half-duplex |
| **Addressing** | Hardware (SS line) | Software (7/10-bit address) |
| **IoT applications** | Displays, flash memory, high-speed ADC | RTC, low-speed sensors, EEPROM |

**Justification (2 marks):** SPI is preferred for **high-speed, full-duplex, point-to-point** transfers, whereas I²C is preferred when **pin count is limited** and many low-speed peripherals must share a bus.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Q-B(a):** Do not write that I²C is *always* slower than SPI. I²C's Ultra-Fast mode reaches **5 MHz**, but SPI still wins on full-duplex and peak clock. Mentioning the **open-drain pull-up resistor** requirement of I²C also scores bonus marks.

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Daisy-chain diagram (2 marks):**
[Use the diagram from Section 4.3: MISO of slave 1 → MOSI of slave 2 → … → MISO of last slave → master.]

**Explanation (2 marks):** All slaves share a single SS line. Data shifts sequentially through each slave's shift register. GPIO pin count is minimized, but total frame length grows linearly with the number of slaves.

**Numerical calculation (3 marks):**

Given $f_{SCLK} = 8 \times 10^6\ \text{Hz}$ and $N = 12$ bits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{frame} &= \frac{N}{f_{SCLK}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{12}{8 \times 10^{6}} \\[6pt]
&= 1.5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{seconds} \\[6pt]
&= 1.5\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

Maximum sampling rate:

$$
f_{sample,\ max} = \frac{1}{T_{frame}} = \frac{1}{1.5\ \mu s} \approx 666{,}667\ \text{samples/sec}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Q-B(b):** A common error is forgetting that the **clock period** is $1/f_{SCLK}$, not $f_{SCLK}$ itself. Multiplying instead of dividing yields nonsensical answers and zero marks for the numerical part.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **SPI = Synchronous, Full-Duplex, Master-Slave, 4-wire protocol** by Motorola.
- ✅ The **four mandatory lines** are **MOSI, MISO, SCLK, and SS (active-low)**.
- ✅ A **common GND** is required between master and slave(s).
- ✅ **CPOL** controls SCLK idle level; **CPHA** controls the sampling edge.
- ✅ **Four SPI modes** (0, 1, 2, 3) cover all CPOL × CPHA combinations.
- ✅ **SD cards default to Mode 0** (CPOL=0, CPHA=0).
- ✅ The **circular shift-register model** is the best mental image for SPI.
- ✅ **Independent** multi-slave uses N SS lines; **daisy-chain** uses 1 SS line.
- ✅ Frame time formula: $T_{frame} = \dfrac{N}{f_{SCLK}}$.
- ✅ SPI is **faster than I²C and UART** but uses more pins.
- ✅ SPI has **no native addressing, no error checking, and no multi-master** support.
- ✅ Common IoT peripherals: **SD card, OLED/TFT, ADC, flash memory, sensors (BME280, MPU6050)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: UART/USART Serial Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS AND SMART COMPUTING.
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** UART/USART Serial Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# UART/USART Serial Communication — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter)** is a hardware peripheral (or stand-alone IC) that converts parallel data from a microprocessor/microcontroller into a **serial asynchronous** format for transmission, and conversely converts received serial data back into parallel form for the processor. It operates without a shared clock line, relying instead on pre-agreed **baud rate** settings and framing bits to keep sender and receiver synchronised.

**USART (Universal Synchronous/Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter)** is a superset of the UART. It supports **both** asynchronous mode (identical to UART) and **synchronous** mode, where a dedicated clock line is transmitted alongside the data so the receiver can sample bits at precise instants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In IoT-centric microcontrollers (ESP32, STM32, ATmega328P, MSP430), the USART peripheral is the de-facto standard for debugging logs, GPS module interfacing, GSM modules, and inter-MCU communication. Examiners frequently test framing, baud rate, and asynchronous timing equations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Talking Through a Pipe" Model

Imagine two friends (say, **A** the transmitter and **B** the receiver) connected by a **single thin pipe** (the serial wire). They cannot send a full A4 sheet (parallel data) at once, so they:

1. **Agree in advance** on a speaking speed — say, "**one word per second**". This pre-agreed rate is the **baud rate** (bits per second).
2. **A coughs** (the **start bit**) to grab B's attention — B was idling, and this cough says "data is coming".
3. **A speaks 5–9 words** of the message in a fixed rhythm (the **data bits**, usually 8).
4. Optionally, **A whispers a check word** (the **parity bit**) so B can verify nothing got garbled in the pipe.
5. Finally, **A pauses for 1 or 2 seconds** (the **stop bits**) signalling "message over".

This is **exactly** how asynchronous serial works. If they instead ran a **second pipe with a metronome ticking** (the clock line), they would be in **synchronous** (USART) mode — no need to cough, because every tick marks a bit.

## 1.3 Why This Matters in IoT

In the **Internet of Things**, sensors, actuators, and edge MCUs are resource-constrained: limited pins, limited power, limited bandwidth. UART/USART offers:

- **Only 2 wires (TX, RX)** for full-duplex communication.
- **No clock line required** in async mode → saves pins and power.
- **Mature, robust, hardware-implemented** — no CPU overhead once configured.
- **Universally supported** — virtually every MCU and SoC exposes at least one USART.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Default Settings (the "9600-8-N-1" holy grail):**
> - **Baud Rate:** 9600 bps
> - **Data bits:** 8
> - **Parity:** None
> - **Stop bits:** 1
> - **Flow control:** None
>
> This 8-N-1 configuration is so ubiquitous that Bluetooth modules (HC-05), GPS modules (NEO-6M), and GSM modules (SIM800) all boot into it by default.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Baud Rate** | Number of **symbol changes per second** on the line; in binary UART = bits per second (bps). |
| **Start Bit** | A logic-**0** bit that signals the beginning of a frame. Idle line is logic-**1**. |
| **Data Bits** | 5, 6, 7, 8, or 9 bits carrying the actual payload (LSB first). |
| **Parity Bit** | Optional error-detection bit (Even, Odd, Mark, Space). |
| **Stop Bit(s)** | 1, 1.5, or 2 logic-**1** bits marking frame end. |
| **Framing Error** | Receiver expected a stop bit (1) but sampled 0 → synchronisation lost. |
| **Overrun Error** | New byte arrived before previous one was read. |
| **Full Duplex** | TX and RX can operate simultaneously on separate wires. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Asynchronous Frame Architecture

A UART frame is a strictly ordered sequence of bits transmitted on a single wire. The line is held **HIGH (logic 1, "MARK")** when idle. The structure of one frame, from left to right (first transmitted to last), is:

**Idle (1) → Start (0) → D0 → D1 → … → D7 → [Parity] → Stop (1) → Idle (1)**

### Step-by-Step Transmission Logic

1. **Line Idle State:** The TX line is held at logic **1** (VCC). This is the "MARK" condition.
2. **Start Bit Generation:** When the transmit data register is loaded, the UART hardware pulls the line **LOW (logic 0)** for exactly **one bit-period**. This edge (1 → 0) alerts the receiver that a frame is starting.
3. **Data Bit Clocking:** The transmitter shifts out the LSB first, one bit per bit-period, until all data bits (typically 8) are sent. The receiver, having detected the falling edge, waits **1.5 bit-periods** (to land in the middle of the first data bit) and then samples every bit-period.
4. **Parity Bit (Optional):** If parity is enabled, the UART calculates XOR of all data bits (Even) or its complement (Odd) and appends it.
5. **Stop Bit(s):** Line returns to **logic 1** for 1, 1.5, or 2 bit-periods, validating frame end and giving the receiver time to process.
6. **Inter-frame Gap:** The next frame's start bit can begin immediately, or after an arbitrary idle period.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why 1.5 bit-periods wait" trick:** The receiver detects the start bit's **falling edge** at the *beginning* of the bit. Sampling then would be unreliable. So it waits **half a bit-period** to reach the *middle* of the start bit, and then samples every full bit-period thereafter — landing in the centre of every subsequent bit where signal integrity is highest.

## 2.2 Baud Rate Generation & Timing Equations

The baud rate is derived from the system clock through a **baud-rate generator** — typically a prescaler / fractional divider inside the USART peripheral.

### 2.2.1 Asynchronous Mode — Standard Equation

For most KTU-referenced MCUs (ATmega328P, STM32, MSP430, LPC2148), the relationship between the peripheral clock $f_{clk}$ and the baud rate $B$ is:

$$B = \frac{f_{clk}}{N \cdot (UBR R + 1)}$$

Where:
- $f_{clk}$ = peripheral clock frequency (Hz) feeding the USART.
- $UBRR$ = **U**SART **B**aud **R**ate **R**egister value (16-bit unsigned integer).
- $N$ = oversampling factor:
  - $N = 16$ → standard mode (receiver samples 16 times per bit).
  - $N = 8$ → high-speed mode (better for very high baud, less noise tolerance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverse form (used to size UBRR for a target baud rate):**
> $$UBRR = \frac{f_{clk}}{N \cdot B} - 1$$
> Always round to the nearest integer, then compute the **percent error** to ensure it stays within the **±2 %** boundary mandated by reliable UART communication.

### 2.2.2 Percent Error Equation

$$\%\text{ Error} = \left( \frac{B_{\text{achieved}} - B_{\text{target}}}{B_{\text{target}}} \right) \times 100\%$$

Where $B_{\text{achieved}}$ is the actual baud rate produced by your chosen integer $UBRR$:

$$B_{\text{achieved}} = \frac{f_{clk}}{N \cdot (UBRR + 1)}$$

**Receiver tolerance rule:** The cumulative drift between sender and receiver over 10 bits must be **< 10 %**, which is why each side's individual clock error should be **< 2 %**.

### 2.2.3 Bit Period

$$T_{bit} = \frac{1}{B} \quad [\text{seconds per bit}]$$

For $B = 9600$ bps: $T_{bit} = 104.17\ \mu s$. This is the unit of every timing calculation.

## 2.3 Synchronous (USART) Mode Operation

In synchronous mode, an additional **CK (clock)** pin is driven by the master. The USART becomes **clock-driven**:

- The clock line is active **only during** the actual data bits (and parity).
- There is **no start/stop bit overhead** — pure payload bits + optional parity.
- The transmitter and receiver are locked in bit-synchrony, so higher baud rates and 9-bit data words (common in RS-485 multi-drop addressing) are feasible.
- **Frame structure:** Idle → Data LSB first (8 or 9 bits) → [Parity] → Idle.

Synchronous mode is **infrequently** used in IoT because it consumes an extra pin — but it is essential for high-throughput chip-to-chip links (e.g., SPI-like peripherals using USART in master sync mode).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Cheat Sheet — Memorise this table for any UART/USART numerical.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bit Period | $T_{bit} = 1/B$ | seconds |
| 2 | Frame Time | $T_{frame} = (1 + n_d + n_p + n_s)/B$ | seconds |
| 3 | UBRR (target) | $UBRR = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{N \cdot B} - 1$ | dimensionless integer |
| 4 | Achieved Baud | $B_{ach} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{N \cdot (UBRR + 1)}$ | bps |
| 5 | Percent Error | $\%\Delta = \left(\dfrac{B_{ach} - B}{B}\right)\times 100$ | $\vert\%\Delta\vert \le 2\%$ |
| 6 | Throughput Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{n_d}{1 + n_d + n_p + n_s} \times 100$ | percent |
| 7 | Oversampling | $N \in \{8, 16\}$ | — |
| 8 | Idle Level | Logic **HIGH (1)** | — |
| 9 | Bit Order | **LSB first** | (RS-232 convention) |
| 10 | Voltage Levels (RS-232) | Logic 1 = $-3$V to $-25$V, Logic 0 = $+3$V to $+25$V | inverted vs TTL |

> **In the table above, $n_d$ = data bits, $n_p$ = parity bits (0 or 1), $n_s$ = stop bits (1, 1.5, or 2).**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Debugging / Logging:** `printf()` redirected to UART is the universal MCU debugging method.
- **GPS Receivers (NEO-6M):** Output NMEA sentences at 9600 bps, 8-N-1.
- **GSM (SIM800/SIM900):** AT-command interface on UART, default 9600 or 115200 bps.
- **Bluetooth SPP (HC-05):** Bridges Bluetooth ↔ UART transparently.
- **RS-485 Industrial Buses:** Use UART with external transceivers (e.g., MAX485) for multi-drop, long-distance (up to 1200 m) industrial IoT networks.
- **Bootloaders:** STM32, ESP32, AVR — all expose USART-based bootloaders for in-field firmware updates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Solved Example 1 — UBRR Calculation for ATmega328P @ 16 MHz

> **Problem:** An ATmega328P runs on a 16 MHz crystal. Configure its USART0 for **9600 bps, 8-N-1** in normal asynchronous mode. Find the UBRR0 register value, the achieved baud rate, and the percentage error.

### Step 1: Identify the Inputs

$$f_{clk} = 16\,000\,000\ \text{Hz}, \quad B_{target} = 9600\ \text{bps}, \quad N = 16$$

### Step 2: Apply the UBRR Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
UBRR &= \frac{f_{clk}}{N \cdot B_{target}} - 1 \\
UBRR &= \frac{16\,000\,000}{16 \times 9600} - 1 \\
UBRR &= \frac{16\,000\,000}{153\,600} - 1 \\
UBRR &= 104.1\overline{6} - 1 \\
UBRR &= 103.1\overline{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Round to the Nearest Integer

The UBRR register must hold an integer, so we **round to the nearest integer**:

$$UBRR = 103$$

This is the value loaded into the UBRR0H:UBRRR0L register pair (UBRR0H = 0, UBRR0L = 103).

### Step 4: Compute the Achieved Baud Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{ach} &= \frac{f_{clk}}{N \cdot (UBRR + 1)} \\
B_{ach} &= \frac{16\,000\,000}{16 \times (103 + 1)} \\
B_{ach} &= \frac{16\,000\,000}{16 \times 104} \\
B_{ach} &= \frac{16\,000\,000}{1664} \\
B_{ach} &= 9615.38\ \text{bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compute the Percentage Error

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\Delta &= \left( \frac{B_{ach} - B_{target}}{B_{target}} \right) \times 100 \\
\%\Delta &= \left( \frac{9615.38 - 9600}{9600} \right) \times 100 \\
\%\Delta &= \left( \frac{15.38}{9600} \right) \times 100 \\
\%\Delta &= 0.160\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Validate Against the 2% Tolerance

$$\vert 0.160\ \% \vert < 2\ \% \quad \checkmark \text{ (Acceptable)}$$

> **Verdict:** UBRR0 = 103 is a valid choice, achieving 9615 bps with 0.16 % error — well within tolerance.

---

## 3.2 Solved Example 2 — Frame Time & Throughput Efficiency

> **Problem:** A UART transmits 8 data bits with **Even parity** and **2 stop bits** at 115200 bps. Compute the total frame time and the throughput efficiency.

### Step 1: Identify the Frame Components

$$n_d = 8\ \text{data bits}, \quad n_p = 1\ \text{parity bit}, \quad n_s = 2\ \text{stop bits}, \quad n_{start} = 1$$

### Step 2: Count Total Bits per Frame

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{total} &= n_{start} + n_d + n_p + n_s \\
n_{total} &= 1 + 8 + 1 + 2 \\
n_{total} &= 12\ \text{bits per frame}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Bit Period

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{bit} &= \frac{1}{B} = \frac{1}{115200} \\
T_{bit} &= 8.681\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Frame Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{frame} &= n_{total} \times T_{bit} \\
T_{frame} &= 12 \times 8.681\ \mu s \\
T_{frame} &= 104.17\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compute the Throughput Efficiency

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{n_d}{n_{total}} \times 100 \\
\eta &= \frac{8}{12} \times 100 \\
\eta &= 66.67\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Only **2/3** of the line's bandwidth carries actual payload. The remaining 33 % is overhead (start, parity, stop). KTU frequently tests this trade-off — **minimise stop bits and disable parity** for high-throughput links.

---

## 3.3 Solved Example 3 — Edge Case with 1.5 Stop Bits

> **Problem:** With $N = 16$ oversampling, what is the actual "real" time the receiver waits for 1.5 stop bits? At 19200 bps, calculate it.

### Step 1: Bit Period

$$
T_{bit} = \frac{1}{19200} = 52.08\ \mu s
$$

### Step 2: 1.5 Stop-Bit Duration

$$
T_{1.5\,stop} = 1.5 \times 52.08\ \mu s = 78.125\ \mu s
$$

> **Note:** 1.5 stop bits are only valid when **$n_d = 5$** (legacy teletype use). Modern MCUs typically support 1, 2, or (rarely) 1.5 stop bits. KTU may include this as a trick question.

---

## 3.4 Solved Example 4 — Maximum Practical Baud on ATmega328P @ 16 MHz

> **Problem:** What is the **maximum achievable baud rate** with $\le 2\%$ error when $N = 16$ and $f_{clk} = 16$ MHz?

### Step 1: Minimum Achievable UBRR

The minimum UBRR is **0**, so:

$$
B_{max} = \frac{16\,000\,000}{16 \times (0 + 1)} = 1\,000\,000\ \text{bps}
$$

So the **hardware upper bound** is 1 Mbps. In practice, designers cap UART at **115200 bps** because:
- Higher rates have shorter bit periods → more susceptible to EMI.
- PC-side USB-to-serial bridges (FT232, CH340) often cap at 1 Mbps or 3 Mbps.
- Cable capacitance and ground bounce dominate at >1 Mbps.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Trade-off Table

| Configuration | Payload Efficiency | Robustness | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8-N-1 | **80.0 %** | Medium | Standard IoT / debug |
| 8-E-1 | 72.7 % | High | Industrial noisy links |
| 8-N-2 | 72.7 % | High (sync recovery) | Slow half-duplex modems |
| 9-N-1 (multi-proc) | 81.8 % | Medium | RS-485 addressing |
| 5-N-1 | 55.6 % | Low | Legacy teletypes |

> **KTU Insight:** 8-N-1 is the **default convention** — the moment you see "UART configuration" in an exam without further details, this is the implied setting.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UART Transmitter & Receiver Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MCU[Microcontroller Core]
        CPU[CPU or DMA Controller]
        TXREG[Transmit Data Register]
        RXREG[Receive Data Register]
    end

    subgraph TXUNIT[Transmitter Engine]
        TXSHIFT[Transmit Shift Register]
        PARITYGEN[Parity Generator]
        FRAMERT[Frame Formatter - Start Stop Bits]
    end

    subgraph RXUNIT[Receiver Engine]
        RXSHIFT[Receive Shift Register]
        PARITYCHK[Parity Checker]
        SAMPCLK[16X Oversampling Clock and Synchroniser]
        FRAMERX[Frame Parser - Start Stop Detection]
    end

    subgraph BAUDGEN[Baud Rate Generator]
        CLK[System Clock FCLK]
        DIV[Programmable Divider UBRR]
    end

    subgraph LINE[Physical Line]
        TXPIN[TX Pin]
        RXPIN[RX Pin]
    end

    CPU --> TXREG
    TXREG --> TXSHIFT
    PARITYGEN --> TXSHIFT
    FRAMERT --> TXSHIFT
    TXSHIFT --> TXPIN
    TXPIN --> LINE_TX((Serial Wire))
    LINE_TX --> RXPIN
    RXPIN --> SAMPCLK
    SAMPCLK --> FRAMERX
    FRAMERX --> RXSHIFT
    RXSHIFT --> PARITYCHK
    RXSHIFT --> RXREG
    RXREG --> CPU

    CLK --> DIV
    DIV --> TXSHIFT
    DIV --> SAMPCLK
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The single **serial wire** (centre) carries frames in both directions simultaneously (full-duplex). The **Baud Rate Generator** is the heartbeat — it clocks both transmitter and receiver shift registers from the same $f_{clk}$ so that bit periods stay aligned.

---

## 4.2 Asynchronous Frame Timeline (8-N-1 Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FRAME[One UART Frame - Byte 0x55 - LSB First]
        IDLE1[Idle - Logic 1]
        START[Start Bit - Logic 0 - 1 Bit Period]
        D0[Bit 0 - LSB - 1]
        D1[Bit 1 - 1]
        D2[Bit 2 - 0]
        D3[Bit 3 - 0]
        D4[Bit 4 - 1]
        D5[Bit 5 - 0]
        D6[Bit 6 - 1]
        D7[Bit 7 - MSB - 0]
        STOP1[Stop Bit - Logic 1 - 1 Bit Period]
        IDLE2[Idle - Logic 1 - Inter Frame Gap]
    end

    IDLE1 --> START --> D0 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4 --> D5 --> D6 --> D7 --> STOP1 --> IDLE2
```

> **The byte 0x55 (binary `0101 0101`) sent LSB-first yields the waveform `1 0 1 0 1 0 1 0`** — a textbook "alternating" pattern used by loopback tests. Note the leading **start bit (0)** and trailing **stop bit (1)**.

---

## 4.3 UART vs USART Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Need Serial Communication]
    Q1{Is a shared clock line feasible?}
    Q2{Is high throughput required?}
    Q3{Is hardware pin budget tight?}

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2
    Q1 -- NO --> Q3
    Q2 -- YES --> USARTSYNC[USART - Synchronous Mode]
    Q2 -- NO --> UART[UART - Asynchronous Mode]
    Q3 -- YES --> UART
    Q3 -- NO --> USARTASYN[USART - Asynchronous Mode]
```

> **Rule of thumb:** Always use **asynchronous (UART) mode** unless the application requires either **(a)** sustained high data rates with low jitter, or **(b)** multi-drop addressing (9-bit synchronous). For most KTU IoT lab work, 8-N-1 async is the default.

---

## 4.4 Receiver Sampling Strategy (16× Oversampling)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TIMELINE[Bit Period Tbit at Receiver]
        EDGE[Falling Edge of Start Bit Detected]
        WAIT[Wait 0.5 Tbit - Reach Centre of Start Bit]
        VERIFY[Verify Start Bit is Still LOW]
        S0[Sample Bit 0 at T equals 1.5 Tbit]
        S1[Sample Bit 1 at T equals 2.5 Tbit]
        S2[Sample Bit 2 at T equals 3.5 Tbit]
        S3[Sample Bit 3 at T equals 4.5 Tbit]
        S4[Sample Bit 4 at T equals 5.5 Tbit]
        S5[Sample Bit 5 at T equals 6.5 Tbit]
        S6[Sample Bit 6 at T equals 7.5 Tbit]
        S7[Sample Bit 7 at T equals 8.5 Tbit]
        SP[Verify Stop Bit at T equals 9.5 Tbit]
    end

    EDGE --> WAIT --> VERIFY --> S0 --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> SP
```

> **Why sample at "half-bit" offsets?** Sampling in the middle of each bit gives the **maximum noise margin** — the signal is most stable there. Sampling at edges risks metastability.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Explain the asynchronous serial communication frame format used in UART. List all bits transmitted for a typical 8-N-1 configuration.**

**Model Answer:**

Asynchronous serial communication in UART transmits data in **discrete frames** without a shared clock. The line is held **HIGH (logic 1)** in the idle state. For an **8-N-1** configuration, each frame consists of the following bits, in order:

1. **Start bit (1 bit, logic 0):** Pulls the line LOW to signal the receiver that a new frame is starting.
2. **Data bits (8 bits, LSB first):** The actual payload byte, transmitted least-significant-bit first.
3. **Parity bit (0 bits — "None"):** No error-detection bit is sent in 8-N-1.
4. **Stop bit (1 bit, logic 1):** Returns the line to HIGH, confirming the frame's end.

Total = **1 + 8 + 0 + 1 = 10 bit-periods per frame**. The receiver detects the start bit's falling edge, waits 1.5 bit-periods, and samples the centre of every subsequent bit using an internal 16× oversampling clock.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Differentiate between UART and USART in 3 distinct technical points.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | UART | USART |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Asynchronous only** — no clock line. | **Both async and synchronous** — clock line in sync mode. |
| 2 | Lower maximum reliable baud rate; limited by ±2 % clock tolerance. | Higher throughput in synchronous mode (clock-driven sampling). |
| 3 | Frame contains start, data, optional parity, stop bits. | Sync mode can omit start/stop bits, giving **higher efficiency**. |
| 4 | 2-wire interface (TX, RX) only. | 3-wire interface in sync mode (TX, RX, CK). |
| 5 | Cheaper, simpler — ubiquitous in basic MCUs. | More flexible, supports 9-bit data, multi-drop RS-485. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Scale Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of a UART. List and define the function of each major block.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An ATmega328P microcontroller runs on a 16 MHz crystal. Compute the UBRR register value needed to achieve **19200 bps** in normal asynchronous mode ($N = 16$). Calculate the achieved baud rate and the percent error. Comment on whether the configuration is acceptable.

---

#### Solution to B-A (a)

The UART hardware is composed of the following functional blocks, as illustrated in § 4.1 above:

1. **Transmit Data Register (TXREG):** An 8-bit buffer where the CPU writes the byte to be transmitted.
2. **Transmit Shift Register:** Serialises the parallel byte into bits, appending the start, parity, and stop bits as configured.
3. **Receive Shift Register:** De-serialises the incoming bit stream, stripping framing bits and assembling a parallel byte.
4. **Receive Data Register (RXREG):** An 8-bit buffer from which the CPU reads the received byte.
5. **Baud Rate Generator:** A programmable divider (driven by UBRR) that produces the $16\times$ or $8\times$ oversampling clock from the system clock.
6. **Parity Generator/Checker:** Computes the parity bit on transmission; verifies it on reception.
7. **Status & Control Registers:** Hold flags like TXC, RXC, UDRE (data-register empty), framing error (FE), and overrun error (DOR).
8. **TX and RX Pins:** Physical interfaces to the serial line (logic-level or RS-232 levels via a transceiver like MAX232).

> **For 7 marks**, students should mention **at least 6 blocks** with their functions and draw a labelled diagram. A single block diagram covering both TX and RX paths is sufficient.

---

#### Solution to B-A (b)

**Given:** $f_{clk} = 16\,000\,000$ Hz, $B_{target} = 19200$ bps, $N = 16$.

**Step 1 — Compute UBRR:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
UBRR &= \frac{f_{clk}}{N \cdot B} - 1 \\
UBRR &= \frac{16\,000\,000}{16 \times 19200} - 1 \\
UBRR &= \frac{16\,000\,000}{307\,200} - 1 \\
UBRR &= 52.08\overline{3} - 1 \\
UBRR &= 51.08\overline{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Round to the nearest integer:**

$$UBRR = 51$$

**Step 3 — Compute the achieved baud rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{ach} &= \frac{16\,000\,000}{16 \times (51 + 1)} \\
B_{ach} &= \frac{16\,000\,000}{16 \times 52} \\
B_{ach} &= \frac{16\,000\,000}{832} \\
B_{ach} &= 19\,230.77\ \text{bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the percent error:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\Delta &= \left( \frac{19\,230.77 - 19\,200}{19\,200} \right) \times 100 \\
\%\Delta &= \left( \frac{30.77}{19\,200} \right) \times 100 \\
\%\Delta &= 0.160\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Validate against $\pm 2\%$ tolerance:**

$$\vert 0.160\ \% \vert < 2\ \% \quad \checkmark \text{ (Acceptable)}$$

**Verdict:** The UBRR = 51 setting is **fully acceptable** for 19200 bps communication with 0.16 % error.

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the data transmission and reception mechanism in UART. Explain the role of the 16× oversampling clock.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a UART link configured as **115200 bps, 8-E-2** (8 data bits, Even parity, 2 stop bits), calculate:
  (i) The bit period.
  (ii) The total number of bits per frame and the total frame time.
  (iii) The throughput efficiency in percent.

---

#### Solution to B-B (a)

**Data Transmission Mechanism:**
- The CPU writes a byte into the **Transmit Data Register (TXREG)**.
- The byte is transferred to the **Transmit Shift Register**, which prepends the **start bit (0)**, optionally appends the **parity bit**, and trails with the **stop bit(s) (1)**.
- Bits are shifted out serially on the **TX pin** at the configured baud rate, **LSB first**.
- The line returns to idle (logic 1) awaiting the next byte.

**Data Reception Mechanism:**
- The receiver monitors the **RX pin**. Upon detecting a **falling edge** (1 → 0), it starts its internal counter.
- After **0.5 bit-periods**, the receiver samples the line to **verify the start bit** is still LOW. If not, the receiver treats the edge as noise and resumes waiting.
- The receiver then samples the line at the **centre of every subsequent bit** (every 1 bit-period), assembling the data LSB-first into the **Receive Shift Register**.
- The assembled byte is moved to the **Receive Data Register (RXREG)**. The CPU (or DMA) reads it.
- The receiver checks the **parity bit** and the **stop bit(s)**; deviations raise **framing** or **parity error** flags.

**Role of the 16× Oversampling Clock:**
- The baud rate generator produces a clock **16 times faster** than the baud rate (i.e., 16 samples per bit).
- This high-frequency clock allows the receiver to **pinpoint the falling edge** of the start bit with **$\frac{1}{16}$-bit resolution**, ensuring subsequent samples land in the **middle of each data bit**.
- Sampling at the centre minimises the chance of metastability and provides maximum **noise immunity** — critical for asynchronous links without a clock line.
- An alternative $N = 8$ mode offers higher maximum baud at the cost of noise tolerance.

---

#### Solution to B-B (b)

**Given:** $B = 115200$ bps, $n_d = 8$, $n_p = 1$ (Even), $n_s = 2$, $n_{start} = 1$.

**(i) Bit Period:**

$$
T_{bit} = \frac{1}{B} = \frac{1}{115200} = 8.681\ \mu s
$$

**(ii) Total bits per frame and frame time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{total} &= 1 + 8 + 1 + 2 = 12\ \text{bits per frame} \\
T_{frame} &= n_{total} \times T_{bit} \\
T_{frame} &= 12 \times 8.681\ \mu s \\
T_{frame} &= 104.17\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Throughput Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{n_d}{n_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{8}{12} \times 100 = 66.67\ \%
$$

> **Interpretation:** Despite 115200 bps being a "high" baud rate, only **66.67 %** of the channel capacity carries payload. To improve efficiency, reduce stop bits to 1 and disable parity — yielding $8/10 = 80\ \%$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in UART/USART questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "-1" in the UBRR formula.** A common slip: writing $UBRR = f_{clk}/(N \cdot B)$ instead of $UBRR = f_{clk}/(N \cdot B) - 1$. This off-by-one error gives a UBRR that is **1 too high**, halving the baud rate and producing $\sim$50 % error. **Always include the "$-1$"**.
> 2. **Not reporting the achieved baud rate AND percent error.** A full solution requires **both** $B_{ach}$ and $\%\Delta$. Stopping at UBRR alone earns only partial marks.
> 3. **Confusing oversampling factor $N$.** ATmega in normal mode = **16**. In double-speed (U2X) mode = **8**. STM32 has a programmable OVER8 bit. Mixing these up gives wrong UBRR.
> 4. **Stating idle level as LOW.** The idle line is **HIGH (logic 1, MARK)**. The **start bit is LOW** — this is a deliberate "break" from idle. Examiners mark this strictly.
> 5. **Bit order confusion.** UART transmits **LSB first**, not MSB. This is the opposite of SPI and I²C. Examiners often test this as a one-mark trap.
> 6. **Voltage levels.** Writing "UART uses TTL 0/5 V" without mentioning RS-232 inversion (logic 1 = $-12$ V, logic 0 = $+12$ V) loses marks. Always note that **RS-232 is inverted** relative to the MCU's TX logic.
> 7. **Confusing 1.5 stop bits with 1 or 2.** 1.5 stop bits are valid only for legacy 5-bit data; modern MCUs default to 1 stop bit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — UART/USART**

- **UART** = asynchronous, no clock; **USART** = both async and synchronous.
- **Idle line = logic 1 (MARK).** Start bit = logic 0 (SPACE).
- **Bit order:** **LSB first** (RS-232 convention).
- **Frame structure (8-N-1):** 1 start + 8 data + 0 parity + 1 stop = **10 bit-periods**.
- **Baud Rate Formula:** $\displaystyle B = \frac{f_{clk}}{N \cdot (UBRR + 1)}$ where $N = 16$ (or 8).
- **UBRR Formula:** $\displaystyle UBRR = \frac{f_{clk}}{N \cdot B} - 1$ (always round to nearest integer).
- **Percent Error must be ≤ ±2 %** for reliable communication.
- **Bit Period:** $T_{bit} = 1/B$. At 9600 bps → ~104.17 µs.
- **Throughput Efficiency:** $\eta = n_d / n_{total} \times 100$ — overhead from start, parity, stop bits reduces efficiency.
- **RS-232 voltage levels are inverted:** 1 = $-3$V to $-25$V, 0 = $+3$V to $+25$V. Use **MAX232** transceiver to convert.
- **Synchronous USART** uses a **CK (clock) line** — no start/stop bits, supports 9-bit data, ideal for RS-485 multi-drop.
- **Standard configurations to memorise:** 8-N-1 (80 % efficiency), 8-E-1 (72.7 %), 8-N-2 (72.7 %).
- **Oversampling clock (16×):** lets the receiver sample in the **middle** of each bit, maximising noise margin.
- **Frame-time budget:** for 8-N-2 at 115200 → 12 × 8.68 µs = 104.17 µs per frame → 9600 frames/sec → 76.8 kBps effective payload.
- **Hardware flags to know:** **TXC** (TX complete), **RXC** (RX complete), **UDRE** (data register empty), **FE** (framing error), **DOR** (data overrun), **UPE** (parity error).
- **Most common default baud rates:** 9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200 — PC COM ports and most peripherals boot into 9600.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Sensors: Analog & Digital Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS AND SMART COMPUTING.
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** IoT Sensors: Analog & Digital Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Sensor? (Formal KTU Definition)

A **sensor** is a physical device, module, or subsystem that **detects physical, chemical, or biological quantities** (such as temperature, pressure, humidity, light, motion, or chemical concentration) and **converts them into an electrical signal** that can be read by an observer, a microcontroller, or an electronic instrument.

In the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)**, sensors form the foundational **"Things"** layer of the architecture. They are the interface between the physical world and the digital domain.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A sensor is an electronic device that senses physical changes and produces a corresponding electrical output. In IoT, sensors are responsible for **sensing** (data acquisition) and form the first tier of the **3-tier or 5-tier IoT architecture**.

The mathematical representation of a sensor is given by the transfer function:

$$y = f(x)$$

Where:
- $x$ = physical stimulus (input)
- $y$ = electrical output signal
- $f$ = the transfer function describing the relationship

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Human Body

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Think of the human body as a giant IoT system.
> - Your **eyes** are like **light sensors** (photodetectors)
> - Your **skin** is a vast network of **temperature and pressure sensors**
> - Your **ears** act as **acoustic/vibration sensors**
> - Your **tongue** is a **chemical sensor** (taste)
> - Your **nervous system** transmits these signals to your **brain** (the microcontroller/processor)
>
> Just as your brain receives continuous signals about the environment, an IoT system's microcontroller receives continuous sensor data to make decisions — like automatically turning on the AC when it "feels" hot.

## 1.3 Classification of IoT Sensors

Sensors are broadly classified based on the **nature of their output signal**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Primary Classes (KTU High-Yield):**
> 1. **Analog Sensors** → Produce a **continuous** output signal (voltage, current, resistance) proportional to the measured quantity.
> 2. **Digital Sensors** → Produce a **discrete** output signal (binary 0s and 1s, or digital words via protocols like I²C, SPI, UART).

### 1.3.1 Analog Sensors — Deep Overview

**Definition:** An analog sensor produces a **continuous output signal** whose magnitude is **directly proportional** to the physical quantity being measured. The output is typically an analog voltage (e.g., **0–5 V**), current (e.g., **4–20 mA**), or resistance change.

**Examples:**
- **LM35** Temperature Sensor (output: 10 mV/°C)
- **TMP36** Temperature Sensor
- **Photoresistor (LDR)** — resistance changes with light
- **Thermistor (NTC/PTC)** — resistance changes with temperature
- **Potentiometer** — for angular/linear position
- **Flex Sensor** — resistance changes with bending
- **Gas sensors (MQ-2, MQ-135)**
- **Soil moisture sensor** (resistive type)
- **Piezoelectric sensor** (vibration/pressure)

**Key characteristics:**
- Continuous output curve (smooth, infinite values)
- Requires **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** to interface with digital microcontrollers
- Susceptible to **noise** and **signal degradation** over long distances
- Resolution depends on ADC bit-depth

### 1.3.2 Digital Sensors — Deep Overview

**Definition:** A digital sensor produces a **discrete output signal** in the form of digital data (binary) or a defined HIGH/LOW voltage level. The output is already processed and quantized internally.

**Examples:**
- **DHT11 / DHT22** — Temperature and Humidity (single-wire digital protocol)
- **DS18B20** — Temperature (1-Wire protocol)
- **BMP180 / BMP280** — Pressure, Temperature, Altitude (I²C/SPI)
- **MPU6050** — Accelerometer + Gyroscope (I²C)
- **HC-SR04** — Ultrasonic distance sensor
- **IR sensor modules** (digital output version)
- **PIR motion sensor**
- **TCS3200** — Color sensor
- **MQ-2 with comparator circuit**

**Key characteristics:**
- Direct digital output (no ADC required at MCU end)
- Includes **on-chip signal conditioning, ADC, and digital interface**
- Communication protocols: **I²C, SPI, UART, 1-Wire**
- Higher **noise immunity** over long distances
- Often configurable via registers

## 1.4 Why This Distinction Matters in IoT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** In a real KTU exam or viva, you must explain that:
> - **Analog sensors** are typically **cheaper** but require **signal conditioning** (amplification, filtering) and **external ADC**.
> - **Digital sensors** are typically **more expensive** but provide **plug-and-play** interfacing, **better accuracy**, and are **easier to integrate** with modern microcontrollers (ESP32, Arduino, Raspberry Pi).

---

## 📌 Quick Reference Table (Section 1)

| Parameter | Analog Sensor | Digital Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| **Output** | Continuous voltage/current/resistance | Discrete HIGH/LOW or digital word |
| **Signal type** | Continuous | Discrete |
| **ADC needed?** | Yes (external) | No (internal) |
| **Noise immunity** | Low | High |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate to High |
| **Examples** | LM35, LDR, Thermistor | DHT11, DS18B20, MPU6050 |
| **Interface** | ADC pin of MCU | I²C, SPI, UART, 1-Wire |
| **Resolution** | Limited by external ADC | Defined by internal ADC bits |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sensor Characteristics — The Engineering Metrics

Every sensor, whether analog or digital, is characterized by a set of **performance parameters** that engineers use to compare, select, and validate them for IoT applications. These are **guaranteed KTU exam questions**.

### 2.1.1 Sensitivity ($S$)

**Definition:** The ratio of the change in output signal to the change in the measured input (physical quantity).

$$S = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{dy}{dx}$$

**Units:** V/°C, V/lux, mV/kPa, etc.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** Sensitivity tells you *"how much does the output swing for a small change in input?"* A high-sensitivity temperature sensor will produce a large voltage change for a 1°C change.

**Example:** The LM35 has a sensitivity of **10 mV/°C**.

### 2.1.2 Range (Span)

**Definition:** The minimum and maximum values of input that the sensor can measure.

$$\text{Range} = x_{max} - x_{min}$$

**Example:** A temperature sensor might have a range of **−55°C to +150°C**.

### 2.1.3 Resolution

**Definition:** The **smallest change** in input that can be detected by the sensor (or the smallest change in output that can be meaningfully resolved).

For an N-bit ADC with reference voltage $V_{ref}$:

$$\text{Resolution (LSB)} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$$

**Example:** With $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$ and a 10-bit ADC ($N = 10$):

$$\text{LSB} = \frac{5}{2^{10}} = \frac{5}{1024} \approx 4.88\,\text{mV}$$

### 2.1.4 Accuracy

**Definition:** The closeness of the measured value to the **true value**.

$$\text{Accuracy} = \left|\frac{\text{Measured Value} - \text{True Value}}{\text{True Value}}\right| \times 100\%$$

### 2.1.5 Precision

**Definition:** The **reproducibility** of the measurement — how close multiple measurements are to each other, irrespective of whether they are close to the true value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (KTU Favourite):** A sensor can be **precise but not accurate** (e.g., consistently reads 2°C higher than true value). Accuracy is closeness to truth; precision is closeness of repeated readings to each other.

### 2.1.6 Linearity

**Definition:** The degree to which the actual sensor output curve matches a **straight line**.

$$\text{Linearity Error} = \frac{\Delta y_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100\%$$

Where $\Delta y_{max}$ is the maximum deviation from the best-fit straight line, and $y_{FS}$ is the full-scale output.

### 2.1.7 Drift

**Definition:** The change in sensor output over time **even when the input remains constant**. Caused by aging, temperature effects, or environmental factors.

### 2.1.8 Hysteresis

**Definition:** The maximum difference in output for the **same input value** approached from opposite directions (increasing vs. decreasing).

$$\text{Hysteresis Error} = \frac{|y_{inc} - y_{dec}|}{y_{FS}} \times 100\%$$

### 2.1.9 Dynamic Range (in dB)

$$\text{DR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{x_{max}}{x_{min}}\right)$$

### 2.1.10 Response Time ($\tau$)

The time taken for the sensor output to reach **63.2%** of its final value after a step input. For a first-order system:

$$y(t) = y_{final}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

## 2.2 Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC) — The Bridge

Since microcontrollers (ESP32, Arduino, etc.) work in the digital domain, **all analog sensor outputs must be converted to digital form** using an **ADC**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ADC Resolution Formula (MUST memorize for KTU):**

$$\boxed{\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N} = V_{ref} \cdot 2^{-N}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Digital Output} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^N - 1) \right\rfloor}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Quantization Error} = \pm \frac{1}{2} \text{LSB} = \pm \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{N+1}}}$$

Where:
- $N$ = number of bits of the ADC
- $V_{ref}$ = reference voltage
- $V_{in}$ = input analog voltage
- LSB = Least Significant Bit

### 2.2.1 Quantization Error Derivation (Conceptual)

An N-bit ADC divides the input range $[0, V_{ref}]$ into $2^N$ discrete levels. Each level represents one LSB. The actual analog value can be anywhere within $\pm\frac{1}{2}$ LSB of the digital code. Hence the **maximum quantization error** is $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB.

### 2.2.2 Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)

For an ideal N-bit ADC:

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02N + 1.76 \quad (\text{in dB})$$

**Example:** A 12-bit ADC has SQNR $\approx$ $6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 73.99 \approx 74$ dB.

## 2.3 Signal Conditioning — Preparing the Analog Signal

Before an analog signal is fed to an ADC, it usually needs **signal conditioning**:

> [!NOTE]
> **The 4 main blocks of signal conditioning:**
> 1. **Amplification** — using op-amps (e.g., non-inverting, instrumentation amp for small signals like thermocouple mV output)
> 2. **Filtering** — Low-pass filter to remove high-frequency noise (RC filter: $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$)
> 3. **Linearization** — for non-linear sensors (thermistor, NTC)
> 4. **Isolation** — optical or transformer isolation to protect MCU from high voltages

## 2.4 Voltage Divider — Fundamental Analog Sensor Interface

Many analog resistive sensors (LDR, thermistor) are interfaced using a **voltage divider**:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_{sensor}}{R_{sensor} + R_{ref}}$$

**Example:** An LDR with $R_{ref} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ in series. As light increases, $R_{LDR}$ decreases, and $V_{out}$ decreases.

## 2.5 Wheatstone Bridge — For Precision Measurement

Used with strain gauges, load cells, RTDs:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \left(\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4}\right)$$

At balance ($V_{out} = 0$): $\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_4}$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — guaranteed to appear in KTU Part A (3 marks).**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | Output/Input |
| 2 | Range / Span | $x_{max} - x_{min}$ | Same as input |
| 3 | ADC Resolution (LSB) | $\dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$ | Volts |
| 4 | Digital Output Code | $\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^N - 1)$ | Integer |
| 5 | Quantization Error | $\pm \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{N+1}}$ | Volts |
| 6 | Accuracy | $\dfrac{\vert M - T \vert}{T} \times 100\%$ | % |
| 7 | Linearity Error | $\dfrac{\Delta y_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100\%$ | % |
| 8 | Hysteresis Error | $\dfrac{\vert y_{inc} - y_{dec} \vert}{y_{FS}} \times 100\%$ | % |
| 9 | Dynamic Range | $20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{x_{max}}{x_{min}}\right)$ | dB |
| 10 | SQNR (ADC) | $6.02N + 1.76$ | dB |
| 11 | RC Low-pass cutoff | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz |
| 12 | First-order response | $y(t) = y_{final}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | Output units |
| 13 | Voltage Divider | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_s}{R_s + R_{ref}}$ | Volts |
| 14 | Wheatstone Balance | $\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_4}$ | Dimensionless |
| 15 | LM35 Sensitivity | $10\,\text{mV/°C}$ | mV/°C |
| 16 | Thermistor (β-equation) | $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | Ohms |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — ADC Resolution & Quantization

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem (KTU Style):** An IoT temperature monitoring system uses an LM35 sensor (sensitivity = 10 mV/°C) and a 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$. The sensor measures a temperature of **37°C** (body temperature). Calculate:
> 1. The sensor output voltage
> 2. The ADC resolution (LSB)
> 3. The digital output code
> 4. The quantization error
> 5. The temperature resolution of the entire system

### Step 1 — Sensor Output Voltage

The LM35 produces a linear output of $10\,\text{mV/°C}$.

$$V_{out} = \text{Sensitivity} \times \text{Temperature}$$

$$V_{out} = 10\,\text{mV/°C} \times 37\,°\text{C}$$

$$V_{out} = 370\,\text{mV} = 0.37\,\text{V}$$

### Step 2 — ADC Resolution (LSB)

The smallest voltage step the 10-bit ADC can resolve is:

$$\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N} = \frac{5}{2^{10}}$$

$$2^{10} = 1024$$

$$\text{LSB} = \frac{5}{1024} = 0.0048828125\,\text{V} \approx 4.88\,\text{mV}$$

### Step 3 — Digital Output Code

$$\text{Digital Code} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^N - 1) \right\rfloor$$

$$\text{Digital Code} = \left\lfloor \frac{0.37}{5} \times 1023 \right\rfloor$$

$$= \left\lfloor 0.074 \times 1023 \right\rfloor$$

$$= \left\lfloor 75.702 \right\rfloor$$

$$= 75 \quad \text{(in decimal)}$$

Converting to binary (KTU sometimes asks this):

$$75_{10} = 64 + 8 + 2 + 1 = 01001011_2$$

### Step 4 — Quantization Error

$$\text{Error} = \pm \frac{1}{2}\,\text{LSB} = \pm \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{N+1}} = \pm \frac{5}{2^{11}}$$

$$= \pm \frac{5}{2048} = \pm 0.002441\,\text{V} = \pm 2.44\,\text{mV}$$

### Step 5 — System Temperature Resolution

Since the sensor produces $10\,\text{mV/°C}$ and the ADC can resolve $4.88\,\text{mV}$:

$$\text{Temperature Resolution} = \frac{\text{LSB}}{\text{Sensitivity}} = \frac{4.88\,\text{mV}}{10\,\text{mV/°C}}$$

$$= 0.488\,°\text{C} \approx 0.49\,°\text{C}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The system cannot distinguish temperature changes smaller than **0.49°C**. To improve resolution, we could use a 12-bit ADC (LSB = 1.22 mV → 0.12°C) or use an external amplifier.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Thermistor Temperature Calculation (β-Equation)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** An NTC thermistor has $\beta = 3950$, $R_0 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ at $T_0 = 25\,°\text{C} = 298.15\,\text{K}$. Calculate its resistance at $T = 50\,°\text{C} = 323.15\,\text{K}$.

### Step 1 — Recall the β-equation

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the exponent

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{323.15} - \frac{1}{298.15}$$

$$= 0.003094 - 0.003354$$

$$= -0.000260\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

### Step 3 — Multiply by β

$$\beta \times (-0.000260) = 3950 \times (-0.000260) = -1.027$$

### Step 4 — Compute the exponential

$$e^{-1.027} = 0.3581$$

### Step 5 — Final resistance

$$R_T = 10{,}000 \times 0.3581 = 3581\,\Omega \approx 3.58\,\text{k}\Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** NTC thermistors are **negative temperature coefficient** — as temperature increases, resistance decreases. This matches our result: 10 kΩ at 25°C dropped to ~3.58 kΩ at 50°C.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Voltage Divider with LDR

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** An LDR ($R_{LDR} = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$ in bright light) is connected in a voltage divider with $R_{ref} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $V_{in} = 5\,\text{V}$. Calculate the output voltage and the digital code from a 10-bit ADC.

### Step 1 — Apply voltage divider formula

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_{LDR}}{R_{LDR} + R_{ref}}$$

$$V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{5\,\text{k}\Omega}{5\,\text{k}\Omega + 10\,\text{k}\Omega}$$

$$V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{5}{15} = 5 \cdot 0.3333 = 1.667\,\text{V}$$

### Step 2 — ADC Digital Code

$$\text{Code} = \left\lfloor \frac{1.667}{5} \times 1023 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 0.3333 \times 1023 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 341.0 \right\rfloor = 341$$

$$341_{10} = 101010101_2$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Accuracy and Precision Numerical

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A temperature sensor reads 28.5°C, 28.6°C, 28.5°C, 28.4°C, 28.5°C for a true value of 27.0°C. Calculate the accuracy and comment on precision.

### Step 1 — Calculate Mean

$$\bar{T} = \frac{28.5 + 28.6 + 28.5 + 28.4 + 28.5}{5} = \frac{142.5}{5} = 28.5\,°\text{C}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Standard Deviation (Precision Indicator)

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (T_i - \bar{T})^2}{N-1}}$$

Differences from mean: $0, +0.1, 0, -0.1, 0$
Squared differences: $0, 0.01, 0, 0.01, 0$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{0.02}{4}} = \sqrt{0.005} = 0.0707\,°\text{C}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Accuracy

$$\text{Accuracy} = \left|\frac{28.5 - 27.0}{27.0}\right| \times 100\% = \frac{1.5}{27.0} \times 100\% = 5.56\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** The sensor is **highly precise** (very low standard deviation of 0.07°C) but **inaccurate** (off by 5.56%). The systematic error can be corrected via **calibration offset**.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Analog vs Digital Sensors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is the most important KTU exam point. Memorize it.**

| Property | Analog Sensor | Digital Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| **Output type** | Continuous | Discrete (binary / digital word) |
| **Resolution basis** | External ADC bits | Internal ADC bits |
| **Noise immunity** | Poor (susceptible to EMI) | Excellent (threshold-based) |
| **Long-distance transmission** | Bad (voltage drop, noise) | Good (protocols regenerate signals) |
| **Cost** | Low (Rs. 20–200 typical) | Moderate (Rs. 100–2000 typical) |
| **Power consumption** | Generally lower | Slightly higher (due to on-chip logic) |
| **Calibration** | Frequent | Less frequent |
| **Integration with MCU** | Needs ADC, amplifier, filter | Direct pin or bus connection |
| **Examples** | LM35, LDR, Potentiometer | DHT11, DS18B20, MPU6050 |
| **Use case** | Simple, low-cost, educational | Industrial, precise, scalable IoT |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 🎯 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

### Q1. Define a sensor. Differentiate between analog and digital sensors with two examples each. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Sensor Definition:**
A sensor is a device that detects a physical, chemical, or biological quantity and converts it into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, or resistance). In IoT, sensors form the "Things" layer.

**Analog Sensor:**
- **Definition:** Produces a **continuous output signal** proportional to the measured quantity.
- **Examples:** (1) **LM35** temperature sensor — outputs 10 mV/°C continuously. (2) **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)** — resistance changes continuously with light intensity.

**Digital Sensor:**
- **Definition:** Produces a **discrete output signal** in the form of HIGH/LOW voltage levels or digital data words via communication protocols.
- **Examples:** (1) **DS18B20** digital temperature sensor — outputs digital temperature values via 1-Wire protocol. (2) **MPU6050** IMU — outputs acceleration/gyro data via I²C.

**Key Difference:** Analog sensors need an external **ADC** for microcontroller interfacing, whereas digital sensors have a built-in ADC and communicate via digital buses (I²C, SPI, UART, 1-Wire).

---

### Q2. List any six performance characteristics of a sensor. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Sensitivity** — ratio of change in output to change in input
2. **Range/Span** — minimum and maximum measurable input
3. **Resolution** — smallest detectable change in input
4. **Accuracy** — closeness of reading to true value
5. **Precision** — reproducibility of repeated measurements
6. **Linearity** — how closely output follows a straight line vs. input
7. **Hysteresis** — difference in output for same input approached from opposite directions
8. **Drift** — change in output over time at constant input
9. **Response Time** — time to reach 63.2% of final value
10. **Dynamic Range** — ratio of largest to smallest measurable input (in dB)

*(Any six with brief definition = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 🎯 Part B: Module Internal Choice Questions (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks

**Q (A)(a)** Explain the block diagram of an analog sensor signal conditioning and acquisition system with ADC interfacing. List the four blocks of signal conditioning. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

An analog IoT sensor signal chain consists of the following sequential blocks:

1. **Sensor / Sensing Element:** Converts physical quantity (temperature, pressure, light) into a weak electrical signal (mV-level voltage, resistance change, or small current).

2. **Signal Conditioning Block** — performs four operations:
   - **Amplification:** Op-amp (e.g., LM358, INA128 instrumentation amplifier) boosts weak sensor signals (like thermocouple's mV output) to a 0–5V range suitable for the ADC.
   - **Filtering:** RC low-pass filter removes high-frequency noise. Cutoff frequency: $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$
   - **Linearization:** For non-linear sensors (thermistor, RTD), look-up table or polynomial fitting is used to linearize the response.
   - **Isolation:** Optical or transformer-based isolation to protect MCU from high-voltage transients.

3. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** Quantizes the continuous analog signal into a discrete digital code.
   - N-bit ADC divides $V_{ref}$ into $2^N$ levels.
   - Resolution (LSB) = $\frac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$

4. **Microcontroller / Processor:** Reads the digital code, performs further processing (averaging, threshold detection), and transmits data via WiFi/BLE/LoRa to the cloud.

```
Physical → Sensor → Signal Conditioning → ADC → MCU → Cloud
```

---

**Q (A)(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of an LM35 temperature sensor. An LM35 is interfaced to a 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$. The ADC output code is **250**. Determine the temperature measured. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**LM35 Working:**
The LM35 is a precision centigrade temperature sensor from Texas Instruments. It produces a linear analog output voltage directly proportional to the Celsius temperature:

$$V_{out} = 10\,\text{mV/°C} \times T(°C)$$

- Output range: **0 mV to 1500 mV** for 0°C to 150°C
- Calibrated directly in °C (no external calibration needed)
- Operates on 4–30V supply, low self-heating

**Working Principle:** The internal circuitry uses the temperature-dependent base-emitter voltage ($V_{BE}$) of bipolar transistors, where $V_{BE}$ decreases linearly by ~2 mV/°C. Internal gain stages scale this to 10 mV/°C at the output pin.

**Calculation (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1:** Convert ADC code to voltage.

$$V_{in} = \frac{\text{Code} \times V_{ref}}{2^N - 1} = \frac{250 \times 5}{1023}$$

$$V_{in} = \frac{1250}{1023} = 1.2219\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2:** Convert voltage to temperature using LM35 sensitivity.

$$T = \frac{V_{in}}{10\,\text{mV/°C}} = \frac{1.2219\,\text{V}}{0.01\,\text{V/°C}}$$

$$T = 122.19\,°\text{C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** This is well within the LM35's range of 0–150°C. To improve accuracy, we could use a 12-bit ADC (LSB = 1.22 mV → 0.12°C resolution).

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**Q (B)(a)** Compare analog and digital sensors in a tabular form with at least 8 points. With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of a digital sensor. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| # | Property | Analog Sensor | Digital Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Output type | Continuous (voltage/current) | Discrete (binary/digital code) |
| 2 | ADC requirement | External ADC mandatory | Internal ADC included |
| 3 | Noise immunity | Low | High (threshold-based logic) |
| 4 | Cost | Low | Moderate to high |
| 5 | Resolution | Limited by external ADC | Defined by sensor's internal ADC |
| 6 | Calibration | Frequently needed | Factory-calibrated |
| 7 | Long-distance transmission | Lossy (voltage drop) | Reliable (digital protocols) |
| 8 | Examples | LM35, LDR, Thermistor | DS18B20, MPU6050, DHT11 |
| 9 | Communication | Direct pin to ADC | I²C, SPI, UART, 1-Wire |
| 10 | Signal conditioning | External (amp + filter) | Internal (on-chip) |

**Block Diagram of a Digital Sensor:**

```
Physical Quantity → Sensing Element → On-chip Signal Conditioning 
→ Internal ADC → Digital Processor / Calibration Engine 
→ Memory (Configuration Registers) → Digital Interface (I²C/SPI/UART/1-Wire) 
→ External MCU
```

**Explanation of Internal Architecture:**

1. **Sensing Element:** MEMS-based or silicon-based transducer (e.g., piezo-resistor, capacitive plate) that converts physical quantity to an electrical signal.

2. **On-Chip Signal Conditioning:** Built-in amplifiers, filters, and temperature compensation circuits.

3. **Internal ADC:** High-resolution (16-bit, 24-bit common) sigma-delta or SAR ADC quantizes the signal.

4. **Digital Processor:** Performs linearization, calibration compensation, and data formatting.

5. **Memory:** Stores calibration coefficients and configuration registers (the user can configure resolution, sampling rate, etc.).

6. **Digital Interface:** Communicates via standard protocols — I²C (2-wire), SPI (4-wire), UART (asynchronous serial), or 1-Wire (single data line + ground).

---

**Q (B)(b)** A 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3\,\text{V}$ is used in an IoT system. Calculate:
(i) The resolution in volts per LSB.
(ii) The digital output code for an analog input of $1.85\,\text{V}$.
(iii) The quantization error in volts.
(iv) The SQNR in dB. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $N = 12$ bits
- $V_{ref} = 3.3\,\text{V}$
- $V_{in} = 1.85\,\text{V}$

---

**(i) Resolution per LSB:**

$$\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N} = \frac{3.3}{2^{12}} = \frac{3.3}{4096}$$

$$\text{LSB} = 0.0008057\,\text{V} = 0.8057\,\text{mV}$$

---

**(ii) Digital Output Code:**

$$\text{Code} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^N - 1) \right\rfloor$$

$$\text{Code} = \left\lfloor \frac{1.85}{3.3} \times 4095 \right\rfloor$$

$$= \left\lfloor 0.5606 \times 4095 \right\rfloor$$

$$= \left\lfloor 2295.66 \right\rfloor = 2295$$

Converting to binary (for completeness):

$$2295_{10} = 2048 + 128 + 64 + 32 + 16 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 100011110111_2$$

So 12-bit code = `100011110111`

---

**(iii) Quantization Error:**

$$\text{Error}_{max} = \pm \frac{1}{2}\,\text{LSB} = \pm \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{N+1}} = \pm \frac{3.3}{2^{13}}$$

$$= \pm \frac{3.3}{8192} = \pm 0.0004028\,\text{V} = \pm 0.4028\,\text{mV}$$

---

**(iv) Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR):**

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \cdot N + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76$$

$$= 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0\,\text{dB}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Using $2^N$ instead of $(2^N - 1)$ in the digital code formula. KTU strictly expects $(2^N - 1)$ since the digital code range is **0 to $2^N - 1$**, not 0 to $2^N$.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing LSB (the resolution) with LSB (the **least significant bit of the output code**). In ADC formulas, LSB = $V_{ref}/2^N$.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to convert $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V to consistent units with $V_{in} = 1.85$ V. Always use **volts** or **millivolts** consistently.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Writing "**−1.76 dB**" or "**6.02 × N − 1.76**" — the correct formula is **6.02N + 1.76**.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** In comparison tables, not mentioning **I²C/SPI/UART** for digital sensors — KTU specifically tests protocol awareness.
> 6. **Common Mistake 6:** Confusing **accuracy with precision** in numerical problems — calculate both, but present the correct one as the answer.
> 7. **Common Mistake 7:** In LM35 questions, writing the formula as $T = V_{out} \times 10$ instead of $T = V_{out} / 0.01$ — both are mathematically equivalent, but show the **division** form to avoid the 0.5 mark cut.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Tick off as you memorize:**

### 🟦 Sensor Fundamentals
- [ ] Sensor = device that converts physical quantity to electrical signal
- [ ] Sensor is the **first layer** of IoT architecture (Things layer)
- [ ] Transfer function: $y = f(x)$

### 🟧 Analog Sensors
- [ ] Output is **continuous** (voltage, current, or resistance)
- [ ] Needs **external ADC** to interface with MCU
- [ ] Examples: **LM35, LDR, Thermistor, Potentiometer, Flex sensor**
- [ ] LM35 sensitivity: **10 mV/°C**
- [ ] NTC thermistor: **resistance decreases** as temperature rises
- [ ] Voltage divider formula: $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_s}{R_s + R_{ref}}$
- [ ] Susceptible to **noise**, requires **signal conditioning**
- [ ] 4 blocks of signal conditioning: **Amplification, Filtering, Linearization, Isolation**

### 🟩 Digital Sensors
- [ ] Output is **discrete** (binary 0/1 or digital data words)
- [ ] Has **on-chip ADC**, signal conditioning, and processor
- [ ] Communication: **I²C, SPI, UART, 1-Wire**
- [ ] Examples: **DHT11, DS18B20, MPU6050, BMP280**
- [ ] Higher noise immunity, factory calibrated, easier integration
- [ ] More expensive than analog counterparts

### 🟥 ADC Mathematics (MUST MEMORIZE)
- [ ] Resolution (LSB) = $\frac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$
- [ ] Digital code = $\left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^N - 1) \right\rfloor$
- [ ] Quantization error = $\pm \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{N+1}}$ (i.e., $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB)
- [ ] SQNR (dB) = $6.02N + 1.76$
- [ ] For $N = 10, V_{ref} = 5V$: LSB = **4.88 mV**
- [ ] For $N = 12, V_{ref} = 3.3V$: LSB = **0.806 mV**

### 🟪 Performance Characteristics
- [ ] **Sensitivity** = $dy/dx$ (output change per unit input change)
- [ ] **Accuracy** = $\frac{|M - T|}{T} \times 100\%$ (closeness to truth)
- [ ] **Precision** = closeness of repeated measurements (low standard deviation)
- [ ] **Range** = $x_{max} - x_{min}$
- [ ] **Linearity error** = $\frac{\Delta y_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100\%$
- [ ] **Hysteresis error** = $\frac{|y_{inc} - y_{dec}|}{y_{FS}} \times 100\%$
- [ ] **Dynamic Range (dB)** = $20 \log_{10}(x_{max}/x_{min})$
- [ ] **Response time** = time to reach **63.2%** of final value

### 🟨 RC Filter & Signal Conditioning
- [ ] Low-pass cutoff: $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$
- [ ] Op-amp non-inverting gain: $A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$
- [ ] Wheatstone bridge balance: $\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_4}$

### ⭐ Examiner's Golden Tips
- [ ] Always convert units to **V or mV** consistently
- [ ] Use **floor function** $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ for digital codes
- [ ] Mention **noise immunity, cost, integration ease** in comparison answers
- [ ] In numerical questions, **show all steps** — KTU awards step marks
- [ ] Always include the **digital protocol** (I²C/SPI/UART/1-Wire) when discussing digital sensors
- [ ] Use the term **"smart sensor"** for digital sensors with on-chip processing

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS AND SMART COMPUTING.
> - **Module:** Module 3
