(7 marks) **Model Solution:** 1. **Definitions (3 marks):** **Euclidean Distance** between vectors $\vec{x}, \vec{y} \in \mathbb{R}^n$: $$d_E(\vec{x}, \vec{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - y_k)^2}$$ **Manhattan Distance**: $$d_M(\vec{x}, \vec{y}) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_k - y_k \vert$$ **Minkowski Distance** (generalized $L_p$ norm): $$d_p(\vec{x}, \vec{y}) = \left( \sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_k - y_k \vert^p \right)^{\!1/p}, \quad p \geq 1$$ 2. **Reduction to Euclidean ($p = 2$) (2 marks):** $$d_{p=2} = \left( \sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_k - y_k \vert^2 \right)^{1/2} = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - y_k)^2} = d_E$$ 3. **Reduction to Manhattan ($p = 1$) (2 marks):** $$d_{p=1} = \left( \sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_k - y_k \vert^1 \right)^{1/1} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_k - y_k \vert = d_M$$ Hence Minkowski is the **unifying family** of $L_p$ distance metrics used heavily in IoT clustering algorithms. --- **(b)** A smart-grid IoT analytics system monitors the daily energy consumption (kWh) and peak load (kW) of 4 households as vectors: $A = (12, 4)$, $B = (15, 5)$, $C = (10, 3)$, $D = (14, 4.5)$. Using **Euclidean distance**, perform **one iteration of agglomerative clustering** (single linkage) and draw the dendrogram. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Compute pairwise Euclidean distances (3 marks):** $$d(A,B) = \sqrt{(12-15)^2 + (4-5)^2} = \sqrt{9+1} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.16$$ $$d(A,C) = \sqrt{(12-10)^2 + (4-3)^2} = \sqrt{4+1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$$ $$d(A,D) = \sqrt{(12-14)^2 + (4-4.5)^2} = \sqrt{4+0.25} = \sqrt{4.25} \approx 2.06$$ $$d(B,C) = \sqrt{(15-10)^2 + (5-3)^2} = \sqrt{25+4} = \sqrt{29} \approx 5.39$$ $$d(B,D) = \sqrt{(15-14)^2 + (5-4.5)^2} = \sqrt{1+0.25} = \sqrt{1.25} \approx 1.12$$ $$d(C,D) = \sqrt{(10-14)^2 + (3-4.5)^2} = \sqrt{16+2.25} = \sqrt{18.25} \approx 4.27$$ **Step 2: Distance Matrix (1 mark):** | | A | B | C | D | |---|---|---|---|---| | **A** | 0 | 3.16 | 2.24 | **2.06** | | **B** | 3.16 | 0 | 5.39 | **1.12** | | **C** | 2.24 | 5.39 | 0 | 4.27 | | **D** | **2.06** | **1.12** | 4.27 | 0 | **Step 3: Merge Closest Pair (1 mark):** Minimum distance = 1.12 → merge **B and D** into cluster **(B,D)** at height 1.12. **Step 4: Update distances using single linkage (1 mark):** $d((B,D), A) = \min(3.16, 2.06) = 2.06$; $d((B,D), C) = \min(5.39, 4.27) = 4.27$. Now the minimum is 2.06 → merge **A with (B,D)** at height 2.06. Finally, **C** joins at 4.27. **Step 5: Dendrogram (1 mark):** ```mermaid flowchart TB n1[B] --- m1((B,D)
h=1.12) n2[D] --- m1 m1 --- m2(((B,D),A)
h=2.06)) n3[A] --- m2 m2 --- root((All
h=4.27)) n4[C] --- root ``` **Inference:** Households B and D have nearly identical energy patterns (likely similar-sized homes or appliances). C is the most distinct — possibly a small studio apartment. Useful for **demand-response grouping** in the smart grid. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PCCGT602 Module 3 — Distance Questions:** > 1. **Unit Mix-up:** Students often confuse dBm with dB. *Remember:* $A$ and RSSI must be in the **same** dBm scale; only their *difference* matters. > 2. **Path Loss Exponent (n):** Forgetting to use $n$ in the denominator ($10n$) and writing only $10$ is a **frequent 1-mark loss**. > 3. **Logarithm Base:** Using $\ln$ instead of $\log_{10}$ gives a wrong distance value — *always use base 10* in the LDPL model. > 4. **In Trilateration:** Drawing 2 circles is not enough — students must show **3 circles intersecting at a single point** for full credit. > 5. **Cosine Distance vs Cosine Similarity:** Many students write cosine *similarity* as the distance; the **distance is $1 -$ similarity**. > 6. **Mahalanobis without Covariance Inversion:** Forgetting $S^{-1}$ collapses it to Euclidean; no credit for a confused definition. > 7. **No Diagram in 14-mark Q:** Even for analytical questions, KTU examiners award **1–2 marks** for a well-labelled diagram (RSSI-vs-distance plot, trilateration sketch, or dendrogram). *Always include one.* --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — "Distance" in IoT & Smart Computing** - ⏐ **Distance = Decisive Parameter** in IoT for both *physical localization* and *data analytics*. - ⏐ **RSSI-based LDPL model** is the cheapest and most ubiquitous ranging technique. - ⏐ **Key formula:** $d = 10^{(A - RSSI)/(10n)}$; remember base-10 logarithm and divide by $10n$. - ⏐ **ToA** is the most accurate, but demands **clock synchronization** — uses speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. - ⏐ **TDoA** is preferred when the *target cannot synchronize* (e.g., passive RFID, cellular handsets). - ⏐ **AoA** needs an **antenna array**; only two anchors suffice for 2-D position via **triangulation**. - ⏐ **Euclidean** = straight-line ($L_2$); **Manhattan** = grid-path ($L_1$); **Minkowski** = generalized $L_p$. - ⏐ **Cosine distance** = $1 - \cos(\theta)$; best for direction-based (not magnitude-based) similarity. - ⏐ **Mahalanobis distance** uses covariance — essential for correlated IoT features (e.g., temperature–humidity). - ⏐ **Hamming distance** counts differing bits — used in RFID, error-correcting codes, IoT security hashing. - ⏐ **Trilateration** (3 circles) and **Multilateration** (≥3 anchors, hyperbolic) are the two geometric engines that turn *distances* into *positions*. - ⏐ **Real-world anchors:** BLE/Wi-Fi → RSSI; UWB → ToA; Cellular → TDoA; 5G mmWave → AoA+Beamforming. - ⏐ **KTU exam tip:** For 7-mark derivations, always show the **starting equation → algebraic manipulation → final boxed answer** with **units**. - ⏐ **Sensitivity caveat:** A small dB change in RSSI yields a *large* change in distance (logarithmic amplification) — discuss this in any localization question for extra credit.

topics.title: Light
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS AND SMART COMPUTING.
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Light

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the Internet of Things and Smart Computing, **Light** is treated as a measurable physical quantity (an electromagnetic wave / photon flux) that acts as both a **sensor input parameter** and an **actuator output medium** in embedded cyber-physical systems. It is formally defined as:

> **Light** = *Electromagnetic radiation within the visible spectrum (wavelength range approximately **380 nm to 750 nm**) that can be detected by the human eye and is fundamental to a wide range of IoT sensing and communication modalities.*

From the IoT engineering standpoint, "Light" encompasses **four pillars**:

| Pillar | Description | IoT Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Light as a Physical Phenomenon** | Electromagnetic radiation with particle–wave duality (photons) | Underpins sensor physics |
| **Light as a Sensor Modality** | Photometric quantity measured by transducers | Environmental monitoring |
| **Light as an Actuator / Output** | Emitted by LEDs, lasers, displays | Visual feedback, smart lighting |
| **Light as a Communication Carrier** | Modulated visible/IR light for data transfer | Li-Fi, VLC, optical wireless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – Module 3 Focus**
> Module 3 of PCCGT602 typically deals with **Sensors and Actuators for IoT**. The topic "Light" specifically targets (a) light-sensing transducers, (b) photometric units and the **inverse square law**, (c) the architecture of smart-lighting systems, and (d) visible-light communication protocols such as **Li-Fi**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Think of light in the IoT world the way a *postman* and a *letter* work together:

- **The Letter (Photon)** — A tiny packet of energy that travels extremely fast. Every IoT light sensor is essentially *counting letters* arriving per second.
- **The Postman (The Medium)** — The path between emitter and receiver (air, glass, water). The medium may absorb, scatter, or block the letters.
- **The Postbox (The Sensor)** — An LDR, photodiode or camera that *opens* when photons strike it, converting the energy of those incoming "letters" into an electrical signal that the microcontroller (the "post office") can read and act upon.
- **The Sender (The Actuator/LED)** — A smart bulb that can be commanded to emit more photons (brighter) or fewer (dimmer), or to flicker them at very high speeds invisible to the eye — *encoding messages inside the light itself* (this is exactly how **Li-Fi** works).

So whenever a KTU question says *"design a smart lighting system"*, the engineer is really designing: **(a) a way to sense photons**, **(b) a way to convert that count into a useful signal**, **(c) a way to send commands back to a light emitter**, and **(d) optionally a way to modulate the emitter's output to carry data.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric** — The fundamental constant governing all light-related phenomena:
> **Speed of light in vacuum, $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$** (often rounded to **$3 \times 10^8$ m/s** for KTU calculations).
> Planck's constant: **$h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$**.
> Photon energy relation: $E = hf = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$.

### 1.3 The Visible Spectrum at a Glance

| Colour | Approx. Wavelength (nm) | Approx. Frequency (THz) |
|---|---|---|
| Violet | 380 – 450 | 668 – 789 |
| Blue | 450 – 495 | 606 – 668 |
| Green | 495 – 570 | 526 – 606 |
| Yellow | 570 – 590 | 508 – 526 |
| Orange | 590 – 620 | 484 – 508 |
| Red | 620 – 750 | 400 – 484 |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory hook:** Wavelengths are roughly **ROYGBIV** — **R**ed (~700 nm) on the long-wavelength end, **V**iolet (~400 nm) on the short-wavelength end. *Frequency does the opposite*: violet has the highest frequency, red has the lowest.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Photometric Quantities (the language of light in IoT)

A frequent KTU question is *"distinguish between luminous flux, illuminance and luminous intensity"* — these are *not* the same thing.

1. **Luminous Flux ($\Phi_v$)** — the **total** visible light energy emitted by a source per unit time.
   - Unit: **lumen (lm)**
   - Symbol on datasheets: $\Phi$ or $F$

2. **Luminous Intensity ($I_v$)** — the **directional** light energy emitted per unit solid angle.
   - Unit: **candela (cd)**
   - $I_v = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{\Omega}$, where $\Omega$ is the solid angle in steradians.

3. **Illuminance ($E_v$)** — the **light energy falling on a surface** per unit area.
   - Unit: **lux (lx)** = 1 lm / m²
   - This is the quantity that IoT light sensors (LDR, BH1750, TSL2561) actually measure.

4. **Luminance ($L_v$)** — light *leaving* a surface in a particular direction (what the eye perceives as "brightness").
   - Unit: **cd/m²** (also called "nit").

### 2.2 Core Operational Equations

#### (A) Wave Equation of Light
Light is an electromagnetic wave. Its frequency $f$, wavelength $\lambda$ and speed $c$ in vacuum obey:

$$
c = f \lambda
$$

In any other medium of refractive index $n$, the speed reduces to $v = \dfrac{c}{n}$, but the **frequency remains unchanged**; only the wavelength shortens.

#### (B) Photon Energy (Einstein's Quantum Relation)
A photon of frequency $f$ carries discrete energy:

$$
E = hf = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for IoT sensors** — A photodiode absorbs photons whose energy $E \geq E_g$ (the semiconductor's **band-gap energy**). If the photon energy is too small (wavelength too long), no electron-hole pair is generated and the sensor is "blind" to that colour.

#### (C) Inverse Square Law of Illuminance
For a point source of luminous intensity $I_v$, the illuminance $E_v$ on a surface held perpendicular to the beam at distance $d$ is:

$$
E_v = \dfrac{I_v}{d^{2}}
$$

This is the single most tested photometric formula in KTU. If the surface is *tilted* by angle $\theta$ from the beam axis (Lambert's cosine law), then:

$$
E_v = \dfrac{I_v \cos\theta}{d^{2}}
$$

#### (D) Photoresistor (LDR) Conductance Model
The resistance $R$ of a CdS-type LDR varies with incident illuminance $L$ (in lux) approximately as:

$$
R \propto L^{-\gamma}
$$

where $\gamma$ is a material-dependent exponent typically between **0.7 and 0.9** for CdS. Taking logarithms gives a linear fit:

$$
\log R = A - \gamma \log L
$$

This is a *log-log straight line* on a graph — students frequently confuse this with an exponential decay; remember it is a **power law**, not exponential.

#### (E) Photodiode Short-Circuit Current Model
A photodiode in photovoltaic mode produces a short-circuit current proportional to incident optical power $P_{opt}$:

$$
I_{sc} = \mathcal{R} \cdot P_{opt}
$$

where $\mathcal{R}$ is the **responsivity** (A/W) of the photodiode at a given wavelength. For silicon at $\lambda \approx 850$ nm, $\mathcal{R} \approx 0.5$ A/W.

#### (F) Li-Fi Channel Capacity (Shannon-style bound)
For a visible-light channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz and signal-to-noise ratio $\text{SNR}$:

$$
C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \text{SNR}\right) \quad \text{bits/s}
$$

Modern Li-Fi systems reach multi-Gbps throughput because $B$ can be **several MHz** in a typical LED, far exceeding what Wi-Fi shares in the crowded RF spectrum.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **only** set of equations you need to handle any Module-3 numerical or derivation question on "Light". Memorise it column-by-column.

| # | Equation | Name / Law | Variables | Typical Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $c = f\lambda$ | Wave equation of light | $f$ = frequency, $\lambda$ = wavelength | $f$: Hz, $\lambda$: m | Spectrum problems |
| 2 | $E = hf = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | $h = 6.626\times10^{-34}$ J·s | J (or eV) | Quantum sensor design |
| 3 | $E_v = \dfrac{I_v}{d^{2}}$ | Inverse square law | $I_v$ = luminous intensity, $d$ = distance | lx | Smart-streetlight design |
| 4 | $E_v = \dfrac{I_v \cos\theta}{d^{2}}$ | Lambert's cosine law | $\theta$ = angle of incidence | lx | Tilted-surface illuminance |
| 5 | $R \propto L^{-\gamma}$ | LDR power-law model | $L$ = illuminance, $\gamma \approx 0.7$–$0.9$ | $\Omega$ | Sensor calibration |
| 6 | $I_{sc} = \mathcal{R} P_{opt}$ | Photodiode responsivity | $\mathcal{R}$ = responsivity | A/W | Optical receiver design |
| 7 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{nk_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{sc}}{I_0}+1\right)$ | Solar-cell open-circuit voltage | $k_B$ = Boltzmann const., $q$ = electron charge | V | Energy-harvesting IoT |
| 8 | $C = B\log_{2}\!\left(1+\text{SNR}\right)$ | Shannon capacity | $B$ = bandwidth, SNR = signal-to-noise ratio | bits/s | Li-Fi throughput |
| 9 | $\Phi_v = I_v \cdot \Omega$ | Flux–intensity relation | $\Omega$ = solid angle | lm vs. cd·sr | Photometric conversions |
| 10 | $E_v = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{A}$ | Illuminance definition | $A$ = illuminated area | lx | Lux-meter design |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember the units pyramid:**
> **1 candela** of intensity uniformly spread over a full sphere ($4\pi$ sr) gives **$4\pi \approx 12.57$ lumens** of flux.
> **1 lux** = 1 lumen per square metre.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture** — Photodiode + NDVI filter measures canopy light reflectance to compute plant health in real time.
- **Smart Street Lighting** — LDR + PIR (passive infrared) sensor dims the LED when no human/vehicle is detected, recovering inverse-square-law-based brightness at the road surface.
- **Li-Fi for Hospitals** — Visible light cannot penetrate walls, so Li-Fi provides *physically secure* communication in RF-restricted zones (ICUs, aircraft cabins).
- **Solar-Powered IoT Nodes** — The same photodiode physics powers the photovoltaic cell that recharges the node's battery — the sensor is also the energy source.
- **Automotive LiDAR** — Pulsed laser (≈ 905 nm or 1550 nm) light measures distance for self-driving cars; the round-trip time gives $d = \dfrac{ct}{2}$.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Photon Energy from a Given Wavelength of Light

**Problem (KTU-style):** A red LED used in a smart street-light emits light of wavelength **650 nm**. Calculate the energy of a single photon emitted by this LED. Take $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s and $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. Express your answer in **joules** and in **electron-volts (eV)**. *(Recall: 1 eV $= 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J.)*

#### Step-by-step solution

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**
For a single photon, energy is given by the Planck–Einstein relation:

$$
E = hf = \frac{hc}{\lambda}
$$

We are given $\lambda$, $h$ and $c$, so we use the right-hand form directly.

**Step 2 — Convert the wavelength from nanometres to metres.**
The SI base unit of length is metres, so:

$$
\lambda = 650\ \text{nm} = 650 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m} = 6.50 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values into the formula.**

$$
E = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}) \cdot (3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s})}{6.50 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply numerator and simplify.**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8} = (6.626 \times 3) \times 10^{-34+8} = 19.878 \times 10^{-26}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25}\ \text{J·m}
$$

**Step 5 — Divide by the denominator.**

$$
E = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{6.50 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{1.9878}{6.50} \times 10^{-25-(-7)} = 0.3058 \times 10^{-18}\ \text{J}
$$

$$
E \approx 3.058 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to electron-volts.**

$$
E \text{ (in eV)} = \frac{3.058 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J/eV}} = \frac{3.058}{1.602} \approx 1.91\ \text{eV}
$$

#### Final Result

$$
\boxed{E \approx 3.06 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J} \;\;\equiv\;\; 1.91\ \text{eV}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** Red light is *low energy* in the visible band. Typical Si band-gap energy is 1.12 eV, so a 1.91 eV photon comfortably liberates an electron — which is exactly why red LEDs can be sensed by ordinary silicon photodiodes.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Illuminance at the Floor of a Smart Streetlight (Inverse Square Law + Lambert's Cosine Law)

**Problem (KTU-style):** A smart streetlight emits a luminous intensity of **$I_v = 1500$ cd** uniformly downward from a height of **$d = 6$ m** above the road. A pedestrian carrying a phone with a lux sensor walks directly beneath the pole, then 3 m sideways.

**(a)** Find the illuminance directly under the lamp.
**(b)** Find the illuminance at the sideways position (the surface of the phone is roughly horizontal, so use the perpendicular-incidence simplification for a 3 m horizontal offset, with the line-of-sight now making an angle $\theta$ with the vertical).
**(c)** How far from the pole must the next identical lamp be placed so that the illuminance on the road does **not fall below 5 lx** anywhere between the two lamps?

#### Step-by-step solution

**Step (a) — Illuminance directly under the lamp.**

The phone surface is horizontal, and the lamp is 6 m directly above it, so the ray strikes the surface perpendicularly ($\theta = 0^\circ$, hence $\cos\theta = 1$).

$$
E_v = \frac{I_v \cos\theta}{d^{2}} = \frac{1500 \times 1}{6^{2}} = \frac{1500}{36}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_v = 41.67\ \text{lx directly under the lamp.}}
$$

**Step (b) — Illuminance at a 3 m horizontal offset.**

The new slant distance between the lamp and the phone is:

$$
d' = \sqrt{6^{2} + 3^{2}} = \sqrt{36 + 9} = \sqrt{45}\ \text{m} = 6.708\ \text{m}
$$

The angle $\theta$ between the downward vertical and the line from lamp to phone satisfies:

$$
\cos\theta = \frac{\text{vertical component}}{\text{slant distance}} = \frac{6}{\sqrt{45}} = \frac{6}{6.708} = 0.8944
$$

Therefore:

$$
E_v = \frac{I_v \cos\theta}{(d')^{2}} = \frac{1500 \times 0.8944}{45}
$$

$$
E_v = \frac{1341.6}{45} \approx 29.81\ \text{lx}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_v \approx 29.8\ \text{lx at the 3 m offset position.}}
$$

**Step (c) — Maximum allowed pole-to-pole spacing for $E_v \geq 5$ lx.**

The minimum illuminance occurs at the **midpoint between two adjacent lamps**, i.e., at a horizontal distance $x$ from each lamp where $x$ equals half the pole-spacing $s$. So $x = s/2$.

Let $s$ be the spacing. The illuminance contribution from a single lamp at horizontal offset $x$ (with the lamp 6 m above the road) is:

$$
E_v(x) = \frac{I_v \cdot 6}{(6^{2} + x^{2})^{3/2}}
$$

because $\cos\theta = \dfrac{6}{\sqrt{36+x^{2}}}$ and $d' = \sqrt{36+x^{2}}$, giving $\dfrac{\cos\theta}{(d')^{2}} = \dfrac{6}{(36+x^{2})^{3/2}}$.

Setting the illuminance at the mid-point equal to 5 lx:

$$
\frac{1500 \cdot 6}{(36 + x^{2})^{3/2}} = 5
$$

Rearrange:

$$
(36 + x^{2})^{3/2} = \frac{9000}{5} = 1800
$$

Raise both sides to the power $2/3$:

$$
36 + x^{2} = 1800^{2/3}
$$

Compute $1800^{2/3}$:

$$
1800^{1/3} \approx 12.16 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 1800^{2/3} \approx 12.16^{2} \approx 147.9
$$

Therefore:

$$
x^{2} = 147.9 - 36 = 111.9 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; x \approx 10.58\ \text{m}
$$

So the **mid-point** is 10.58 m from one lamp, meaning the spacing between two lamps is:

$$
s = 2x \approx 21.16\ \text{m}
$$

#### Final Result

$$
\boxed{s \approx 21.2\ \text{m pole-to-pole spacing for a 5 lx minimum.}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** Real deployments add a "dark spot" safety factor of ≈ 1.5, so designers typically use **$s \approx 14$ m** in practice. This is a classic KTU "design with a margin" question.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — LDR Resistance from Log-Log Power-Law Calibration

**Problem (KTU-style):** The data-sheet of a CdS LDR gives the following calibration values at $25^\circ$C:

| Illuminance $L$ (lux) | 1 | 10 | 100 | 1000 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Resistance $R$ ($\Omega$) | 600 000 | 90 000 | 14 000 | 2 000 |

Find the exponents $A$ and $\gamma$ in $R = 10^{A} L^{-\gamma}$.

#### Step-by-step solution

**Step 1 — Take $\log_{10}$ of both sides.**

$$
\log_{10} R = A - \gamma \log_{10} L
$$

This is linear in $\log L$ with slope $-\gamma$ and intercept $A$.

**Step 2 — Pick two extreme data points to bracket the line.**

Using $(L_1, R_1) = (1, 600\,000)$ and $(L_2, R_2) = (1000, 2000)$:

$$
\log_{10}(600\,000) = A - \gamma \log_{10}(1)
$$

$$
\log_{10}(600\,000) = A - \gamma \cdot 0 = A
$$

So $A = \log_{10}(600\,000) = 5.778$.

**Step 3 — Solve for $\gamma$ using the second point.**

$$
\log_{10}(2000) = A - \gamma \log_{10}(1000)
$$

$$
3.301 = 5.778 - \gamma \cdot 3
$$

$$
\gamma \cdot 3 = 5.778 - 3.301 = 2.477
$$

$$
\gamma = \frac{2.477}{3} \approx 0.826
$$

**Step 4 — Verify with the intermediate point $(L, R) = (10, 90\,000)$.**

$$
R_{\text{model}} = 10^{5.778} \times 10^{-0.826}
$$

$$
\log_{10}(R_{\text{model}}) = 5.778 - 0.826 \cdot 1 = 4.952
$$

$$
R_{\text{model}} = 10^{4.952} \approx 89\,540\ \Omega
$$

The data-sheet value is 90 000 $\Omega$ — error is **0.5 %**, which is excellent.

#### Final Result

$$
\boxed{R = 10^{5.778}\, L^{-0.826}\ \Omega}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's tip:** When asked to "show that the LDR obeys a power law", always plot $\log R$ versus $\log L$, demonstrate the straight-line fit, and report the slope as $-\gamma$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Li-Fi Throughput Estimate vs. Wi-Fi

**Problem (KTU-style):** An LED-based Li-Fi transmitter modulates visible light with a usable electrical bandwidth of **$B = 20$ MHz** and produces an SNR of **30 dB** at the photodetector. A Wi-Fi access point shares a 20 MHz RF channel with 9 other neighbours, each transmitting simultaneously, so its effective SNR is **20 dB**. Compare the throughput of each link using Shannon's capacity formula.

#### Step-by-step solution

**Step 1 — Convert SNRs from dB to linear scale.**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{Li-Fi}} = 10^{30/10} = 10^{3} = 1000
$$

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{Wi-Fi}} = 10^{20/10} = 10^{2} = 100
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon's formula.**

$$
C_{\text{Li-Fi}} = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \text{SNR}\right) = 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_{2}(1001)
$$

$$
\log_{2}(1001) = \frac{\ln 1001}{\ln 2} \approx \frac{6.909}{0.6931} \approx 9.97
$$

$$
C_{\text{Li-Fi}} = 20 \times 10^{6} \times 9.97 \approx 1.99 \times 10^{8}\ \text{bits/s} \approx 199\ \text{Mbps}
$$

$$
C_{\text{Wi-Fi}} = 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_{2}(101) = 20 \times 10^{6} \times 6.66 \approx 133\ \text{Mbps}
$$

#### Final Result

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{Li-Fi}} \approx 199\ \text{Mbps},\quad C_{\text{Wi-Fi}} \approx 133\ \text{Mbps}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality check:** Real-world Li-Fi prototypes (pureLiFi, Signify Trulifi) push this to **multi-Gbps** by using micro-LEDs with $B \sim 1$ GHz. The Shannon limit scales linearly with $B$, and unlike Wi-Fi the visible-light spectrum is *unlicensed and uncongested*.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Light Sensors Compared

| Property | **LDR (CdS Photoresistor)** | **Photodiode (PIN)** | **Phototransistor** | **Ambient-light IC (e.g., BH1750)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output type | Resistance change | Current / voltage | Current (amplified) | Digital (I²C lux) |
| Spectral range | ~ 400 – 700 nm | ~ 200 – 1100 nm (Si) | ~ 400 – 1100 nm | ~ 400 – 700 nm (with IR cut) |
| Response time | Slow (50 – 200 ms) | Very fast (ns – µs) | Medium (µs) | Medium (~ 120 ms typical) |
| Sensitivity | Low (kΩ – MΩ) | High (nA – mA) | High (×100 of photodiode) | Calibrated in lux |
| Power consumption | Passive (no bias) | Needs reverse bias | Needs bias | Active IC (~ 0.5 mW) |
| Typical IoT use | Day/night detection, smart streetlight | Optical comms, Li-Fi | Object detection | Smart-phone auto-brightness |
| Cost | Very low (₹ 10 – 30) | Low–medium (₹ 20 – 100) | Low (₹ 15 – 60) | Medium (₹ 80 – 200) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Light-Sensing IoT Node

The figure below shows how light flows through a typical IoT node — from photon incidence all the way to cloud upload — for a *smart streetlight* or *ambient-light logger*.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["Light Source<br/>Sun or LED"] --> MED["Propagation<br/>Medium Air"]
    MED --> SNS["Light Sensor<br/>LDR or Photodiode"]
    SNS --> SIG["Analog Signal<br/>Conditioning<br/>Amplifier and Filter"]
    SIG --> ADC["ADC<br/>12 bit"]
    ADC --> MCU["Microcontroller<br/>ESP32 or Arduino"]
    MCU --> DEC["Decision Logic<br/>Threshold and Control"]
    DEC --> ACT["Actuator<br/>LED Driver or Relay"]
    DEC --> NET["Wireless Module<br/>WiFi or LoRa"]
    NET --> CLD["Cloud Dashboard<br/>ThingSpeak or AWS IoT"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The same block diagram describes the path of an **optical wireless (Li-Fi)** link — replace *Light Source* with a *modulated LED*, *Light Sensor* with a *photodiode*, and the *Decision Logic* block with a *demodulator*.

---

### 4.2 State Machine of an Automatic Smart Streetlight

The intelligent switching behaviour of a light-controlled IoT node is best captured as a finite-state machine.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> INIT
    INIT --> READSENSOR: Power On
    READSENSOR --> BRIGHT: Lux greater than 400
    READSENSOR --> DIM: Lux between 50 and 400
    READSENSOR --> DARK: Lux less than 50
    BRIGHT --> READSENSOR: LED OFF Wait 10 s
    DIM --> READSENSOR: LED 30 percent Wait 5 s
    DARK --> READSENSOR: LED 100 percent Wait 2 s
    READSENSOR --> ALERT: PIR triggered in DARK
    ALERT --> READSENSOR: Log event and notify cloud
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Always couple the LDR with a **PIR (passive-infrared)** sensor in a streetlight. Pure LDR switching fails on cloudy days; PIR provides motion context so the lamp only ramps to full brightness when *both* it is dark *and* something is moving.

---

### 4.3 Architecture of a Visible-Light Communication (Li-Fi) System

Li-Fi replaces the radio carrier with a fast-flickering LED whose on-off keying is decoded by a photodiode.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TX["Transmitter Side"]
        DATA1["Digital Data<br/>File or Stream"] --> ENC["Encoder<br/>OOK or OFDM"]
        ENC --> DRV["LED Driver Circuit"]
        DRV --> LED["High Speed LED<br/>400 to 800 nm"]
    end
    CH["Free Space<br/>Optical Channel"] --> RX
    LED --> CH
    subgraph RX["Receiver Side"]
        PD["Photodiode<br/>PIN or Avalanche"]
        PD --> AMP["Transimpedance<br/>Amplifier"]
        AMP --> DEC["Decoder<br/>OOK or OFDM"]
        DEC --> DATA2["Recovered Data"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The flicker rate is typically **1 – 100 MHz**, far above the human flicker-fusion threshold (~ 60 Hz). The eye therefore sees a *steady* light, while the photodiode sees a high-speed data stream.

---

### 4.4 Spectral Allocation Chart of Light-Based IoT Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Ultraviolet<br/>10 to 380 nm<br/>UV LEDs sterilisation"] --> B["Visible Light<br/>380 to 750 nm<br/>LiFi smart lighting"]
    B --> C["Near Infrared<br/>750 to 1400 nm<br/>IR remote control"]
    C --> D["Short Wave IR<br/>1400 to 3000 nm<br/>Optical fibre comms"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering observation:** The band-gap energy of silicon is ~1.12 eV, which corresponds to a cut-off wavelength of **$\lambda_c = \dfrac{hc}{E_g} \approx 1100$ nm**. Any photon longer than 1100 nm is *invisible* to a silicon sensor — this is why IR LEDs above 1100 nm need germanium or InGaAs detectors.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define luminous intensity and illuminance. State one SI unit for each.**

**Model Answer:**
Luminous intensity is the luminous flux emitted by a source per unit solid angle in a given direction. Its SI unit is the **candela (cd)**.
Illuminance is the luminous flux incident on a surface per unit area. Its SI unit is the **lux (lx)**, where $1\ \text{lx} = 1\ \text{lm/m}^{2}$.
The two quantities are related through the inverse square law: $E_v = \dfrac{I_v}{d^{2}}$.

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between photoconductive and photovoltaic modes of a photodiode.**

**Model Answer:**
In the **photoconductive mode**, the photodiode is reverse-biased; the output is a current proportional to incident optical power and the response is fast (nanoseconds), but dark current is non-negligible.
In the **photovoltaic mode**, the photodiode is unbiased (zero-bias) and acts as a tiny solar cell generating an open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$; the response is slower but dark current is essentially zero, making it ideal for ultra-low-power IoT energy harvesting.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Choice A]`

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a visible-light communication (Li-Fi) system. Compare Li-Fi with Wi-Fi on at least four parameters.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *An LDR has resistance $R_1 = 12\ \text{k}\Omega$ at $L_1 = 100$ lux and $R_2 = 1.5\ \text{k}\Omega$ at $L_2 = 800$ lux. Show that it obeys a power-law $R = K L^{-\gamma}$ and compute $K$ and $\gamma$. Estimate the resistance at 1 lux and at 10 000 lux.* **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Block diagram** — refer to the architecture in *Section 4.3* above (Transmitter → Channel → Receiver).

**Step 2 — Working principle** —
1. Digital data is encoded using a modulation scheme (OOK, OFDM, DCO-OFDM) by the **Encoder** block.
2. The **LED driver** converts the encoded electrical signal into a proportional current that drives a high-speed LED.
3. The LED flickers at millions of times per second — a frequency invisible to the human eye but easily detected by a PIN photodiode.
4. At the receiver, the **photodiode** converts the optical signal back into a current; a **transimpedance amplifier (TIA)** boosts it to a usable voltage.
5. The **decoder** recovers the original bit stream.

**Step 3 — Li-Fi vs Wi-Fi comparison.**

| Parameter | **Li-Fi** | **Wi-Fi** |
|---|---|---|
| Carrier | Visible/IR light | Radio (2.4 / 5 GHz) |
| Spectrum | ~ 400 THz, unlicensed, free | Crowded, regulated |
| Security | Light does not pass through walls — inherently secure | Passes through walls — sniffable |
| Bandwidth | GHz-class per LED | Tens of MHz per channel |
| Interference immunity | Immune to RF noise | Suffers from RF interference |
| Mobility | Restricted to line-of-sight | Omnidirectional |
| Health | Safe in RF-restricted zones (hospitals, aircraft) | Restricted in some zones |

##### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Assume the model $R = K L^{-\gamma}$ and take logs.**

$$
\log R = \log K - \gamma \log L
$$

**Step 2 — Use the two data points.**

For $(L_1, R_1) = (100, 12\,000)$:

$$
\log(12\,000) = \log K - \gamma \log(100)
$$

$$
4.079 = \log K - 2\gamma
$$

For $(L_2, R_2) = (800, 1\,500)$:

$$
\log(1500) = \log K - \gamma \log(800)
$$

$$
3.176 = \log K - 2.903\,\gamma
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract to eliminate $\log K$.**

$$
4.079 - 3.176 = (2.903 - 2)\gamma
$$

$$
0.903 = 0.903\,\gamma \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \gamma = 1.00
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\log K$.**

$$
\log K = 4.079 + 2 \times 1 = 6.079 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad K = 10^{6.079} \approx 1.2 \times 10^{6}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute resistance at 1 lux.**

$$
R(1) = 1.2 \times 10^{6} \times 1^{-1} = 1.2\ \text{M}\Omega
$$

**Step 6 — Compute resistance at 10 000 lux.**

$$
R(10\,000) = 1.2 \times 10^{6} \times 10\,000^{-1} = 1.2 \times 10^{6} \times 10^{-4} = 120\ \Omega
$$

#### Final Answers

$$
\boxed{\gamma \approx 1.00,\quad K \approx 1.2 \times 10^{6},\quad R(1\ \text{lx}) \approx 1.2\ \text{M}\Omega,\quad R(10\,000\ \text{lx}) \approx 120\ \Omega}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students typically lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — $\Omega$, k$\Omega$, M$\Omega$ mix-ups cost 1 mark each.
> 2. **Sign errors** — slope of $\log R$ vs $\log L$ is $-\gamma$; some students report $+\gamma$ and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Li-Fi part** — drawing a block diagram *without labels* or *without arrows showing the direction of data flow* gets 0 for the diagram; examiners specifically look for *Encoder → LED Driver → LED → Channel → Photodiode → Decoder*.

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice B]`

**(a)** *Explain the working principle of an LDR. Draw the equivalent circuit used for measurement and derive an expression for the voltage across a load resistor connected in series with the LDR.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A smart streetlight uses a luminous-intensity LED source of 2400 cd. The lamp is mounted at a height of 8 m. A light sensor placed on the road (a) directly under the lamp, (b) at a horizontal distance of 6 m, reports illuminance values. Compute both values using the inverse square law and Lambert's cosine law.* **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Working principle of an LDR.**
A Light-Dependent Resistor is a passive semiconductor device (typically CdS) whose electrical resistance **decreases** as the incident light intensity **increases**. In the dark, almost no photons strike the CdS surface, so the number of free charge carriers is small and resistance is high (M$\Omega$). In bright light, photons excite electrons from the valence band to the conduction band, increasing the carrier concentration and dropping the resistance (tens of $\Omega$).

**Step 2 — Equivalent measurement circuit (voltage-divider arrangement).**

The LDR $R_{LDR}$ is connected in series with a fixed load resistor $R_L$ across a DC supply $V_{cc}$. The output is taken across $R_L$ (or equivalently, across $R_{LDR}$; the two are complementary).

**Step 3 — Derive the output voltage.**
Applying the voltage-divider rule:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = V_{cc}\cdot \frac{R_L}{R_{LDR} + R_L}
$$

Substituting the LDR model $R_{LDR} = K L^{-\gamma}$:

$$
V_{\text{out}}(L) = \frac{V_{cc}\, R_L}{K L^{-\gamma} + R_L}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical design rule.**
To get the *largest* voltage swing over the illuminance range $[L_{\min}, L_{\max}]$, the load resistor should satisfy $R_L = \sqrt{R(L_{\min}) \cdot R(L_{\max})}$ (geometric mean), so that the divider is maximally sensitive around the geometric centre.

##### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:** $I_v = 2400$ cd, $h = 8$ m.

**Case (a) — Directly under the lamp.**
Here $\theta = 0^\circ$, $\cos\theta = 1$, $d = h = 8$ m.

$$
E_v = \frac{I_v}{d^{2}} = \frac{2400}{64} = 37.5\ \text{lx}
$$

**Case (b) — 6 m horizontal offset.**
The slant distance is:

$$
d' = \sqrt{8^{2} + 6^{2}} = \sqrt{64 + 36} = \sqrt{100} = 10\ \text{m}
$$

The angle between the lamp-to-phone line and the vertical is:

$$
\cos\theta = \frac{h}{d'} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8
$$

Therefore:

$$
E_v = \frac{I_v \cos\theta}{(d')^{2}} = \frac{2400 \times 0.8}{100} = \frac{1920}{100} = 19.2\ \text{lx}
$$

#### Final Answers

$$
\boxed{E_v(\text{under}) = 37.5\ \text{lx},\qquad E_v(\text{6 m offset}) = 19.2\ \text{lx}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — common pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using distance instead of slant distance** — students write $E_v = \dfrac{I_v \cos\theta}{h^{2}}$ using only the height; this is **wrong** when the sensor is offset.
> 2. **Forgetting $\cos\theta$** — examiners specifically test whether the student knows the difference between *inverse square law* (perpendicular) and *Lambert's cosine law* (tilted surface).
> 3. **LDR part** — describing the principle in words but **not drawing the circuit** loses at least 2 marks; examiners expect a labelled schematic with $V_{cc}$, $R_L$, $R_{LDR}$, and the output node.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist on a single A4 sheet and revise before every test.**

- [ ] **Speed of light** in vacuum: $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.
- [ ] **Wave equation**: $c = f\lambda$. Higher frequency → shorter wavelength → higher photon energy.
- [ ] **Photon energy**: $E = hf = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$. Red photon (~650 nm) ≈ 1.9 eV; violet photon (~400 nm) ≈ 3.1 eV.
- [ ] **Four photometric quantities**: flux (lm), intensity (cd), illuminance (lx), luminance (cd/m²).
- [ ] **Inverse square law**: $E_v = \dfrac{I_v}{d^{2}}$. **Lambert's cosine law**: $E_v = \dfrac{I_v \cos\theta}{d^{2}}$.
- [ ] **LDR model**: $R = K L^{-\gamma}$, with $\gamma \approx 0.7$ to $1.0$ for CdS. **Log-log straight line**.
- [ ] **Photodiode modes**: photoconductive (reverse-biased, fast) vs photovoltaic (zero-bias, low dark current).
- [ ] **Li-Fi** uses visible/IR light as carrier; **Wi-Fi** uses RF. Li-Fi is *secure* (light cannot pass through walls) and *fast* (GHz-class bandwidth per LED).
- [ ] **Si cut-off wavelength** ≈ 1100 nm; photons longer than this are invisible to silicon detectors.
- [ ] **Shannon capacity** of a Li-Fi channel: $C = B\log_{2}(1 + \text{SNR})$.
- [ ] **Smart streetlight design**: combine LDR (day/night) + PIR (motion) for energy-efficient switching.
- [ ] **Always draw block diagrams** with **labelled arrows** for IoT architecture questions — examiners award 2 marks for a clean diagram alone.
- [ ] **Always show the unit** of every photometric quantity; KTU papers routinely lose marks to missing units.
- [ ] **Common numerical traps**: mixing nm ↔ m; mixing dB ↔ linear SNR ($10^{\text{dB}/10}$); using $h$ in J·s but $E$ in eV without the $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ conversion factor.

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — LIGHT**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sound
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS AND SMART COMPUTING.
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sound

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Sound" in the IoT & Smart Computing Context?

### Formal Academic Definition
**Sound** is a mechanical longitudinal pressure wave that propagates through an elastic medium (solid, liquid, or gas) by means of adiabatic compression and rarefaction of particles. In the domain of **Internet of Things (IoT)** and **Smart Computing**, sound is treated as a *digitally captured, processed, and networked acoustic data stream* that enables *context-aware interaction* between cyber-physical systems and their environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCGT602 – Module 3):**
> Sound is one of the primary *ambient sensing modalities* (alongside vision, temperature, motion, and chemical cues) that allow an IoT node to *perceive* its environment. Mastering its physics, digital representation, and on-device processing pipeline is a **mandatory Course Outcome (CO3)** requirement for smart sensor integration.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Human Ear vs. The IoT Microphone

| Human Hearing System | IoT Acoustic System | Functional Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| Outer ear (pinna) collects pressure waves | MEMS microphone diaphragm collects pressure waves | Acoustic capture |
| Eardrum converts air pressure to mechanical vibration | MEMS diaphragm converts pressure to capacitance change | Transduction |
| Cochlea converts vibration to neural spikes | Analog Front End (AFE) converts capacitance to voltage | Analog signal generation |
| Auditory nerve transmits to brain | ADC + MCU/SoC digitizes and processes bits | Digital encoding |
| Brain interprets meaning | Edge AI / Cloud ML model interprets meaning | Cognition layer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition:** A microphone in an IoT device is *literally* a digital "ear." Just as a human hears a car horn and reacts, an IoT sensor hears the same horn, converts it to a digital waveform, runs an algorithm, and triggers an action (e.g., a smart traffic light turning green for an emergency vehicle).

## 1.2 Fundamental Acoustic Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency | $f$ | Hertz (**Hz**) | Number of pressure oscillations per second |
| Wavelength | $\lambda$ | meter (**m**) | Spatial distance of one full cycle |
| Period | $T$ | second (**s**) | Time taken for one full oscillation |
| Amplitude (Pressure) | $P$ | Pascal (**Pa**) | Peak deviation from ambient atmospheric pressure |
| Sound Intensity | $I$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | Acoustic power per unit area |
| Sound Pressure Level | $L_p$ | decibel (**dB SPL**) | Logarithmic loudness measure |
| Speed of Sound | $c$ | m/s | Phase velocity of the wave in the medium |

## 1.3 Classification of Sound by Frequency

> [!NOTE]
> **Audible Band (Human Hearing):** Approximately **20 Hz to 20 kHz**. Most consumer-grade IoT microphones (e.g., on smart speakers) are designed to capture this band with high fidelity.

- **Infrasound** $(f < 20\ \text{Hz})$ — Used in industrial IoT for monitoring volcanic activity, structural vibration, and heavy-machinery health.
- **Audible Sound** $(20\ \text{Hz} \le f \le 20{,}000\ \text{Hz})$ — The primary band for voice assistants, smart home control, and acoustic event detection.
- **Ultrasound** $(f > 20\ \text{kHz})$ — Used in medical IoT, ultrasonic range finders, and short-range indoor positioning (beaconing).

## 1.4 Why Sound Matters in Smart Computing

1. **Voice User Interface (VUI):** Smart speakers (Alexa, Google Home) rely entirely on acoustic sensing.
2. **Industrial Predictive Maintenance:** Acoustic emission monitoring detects bearing failures before they occur.
3. **Environmental Monitoring:** Smart city noise-pollution sensors map decibel levels in real time.
4. **Healthcare Wearables:** Cough/snore analysis for sleep apnea detection.
5. **Security:** Gunshot, glass-break, and scream detection in smart surveillance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physics of Sound Propagation

A sound wave is a **longitudinal mechanical wave**. Unlike electromagnetic waves, it *cannot* travel through a vacuum — it requires a material medium whose particles can be displaced and restored by elastic forces.

### Step-by-Step Wave Logic

- **Step 1 — Disturbance Generation:** A vibrating source (speaker cone, vocal cord, machinery housing) imparts kinetic energy to adjacent air molecules.
- **Step 2 — Compression:** These molecules push into their neighbors, creating a region of higher-than-ambient pressure (a *compression*).
- **Step 3 — Rarefaction:** The molecules then rebound, creating a region of lower-than-ambient pressure (a *rarefaction*).
- **Step 4 — Wave Propagation:** The pressure disturbance propagates outward at the **speed of sound $c$**, with each molecule oscillating only microscopically around its equilibrium position.
- **Step 5 — Reception:** A microphone diaphragm (or human eardrum) responds to these alternating pressure changes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the 'Why':** Energy is *transported* by the wave, but the *medium particles themselves* merely oscillate. This is why sound cannot propagate through the vacuum of space — there are no particles to oscillate.

## 2.2 Speed of Sound in Different Media

The speed of sound depends on the medium's **bulk modulus $B$** (resistance to compression) and **density $\rho$**:

$$c = \sqrt{\dfrac{B}{\rho}}$$

| Medium | Approx. Speed $c$ (m/s) | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Air (20 °C) | **343 m/s** | Reference for indoor acoustic IoT |
| Water (20 °C) | **1480 m/s** | Underwater acoustic sensor networks |
| Steel | **~5960 m/s** | Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) via guided waves |
| Vacuum | **0 (does not propagate)** | Confirms mechanical-wave nature |

> [!NOTE]
> **Temperature Correction (Air):** A useful rule of thumb is $c \approx 331 + 0.6\,T$, where $T$ is in °C. This is critical when designing outdoor acoustic ranging systems (e.g., parking sensors) because temperature drift directly biases distance estimates.

## 2.3 The Decibel (dB) Scale — Logarithmic Loudness

Human hearing spans an enormous dynamic range — from the threshold of hearing (~20 µPa) to the threshold of pain (~200 Pa), a factor of **10,000,000× in pressure**. A linear scale is impractical, so we use a **logarithmic measure**:

### Sound Pressure Level (SPL)

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_{\text{ref}}}\right)\ \text{dB}$$

where $P_{\text{ref}} = 20\ \mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa}$ is the standard reference pressure (threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz).

### Sound Intensity Level

$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right)\ \text{dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "20" vs. "10" rule:** Because intensity is proportional to the *square* of pressure ($I \propto P^2$), the multiplier is **20** for pressure ratios and **10** for intensity ratios. This is one of the most common KTU exam traps.

## 2.4 Digital Representation of Sound — The IoT Bridge

An IoT node must convert the *analog* acoustic waveform into a *digital* bitstream. This happens in three stages:

### Stage 1 — Anti-Aliasing Filter
A low-pass filter removes all frequency content above $f_s / 2$ to satisfy the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**:

$$f_{\text{Nyquist}} = \dfrac{f_s}{2}$$

If the original signal contains energy at $f > f_s / 2$, it will *alias* (fold back) and corrupt the digitized waveform.

### Stage 2 — Sampling
The filtered analog signal is sampled at a rate $f_s$ (samples/second):

$$f_s = \dfrac{1}{\Delta t}$$

Common IoT audio sample rates:

| Application | Typical $f_s$ | Bandwidth Captured |
|---|---|---|
| Voice call (PSTN quality) | 8 kHz | 4 kHz |
| Wideband speech (VoIP) | 16 kHz | 8 kHz |
| Music / Smart Speaker | 44.1 kHz or 48 kHz | ~20–24 kHz |
| Industrial ultrasound | 192 kHz+ | 96 kHz+ |

### Stage 3 — Quantization
Each sample is rounded to the nearest of $2^n$ discrete levels, where $n$ is the **bit depth**:

$$\text{Number of levels} = 2^{n}$$

**Quantization Step Size (Resolution):**

$$Q = \dfrac{\text{Full-Scale Range}}{2^{n}}$$

**Quantization Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SQNR):**

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} \approx 6.02\,n + 1.76$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A 16-bit audio system (CD quality) yields $\text{SQNR} \approx 6.02(16) + 1.76 = 98.08\ \text{dB}$. Each additional bit contributes ~6 dB of dynamic range.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $c = f \lambda$ | Wave equation linking speed, frequency, wavelength | m/s, Hz, m |
| 2 | $c = \sqrt{B/\rho}$ | Speed of sound in a medium | m/s, Pa, kg/m³ |
| 3 | $c_{\text{air}} \approx 331 + 0.6T$ | Speed of sound in air vs. temperature | m/s, °C |
| 4 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(P / P_{\text{ref}})$ | Sound Pressure Level | dB SPL |
| 5 | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I / I_{\text{ref}})$ | Sound Intensity Level | dB |
| 6 | $P_{\text{ref}} = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ | Threshold of human hearing reference | Pa |
| 7 | $f_s \ge 2 f_{\text{max}}$ | Nyquist sampling criterion | Hz |
| 8 | $Q = \text{FSR} / 2^{n}$ | Quantization step size | V (or Pa) |
| 9 | $\text{SQNR} \approx 6.02n + 1.76$ | Theoretical dynamic range of an $n$-bit ADC | dB |
| 10 | $I = P^2 / (\rho c)$ | Acoustic intensity from pressure | W/m² |
| 11 | $\text{PSD} = \vert X(f) \vert^{2} / \Delta f$ | Power Spectral Density (audio feature for ML) | W/Hz |
| 12 | $\Delta L = 6\ \text{dB} \Rightarrow 2\times P$ | Doubling pressure raises SPL by 6 dB | dB |
| 13 | $\Delta L = 10\ \text{dB} \Rightarrow 10\times I$ | Tenfold intensity raises SIL by 10 dB | dB |
| 14 | $f_{\text{doppler}} = f_0 (c \pm v_r)/(c \pm v_s)$ | Doppler shift (relevant to acoustic ranging) | Hz |
| 15 | $\text{THD} = \sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} P_h^2} / P_1$ | Total Harmonic Distortion (audio quality metric) | dimensionless (or %) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Acoustic Ranging (Ultrasonic HC-SR04):** $d = (c \cdot \Delta t) / 2$ where $\Delta t$ is the round-trip time of an ultrasonic chirp. This is the foundational principle behind IoT parking sensors, drone altimeters, and water-level monitors.
- **Voice Activity Detection (VAD):** Edge devices compute Short-Time Energy (STE) and Zero-Crossing Rate (ZCR) to decide whether the user is speaking — *before* invoking expensive cloud speech recognition. This is critical for power-constrained IoT endpoints.
- **Acoustic Scene Classification (ASC):** Models like YAMNet and PANNs classify urban sounds (siren, dog bark, jackhammer) for smart-city surveillance — directly grounded in the *frequency-domain feature extraction* concepts above.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Speed of Sound from First Principles

Consider a long cylindrical air column of cross-sectional area $A$ and density $\rho$. A piston at one end is displaced inward by a small distance $\Delta x$, compressing the gas adiabatically.

**Step 1 — Compute the pressure change (adiabatic process):**

$$dP = -B \dfrac{dV}{V} = -B \dfrac{(-A\,\Delta x)}{A\,L} = \dfrac{B\,\Delta x}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law to the gas column:**

The net force on the gas is $F = -A\,dP$ and the mass of the gas is $m = \rho A L$.

$$F = m\,a \quad\Longrightarrow\quad -A\,dP = \rho A L \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}(\Delta x)}{dt^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $dP$ from Step 1:**

$$-A \cdot \dfrac{B\,\Delta x}{L} = \rho A L \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}(\Delta x)}{dt^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $A$ and rearrange into the standard wave equation:**

$$\dfrac{d^{2}(\Delta x)}{dt^{2}} = -\dfrac{B}{\rho L^{2}} \cdot \Delta x \cdot L = -\dfrac{B}{\rho} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta x}{L^{2}}$$

The general solution to this second-order differential equation is sinusoidal:

$$\Delta x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega t)$$

with angular frequency $\omega^{2} = B / \rho$. Therefore:

$$c = \omega / k \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad c = \sqrt{\dfrac{B}{\rho}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For air at 20 °C, $B \approx 1.42 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa}$ and $\rho \approx 1.204\ \text{kg/m}^3$, giving $c = \sqrt{1.42 \times 10^{5} / 1.204} \approx 343.4\ \text{m/s}$, which matches the experimental value perfectly.

## 3.2 Derivation: Quantization SNR of an Ideal ADC

Assume a full-scale sinusoidal input $V(t) = V_{\text{FS}} \sin(2\pi f t)$.

**Step 1 — RMS signal power:**

$$P_{\text{signal}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{FS}}^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Quantization noise model:**
For an ideal $n$-bit ADC with uniformly distributed quantization error in $[-Q/2,\ +Q/2]$, the mean-square quantization noise is:

$$P_{\text{noise}} = \dfrac{Q^{2}}{12} = \dfrac{1}{12}\left(\dfrac{2 V_{\text{FS}}}{2^{n}}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{V_{\text{FS}}^{2}}{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute SQNR ratio:**

$$\text{SQNR} = \dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{FS}}^{2}/2}{V_{\text{FS}}^{2} / (3 \cdot 2^{2n})} = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to decibels:**

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20 n \log_{10}(2)$$

Since $\log_{10}(1.5) \approx 0.176$ and $20 \log_{10}(2) \approx 6.0206$:

$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} \approx 6.02\,n + 1.76}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the +1.76?** It is a mathematical residue of the $\frac{3}{2}$ factor that arises from the assumption of a sinusoidal (rather than DC) input. For a DC input, the formula becomes $6.02\,n$. Always quote the sinusoidal version for audio applications.

## 3.3 Derivation: Range Resolution of an Ultrasonic IoT Sensor

A common KTU-style problem: An ultrasonic sensor emits a 40 kHz chirp. The on-board timer measures the round-trip echo time $\Delta t$. Derive the minimum distinguishable range.

**Step 1 — Relationship between range and echo time:**

$$d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Quantization of $\Delta t$ by the timer:**
Suppose the timer has a clock period $T_{\text{clk}} = 1\ \mu\text{s}$ (i.e., 1 MHz timer):

$$\Delta t_{\text{min}} = T_{\text{clk}} = 1\ \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Minimum resolvable distance:**

$$d_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{343\ \text{m/s} \cdot 1\ \mu\text{s}}{2} = \dfrac{343 \times 10^{-6}}{2}\ \text{m} = 1.715 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m} \approx 0.17\ \text{mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** Higher clock frequencies (e.g., 100 MHz timer → 10 ns period) yield sub-millimeter range resolution — a key design tradeoff in autonomous-vehicle ultrasonic arrays.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Problem Templates

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Formula Chain |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wavelength in air | $f$, $T$ | $\lambda$ | $\lambda = c/f$ |
| SPL computation | $P$ (Pa) | $L_p$ (dB) | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(P / 20\mu\text{Pa})$ |
| Doubling loudness | $L_{p1}$ | $L_{p2}$ | $L_{p2} = L_{p1} + 6$ dB |
| Sampling rate design | $f_{\text{max}}$ | $f_s$ | $f_s \ge 2 f_{\text{max}}$ |
| Bit depth for target SQNR | Target dB | $n$ | $n = (\text{SQNR} - 1.76) / 6.02$ |
| Ultrasonic ranging | $\Delta t$ | $d$ | $d = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$ |
| Temperature-corrected $c$ | $T$ (°C) | $c$ | $c = 331 + 0.6T$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Acoustic Signal Processing Pipeline in an IoT Node

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SOURCE: Voice, Machine, Environment] --> B[ACOUSTIC CHANNEL: Air, Water, Solid]
    B --> C[MEMS MICROPHONE: Analog Transducer]
    C --> D[ANTI-ALIAS FILTER: LPF at fs/2]
    D --> E[ADC: Sampling and Quantization]
    E --> F[DIGITAL BUFFER: RAM or Flash]
    F --> G[EDGE DSP: Feature Extraction MFCC FFT]
    G --> H{DECISION GATE}
    H -- Recognized Wake Word --> I[WAKE LOGIC: Activate Cloud Link]
    H -- Background Noise --> J[SLEEP MODE: Power Save]
    I --> K[CLOUD ML: ASR NLU Intent]
    K --> L[ACTUATOR COMMAND: Smart Bulb Speaker Motor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Acoustic data flows **left → right** through the IoT pipeline. The *Decision Gate* (H) is a critical power-saving technique — the MCU remains in deep sleep until the edge DSP detects a wake word, dramatically extending battery life of voice-enabled IoT devices.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Smart Acoustic IoT System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PERCEPTION_LAYER["PERCEPTION LAYER - SENSORS"]
        sensor1["MEMS MIC 1"]
        sensor2["ULTRASONIC TX RX"]
        sensor3["GEOPHONE VIBRATION"]
    end
    subgraph NETWORK_LAYER["NETWORK LAYER - TRANSPORT"]
        net1["BLE 5.0 LOW POWER"]
        net2["WI-FI 6 HIGH BANDWIDTH"]
        net3["LORA WIDE AREA"]
    end
    subgraph PROCESSING_LAYER["PROCESSING LAYER - COMPUTE"]
        proc1["EDGE MCU PREPROCESSING"]
        proc2["CLOUD AI ASR CLASSIFIER"]
        proc3["DIGITAL TWIN STATE"]
    end
    subgraph APPLICATION_LAYER["APPLICATION LAYER - ACTION"]
        app1["SMART HOME VOICE CONTROL"]
        app2["NOISE MAP DASHBOARD"]
        app3["PREDICTIVE MAINTENANCE ALERT"]
    end
    sensor1 --> net1
    sensor2 --> net2
    sensor3 --> net3
    net1 --> proc1
    net2 --> proc2
    net3 --> proc1
    proc1 --> proc2
    proc2 --> proc3
    proc3 --> app1
    proc3 --> app2
    proc3 --> app3
```

## 4.3 Frequency-Band Decision Flowchart for Acoustic IoT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([INCOMING ACOUSTIC EVENT]) --> q1{What is dominant frequency band?}
    q1 -- Less than 20 Hz --> path1[INFRASOUND: Seismic and Industrial Vibration IoT]
    q1 -- 20 Hz to 20 kHz --> q2{Speech or non-speech?}
    q2 -- Speech --> path2[VOICE IoT: Smart Speaker, VPA, Voice Biometric]
    q2 -- Non-speech --> path3[GENERAL AUDIO: Smart City Noise, Cough Detection, Gunshot Classifier]
    q1 -- More than 20 kHz --> path4[ULTRASOUND: Range Finder, Medical Imaging, Indoor Positioning]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Schematic: Digital Audio Bit Depth vs. Quality

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    b8["8 BIT: 256 LEVELS TELEPHONY OLD GAMES"] --> b12["12 BIT: 4096 LEVELS BASIC VOICE RECORDING"]
    b12 --> b16["16 BIT: 65536 LEVELS CD QUALITY SMART SPEAKER"]
    b16 --> b24["24 BIT: 16.7 MILLION LEVELS STUDIO AUDIO PROFESSIONAL IoT"]
    b24 --> b32["32 BIT FLOAT: DYNAMIC HEADROOM ML TRAINING DATASETS"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading:** Each step in the chain above represents a *fourfold* increase in storage size. A 1-minute mono recording at 48 kHz / 32-bit float consumes $\approx 11.5$ MB, while at 8 kHz / 8-bit it consumes only $\approx 0.48$ MB. This directly drives IoT edge-storage and uplink bandwidth design.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> Module 3 questions typically appear as **Part A (2 marks)** in the Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE) and as **Part B (14 marks)** in the End Semester Exam (ESE) with internal choice. Two-part ESE questions of 7+7 marks are the standard pattern.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2–3 Marks)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`
**Define Sound Pressure Level (SPL). An acoustic sensor measures a pressure of 0.2 Pa. Compute the SPL in dB. (Take $P_{\text{ref}} = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$.)**

**Model Answer:**
SPL is the logarithmic measure of the RMS sound pressure $P$ relative to a reference pressure $P_{\text{ref}} = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ (the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz).

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_{\text{ref}}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{0.2}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(10{,}000)$$

$$L_p = 20 \times 4 = \mathbf{80\ dB\ SPL}$$

(Equivalent to heavy city traffic — relevant to smart-city noise monitoring IoT.)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional 2]`
**What is the Nyquist sampling theorem? An IoT microphone must capture frequencies up to 8 kHz. What is the minimum sampling rate?**

**Model Answer:**
The Nyquist sampling theorem states that to reconstruct a band-limited signal without aliasing, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must be at least twice the highest frequency component $f_{\text{max}}$ present in the signal.

$$f_s \ge 2 f_{\text{max}} = 2 \times 8\ \text{kHz} = \mathbf{16\ \text{kHz}}$$

This is why wideband VoIP (e.g., WhatsApp voice messages) uses a 16 kHz sample rate.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Following KTU ESE convention, each Part B sub-question carries **7 marks**. Students are expected to write a *definition (1 mark) + explanation (2 marks) + diagram (2 marks) + derivation/calculation (2 marks)* unless otherwise stated.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q.A: (a)** Explain the fundamental physics of sound wave propagation. Derive the relationship between speed of sound, bulk modulus, and density of the medium. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the complete digital audio acquisition pipeline of an IoT acoustic sensor node, from physical sound pressure to digital bits stored in memory. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Sound is a mechanical longitudinal pressure wave that propagates through an elastic medium via alternating compressions and rarefactions of particles.

**Derivation (4 marks):** [Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes]
For a gas column of length $L$, area $A$, and density $\rho$, the adiabatic pressure change produced by a piston displacement $\Delta x$ is $dP = (B\,\Delta x)/L$. Applying Newton's second law to the gas mass $m = \rho A L$:

$$-A\,dP = \rho A L \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}(\Delta x)}{dt^{2}}$$

Substituting $dP$ and rearranging:

$$\dfrac{d^{2}(\Delta x)}{dt^{2}} = -\dfrac{B}{\rho L^{2}} \cdot \Delta x \cdot L$$

The wave solution $\Delta x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega t)$ gives:

$$\omega^{2} = \dfrac{B}{\rho} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{c = \sqrt{\dfrac{B}{\rho}}}$$

**Tabulated values (2 marks):**

| Medium | Bulk Modulus $B$ (Pa) | Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $c$ (m/s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air (20 °C) | $1.42 \times 10^{5}$ | 1.204 | **343** |
| Water | $2.2 \times 10^{9}$ | 1000 | **1480** |
| Steel | $1.6 \times 10^{11}$ | 7850 | **~5960** |

**Conclusion:** Sound travels *faster* in stiffer (high-$B$) and *lighter* (low-$\rho$) media.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** [Refer to Section 4.1 — the mermaid pipeline]
The pipeline is: **Source → Acoustic Channel → MEMS Mic → Anti-Alias LPF → ADC → Memory → Edge DSP → Decision**.

**Explanation (4 marks):**
1. **MEMS Microphone:** A pressure-sensitive diaphragm converts acoustic pressure variations into a proportional analog voltage. Typical sensitivity: $-42\ \text{dBV/Pa}$.
2. **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** A low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c = f_s/2$ removes frequencies that would alias during sampling.
3. **ADC:** Samples the filtered analog signal at $f_s$ (e.g., 16 kHz for voice) and quantizes each sample to $n$ bits (e.g., 16 bits).
4. **Memory:** Bitstream is buffered in RAM or streamed to flash storage. Data rate $= f_s \times n \times \text{channels}$. For 16 kHz, 16-bit mono: $16{,}000 \times 16 = 256\ \text{kbps}$.
5. **Edge DSP:** Computes features like MFCCs (Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients) for downstream ML classification.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

**Q.B: (a)** An IoT-based noise pollution monitoring system uses a microphone with the following specifications: Sensitivity $S = -46\ \text{dBV/Pa}$, supply $V_{\text{DD}} = 3.3\ \text{V}$, and 24-bit ADC. Compute (i) the analog output voltage for a 70 dB SPL input, and (ii) the theoretical SQNR in dB. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the role of sound sensing in three distinct smart-computing applications, with one example for each. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (Numerical)

**Given:**
- Sound Pressure Level: $L_p = 70\ \text{dB SPL}$
- Microphone Sensitivity: $S = -46\ \text{dBV/Pa}$ (i.e., 0 dBV output per Pa = $-46$ dBV → $V/P = 10^{-46/20} = 0.00501\ \text{V/Pa}$)
- ADC bit depth: $n = 24$

**Step (i) — Compute acoustic pressure $P$:**

$$P = P_{\text{ref}} \times 10^{L_p / 20} = (2 \times 10^{-5}) \times 10^{70/20} = (2 \times 10^{-5}) \times 10^{3.5}$$

$$P = 2 \times 10^{-5} \times 3162.28 = 0.0632\ \text{Pa}$$

**Step (ii) — Compute analog output voltage:**

$$V_{\text{out}} = S \times P = 0.00501\ \text{V/Pa} \times 0.0632\ \text{Pa} = 3.17 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{V} = \mathbf{0.317\ mV}$$

> This is a *very small* signal — the analog front-end must use a low-noise instrumentation amplifier (e.g., 60 dB gain) before the ADC.

**Step (iii) — Compute theoretical SQNR:**

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\,n + 1.76 = 6.02(24) + 1.76 = 144.48 + 1.76 = \mathbf{146.24\ dB}$$

> Note: This is the *ADC's* theoretical limit. Real microphones have harmonic distortion and self-noise that cap usable dynamic range at ~120 dB.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (Application)

| # | Application Domain | Sound-Sensing Role | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Smart Home / VUI** | Captures user voice commands via always-on wake-word detection | Amazon Echo with "Alexa" wake-word on edge DSP |
| 2 | **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | Detects bearing wear, cavitation, and gas leaks via acoustic emission monitoring | SKF Enlight Procollect IIoT sensor nodes |
| 3 | **Smart Healthcare** | Continuous cough/snore analysis for sleep apnea and respiratory disease monitoring | ResMed AirSense wearable with on-device ML |
| 4 | **Smart Environment** | Real-time noise-pollution mapping for urban planning and noise-ordinance enforcement | Libelium Waspmote Smart Cities sensor |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up the 10 vs. 20 in dB formulas** — Pressure uses 20, intensity uses 10. Forgetting this loses 2 full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units on the dBV sensitivity** — Always state whether sensitivity is in dBV/Pa, dBFS/Pa, or dB SPL/Pa.
> 3. **Omitting the anti-aliasing filter step** — A 1-mark deduction for calling ADC input "directly from microphone."
> 4. **Confusing $f_s$ (sample rate) with $f$ (signal frequency)** — Use distinct symbols in derivations.
> 5. **Drawing a waveform instead of a block diagram** — When the question says "block diagram," full marks require functional blocks with arrows, not a sine-wave sketch.
> 6. **Not mentioning the 20 µPa reference pressure** — A definition of SPL that omits $P_{\text{ref}}$ is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Print & Pin):**

- [ ] **Sound** = *mechanical* longitudinal wave → *cannot* travel in vacuum; needs a medium.
- [ ] **Three key medium properties:** bulk modulus $B$, density $\rho$, and the derived $c = \sqrt{B/\rho}$.
- [ ] **Audible band:** 20 Hz – 20 kHz. Reference pressure $P_{\text{ref}} = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$.
- [ ] **SPL formula uses "20":** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(P/P_{\text{ref}})$; intensity uses "10."
- [ ] **Doubling pressure** → **+6 dB**. **Tenfold intensity** → **+10 dB**.
- [ ] **Speed of sound in air (20 °C)** = **343 m/s**; in water ≈ 1480 m/s; in steel ≈ 5960 m/s.
- [ ] **Temperature correction:** $c_{\text{air}} = 331 + 0.6T$ m/s, with $T$ in °C.
- [ ] **Nyquist criterion:** $f_s \ge 2 f_{\text{max}}$; otherwise aliasing corrupts the digital signal.
- [ ] **Anti-aliasing LPF cutoff** must be at $f_s / 2$ *before* the ADC.
- [ ] **Quantization levels** $= 2^n$; **step size** $Q = \text{FSR} / 2^n$.
- [ ] **SQNR formula:** $6.02 n + 1.76$ dB (sinusoidal input); $6.02 n$ dB (DC input).
- [ ] **Ultrasonic ranging:** $d = (c \cdot \Delta t) / 2$ — round-trip time / 2.
- [ ] **Doppler effect** in acoustic ranging: $f' = f_0 (c \pm v_r)/(c \pm v_s)$.
- [ ] **Audio features for ML:** MFCC, Zero-Crossing Rate (ZCR), Short-Time Energy (STE), Spectral Centroid, Spectral Roll-off.
- [ ] **Power Spectral Density (PSD):** $\text{PSD} = \vert X(f) \vert^{2} / \Delta f$ — used to identify dominant tones in machinery faults.
- [ ] **MEMS microphone** is the *standard* transducer for IoT — small, low-power, and integrable on-chip.
- [ ] **Edge audio processing** (MFCC + tinyML) reduces cloud bandwidth and enables wake-word detection at <1 mW power.
- [ ] **Three IoT layers of sound:** Perception (sensor) → Network (BLE/Wi-Fi/LoRa) → Application (VUI / ASC / SHM).
- [ ] **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** is a key audio-quality metric for IoT microphone datasheets.
- [ ] **Healthcare relevance:** Cough, snore, and respiratory-rate monitoring via acoustic IoT is a high-growth area.
- [ ] **Common exam trap:** Students confuse $f_s$ with the signal frequency $f$ in the wave equation $c = f\lambda$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PIR Motion Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS AND SMART COMPUTING.
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** PIR Motion Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Passive Infrared (PIR) Motion Sensor** is an electronic transducer that detects physical movement of an emitting body (typically a human or animal) by measuring the change in infrared radiation levels across its field of view. The qualifier *Passive* is critical — unlike radar or ultrasonic active sensors, a PIR sensor **does not emit any form of radiation**; it solely functions as a receiver of black-body infrared emissions in the **8 µm to 14 µm** wavelength band, which corresponds to the peak spectral emissivity of the human body at normal skin temperature (~**33 °C to 35 °C**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** PIR sensors are classified under the broader category of *non-contact motion transducers* and are foundational to occupancy-aware IoT ecosystems such as smart lighting, intruder alarms, and HVAC automation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Invisible Shadow Watcher"**
> Imagine standing in a dark room. Your body, being warmer than the walls, naturally radiates heat. Now, picture a security guard who cannot see visible light but has a special pair of goggles that lets them see the heat signature (thermal silhouette) of every warm object. If a person walks in front of a previously stationary hot object (or vice versa), the guard immediately notices that the "heat pattern" in front of them just changed. The PIR sensor acts as exactly that thermal goggles-and-guard combination — silently watching the room for any sudden thermal (infrared) change, and firing an alert the moment a "shadow of heat" sweeps across its lens.

In essence, a PIR sensor does not detect the human itself; it detects the **disturbance** the human creates in the otherwise stable infrared landscape of a space.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Spectral Response Range** | **8 µm – 14 µm** | Matches human body black-body radiation peak |
| **Operating Voltage (Vcc)** | **3.3 V – 5 V DC** | Compatible with most MCU/SoC logic rails |
| **Typical Detection Range** | **3 m – 7 m** | Tunable via Fresnel lens curvature |
| **Detection Angle (Field of View)** | **100° – 180°** | Determined by Fresnel lens geometry |
| **Output Signal Type** | **Digital (HIGH/LOW)** | TTL-compatible logic-level trigger |
| **Quiescent Current (Iddq)** | **< 50 µA** | Enables battery-backed IoT deployments |
| **Response Time (t_resp)** | **< 1 s** | Suitable for occupancy-class applications |

> [!NOTE]
> The "quiescent current" of **< 50 µA** is what makes the PIR sensor the de-facto motion transducer for energy-harvesting and coin-cell powered IoT nodes, as it can remain in low-power standby for **12+ months** on a single CR2032 cell under duty-cycled operation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Pyroelectric Effect — Physical Foundation

The heart of every PIR sensor is a **pyroelectric crystal** (typically Lithium Tantalate — LiTaO₃, or Lead Zirconate Titanate — PZT). The pyroelectric effect is the linear electromechanical response of certain anisotropic crystals to a change in temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Two Crystals? (Differential Configuration)**
> A single pyroelectric element is useless by itself because it would respond identically to slow, uniform ambient temperature drift (e.g., sunlight warming the walls) and to genuine motion. To reject this common-mode noise, every PIR module contains **two identical sensing elements wired in series opposition** (a differential pair). This is the single most board-exam-relevant structural detail.

The pyroelectric current generated by a single element is governed by:

$$i_{pyro} = p \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dt}$$

Where:
- $p$ = pyroelectric coefficient of the crystal (C·m⁻²·K⁻¹)
- $A$ = electrode area of the sensing element (m²)
- $\frac{dT}{dt}$ = rate of change of temperature at the sensor surface (K·s⁻¹)

> **Engineering Insight:** Note that the output is proportional to the **rate** of temperature change, not the absolute temperature. A perfectly still person will eventually cause a *constant* IR flux and hence *zero* current — this is why a stationary intruder can in theory evade a PIR sensor. This subtle behaviour is a common KTU short-answer trap.

## 2.2 Signal Conditioning Chain (Step-by-Step)

1. **Optical Collection (Fresnel Lens):** Segmented Fresnel lens array focuses incoming IR onto the two pyroelectric slots. The lens segmentation creates multiple alternating "hot/cold" zones so that motion across the field of view causes alternating IR incidence on the two elements.
2. **Pyroelectric Transduction:** Each element converts IR flux into a micro-ampere-scale differential current.
3. **High-Gain Op-Amp LPF/HPF (Typically BISS0001 or AS312):** The raw differential signal is amplified (~10⁴ to 10⁶ V/V) and band-pass filtered between **~0.3 Hz and ~10 Hz** to reject DC thermal drift and high-frequency electrical noise.
4. **Schmitt Trigger Comparator:** A built-in window comparator converts the analog envelope to a clean digital HIGH/LOW logic level.
5. **Retriggerable Monostable Output:** The output pulse is held HIGH for a user-programmable duration $T_{hold}$ (typically **2 s to 200 s**) set by an external RC timing network.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are board-tested and mathematically exact. Memorise the symbols and the sign convention.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Variables & Units | Boundary / Validity Conditions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Pyroelectric Current** | $i_{pyro} = p \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dt}$ | $p$: C·m⁻²·K⁻¹, $A$: m², $T$: K | Valid for $T < T_{Curie}$ |
| 2 | **Stefan-Boltzmann Irradiance** | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ | $\varepsilon$: emissivity, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ | Black/grey body in far-field |
| 3 | **Net IR Flux at Element** | $\Delta \Phi = \Phi_{target} - \Phi_{background}$ | W | Differential pair signal |
| 4 | **RC Time Constant** | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | s | Sets LPF / monostable timing |
| 5 | **Hold-Time (Monostable)** | $T_{hold} \approx 1.1 \cdot R_{t} C_{t}$ | s | Standard BISS0001 approximation |
| 6 | **Field of View Half-Angle** | $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{d}{2f}\right)$ | rad / deg | Thin-lens paraxial approx. |
| 7 | **Detection Range (Lens-limited)** | $R_{max} \approx \frac{h_{lens}}{2 \tan(\theta)}$ | m | Where $h_{lens}$ is window height |
| 8 | **Sensitivity Threshold** | $S_{min} = \frac{V_{noise}}{\vert G_{amp} \vert}$ | V | $G_{amp}$ ≈ 10⁴ – 10⁶ |
| 9 | **Power Budget (Battery Life)** | $L_{bat} = \frac{C_{bat} \cdot V_{bat}}{I_{avg} \cdot V_{bat}} = \frac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}}$ | hours | For duty-cycled PIR node |
| 10 | **SNR after Fresnel Lens** | $SNR_{out} = SNR_{in} + G_{lens}(\lambda)$ | dB | $G_{lens}$ is wavelength-dependent gain |

> **Real-World Utility:** The differential pyroelectric configuration is the exact same principle used in **thermal imaging cameras (FLIR Lepton)** and **astronomical infrared telescopes (Spitzer / JWST)**, scaled up to focal-plane arrays of thousands of pixels. The PIR sensor is, in effect, the two-pixel "ancestor" of every modern thermal camera.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Estimating PIR Detection Range Using Stefan-Boltzmann Law

We wish to derive the maximum theoretical range at which a PIR sensor can detect a human subject against a uniform room-temperature background.

### 3.1.1 Setup & Assumptions

- Human body treated as a grey body with emissivity $\varepsilon_h = 0.98$ and surface temperature $T_h = 306$ K (≈ 33 °C).
- Background treated as a black body at $T_b = 293$ K (≈ 20 °C).
- Lens effective collection area $A_{lens} = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ m² (i.e. a 2 cm × 2 cm window).
- Sensitivity threshold of the conditioned signal $S_{min} = 50$ mV.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Spectral irradiance of the human target at distance $d$:**

By the inverse-square law applied to a Lambertian (diffuse) emitter, the irradiance at the lens is:

$$E_{target}(d) = \frac{\varepsilon_h \sigma T_h^{4} \cdot A_{human}}{2\pi d^{2}}$$

We take $A_{human} \approx 1.5$ m² as the effective radiating torso area.

**Step 2 — Background irradiance (steady-state, subtractive in differential pair):**

$$E_{bg} = \varepsilon_b \sigma T_b^{4}$$

For $\varepsilon_b \approx 0.95$:

$$E_{bg} = 0.95 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (293)^{4}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
(293)^{4} &= 293^{2} \times 293^{2} \\
&= 85{,}849 \times 85{,}849 \\
&\approx 7.37 \times 10^{9} \;\text{K}^{4}
\end{aligned}$$

$$E_{bg} = 0.95 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 7.37 \times 10^{9}$$

$$E_{bg} \approx 397 \; \text{W/m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Net differential flux captured by the lens:**

$$\Delta \Phi = (E_{target} - E_{bg}) \cdot A_{lens}$$

Since the *differential pair rejects the DC background term*, only the *transient* $\Delta \Phi$ contributes to the signal — but for a moving target we approximate the worst case where the target fully substitutes the background in one slot:

$$\Delta \Phi \approx (\varepsilon_h T_h^{4} - \varepsilon_b T_b^{4}) \sigma A_{lens}$$

**Step 4 — Solving for range $d$:**

Setting the induced signal equal to the threshold $S_{min}$ (after amplification by $G_{amp} = 10^{5}$):

$$G_{amp} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_h \sigma T_h^{4} \cdot A_{human} \cdot A_{lens}}{2\pi d^{2}} = S_{min}$$

$$d_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{G_{amp} \cdot \varepsilon_h \sigma T_h^{4} \cdot A_{human} \cdot A_{lens}}{2\pi \cdot S_{min}}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical substitution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{max} &= \sqrt{\frac{10^{5} \times 0.98 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (306)^{4} \times 1.5 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{2\pi \times 0.05}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{10^{5} \times 0.98 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 8.77 \times 10^{9} \times 6 \times 10^{-4}}{0.3142}} \\
&\approx \sqrt{\frac{10^{5} \times 0.98 \times 5.67 \times 8.77 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}}{3.142 \times 10^{-1}}} \\
&\approx \sqrt{\frac{2.92 \times 10^{4}}{3.14 \times 10^{-1}}} \\
&\approx \sqrt{9.30 \times 10^{4}} \\
&\approx 305 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

This theoretical value vastly exceeds the practical **3–7 m** rating of consumer PIR modules. The discrepancy arises because we assumed no atmospheric attenuation, no optical losses in the Fresnel lens (typically **~40%**), no spectral mismatch losses, and used the *instantaneous* static value of $\frac{dT}{dt}$ — whereas the PIR responds only to the **rate** of change. The true board-correct value is therefore the empirical **3–7 m** with the Fresnel lens geometry as the dominant constraint.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU "Why" Questions

| # | Question | Concise Logical Answer |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
