| 1 | **Why two elements, not one?** | Differential cancellation of common-mode thermal drift (sunlight, HVAC) |
| 2 | **Why a Fresnel lens, not a convex glass lens?** | Fresnel is flat, lightweight, and segmentable into multiple zones |
| 3 | **Why is the response proportional to dT/dt, not T?** | Pyroelectric coefficient is a *rate* response; static IR gives zero current |
| 4 | **Why 8–14 µm windowing?** | Matches atmospheric IR transmission window AND human body peak |
| 5 | **Why is the output digital, not analog?** | On-board BISS0001 IC includes comparator + monostable |
| 6 | **Why can a PIR miss a stationary person?** | After dT/dt → 0, signal decays; relies on *transient* detection |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a PIR Module

The following Mermaid block-diagram shows the internal signal-flow architecture of a generic HC-SR501-class PIR module and its interface to an IoT node (e.g., ESP32 / Arduino / Raspberry Pi).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[IR Source A\nHuman Body]:::emit
    srcB[IR Source B\nBackground Wall]:::emit
    lens[Fresnel Lens Array\nMulti-Zone]:::opt
    pyro1[Pyroelectric Element 1\nSlot A]:::sens
    pyro2[Pyroelectric Element 2\nSlot B]:::sens
    diffamp[Differential Amplifier\nBISS0001 Stage 1]:::amp
    filt[Band-Pass Filter\n0.3 Hz - 10 Hz]:::filt
    comp[Window Comparator\nSchmitt Trigger]:::logic
    mono[Retriggerable Monostable\nHold Time T_h]:::logic
    out[Digital OUT Pin\n3.3 V TTL]:::io
    mcu[IoT Microcontroller\nESP32 MCU]:::mcu
    cloud[Cloud / MQTT Broker\nOccupancy Event]:::cloud

    srcA --> lens
    srcB --> lens
    lens --> pyro1
    lens --> pyro2
    pyro1 --> diffamp
    pyro2 --> diffamp
    diffamp --> filt
    filt --> comp
    comp --> mono
    mono --> out
    out --> mcu
    mcu --> cloud

    classDef emit fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef opt fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef sens fill:#ffab91,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    classDef amp fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef filt fill:#90caf9,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#80cbc4,stroke:#004d40,color:#000
    classDef mcu fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#311b92,color:#000
    classDef cloud fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#263238,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Detection State Machine — Occupancy Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Armed: Vcc stable > 2 s
    Armed --> Detected: Differential Signal > Vth
    Detected --> Hold: Start Monostable T_h
    Hold --> Hold: Re-triggered within T_h
    Hold --> Armed: T_h elapsed, no further trigger
    Armed --> Idle: System sleep / MCU power-down
    Detected --> Armed: Spurious noise < Vth for > 2 s
    Hold --> [*]: MCU publishes MQTT event
```

> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The `Detected → Hold` transition is critical for **debouncing** the noisy analog front-end. Without the monostable, a single sweep of a person could generate dozens of false edges as they cross Fresnel zone boundaries, flooding the IoT broker.

## 4.3 Physical Pinout Reference (HC-SR501 Module)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pin1[Pin 1: VCC\n5 V DC]:::pwr
    pin2[Pin 2: OUT\nDigital Signal]:::sig
    pin3[Pin 3: GND\n0 V Reference]:::gnd
    pot1[Trimmer POT 1: Sensitivity\n3 m - 7 m Range]:::adj
    pot2[Trimmer POT 2: Time Delay\n2 s - 200 s Hold]:::adj
    hl[Trigger Mode Jumper\nL: Single Trigger\nH: Repeatable]:::cfg

    pin1 --- pin2
    pin2 --- pin3

    classDef pwr fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    classDef sig fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    classDef gnd fill:#212121,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef adj fill:#dcedc8,stroke:#33691e,color:#000
    classDef cfg fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The *single-trigger (L)* mode emits exactly one pulse per detection event, while *repeatable (H)* mode keeps retriggering $T_h$ on every motion within the hold window — the correct choice for **smart lighting** (you want the lamp to stay on while the room is occupied) versus **intruder alarms** (you want one clean event per crossing).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**Differentiate between Active and Passive Infrared sensors. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Active IR sensor** emits its own infrared beam (typically via an IR LED) and detects reflection/interruption; requires both emitter and receiver (e.g., IR break-beam obstacle sensor).
- **Passive IR (PIR) sensor** does **not** emit radiation; it only receives the black-body IR emissions of warm objects (e.g., HC-SR501 occupancy sensor).
- (1 mark) Key distinction: PIR is *passive receiver*; active IR is *emitter + receiver* pair.
- (1 mark) Power: PIR consumes < 50 µA in standby; active IR consumes mA continuously.
- (1 mark) Application: PIR for *human-presence* detection; active IR for *proximity/line-of-sight* detection.

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**Why are two pyroelectric elements used in a PIR sensor instead of a single element?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) A single element cannot distinguish between genuine motion and slow ambient temperature drift (e.g., sunlight warming the wall).
- (1 mark) Two elements are wired in **series opposition (differential configuration)**, so any common-mode thermal change (sunlight, HVAC) appears equally on both and is cancelled.
- (1 mark) A moving person asymmetrically illuminates the two elements, producing a non-zero differential signal that is amplified and detected as motion.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Design & Analysis

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a PIR motion sensor. Clearly identify the role of the Fresnel lens and the differential pyroelectric pair. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PIR-based IoT security node is to be deployed in a 6 m × 4 m room. The sensor has a half-angle field of view of 60° and a maximum practical range of 5 m. Calculate whether a single ceiling-mounted sensor at 2.7 m height can cover the entire room footprint. If not, propose a sensor-placement strategy and justify the choice of **repeatable trigger mode** for this use case. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

1. **(1 mark) Definition:** A PIR sensor is a passive transducer that detects changes in incident infrared radiation (8–14 µm) caused by moving warm bodies.
2. **(2 marks) Block diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes): IR source → Fresnel lens → Differential Pyroelectric pair (Slot A & Slot B) → Differential amplifier → Band-pass filter (0.3–10 Hz) → Comparator → Monostable → Digital OUT.
3. **(1 mark) Role of Fresnel lens:** Segments incoming IR into multiple alternating zones, increasing effective collection aperture while remaining flat, lightweight, and inexpensive.
4. **(1 mark) Role of differential pair:** Cancels common-mode thermal drift (sunlight, HVAC fluctuations) and produces a non-zero signal only when motion asymmetrically illuminates the two elements.
5. **(1 mark) Conditioning chain:** BISS0001 IC provides amplification, band-pass filtering, window comparison, and a user-programmable monostable hold time $T_h \approx 1.1 R_t C_t$.
6. **(1 mark) Output:** A clean 3.3 V TTL digital pulse on motion, directly readable by any IoT-class MCU (ESP32, Arduino, Raspberry Pi).

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

1. **(1 mark) Field-of-view footprint on the floor:**
   At sensor height $h = 2.7$ m and half-angle $\theta = 60°$, the floor radius covered is:
   $$r = h \tan(\theta) = 2.7 \times \tan(60°) = 2.7 \times 1.732 = 4.676 \; \text{m}$$
2. **(1 mark) Maximum floor diameter covered:** $D_{cov} = 2r = 9.35$ m.
3. **(1 mark) Footprint check:** Room is 6 m × 4 m; the longest diagonal is $\sqrt{6^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{52} \approx 7.21$ m. Since $D_{cov} = 9.35$ m $> 7.21$ m, **a single ceiling-mounted sensor CAN cover the entire room footprint** (assuming Fresnel lens orientation is optimal).
4. **(1 mark) Practical limitation note:** Coverage degrades to ~70% at the edges of the cone due to Fresnel zone shaping; for a 6 m × 4 m room, it is recommended to mount the sensor at the **geometric centre of the ceiling** and verify coverage with a walk-test.
5. **(1 mark) Repeatable trigger mode — justification:** In a security context, the goal is not just to log an entry event but to **sustain the alarm** for the entire duration of intrusion. Repeatable mode retriggers the monostable on every new motion sample within $T_h$, so the alarm remains active as long as the intruder keeps moving.
6. **(1 mark) Counter-case (single trigger):** Would emit one pulse per crossing — useful for *entry logging* (door counters) but **inadequate** for *sustained intrusion alerting*.
7. **(1 mark) Mathematical hold-time recommendation:** $T_h \approx 1.1 R_t C_t$; for a 30-second hold, choose $R_t = 100$ kΩ and $C_t = 270$ µF.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **pyroelectric effect** and derive the expression for the current generated by a PIR sensing element. Why is the sensor response proportional to the *rate* of change of temperature and not the absolute temperature? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare PIR sensors with **ultrasonic** and **microwave (Doppler radar)** motion sensors across the dimensions: detection principle, range, power consumption, cost, and suitability for outdoor deployment. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

1. **(1 mark)** The **pyroelectric effect** is the linear generation of surface charge on certain anisotropic crystals (e.g., LiTaO₃) when their temperature changes.
2. **(1 mark) Derivation — start:** Let $p$ be the pyroelectric coefficient. A small thermal perturbation $\Delta T$ over an electrode area $A$ produces a surface charge:
   $$Q = p \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$
3. **(1 mark) Differentiate with respect to time:**
   $$i_{pyro} = \frac{dQ}{dt} = p \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dt}$$
4. **(1 mark) Engineering meaning:** Because current is the *time derivative* of charge, a *constant* temperature (no motion, no flux change) yields zero current.
5. **(1 mark) Static target implication:** A perfectly still human, having reached thermal equilibrium with the surroundings, produces a **constant** IR flux on the lens and hence *no* signal — this is the fundamental reason a stationary intruder can evade a basic PIR.
6. **(1 mark) Mitigation strategies:** Use of multi-element arrays, mechanically modulated/chopped optical paths, or fusion with microwave Doppler for "static-presence" detection.
7. **(1 mark) Bonus:** Note that for $T > T_{Curie}$ (≈ 610 °C for LiTaO₃), the crystal depolarises and the effect vanishes — a thermal limit rarely encountered in practice.

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

| Dimension (1 mark per row) | PIR | Ultrasonic (HC-SR04) | Microwave Doppler (HB100) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Principle** | Pyroelectric IR (8–14 µm) | Time-of-flight of 40 kHz acoustic burst | Doppler shift of 10.525 GHz microwave |
| **Range** | 3–7 m | 0.02–4 m | 8–15 m |
| **Power (Standby)** | < 50 µA | ~2 mA | ~30 mA |
| **Cost (INR)** | ₹80–150 | ₹60–100 | ₹400–700 |
| **Outdoor Suitability** | **Poor** (rain, sun, fog disrupt IR) | **Moderate** (wind/temperature gradients cause false triggers) | **Excellent** (penetrates non-metallic walls, weather-resilient) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** PIR is optimal for **indoor occupancy detection** due to ultra-low power and low cost. Microwave Doppler is preferred for **outdoor perimeter security** at the cost of higher power. Ultrasonic is best for **short-range robotics** obstacle avoidance.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps (Board-Exam Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting the differential pair.** Half the marks for "explain PIR working" come from explicitly stating that **two** elements are used in opposition. Writing "the sensor detects heat from the body" without the differential cancellation logic will cap you at 50% of the credit.
> 2. **Confusing IR "emission" with IR "reflection".** PIR sensors **receive** black-body emission; they do **not** rely on reflected IR (that would be an *active* IR sensor).
> 3. **Wrong units in formulas.** Stefan-Boltzmann constant $\sigma$ is **always** $5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴. Wrong powers of ten are a frequent cause of numerical answer rejection.
> 4. **Stating "PIR detects motion" without qualifying "transient thermal disturbance"** is treated as incomplete. Always include the phrase *"rate of change of IR flux"*.
> 5. **Confusing the Fresnel lens with a convex lens.** Fresnel = flat, segmented, multi-zone; convex = curved, single focal point. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Re-read this section on the morning of the exam.**

- [x] **PIR = Passive Infrared = NO emission, only reception.**
- [x] Spectral band: **8 µm – 14 µm** (human body peak).
- [x] Core physical effect: **Pyroelectric** (LiTaO₃ crystal).
- [x] Formula: $i_{pyro} = p A \frac{dT}{dt}$ — proportional to **rate** of temperature change, not absolute temperature.
- [x] **Two elements in series opposition** for common-mode rejection.
- [x] Optical component: **Fresnel lens** (flat, segmented, multi-zone).
- [x] Signal chain: Pyro pair → **BISS0001** (amp + BPF + comparator + monostable).
- [x] BPF cutoff: **0.3 Hz to 10 Hz** (rejects DC drift & electrical noise).
- [x] Output: **Digital HIGH/LOW** (3.3 V TTL).
- [x] Hold time: $T_h \approx 1.1 R_t C_t$ (programmable, **2 s – 200 s**).
- [x] Modes: **L (single)** vs **H (repeatable)** trigger.
- [x] **Cannot detect stationary warm objects** — a classic KTU trick question.
- [x] Power budget: $I_{ddq} < 50 \; \mu A$ — ideal for **battery-operated IoT** nodes.
- [x] Outdoor use: **Not recommended** without weatherproofing (use microwave instead).
- [x] Key distinction: **PIR ≠ Active IR** (the latter is emitter + receiver pair).
- [x] Key formula for range: $r = h \tan(\theta)$ — ceiling-mount coverage geometry.

> **End of Module 3 — PIR Motion Sensor Notes.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Sensor and Moisture Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS AND SMART COMPUTING.
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pressure Sensor and Moisture Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Pressure Sensor and Moisture Sensor

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Pressure Sensor — Formal Definition

A **Pressure Sensor** is a transducer that converts an applied mechanical force per unit area (pressure) exerted by a gas or liquid into a measurable, proportional electrical signal (voltage, current, or frequency). In the context of IoT, pressure sensors are foundational sensing primitives that allow edge devices to perceive force-related physical phenomena.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A pressure sensor is a device that senses the pressure applied to its sensing element and translates this mechanical input into an electrical output signal that can be read by a microcontroller or ADC.

The SI unit of pressure is the **Pascal (Pa)**, where:

$$1 \text{ Pa} = 1 \text{ N/m}^{2}$$

Other common units include **psi (pounds per square inch)**, **bar**, **Torr**, and **atm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU examinations, students must remember that **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa** and **1 atm = 101325 Pa**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine inflating a balloon inside a closed box. The harder you push air in, the more the balloon pushes against the walls of the box. A pressure sensor works on the same principle — it has an internal diaphragm (a thin flexible membrane) that *flexes* or *deforms* when external pressure is applied. The amount of deformation is then translated into an electrical quantity. Think of the diaphragm as the "skin" of the sensor — it feels the push of the gas or liquid just like your skin feels the push of wind.

---

### 1.2 Moisture Sensor — Formal Definition

A **Moisture Sensor** (also called a **humidity sensor** or **soil moisture sensor** in context-specific applications) is a transducer that detects, measures, and reports the water content present in a given environment — be it soil, air, or another medium. The output is a variable electrical parameter (resistance, capacitance, or voltage) that is proportional to the moisture level.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A moisture sensor is an electronic device that detects the presence and amount of water/moisture in its surrounding environment and produces a corresponding electrical signal for IoT processing.

Two primary metrics:
- **Soil Moisture Content (volumetric water content)** — measured as a percentage.
- **Relative Humidity (RH)** for air — measured as a percentage of maximum water vapor the air can hold at a given temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The output metric of a soil moisture sensor is typically expressed in **% VWC (Volumetric Water Content)**. The range for agricultural soil is generally **0% to 100% VWC**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a moisture sensor like a piece of bread. When the bread is dry, it is hard and non-conductive to electricity; when it is wet, it becomes soft and conducts electricity much better. Soil moisture sensors exploit this exact principle: dry soil resists the flow of electric current, while wet soil conducts electricity more easily. The sensor measures this change in electrical property to infer how much water is present.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pressure Sensor — Operational Analysis

Pressure sensors are classified based on their transduction mechanism, reference pressure, and output type. Let us examine each in detail.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Reference Pressure

| Type | Description | Typical IoT Application |
|------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Absolute Pressure Sensor** | Measures pressure relative to a perfect vacuum (internally sealed reference) | Barometric altitude measurement, weather stations |
| **Gauge Pressure Sensor** | Measures pressure relative to atmospheric pressure | Tire pressure, blood pressure monitors |
| **Differential Pressure Sensor** | Measures the difference between two applied pressures | Flow measurement across an orifice, HVAC filters |

### 2.1.2 Classification by Working Principle

**A. Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor (Strain Gauge Type):**
- Contains a diaphragm with embedded strain gauges arranged in a **Wheatstone Bridge** configuration.
- When pressure deflects the diaphragm, the strain gauges experience a change in resistance.

$$R(\epsilon) = R_0 (1 + \Delta R / R_0)$$

where $\Delta R / R_0 = G_f \cdot \epsilon$, $G_f$ is the **gauge factor**, and $\epsilon$ is the strain.

**B. Capacitive Pressure Sensor:**
- The diaphragm forms one plate of a parallel-plate capacitor. Pressure changes the gap $d$ between plates, altering capacitance.

$$C = \frac{\epsilon_r \epsilon_0 A}{d}$$

where $\epsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m is the **permittivity of free space**.

**C. Piezoelectric Pressure Sensor:**
- Uses materials (e.g., **quartz**, **lead zirconate titanate — PZT**) that generate a charge $Q$ when mechanically stressed.

$$Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot P \cdot A$$

where $d$ is the **piezoelectric charge coefficient** (C/N) and $F$ is the applied force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Compare piezoresistive and piezoelectric pressure sensors."* Remember — piezoelectric sensors are inherently **dynamic** (respond to changes), while piezoresistive sensors measure **static** pressures.

**D. MEMS Pressure Sensor:**
- Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems version. Extremely small, low power, ideal for IoT wearables and smartphones (e.g., **BMP280**).

### 2.1.3 Pressure Sensor Signal Conditioning

The raw output of a pressure sensor's Wheatstone bridge is typically a **millivolt-level differential signal**. Signal conditioning is performed by:
- **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** — amplifies small differential signals.
- **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — converts analog output to digital for the MCU.
- **Temperature Compensation** — corrects thermal drift.

---

## 2.2 Moisture Sensor — Operational Analysis

### 2.2.1 Classification

**A. Resistive Soil Moisture Sensor:**
- Two exposed probes act as a variable resistor. Resistance decreases as moisture increases.
- Simple, cheap, but corrodes over time.

**B. Capacitive Soil Moisture Sensor:**
- Measures changes in the dielectric constant of the soil. Capacitance increases with moisture.
- **No exposed electrodes** → longer lifespan, corrosion-free.

**C. Volumetric Water Content via FDR (Frequency Domain Reflectometry):**
- Measures the soil's dielectric constant by detecting resonant frequency shifts.
- Industry standard for precision agriculture.

**D. TDR (Time Domain Reflectometry):**
- Sends a pulse down a probe; moisture is inferred from travel time of the reflected signal.

### 2.2.2 Working Principle — Capacitive Soil Sensor

The capacitance of a parallel plate embedded in soil is:

$$C = \frac{\epsilon_r \epsilon_0 A}{d}$$

where $\epsilon_r$ is the **relative permittivity (dielectric constant)** of soil:
- Dry soil: $\epsilon_r \approx 3$ to $5$
- Water: $\epsilon_r \approx 80$

> [!NOTE]
> The dramatic contrast in $\epsilon_r$ between dry soil and water (≈80) is what makes capacitive sensing highly sensitive and reliable.

### 2.2.3 Working Principle — Resistive Soil Sensor

The resistance between the two probes is given by Ohm's law extended for soil:

$$R_{soil} = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A_{probe}}$$

where $\rho$ is the **electrical resistivity of soil** (highly dependent on moisture content).

| Soil Condition | Typical Resistance |
|----------------|--------------------|
| Dry Soil | 100 kΩ – 1 MΩ |
| Moist Soil | 10 kΩ – 50 kΩ |
| Wet Soil | 1 kΩ – 5 kΩ |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| Pressure definition | $P = F / A$ | Pa (N/m²) | Fundamental relation |
| Capacitance (parallel plate) | $C = \epsilon_r \epsilon_0 A / d$ | Farads (F) | $\epsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m |
| Piezoelectric charge | $Q = d \cdot F$ | Coulombs (C) | $d$ is the piezoelectric coefficient |
| Strain gauge resistance | $\Delta R / R = G_f \cdot \epsilon$ | dimensionless | $G_f$ gauge factor $\approx 2$ for metal |
| Gauge factor | $G_f = (\Delta R / R) / \epsilon$ | dimensionless | Semiconductor gauges can be $G_f \approx 100$ |
| Sensitivity (pressure) | $S = \Delta V / \Delta P$ | V/Pa | Sensor figure of merit |
| Humidity (Relative) | $RH = (P_{water}/P_{saturation}) \times 100\%$ | % | Up to **100%** |
| Capacitive soil sensor | $C \propto \epsilon_{r,soil}$ | F | $\epsilon_r$ ranges from 3 to 80 |
| Pressure conversion | $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ | — | KTU-favorite conversion |
| Pressure conversion | $1 \text{ atm} = 101325 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.013 \text{ bar}$ | — | Standard atmosphere |
| Hydrostatic pressure | $P = \rho g h$ | Pa | $\rho$ fluid density, $g \approx 9.81$ m/s² |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Output Voltage of a Piezoresistive Wheatstone Bridge

The most common configuration for a pressure sensor is a **Wheatstone bridge** with one or more active strain gauges on the diaphragm. Let us derive its output voltage.

### Step 1 — Bridge Configuration

The bridge consists of four resistors $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$, $R_4$, with an excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ applied across two opposite nodes and the output $V_{out}$ measured across the other two.

### Step 2 — General Output Equation

By voltage division, the output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

### Step 3 — Balanced Condition

At zero applied pressure, the bridge is balanced, i.e., $V_{out} = 0$. This requires:

$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_4}{R_3}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Pressure → Resistance Change

When pressure $P$ is applied, all four resistors change by $\pm \Delta R$. Two increase and two decrease (because the diaphragm bends — one side stretches, the other compresses).

For a typical configuration:
- $R_1 = R_0 + \Delta R$
- $R_2 = R_0 - \Delta R$
- $R_3 = R_0 + \Delta R$
- $R_4 = R_0 - \Delta R$

### Step 5 — Substitute into the Output Equation

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_0 + \Delta R}{R_0 + \Delta R + R_0 - \Delta R} - \frac{R_0 - \Delta R}{R_0 + \Delta R + R_0 - \Delta R} \right)$$

### Step 6 — Simplify the Denominators

Both denominators become $2R_0$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_0 + \Delta R}{2R_0} - \frac{R_0 - \Delta R}{2R_0} \right)$$

### Step 7 — Combine the Fractions

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{2R_0} \left[ (R_0 + \Delta R) - (R_0 - \Delta R) \right]$$

### Step 8 — Cancel Terms

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{2R_0} \left[ 2\Delta R \right]$$

### Step 9 — Final Bridge Equation

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R_0}$$

Using the gauge factor relation $\Delta R / R_0 = G_f \cdot \epsilon$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \epsilon$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Result:** The output voltage of a full-bridge piezoresistive pressure sensor is linearly proportional to the strain and hence to the applied pressure. This is the equation students should memorize for 7-mark derivations.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A piezoresistive pressure sensor uses a Wheatstone bridge with all four active gauges having $R_0 = 350$ Ω and gauge factor $G_f = 2.1$. The sensor is excited with $V_{ex} = 5$ V. The diaphragm experiences a strain of $\epsilon = 0.001$ under applied pressure. Find:
(a) The change in resistance $\Delta R$.
(b) The output voltage $V_{out}$.
(c) The pressure $P$ if the diaphragm area is $A = 10^{-4}$ m² and the diaphragm sensitivity is $S = 0.5$ μV/Pa.

### Solution (a):

Using $\Delta R / R_0 = G_f \cdot \epsilon$:

$$\Delta R = R_0 \cdot G_f \cdot \epsilon = 350 \times 2.1 \times 0.001$$

$$\Delta R = 0.735 \text{ Ω}$$

### Solution (b):

Using the full-bridge output equation:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R_0} = 5 \times 2.1 \times 0.001 = 0.0105 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{out} = 10.5 \text{ mV}$$

### Solution (c):

Using sensitivity $S = \Delta V / \Delta P$:

$$P = \frac{V_{out}}{S} = \frac{0.0105}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$P = 21{,}000 \text{ Pa} = 21 \text{ kPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a *classic KTU 14-mark problem pattern*. Students are expected to: (1) state the relevant formula, (2) substitute numerical values with correct units, and (3) present the final result with a clear unit.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure for Liquid-Level IoT Sensing

Pressure sensors are widely used in IoT water-tank level monitoring. The hydrostatic pressure relation is derived from force balance on a fluid column.

### Step 1 — Force at the Bottom of the Fluid Column

The weight of the fluid column of height $h$ exerts a force on the bottom surface of area $A$:

$$F = m \cdot g = (\rho \cdot V) \cdot g = \rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g$$

### Step 2 — Pressure Definition

$$P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{\rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g}{A}$$

### Step 3 — Final Hydrostatic Equation

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

where:
- $\rho$ = fluid density (**kg/m³**)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $h$ = height of fluid column (**m**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU IoT Application:** A pressure sensor placed at the bottom of a water tank can measure the water level. By inverting the formula, $h = P / (\rho g)$, the IoT system can compute and report the water level in real time.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pressure Sensor — Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete signal-flow architecture of an IoT-enabled pressure sensor system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["PHYSICAL INPUT PRESSURE P PA"] --> nodeB["DIAPHRAGM MECHANICAL DEFLECTION"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["WHEATSTONE BRIDGE PIEZORESISTIVE"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["DIFFERENTIAL MV SIGNAL"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER INA"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["ADC 12 BIT OR 16 BIT"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["MCU ARDUINO OR ESP32"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["WIRELESS MODULE WIFI OR LORA"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["CLOUD DASHBOARD BLYNK AWS"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["LOCAL DISPLAY LCD OR OLED"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Pressure $P$ is applied on the diaphragm → diaphragm bends → strain gauges on the diaphragm change resistance → Wheatstone bridge outputs a millivolt-level differential voltage → INA amplifies it → ADC digitizes it → MCU processes and transmits to cloud or local display.

---

## 4.2 Piezoresistive Wheatstone Bridge — Internal Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeVex["VEX EXCITATION VOLTAGE"] --> nodeR1["R1 R0 PLUS DELTA R"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeGnd["GROUND"]
    nodeVex --> nodeR4["R4 R0 MINUS DELTA R"]
    nodeR4 --> nodeGnd
    nodeR1 --> nodeOutP["VOUT PLUS NODE"]
    nodeR4 --> nodeOutN["VOUT MINUS NODE"]
    nodeR2["R2 R0 MINUS DELTA R"] --> nodeOutP
    nodeR2 --> nodeGnd
    nodeR3["R3 R0 PLUS DELTA R"] --> nodeOutN
    nodeR3 --> nodeGnd
```

---

## 4.3 Moisture Sensor — IoT Functional Architecture (Soil Monitoring)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["SOIL MEDIUM WITH WATER CONTENT"] --> nodeB["SENSOR PROBES CAPACITIVE OR RESISTIVE"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["OSCILLATOR CIRCUIT FOR CAPACITIVE"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["RECTIFIER AND FILTER"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["ANALOG SIGNAL 0 TO 5V"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["MCU ESP32 ADC PIN"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["WIFI TRANSMITTER"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["CLOUD PLATFORM THINGSPEAK"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["BUZZER OR PUMP RELAY ACTUATOR"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["AUTOMATIC IRRIGATION TRIGGER"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This block diagram represents a **Smart Irrigation System**, a flagship IoT application of soil moisture sensors. When moisture drops below a threshold, the MCU triggers a relay/buzzer to activate irrigation.

---

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — When to Use Which Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["NEED TO SENSE"] --> nodeQ1["WHAT PHYSICAL QUANTITY"]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeP["PRESSURE GAS LIQUID FORCE"] --> nodePT["PRESSURE SENSOR"]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeM["MOISTURE SOIL AIR WATER CONTENT"] --> nodeMT["MOISTURE SENSOR"]
    nodePT --> nodePType["STATIC OR DYNAMIC"]
    nodePType --> nodePStatic["STATIC"] --> nodePR["PIEZORESISTIVE GAUGE TYPE"]
    nodePType --> nodePDynamic["DYNAMIC CHANGING"] --> nodePE["PIEZOELECTRIC TYPE"]
    nodeMT --> nodeMType["APPLICATION DOMAIN"]
    nodeMType --> nodeMAgri["AGRICULTURE SOIL"] --> nodeMC["CAPACITIVE TYPE LONG LIFE"]
    nodeMType --> nodeMAir["HVAC AIR HUMIDITY"] --> nodeMD["DHT22 OR SHT31 SENSOR"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a pressure sensor. List any two common types of pressure sensors used in IoT applications.**

**Model Answer:**

A **pressure sensor** is a transducer that converts an applied pressure (force per unit area) into a measurable electrical signal. The output is typically voltage, current, or frequency, proportional to the magnitude of the applied pressure.

Two common types used in IoT:
1. **Piezoresistive pressure sensor** — uses a Wheatstone bridge of strain gauges on a deflecting diaphragm.
2. **Capacitive pressure sensor** — uses diaphragm displacement to change capacitance.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a soil moisture sensor? Mention any two IoT applications.**

**Model Answer:**

A **soil moisture sensor** is an electronic device that detects the water content in soil and converts it into an electrical signal (resistance or capacitance change).

Two IoT applications:
1. **Smart agriculture / automatic irrigation** — water crops only when soil is dry, conserving water.
2. **Greenhouse monitoring** — maintain optimal soil moisture for plant growth.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a **piezoresistive pressure sensor** with a neat block diagram. Derive the output voltage expression for a full Wheatstone bridge configuration.

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**
A piezoresistive pressure sensor operates on the principle that the resistance of certain materials (called *piezoresistors* or strain gauges) changes when they are mechanically deformed. The key elements are:

1. **Diaphragm** — A thin silicon membrane on which strain gauges are fabricated.
2. **Strain gauges** — Four piezoresistors arranged in a Wheatstone bridge, positioned at locations of maximum strain.
3. **Wheatstone bridge** — Converts small resistance changes into a measurable voltage.

When external pressure is applied:
- The diaphragm deflects.
- Two gauges (radially placed) experience *tensile* strain → resistance **increases**.
- Two gauges (tangentially placed) experience *compressive* strain → resistance **decreases**.
- The bridge becomes unbalanced → produces a differential output voltage $V_{out}$.

**Derivation of Output Voltage:**

The general output of a Wheatstone bridge is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

For a full-bridge configuration with $R_1 = R_3 = R_0 + \Delta R$ and $R_2 = R_4 = R_0 - \Delta R$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R_0}$$

Substituting the gauge factor relation $\Delta R / R_0 = G_f \cdot \epsilon$:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \epsilon}$$

This shows that $V_{out}$ is linearly proportional to the applied strain $\epsilon$, which in turn is proportional to applied pressure $P$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A piezoresistive pressure sensor has $R_0 = 120$ Ω, $V_{ex} = 5$ V, $G_f = 2.0$, and a diaphragm sensitivity of $S = 0.4$ mV/kPa. Calculate the output voltage when the applied pressure is **250 kPa**. Also find the change in resistance.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Output voltage using sensitivity:

$$V_{out} = S \times P = 0.4 \text{ mV/kPa} \times 250 \text{ kPa}$$

$$V_{out} = 100 \text{ mV} = 0.1 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2:** Find strain $\epsilon$:

$$\epsilon = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{ex} \cdot G_f} = \frac{0.1}{5 \times 2.0} = 0.01$$

**Step 3:** Change in resistance:

$$\Delta R = R_0 \cdot G_f \cdot \epsilon = 120 \times 2.0 \times 0.01 = 2.4 \text{ Ω}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta R = 2.4 \text{ Ω}}$$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **capacitive soil moisture sensor**. Why is it preferred over the resistive type in IoT agriculture applications?

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**
A capacitive soil moisture sensor works on the principle of **dielectric variation**:

$$C = \frac{\epsilon_r \epsilon_0 A}{d}$$

The sensor has two conductive plates separated by a dielectric layer (the soil itself). When the soil is dry, its relative permittivity is low ($\epsilon_r \approx 3$–$5$), giving a low capacitance. When the soil is wet, water ($\epsilon_r \approx 80$) replaces air, drastically increasing capacitance.

The sensor's internal oscillator circuit converts this capacitance change into a frequency or voltage variation that the ADC can read.

**Block Diagram (described):**
1. Sensor plates embedded in PCB
2. Oscillator producing frequency $f = 1 / (2\pi RC)$
3. Rectifier → DC voltage proportional to moisture
4. Output pin to MCU

**Advantages over resistive type:**
1. **Corrosion-resistant** — no exposed metal probes in direct contact with soil.
2. **Longer lifespan** — resistive probes oxidize in 6–12 months.
3. **Higher accuracy** — capacitive sensors are not affected by soil salinity.
4. **Lower power** — better for battery-operated IoT nodes.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a **smart irrigation IoT system** using a soil moisture sensor and ESP32. Explain the components, signal flow, and the algorithm for automatic pump control.

**Model Answer:**

**System Components:**
- ESP32 microcontroller (with Wi-Fi)
- Capacitive soil moisture sensor (v1.2)
- 5V Relay module
- Submersible water pump
- Blynk/ThingSpeak cloud platform
- Power supply (12V adapter + 5V regulator)

**Signal Flow:**
1. Sensor outputs analog voltage (0–3V) → ESP32 ADC pin (GPIO34).
2. ESP32 reads value and converts to **% VWC** using calibration formula:

$$\text{VWC (\%)} = 100 \times \frac{V_{dry} - V_{read}}{V_{dry} - V_{wet}}$$

3. ESP32 publishes to cloud via Wi-Fi.
4. ESP32 controls relay GPIO pin based on threshold.

**Algorithm (Pseudocode — described, not coded):**
1. Initialize sensor, Wi-Fi, cloud.
2. Read analog value from sensor.
3. Convert to percentage moisture.
4. **If** moisture < 30% → Turn ON pump (relay HIGH).
5. **Else if** moisture > 70% → Turn OFF pump (relay LOW).
6. Transmit value + pump status to cloud.
7. Wait 5 seconds → Repeat from step 2.

This closed-loop system conserves water, prevents over-irrigation, and is a flagship example of **Smart Agriculture IoT**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Pressure Sensor:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Always write **Pa**, **V**, **Ω**. Losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing gauge factor with strain** — $G_f$ is dimensionless, strain $\epsilon$ is also dimensionless but students sometimes confuse them with percentage. Use decimals.
> 3. **Not mentioning reference pressure** — When asked "types of pressure sensor," explicitly classify as **absolute, gauge, or differential**.
> 4. **Sign errors in bridge derivation** — In a full bridge, two resistors **increase** and two **decrease**. Getting this wrong corrupts the sign of $V_{out}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Moisture Sensor:**
> 1. **Confusing humidity and moisture** — Relative humidity is for *air*; soil moisture is for *soil*. Examiners deduct marks for using these interchangeably.
> 2. **No calibration** — Always mention that the sensor must be calibrated (using dry and wet soil reference points) before use in IoT.
> 3. **Ignoring corrosion** — When asked "which is better," the answer must explicitly mention **corrosion resistance** as a key advantage of capacitive over resistive.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pressure** = Force / Area; SI unit **Pascal (Pa)**; **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa**.
- **Piezoresistive** sensors use the **Wheatstone bridge** — output $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot (\Delta R / R_0)$.
- **Piezoelectric** sensors respond to **dynamic (changing) pressure only** — they cannot measure static pressure.
- **Capacitive** pressure sensors use the relation $C = \epsilon_r \epsilon_0 A / d$ — gap $d$ changes with pressure.
- **Absolute vs Gauge vs Differential** — KTU loves this classification; memorize it.
- **Hydrostatic pressure** $P = \rho g h$ is the basis of IoT **water-level sensors**.
- **Soil moisture sensor** types: **Resistive (cheap, corrodes)** and **Capacitive (preferred, durable)**.
- **Capacitive soil sensor** works because water's $\epsilon_r \approx 80$ vs dry soil's $\epsilon_r \approx 3$ — a **27× difference**.
- **Resistive soil sensor** uses Ohm's law — resistance drops sharply as soil gets wetter.
- **Gauge factor** $G_f$ — metals ≈ 2; semiconductors ≈ 100–200.
- **Signal conditioning chain**: Sensor → INA → ADC → MCU → Wireless → Cloud.
- **IoT applications**: Smart irrigation, weather stations, tire-pressure monitoring, water tank level, HVAC, industrial process control.
- **Key formulas to memorize**: $P = F/A$, $C = \epsilon_r \epsilon_0 A / d$, $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \epsilon$, $P = \rho g h$.
- **Calibration** is mandatory for moisture sensors in any IoT deployment — always mention in viva/exam.
- **DHT22 / SHT31** are industry-standard humidity sensors for air-based IoT applications.
- **MEMS pressure sensors (BMP280, BMP180)** dominate consumer IoT — low cost, low power, small size.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Actuators: Servo Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS AND SMART COMPUTING.
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** IoT Actuators: Servo Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Servo Motor** (or **Servomechanism**) is a rotary or linear actuator that allows for **precise control of angular position, velocity, and acceleration** in a closed-loop feedback system. In the context of the Internet of Things (IoT), a servo motor is an electromechanical transducer that converts electrical Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) signals received from an IoT node (such as an ESP32, Raspberry Pi, or Arduino) into a defined mechanical angular displacement at the output shaft.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A servo motor is a self-contained electrical device that rotates parts of a machine with high efficiency and great precision. The output shaft of a servo motor does not rotate freely like a standard DC motor; instead, its angular position is dictated by the width of the input electrical pulse.

## 1.2 Key Standard Specifications (KTU High-Yield Metrics)

The following metrics are universally standardized for hobby and industrial servo motors and are essential for board examination questions:

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **PWM Signal Frequency** | **50 Hz** (Period $T = 20\,\text{ms}$) |
| **Pulse Width for $0^\circ$** | **$1.0\,\text{ms}$** (5% Duty Cycle) |
| **Pulse Width for $90^\circ$** | **$1.5\,\text{ms}$** (7.5% Duty Cycle) |
| **Pulse Width for $180^\circ$** | **$2.0\,\text{ms}$** (10% Duty Cycle) |
| **Nominal Operating Voltage** | **$4.8\,\text{V}$ to $6.0\,\text{V}$ DC** |
| **Stall Torque (SG90)** | **$1.8\,\text{kg·cm}$** at $5\,\text{V}$ |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Robotic Steering Wheel":**
> Imagine you are driving a car. The steering wheel does not spin freely; instead, you tell it *exactly* how many degrees to turn (say, 30° to the left), and it holds that position firmly against any external force. A **servo motor behaves exactly like this steering wheel for machines**. You send a short "command pulse" (like saying "turn 90°"), and the motor's internal brain (the control circuit) drives the shaft to that exact angle and *holds it there*, even if a robotic arm or a sensor mount tries to push it back. The shaft will resist and correct itself continuously, thanks to the hidden **potentiometer** that acts like an "internal eye" reporting the actual position.

## 1.4 Role of Servo Motors in the IoT Ecosystem

In the broader IoT architecture, servo motors function as the **final physical execution node (Actuation Layer)**. While sensors *perceive* the environment, actuators like servos *change* it. They bridge the digital decision-making world (cloud/edge) with the physical world (mechanical motion).

**Common IoT Applications:**
- **Smart Agriculture:** Automatic opening/closing of greenhouse vents based on temperature thresholds.
- **Smart Door Locks:** Rotating the deadbolt latch in remote-access home automation.
- **Robotics:** Joint actuation in IoT-controlled robotic arms (Industry 4.0).
- **Surveillance:** Panning/tilting of security cameras (PTZ systems).
- **Smart Irrigation:** Adjusting valve angles for variable water flow.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Servo Motors

Servo motors are broadly classified based on their operational mechanics:

1. **Positional Rotation Servo (Standard):** Rotates within a constrained angular range (typically $0^\circ$ to $180^\circ$). Most common in IoT projects.
2. **Continuous Rotation Servo:** Spins continuously in either direction; the PWM signal controls *speed* and *direction* rather than position.
3. **Linear Servo:** Uses a rack-and-pinion mechanism to convert rotary motion into precise push-pull linear motion.
4. **DC Servo Motor vs AC Servo Motor:**
   - **DC Servo:** Uses a DC motor, ideal for low-to-medium power IoT applications.
   - **AC Servo:** Uses an AC induction motor, used in heavy industrial CNC machinery.

## 2.2 Internal Architecture and Working Principle

The operation of a servo motor is fundamentally a **closed-loop negative feedback control system**. The internal architecture consists of four critical sub-systems:

- **DC Motor:** Provides the raw rotational torque.
- **Gear Train (Reduction Gears):** Reduces the high-speed, low-torque output of the DC motor into a low-speed, high-torque output suitable for mechanical work.
- **Potentiometer (Position Sensor):** Mechanically coupled to the output shaft; acts as the feedback element, providing an analog voltage proportional to the shaft's current angle.
- **Control Circuit (Comparator):** A small comparator chip (or microcontroller) that continuously compares the *desired position* (incoming PWM pulse width) with the *actual position* (potentiometer reading) and drives the motor in the correct direction until the error is zero.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Closed-Loop Logic:**
> If the **error** (Desired Position $\theta_d$ $-$ Actual Position $\theta_a$) is positive, the motor rotates clockwise.
> If the error is negative, the motor rotates counter-clockwise.
> If the error is zero, the motor **stops and holds** its position. This is why a servo resists being pushed out of position.

## 2.3 PWM Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) Control Logic

A servo motor does NOT use PWM for *power* delivery (unlike a DC motor speed controller). Instead, it uses PWM as a **communication protocol** to encode the target angle.

- The signal frequency is fixed at **$50\,\text{Hz}$**, meaning a new pulse must arrive every **$20\,\text{ms}$**.
- The **width** (duration) of the high-state pulse within that $20\,\text{ms}$ window is the only variable parameter.
- A pulse width of $1\,\text{ms}$ commands the shaft to move to $0^\circ$.
- A pulse width of $1.5\,\text{ms}$ commands the shaft to move to $90^\circ$ (center/neutral).
- A pulse width of $2\,\text{ms}$ commands the shaft to move to $180^\circ$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **most frequently tested formulas** for servo motors in KTU university examinations. Memorize the boundary conditions.

**Table 1: Servo Motor Master Equation Sheet**

| S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Units | Description |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PWM Period | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | $\text{seconds (s)}$ | $T = 1/50 = 20\,\text{ms}$ |
| 2 | Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{t_{\text{high}}}{T} \times 100\%$ | Percent (%) | High-time to total period ratio |
| 3 | Angle from Pulse Width | $\theta = (t_{\text{pulse}} - 1.0) \times 180$ | Degrees ($^\circ$) | $t_{\text{pulse}}$ must be in ms, $1 \le t \le 2$ |
| 4 | Angle from Duty Cycle | $\theta = (20 D - 1) \times 180$ | Degrees ($^\circ$) | Where $D$ is the fractional duty cycle ($0.05 \le D \le 0.10$) |
| 5 | Linear Resolution | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{180^\circ}{1\,\text{ms}} = 180^\circ/\text{ms}$ | Degrees/ms | $0.18^\circ$ per microsecond |
| 6 | Mechanical Power | $P = T \times \omega$ | Watts (W) | $T$ in N·m, $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 7 | Torque Definition | $T = F \times r$ | N·m | $F$ in Newtons, $r$ in meters |
| 8 | Torque Conversion | $1\,\text{kg·cm} = 0.0981\,\text{N·m}$ | N·m | $1\,\text{kgf} = 9.81\,\text{N}$ |
| 9 | Angular Velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s | $N$ in RPM |
| 10 | Torque-Speed Curve | $T = T_{\text{stall}} \left(1 - \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_{\text{max}}}\right)$ | N·m | Linear approximation; valid for DC servos |
| 11 | Pulse Frequency | $f = \dfrac{1}{20\,\text{ms}} = 50\,\text{Hz}$ | Hz | **Strict** KTU standard |

**Critical Boundary Conditions for the Table:**
- $t_{\text{pulse}} \in [1.0, 2.0]\,\text{ms}$ (values outside this range saturate the servo).
- $D \in [0.05, 0.10]$ i.e., $5\% \le D \le 10\%$.
- $\theta \in [0^\circ, 180^\circ]$ for standard positional servos.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial automation and IoT, the precise positional control of servo motors enables **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) control loops** to be implemented at the edge. For example, a drone's flight controller (running on an STM32/ESP32 IoT node) uses servo motors (or their electronic equivalents, ESCs) to adjust propeller speeds thousands of times per second, maintaining stable flight. The mathematical purity of the pulse-to-angle conversion ensures deterministic, jitter-free actuation, which is vital for safety-critical IoT applications such as surgical robots and autonomous vehicles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the exhaustive algebraic transitions and numerical evaluations required for the **Apply** and **Analyze** cognitive levels of the KTU Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Linear Pulse-Width to Angle Conversion

**Problem Statement (KTU Standard):** Derive the linear equation that converts the input PWM pulse width $t_{\text{pulse}}$ (in milliseconds) to the output shaft angle $\theta$ (in degrees) for a standard $0^\circ$–$180^\circ$ positional servo motor.

**Given Data:**
- At $t_1 = 1.0\,\text{ms}$, the shaft angle is $\theta_1 = 0^\circ$.
- At $t_2 = 2.0\,\text{ms}$, the shaft angle is $\theta_2 = 180^\circ$.

**Step 1 — Formulate the Linear Equation Structure**
The relationship between pulse width and angle is assumed to be a first-order linear function of the form $\theta(t) = mt + c$, where $m$ is the slope (sensitivity) in $^\circ/\text{ms}$ and $c$ is the y-intercept in $^\circ$.

**Step 2 — Apply Boundary Condition 1**
Substitute the first boundary point: $\theta(1.0) = 0^\circ$.

$$m(1.0) + c = 0$$

$$c = -m$$

**Step 3 — Apply Boundary Condition 2**
Substitute the second boundary point: $\theta(2.0) = 180^\circ$.

$$m(2.0) + c = 180$$

$$2.0m + c = 180$$

**Step 4 — Solve the System of Equations by Substitution**
Substitute $c = -m$ from Step 2 into the Step 3 equation:

$$2.0m + (-m) = 180$$

$$1.0m = 180$$

$$m = 180^\circ/\text{ms}$$

**Step 5 — Determine the Intercept $c$**
Using $c = -m$ from Step 2:

$$c = -180$$

**Step 6 — Write the Final Closed-Form Equation**

$$\boxed{\theta(t_{\text{pulse}}) = 180 \cdot t_{\text{pulse}} - 180}$$

$$\boxed{\theta = 180\,(t_{\text{pulse}} - 1)}$$

where $t_{\text{pulse}}$ is in **milliseconds** and $\theta$ is in **degrees**.

**Step 7 — Numerical Validation**

| $t_{\text{pulse}}$ (ms) | Calculation | $\theta$ (Degrees) | Expected |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $1.0$ | $180(1.0 - 1)$ | $0^\circ$ | $\checkmark$ |
| $1.5$ | $180(1.5 - 1)$ | $90^\circ$ | $\checkmark$ |
| $2.0$ | $180(2.0 - 1)$ | $180^\circ$ | $\checkmark$ |

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Duty Cycle to Angle Conversion

**Problem Statement:** Derive the equation for shaft angle $\theta$ as a function of the PWM duty cycle $D$ (expressed as a fraction, e.g., $0.075$).

**Step 1 — Express Pulse Width in Terms of Duty Cycle**
The duty cycle is defined as the ratio of the high-time to the total period:

$$D = \frac{t_{\text{pulse}}}{T}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Standard Period**
For a servo motor, the period is $T = 20\,\text{ms}$. Therefore:

$$t_{\text{pulse}} = D \cdot T = 20D$$

where $D$ is now a fractional value (e.g., $0.05$, $0.075$, $0.10$).

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Angle Equation from Section 3.1**

$$\theta = 180 \cdot (t_{\text{pulse}} - 1)$$

$$\theta = 180 \cdot (20D - 1)$$

**Step 4 — Distribute and Simplify**

$$\theta = 3600D - 180$$

**Step 5 — Final Closed-Form Equation**

$$\boxed{\theta = 3600D - 180}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Validation**

| $D$ (Fraction) | $D$ (%) | Calculation | $\theta$ | Expected |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $0.050$ | $5\%$ | $3600(0.05) - 180$ | $0^\circ$ | $\checkmark$ |
| $0.075$ | $7.5\%$ | $3600(0.075) - 180$ | $90^\circ$ | $\checkmark$ |
| $0.100$ | $10\%$ | $3600(0.10) - 180$ | $180^\circ$ | $\checkmark$ |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Torque-Force Conversion and Stall Torque Analysis

**Problem Statement:** A hobby servo motor (SG90) has a stall torque rating of $1.8\,\text{kg·cm}$. A robotic gripper is attached to the servo horn at a radial distance of $r = 2\,\text{cm}$ from the center of the output shaft. Calculate:
(a) The stall torque in SI units (N·m).
(b) The maximum linear force the gripper can exert at the point of contact.

**Step 1 — Convert the Stall Torque from kg·cm to N·m**
The standard conversion chain is: $1\,\text{kgf} = 9.81\,\text{N}$ and $1\,\text{cm} = 0.01\,\text{m}$.

$$\tau_{\text{stall}} = 1.8\,\text{kg·cm} \times 9.81\,\frac{\text{N}}{\text{kgf}} \times 0.01\,\frac{\text{m}}{\text{cm}}$$

**Step 2 — Perform the Multiplication**

$$\tau_{\text{stall}} = 1.8 \times 9.81 \times 0.01\,\text{N·m}$$

$$\tau_{\text{stall}} = 17.658 \times 0.01\,\text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{\text{stall}} \approx 0.1766\,\text{N·m}}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Maximum Linear Force**
The torque-force-radius relationship is:

$$\tau = F \times r \implies F = \frac{\tau}{r}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Values**
The radius is $r = 2\,\text{cm} = 0.02\,\text{m}$.

$$F = \frac{0.1766\,\text{N·m}}{0.02\,\text{m}}$$

**Step 5 — Final Result**

$$\boxed{F = 8.83\,\text{N}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The gripper can lift approximately $883\,\text{grams}$ against gravity at a $2\,\text{cm}$ lever arm before the servo stalls.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Power Dissipation and Angular Velocity

**Problem Statement:** A servo motor rotates at $N = 60\,\text{RPM}$ (no-load) and delivers a torque of $\tau = 0.05\,\text{N·m}$ to a mechanical load. Calculate the mechanical power output.

**Step 1 — Convert RPM to Angular Velocity (rad/s)**
The conversion formula is:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $N = 60$**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 60}{60} = 2\pi \approx 6.283\,\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Mechanical Power Equation**

$$P = \tau \times \omega$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Values**

$$P = 0.05\,\text{N·m} \times 6.283\,\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Final Result**

$$\boxed{P \approx 0.314\,\text{W} = 314\,\text{mW}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: IoT-Driven Servo Control Decision Table

The following matrix summarizes the engineering decisions an IoT developer must make when integrating a servo motor into a smart system:

| IoT Trigger Event | Sensor Input | Microcontroller Action | PWM Command Sent | Servo Response |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature $> 30^\circ\text{C}$ | DHT11/DHT22 | Edge Node computes vent open | $t_{\text{pulse}} = 2.0\,\text{ms}$ | Vent rotates to $180^\circ$ (fully open) |
| Temperature $< 20^\circ\text{C}$ | DHT11/DHT22 | Edge Node computes vent close | $t_{\text{pulse}} = 1.0\,\text{ms}$ | Vent rotates to $0^\circ$ (fully closed) |
| Unauthorized RFID scan | RC522 Reader | Door lock must engage | $t_{\text{pulse}} = 1.5\,\text{ms}$ | Deadbolt rotates to $90^\circ$ (locked) |
| Soil moisture $< 30\%$ | Capacitive Sensor | Irrigation valve must open partially | $t_{\text{pulse}} = 1.25\,\text{ms}$ | Valve rotates to $45^\circ$ (half open) |
| Object detected by PIR | HC-SR501 | Camera pan to intruder zone | $t_{\text{pulse}} = 1.75\,\text{ms}$ | Camera rotates to $135^\circ$ (mid-right pan) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid diagrams visualize the internal architecture, the PWM timing protocol, and the IoT integration flow for servo motors. All node identifiers are alphanumeric to ensure successful rendering.

---

## 4.1 Diagram A — Servo Motor Internal Closed-Loop Architecture

This diagram illustrates how the four internal sub-systems (DC motor, gears, potentiometer, and control circuit) interact to form a closed-loop negative feedback system.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[External PWM Input 50 Hz Signal] --> node2[Control Circuit Comparator Chip]
    node2 --> node3[H-Bridge Motor Driver]
    node3 --> node4[DC Motor Armature]
    node4 --> node5[Gear Train Speed Reduction]
    node5 --> node6[Output Shaft 0 to 180 deg]
    node6 --> node7[Potentiometer Position Sensor]
    node7 --> node8[Analog Voltage Feedback Signal]
    node8 --> node2
    node9[External DC Power 5V to 6V] --> node3
    node9 --> node2
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **outer feedback loop** (via the potentiometer) is the *defining feature* of a servomotor. It is what differentiates a servo from a regular geared DC motor.
- The comparator continuously subtracts the desired angle (encoded in the PWM) from the actual angle (encoded in the potentiometer voltage) and drives the motor until the difference is zero.

---

## 4.2 Diagram B — PWM Signal Timing Protocol

This diagram visualizes the standard $50\,\text{Hz}$ PWM waveform and labels the critical pulse widths that correspond to $0^\circ$, $90^\circ$, and $180^\circ$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Time t equals 0 ms Period Start] --> nodeB[Rising Edge of Pulse]
    nodeB --> nodeC[High State Pulse Width t pulse]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Falling Edge of Pulse]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Low State Remaining 20 minus t pulse ms]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Time t equals 20 ms Period End]
    nodeF --> nodeA
```

**Annotated Pulse-Width Map (KTU Examination Standard):**

| Pulse Width | Duty Cycle | Shaft Angle | Servo Horn State |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| $1.0\,\text{ms}$ | $5\%$ | $0^\circ$ | Fully Counter-Clockwise |
| $1.2\,\text{ms}$ | $6\%$ | $36^\circ$ | Slightly CCW |
| $1.5\,\text{ms}$ | $7.5\%$ | $90^\circ$ | Center Neutral |
| $1.8\,\text{ms}$ | $9\%$ | $144^\circ$ | Slightly CW |
| $2.0\,\text{ms}$ | $10\%$ | $180^\circ$ | Fully Clockwise |

---

## 4.3 Diagram C — IoT Servo Control Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

This diagram maps the complete data and control flow from a cloud-based IoT platform down to the physical servo motor actuator.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[Cloud IoT Platform AWS IoT or Firebase] --> node2[MQTT Broker Message Queue]
    node2 --> node3[Wi Fi Module on ESP32]
    node3 --> node4[ESP32 Microcontroller Firmware]
    node4 --> node5[PWM Generator Hardware Timer]
    node5 --> node6[Servo Motor SG90 or MG996R]
    node6 --> node7[Mechanical Load Robotic Arm or Valve]
    node7 --> node8[Physical Environment]
    node8 --> node9[Feedback Sensors Temperature Light Motion]
    node9 --> node4
    node4 --> node2
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **downward arrows** represent the *actuation path*: a remote user command propagates from the cloud, through the network, into the microcontroller, and finally rotates the physical servo.
- The **upward arrow** (from sensors back to the ESP32 and then to the broker) represents the *sensing path*, enabling bi-directional IoT communication and conditional actuation.

---

## 4.4 Diagram D — Servo Angle-to-Pulse Conversion State Machine

This state machine models the deterministic control flow an IoT firmware must implement to convert a desired angle to a PWM pulse width.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[Initialize System State] --> node2[Receive Desired Angle from User]
    node2 --> node3{Is Angle within 0 to 180?}
    node3 --> node4[Clamp Angle to 0] --> node5
    node3 --> node5[Compute Pulse Width t equals Angle divided by 180 plus 1.0 ms]
    node3 --> node6[Clamp Angle to 180] --> node5
    node5 --> node7[Generate 50 Hz PWM with t pulse high time]
    node7 --> node8[Drive Servo Output Pin GPIO 18]
    node8 --> node9[Wait 20 ms Update Cycle] --> node2
```

**Note for Students:** This state machine is the **algorithmic backbone** of the `Servo.h` library used in Arduino/ESP32 firmware. Understanding this conversion logic is a high-yield KTU question topic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section provides model questions calibrated to the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern, complete with step-by-step model solutions and examiner pitfall callouts.

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions test the **Remember** and **Understand** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers should be concise (3–5 lines) and definition-focused.

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: Define a Servo Motor. With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a servo motor in IoT applications.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Servo Motor** is a rotary actuator that uses a closed-loop feedback mechanism to precisely control its angular position. It consists of a DC motor, a gear train, a potentiometer, and a control circuit, all packed into a single unit.

In an IoT application, the working sequence is as follows:
1. An IoT sensor (e.g., temperature, light, or motion sensor) collects environmental data and sends it to a microcontroller (ESP32/Raspberry Pi).
2. The microcontroller processes the data and, based on a predefined threshold, generates a **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) signal** at **$50\,\text{Hz}$** with a specific pulse width (between $1.0\,\text{ms}$ and $2.0\,\text{ms}$).
3. The servo's internal control circuit compares this pulse width with the current shaft position (read via the potentiometer).
4. The motor rotates the shaft until the potentiometer reading matches the commanded pulse width, at which point the motor stops and **holds** the position.

The pulse width is directly proportional to the desired angle: $\theta = 180(t_{\text{pulse}} - 1)$.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q: Explain the role of PWM in controlling a servo motor. Why is a $50\,\text{Hz}$ frequency used?**

**Model Answer:**

In a servo motor, **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** is not used to control the *power* delivered to the motor (as in a DC fan), but rather as a **digital communication protocol** to encode the *target angular position*.

The signal is a continuous train of pulses with a fixed period of **$T = 20\,\text{ms}$** (frequency $f = 1/20\,\text{ms} = 50\,\text{Hz}$). The only variable parameter is the **duration of the high state** (the pulse width). A pulse width of $1.0\,\text{ms}$ commands $0^\circ$, $1.5\,\text{ms}$ commands $90^\circ$, and $2.0\,\text{ms}$ commands $180^\circ$.

The **$50\,\text{Hz}$ frequency** is the industry standard because:
1. It provides a **$20\,\text{ms}$ refresh window**, which is fast enough for smooth human-perceptible motion but slow enough to be generated by simple hardware timers on microcontrollers.
2. It allows the servo ample time to physically reach the target position and settle before the next command pulse arrives, preventing **jitter** and **oscillation**.
3. It is the de facto standard across virtually all hobby and industrial servo manufacturers, ensuring **interoperability**.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Part B questions in KTU 2024 ESE carry 14 marks and follow a strict **sub-part structure**: typically Part (a) for 7 marks and Part (b) for 7 marks. Full model solutions are provided for every sub-part.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, explain the internal architecture of a servo motor. Discuss the role of the potentiometer in achieving closed-loop control.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A servo motor is commanded to move to an angle of $120^\circ$. Calculate:
(i) The required PWM pulse width in milliseconds.
(ii) The required duty cycle as a percentage.
(iii) The required duty cycle as a decimal fraction.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The internal architecture of a servo motor comprises four essential sub-systems, as shown in the block diagram in **Section 4.1 (Diagram A)** of these notes.

1. **DC Motor:** Provides the raw rotational energy. It typically operates at high RPM (several thousand) but low torque.
2. **Gear Train:** A series of reduction gears that trades the high RPM of the DC motor for a much lower output RPM with proportionally higher torque. This is why small servos can produce substantial turning force.
3. **Potentiometer (Position Sensor):** This is a rotary variable resistor mechanically coupled to the output shaft. As the shaft rotates, the wiper of the potentiometer slides across a resistive track, producing an **analog output voltage** that is directly proportional to the shaft's angular position. This voltage is the *actual position* signal.
4. **Control Circuit (Comparator):** This is a small dedicated IC (or in modern servos, a microcontroller) that performs the closed-loop error calculation.

**Role of the Potentiometer in Closed-Loop Control:**

The potentiometer is the **feedback element** that makes closed-loop control possible. The control circuit has two inputs:
- **Setpoint (Desired Position):** Encoded in the width of the incoming PWM pulse. For example, a $1.5\,\text{ms}$ pulse is internally converted to a target voltage representing $90^\circ$.
- **Feedback (Actual Position):** The analog voltage from the potentiometer representing the current shaft angle.

The control circuit continuously computes the **error signal**:

$$e(t) = \theta_{\text{desired}} - \theta_{\text{actual}}$$

- If $e(t) > 0$, the motor is driven clockwise.
- If $e(t) < 0$, the motor is driven counter-clockwise.
- If $e(t) = 0$, the motor is braked and holds its position.

Without the potentiometer, the servo would have **no knowledge of its actual position**, and the motor would spin freely until the gears jammed — it would essentially behave like a regular DC motor. The potentiometer transforms an open-loop actuator into a precise, self-correcting closed-loop servomechanism.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:** Desired angle $\theta = 120^\circ$.

**(i) Required PWM Pulse Width in Milliseconds:**

Using the standard formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$\theta = 180 \cdot (t_{\text{pulse}} - 1)$$

Substitute $\theta = 120$:

$$120 = 180 \cdot (t_{\text{pulse}} - 1)$$

Divide both sides by $180$:

$$\frac{120}{180} = t_{\text{pulse}} - 1$$

$$0.6667 = t_{\text{pulse}} - 1$$

$$t_{\text{pulse}} = 1.6667\,\text{ms}$$

$$\boxed{t_{\text{pulse}} \approx 1.67\,\text{ms}}$$

**(ii) Required Duty Cycle as a Percentage:**

The duty cycle is defined as:

$$D(\%) = \frac{t_{\text{pulse}}}{T} \times 100$$

With $T = 20\,\text{ms}$ and $t_{\text{pulse}} = 1.6667\,\text{ms}$:

$$D(\%) = \frac{1.6667}{20} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{D(\%) \approx 8.33\%}$$

**(iii) Required Duty Cycle as a Decimal Fraction:**

$$D = \frac{1.6667}{20} = 0.08333$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 0.0833 \text{ (or } 8.33 \times 10^{-2}\text{)}}$$

**Verification using the Section 3.2 formula:** $\theta = 3600D - 180 = 3600(0.0833) - 180 = 299.88 - 180 = 119.88 \approx 120^\circ$ ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between a DC motor, a Stepper motor, and a Servo motor in terms of control mechanism, precision, torque characteristics, and typical IoT use cases.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A robotic arm in an IoT-enabled smart factory uses an MG996R servo motor with a stall torque of $11\,\text{kg·cm}$ at $6\,\text{V}$. The gripper is attached at a radial distance of $3\,\text{cm}$ from the servo center.
(i) Convert the stall torque to SI units (N·m).
(ii) Calculate the maximum linear gripping force in Newtons.
(iii) If the servo rotates at $50\,\text{RPM}$ under load, calculate the mechanical power output in Watts.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

| Feature | DC Motor | Stepper Motor | Servo Motor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Control Mechanism** | Open-loop; voltage controls speed. | Open-loop; pulse count controls angle. | **Closed-loop**; pulse *width* controls angle. |
| **Position Feedback** | None (no internal sensor). | None (assumes no missed steps). | **Potentiometer + comparator** for real-time correction. |
| **Precision** | Low (rotates freely; no defined position). | High for open-loop, but loses steps under overload. | **Very High** — self-corrects against external disturbances. |
| **Holding Torque** | Zero when unpowered (coasts). | High holding torque even at standstill. | **Moderate** — can hold position but consumes current. |
| **Speed Range** | High RPM (1000–10000+). | Moderate RPM, drops with higher torque. | Limited angular range ($0^\circ$–$180^\circ$ or $360^\circ$). |
| **Complexity** | Simple (just a motor + power). | Moderate (requires driver + pulse generator). | Complex (motor + gears + feedback + control IC). |
| **Typical IoT Use Case** | Driving wheels of a small robot, fans. | 3D printers, CNC machines, camera sliders. | **Robotic arms, door locks, camera gimbals, valve control.** |

**Key Distinction:** A stepper motor moves in discrete *steps* based on the *number* of pulses sent. A servo motor moves to a continuous *position* based on the *width* of each pulse, and it actively *verifies and corrects* that position using internal feedback. This makes servos more reliable under variable loads but limited in rotational range.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Stall torque $\tau_{\text{stall}} = 11\,\text{kg·cm}$
- Operating voltage $V = 6\,\text{V}$
- Gripper radius $r = 3\,\text{cm} = 0.03\,\text{m}$
- Rotational speed $N = 50\,\text{RPM}$

**(i) Convert Stall Torque to SI Units (N·m):**

The conversion chain: $1\,\text{kgf} = 9.81\,\text{N}$ and $1\,\text{cm} = 0.01\,\text{m}$.

$$\tau_{\text{stall}} = 11\,\text{kg·cm} \times 9.81\,\frac{\text{N}}{\text{kgf}} \times 0.01\,\frac{\text{m}}{\text{cm}}$$

$$\tau_{\text{stall}} = 11 \times 9.81 \times 0.01\,\text{N·m}$$

$$\tau_{\text{stall}} = 107.91 \times 0.01\,\text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{\text{stall}} \approx 1.0791\,\text{N·m}}$$

**(ii) Maximum Linear Gripping Force:**

Using the torque-radius relationship $F = \tau / r$:

$$F = \frac{1.0791\,\text{N·m}}{0.03\,\text{m}}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 35.97\,\text{N}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The gripper can exert approximately $35.97\,\text{N}$ of force, which is roughly equivalent to gripping an object weighing $3.6\,\text{kg}$ against Earth's gravity at the $3\,\text{cm}$ lever arm.

**(iii) Mechanical Power Output at 50 RPM:**

**Step 1** — Convert RPM to angular velocity:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 50}{60} = \frac{100\pi}{60} = \frac{5\pi}{3}\,\text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega \approx 5.236\,\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2** — Apply the mechanical power formula:

$$P = \tau \times \omega$$

**Step 3** — Substitute the values. Note: At $50\,\text{RPM}$ under load, the *operating* torque is less than the stall torque. For this calculation, we assume the servo is operating near its rated load torque of $\tau \approx 1.0\,\text{N·m}$ (a reasonable approximation for a high-torque servo at moderate speed).

$$P = 1.0\,\text{N·m} \times 5.236\,\text{rad/s}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 5.24\,\text{W}}$$

If we use the full stall torque (worst-case bound):

$$P_{\text{stall}} = 1.0791 \times 5.236 \approx 5.65\,\text{W}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Students often write torque in kg·cm and lose a full mark for not converting to SI units (N·m). Always show the multiplication by $9.81 \times 0.01$.
>
> 2. **Confusing the servo PWM frequency with motor PWM frequency:** A DC motor speed controller uses $1\,\text{kHz}$ to $25\,\text{kHz}$ PWM. A **servo uses exactly $50\,\text{Hz}$**. Writing $1\,\text{kHz}$ for a servo is an automatic zero for that sub-part.
>
> 3. **Inverting the formula:** Students frequently write $\theta = 180 \cdot (1 - t_{\text{pulse}})$, which would give $180^\circ$ at $1\,\text{ms}$ and $0^\circ$ at $2\,\text{ms}$ — physically reversed. Memorize: **short pulse $\rightarrow$ $0^\circ$**, **long pulse $\rightarrow$ $180^\circ$**.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to mention the potentiometer:** In any servo question, the word **"potentiometer"** must appear in the answer. Examiners specifically look for it as evidence that the student understands the closed-loop feedback principle. A servo answer without a potentiometer is incomplete and will be penalized by at least 2 marks.
>
> 5. **Writing $T = 1/f$ without the millisecond-to-second conversion:** The period is $20\,\text{ms} = 0.02\,\text{s}$. Students who write $T = 1/50 = 0.02$ (forgetting units) lose a mark. Always write: $T = 1/50\,\text{Hz} = 0.02\,\text{s} = 20\,\text{ms}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Servo Motor** is a **closed-loop** rotary actuator that uses a DC motor, gear train, potentiometer, and control circuit in a single package.
- Servos use **PWM as a communication protocol**, not for power control. The frequency is strictly **$50\,\text{Hz}$** (period = **$20\,\text{ms}$**).
- The pulse width encodes the target angle: **$1.0\,\text{ms} \rightarrow 0^\circ$**, **$1.5\,\text{ms} \rightarrow 90^\circ$**, **$2.0\,\text{ms} \rightarrow 180^\circ$**.
- The governing equation is: $\theta = 180(t_{\text{pulse}} - 1)$ for $t_{\text{pulse}}$ in ms.
- The duty cycle form is: $\theta = 3600D - 180$ for $D$ as a decimal fraction.
- The **potentiometer** is the defining feedback element — it is what makes a servo a *servo* and not just a geared motor.
- Stall torque is the maximum torque at zero speed; it is the **upper limit** of mechanical force the servo can exert.
- Torque unit conversions: $1\,\text{kg·cm} = 0.0981\,\text{N·m}$.
- Angular velocity conversion: $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ where $N$ is in RPM.
- Mechanical power: $P = \tau \times \omega$ (in Watts, with $\tau$ in N·m and $\omega$ in rad/s).
- In IoT, servos act as the **physical actuation layer**, translating digital cloud commands into precise mechanical motion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ DC Motor — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Direct Current (DC) Motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that transforms **electrical energy** supplied from a DC source into **mechanical rotational energy** (rotary motion) at the output shaft, governed by the interaction between a current-carrying conductor and a magnetic field (Lorentz force principle, as defined by **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCCGT602 — Module 3):**
> In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT)** and **Smart Computing**, the DC motor is classified as a **Smart Actuator** — the physical "muscle" of any IoT node. It converts digital control signals (PWM from a microcontroller such as Arduino, ESP32, or Raspberry Pi Pico) into precisely controlled mechanical motion, enabling physical-world interaction in **smart homes, robotics, industrial automation, and cyber-physical systems (CPS)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The Pedal Boat on a Magnetic Lake** 🚣‍♂️
>
> Imagine a tiny boat floating on a calm lake. Now imagine there is a strong, invisible horizontal wind blowing *only when* you turn on a switch.
>
> - **The wind** = the **magnetic field** (created either by permanent magnets or field windings).
> - **The paddles of the boat** = the **armature conductors** (coils of wire carrying current).
> - **The switch** = your **DC voltage source / battery**.
> - **The resulting forward push** = the **Lorentz force** that rotates the shaft.
>
> When you reverse the switch (reverse the current), the wind direction reverses, and the boat moves *backward*. This is exactly how a **DC motor** works: by controlling the *direction of current*, you control the *direction of rotation*. By controlling the *magnitude of current (PWM duty cycle)*, you control the *speed*.

---

## 1.3 Classification of DC Motors (KTU-Relevant for IoT)

| Category | Sub-Type | Typical IoT Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Brushed DC Motor (BDC)** | Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) | Small fans, toy cars, vibration motors in wearables |
| **Brushless DC Motor (BLDC)** | Outrunner / Inrunner | Drones, computer cooling fans, EV wheels |
| **Servo Motor** | DC Servo with encoder | Robotic arms, smart locks, camera gimbals |
| **Stepper Motor** | Variable Reluctance / Permanent Magnet | 3D printers, CNC, precise positioning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physical Constants Used:**
> - Permeability of free space: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{H/m}$
> - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$ *(rarely used here, but standard)*
> - Acceleration due to gravity (for load calculations): $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$

---

## 1.4 IoT-Specific Relevance

In a typical **IoT smart system**, a DC motor is interfaced as follows:

$$\text{Sensor Input} \rightarrow \text{Microcontroller} \rightarrow \text{Driver Circuit (H-Bridge/MOSFET)} \rightarrow \text{DC Motor}$$

The microcontroller **never** drives the motor directly — it sends low-power **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** signals to a driver IC (e.g., **L293D, L298N, DRV8871, BTS7960**) that handles the higher current/voltage required by the motor windings.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DC Motor — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

The operation of a DC motor rests on **two foundational laws**:

1. **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule** — Determines the direction of force on a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field.
2. **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** — Explains the generation of **Back-EMF**, which is critical for self-regulating speed.

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

1. **Magnetic Field Setup** — A stationary magnetic field (flux density $B$ in Tesla) is established in the air gap between the **stator poles** (north and south).
2. **Current Injection** — A DC voltage $V$ is applied across the **armature winding** (rotor coils), causing current $I_a$ to flow.
3. **Force Generation (Lorentz Force)** — Each conductor of length $L$ (in metres) carrying current $I_a$ in field $B$ experiences a force:
$$F = B \cdot I_a \cdot L \quad \text{(Newtons)}$$
4. **Torque Production** — Since multiple conductors are arranged at a radius $r$ from the shaft axis, the cumulative force produces a **torque** $T$ that rotates the armature.
5. **Commutation** — The **split-ring commutator** with **carbon brushes** reverses the current direction in each coil every half-rotation, ensuring **unidirectional (continuous) rotation**.
