4. **Statistical outlier validation** – e.g., Z-score or IQR test on the data stream.

(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for four techniques)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *Differentiate between syntactic validation and semantic validation in an IoT data pipeline. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Syntactic Validation | Semantic Validation |
|--------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Concern | Structure, format, type | Meaning, domain rules, cross-field logic |
| Performed at | Gateway / Parser layer | Cloud / Application layer |
| Example | "Payload must be valid JSON of length < 1 KB" | "If soil moisture < 30 % AND temperature > 25 °C, irrigation may be ON" |

(1.5 marks for distinction + 1.5 marks for examples)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Choice A]**
> **(a)** Explain the different **levels of data validation** in an IoT system with a neat block diagram. Discuss how each level contributes to overall data trustworthiness. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A smart-grid meter reports the following hourly active-power readings (in kW) for the past 8 hours:
> $\{2.1, 2.3, 2.2, 2.4, 2.3, 2.2, 2.5, 9.8\}$
> Apply the **Z-score outlier test** with $Z_{th} = 2.5$ to identify the suspicious reading. Comment on the limitations of the Z-score test on small contaminated windows and suggest a more robust alternative. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**(a) Levels of Data Validation (7 marks)**

The IoT data pipeline can be conceptually divided into **five hierarchical validation levels**, each addressing a distinct trust concern.

**Level 1 – Sensor / Edge Validation** (1 mark)
Performed directly on the device or micro-controller. Includes range checks, plausibility-of-rate-of-change, and built-in self-tests. The goal is to **suppress obviously bad data at the source**, conserving bandwidth and battery.

**Level 2 – Gateway Validation** (1 mark)
The gateway aggregates traffic from many edge nodes. It enforces **schema conformity, payload format, deduplication, and time-stamp freshness**. It is the first line of syntactic defence.

**Level 3 – Network Validation** (1 mark)
Validates **ordering, completeness, and QoS** of the data stream. Examples: MQTT QoS-2 guarantees exactly-once delivery; sequence numbers detect packet loss.

**Level 4 – Cloud / Platform Validation** (1.5 marks)
Performs **semantic, cross-field, and statistical checks** at scale. The cloud can compare the reading with historical baselines, sibling sensors, and weather feeds.

**Level 5 – Application / ML Validation** (1.5 marks)
The final layer uses **machine-learning models** (Isolation Forest, Autoencoders, LSTM predictors) to detect subtle anomalies that pass all earlier checks. The validated data then drives dashboards, alerts, or actuator commands.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Defense-in-depth across all five levels is essential — a single check is insufficient, especially against **False Data Injection Attacks (FDIA)**.

**Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1 above) supports this answer.**

---

**(b) Z-Score Outlier Detection (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute the mean (1 mark):**

$$
\mu = \frac{2.1 + 2.3 + 2.2 + 2.4 + 2.3 + 2.2 + 2.5 + 9.8}{8} = \frac{25.8}{8} = 3.225\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the sample standard deviation (2 marks):**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2}
$$

Squared deviations:

$$
(2.1 - 3.225)^2 = 1.266
$$

$$
(2.3 - 3.225)^2 = 0.856
$$

$$
(2.2 - 3.225)^2 = 1.051
$$

$$
(2.4 - 3.225)^2 = 0.681
$$

$$
(2.3 - 3.225)^2 = 0.856
$$

$$
(2.2 - 3.225)^2 = 1.051
$$

$$
(2.5 - 3.225)^2 = 0.526
$$

$$
(9.8 - 3.225)^2 = 43.211
$$

Sum $= 49.498$. With $n - 1 = 7$:

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{49.498}{7}} = \sqrt{7.071} \approx 2.660\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Z-scores (1 mark):**

The Z-score for the suspicious reading 9.8 kW:

$$
Z = \frac{9.8 - 3.225}{2.660} = \frac{6.575}{2.660} \approx 2.47
$$

Since $Z = 2.47 \le Z_{th} = 2.5$, **the Z-score test marginally fails to flag 9.8 kW as an outlier** — a clear demonstration of the *masking effect*.

**Step 4 — Limitation of Z-score (1.5 marks):**

The Z-score relies on the **mean and standard deviation**, both of which are themselves **distorted by extreme outliers** (the 9.8 kW reading inflated both $\mu$ and $\sigma$). On small contaminated windows, this **masking effect** lets true outliers slip through.

**Step 5 — Robust alternative (1.5 marks):**

Use the **Modified Z-Score** based on the **Median Absolute Deviation (MAD)**:

$$
M_i = \frac{0.6745 \cdot (x_i - \tilde{x})}{MAD}
$$

where $\tilde{x}$ is the median and $\text{Outlier} \iff \vert M_i \vert > 3.5$.

For our data, $\tilde{x} = 2.3$ kW and deviations $\{0.2, 0, 0.1, 0.1, 0, 0.1, 0.2, 7.5\}$. Median of deviations is $0.1$, so $MAD = 0.1$. Thus:

$$
M = \frac{0.6745 \cdot (9.8 - 2.3)}{0.1} = \frac{0.6745 \cdot 7.5}{0.1} = 50.59
$$

Since $M = 50.59 \gg 3.5$, the 9.8 kW reading is **correctly flagged** as an outlier. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Choice B]**
> **(a)** With a neat architecture diagram, explain the concept of **Defense-in-Depth data validation** against False Data Injection Attacks (FDIA) in IoT. List and briefly explain any three validation layers. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A smart agriculture IoT system reports the soil moisture and temperature values shown in the table below. Apply **Mahalanobis distance validation** to detect the multivariate outlier, given that the historical mean vector is $\boldsymbol{\mu} = [35, 27]^T$ and the historical covariance matrix is
> $$\mathbf{S} = \begin{bmatrix} 25 & -8 \\ -8 & 9 \end{bmatrix}$$
> Use significance level $\alpha = 0.05$. Justify your decision. **(7 marks)**

| Sample | Soil Moisture (%) | Temperature (°C) |
|--------|-------------------|------------------|
| S1 | 34 | 28 |
| S2 | 36 | 26 |
| S3 | **80** | **15** |
| S4 | 35 | 27 |

### Model Solution

**(a) Defense-in-Depth Validation against FDIA (7 marks)**

**Introduction (1.5 marks):**
A False Data Injection Attack is a cyber-attack in which an adversary manipulates sensor measurements or control commands to deceive state estimators and disrupt the IoT/cyber-physical system. A **single validation layer is insufficient** — adversaries can craft attacks that pass one check but fail another. Hence, IoT systems adopt **defense-in-depth**, layering multiple, **independent** validation mechanisms.

**Architecture Diagram (2 marks):**
Refer to Section 4.3 above — the four-layer block diagram showing Physical, Communication, Data, and Application layers with explicit adversary-attack vectors being blocked at each tier.

**Three Validation Layers Explained (3.5 marks = 1 + 1.25 + 1.25):**

1. **Physical / Hardware Layer:** Each IoT device carries a **hardware root of trust** (TPM, secure element) and **cryptographic keys** for signing every measurement. The signature is verified at the gateway, making it computationally infeasible for an attacker to forge a reading without the device's private key.

2. **Data / Statistical Layer:** All incoming readings are subjected to **range, plausibility, Z-score, and Mahalanobis checks**. Even if a malicious reading is signed, it must be physically plausible and statistically consistent with historical data.

3. **Application / Semantic Layer:** Business rules and ML-based anomaly detectors evaluate the reading's **contextual meaning** — for instance, a "soil moisture of 95 %" may be plausible at midnight after rain but implausible at noon with 35 °C heat. Human-in-the-loop approval is required for high-impact actuator commands.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** A coordinated adversary must defeat **all** layers simultaneously, which is exponentially harder — the essence of defense-in-depth.

---

**(b) Mahalanobis Distance Validation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify the candidate outlier (1 mark):**
S3 = $[80, 15]^T$ is the visually suspicious one — soil moisture of 80% is unusually high, and the temperature of 15 °C is unusually low compared to the historical mean $[35, 27]^T$.

**Step 2 — Compute the deviation vector (1 mark):**

$$
\mathbf{d} = \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{bmatrix} 80 - 35 \\ 15 - 27 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 45 \\ -12 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the inverse covariance matrix (1.5 marks):**

$$
\det(\mathbf{S}) = (25)(9) - (-8)(-8) = 225 - 64 = 161
$$

$$
\mathbf{S}^{-1} = \frac{1}{161}\begin{bmatrix} 9 & 8 \\ 8 & 25 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Mahalanobis distance (2 marks):**

$$
D_M^2 = \mathbf{d}^T \mathbf{S}^{-1} \mathbf{d}
$$

First, compute $\mathbf{S}^{-1}\mathbf{d}$:

$$
\mathbf{S}^{-1}\mathbf{d} = \frac{1}{161}\begin{bmatrix} 9 \cdot 45 + 8 \cdot (-12) \\ 8 \cdot 45 + 25 \cdot (-12) \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{161}\begin{bmatrix} 405 - 96 \\ 360 - 300 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{161}\begin{bmatrix} 309 \\ 60 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Now,

$$
D_M^2 = \mathbf{d}^T (\mathbf{S}^{-1}\mathbf{d}) = 45 \cdot \frac{309}{161} + (-12) \cdot \frac{60}{161}
$$

$$
= \frac{13905 - 720}{161} = \frac{13185}{161} \approx 81.89
$$

$$
D_M \approx \sqrt{81.89} \approx 9.05
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with chi-squared threshold (1.5 marks):**

For $k = 2$ sensors at $\alpha = 0.05$:

$$
\chi^2_{2, 0.05} = 5.991
$$

Since $D_M^2 = 81.89 \gg 5.991$, we **strongly reject S3** as a valid reading.

**Decision:** Sample **S3 is a multivariate outlier** and must be quarantined / re-checked / discarded.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to use $n - 1$ in sample standard deviation** — costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Using Euclidean distance instead of Mahalanobis** — the question specifically tests the *covariance-weighted* distance.
> 3. **Forgetting the chi-squared threshold comparison** — without it, $D_M$ is just a number, not a decision.
> 4. **Not discussing the masking effect of Z-score** — KTU awards marks for *critique* of methods.
> 5. **Omitting units (°C, kW, %)** in numerical answers — examiners deduct 0.5 mark per major omission.
> 6. **Drawing the data validation pipeline as a vague "filter" box** — use a proper **multi-level block diagram** with named layers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Data Validation (Module 4)**

- **Definition:** Data validation = verifying accuracy, completeness, consistency, and semantics of IoT data before downstream use.
- **Five Validation Levels:** Sensor/Edge → Gateway → Network → Cloud → Application/ML.
- **Eight Core Techniques to Memorize:** Range, Format, Plausibility-of-Rate, Cross-Sensor Consistency, Temporal (freshness + monotonicity), Statistical (Z, IQR, MAD, Mahalanobis), Semantic/Rules, ML-based.
- **Z-Score Rule:** Accept if $\vert Z_i \vert \le 3$ (default); vulnerable to **masking effect** on small contaminated windows.
- **Modified Z-Score (MAD-based):** Outlier if $\vert M_i \vert > 3.5$ — **robust** alternative.
- **Plausibility-of-Rate:** $\vert x_t - x_{t-1} \vert \le r_{max} \cdot \Delta t$.
- **Mahalanobis Distance:** Multivariate outlier detector for **correlated** sensors; threshold from $\chi^2_{k,\alpha}$.
- **IQR Test:** Outlier if $x \notin [Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR,\ Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR]$.
- **FDIA:** False Data Injection Attack — defeated by **defense-in-depth** (crypto signing + statistical + semantic + ML).
- **Validation Metrics to Memorize Formulas:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, Completeness, MAE, RMSE, Latency, Throughput.
- **Defense-in-Depth Layers:** Physical (root of trust) → Communication (TLS/DTLS) → Data (plausibility + statistical) → Application (semantic + ML + human-in-the-loop).
- **Edge Constraints:** Validation at the edge must be **lightweight** — only range, format, and simple rate-of-change checks. Heavy ML inference goes to the cloud.
- **Always quote units** in numerical KTU answers (°C, kW, %, ppm, etc.).
- **Always show a block diagram** when a question asks for "levels" or "architecture" of validation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Categorization for Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS AND SMART COMPUTING.
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Categorization for Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Categorization for Storage** in the context of IoT refers to the systematic classification of heterogeneous data generated by IoT devices based on intrinsic characteristics such as **structure, velocity, volume, veracity, and value** (the 5V model), enabling the selection of appropriate storage architectures, database technologies, and retrieval mechanisms that satisfy latency, durability, scalability, and analytical requirements.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, this concept is anchored to the understanding that a single storage solution cannot efficiently handle the diverse data types produced by billions of IoT endpoints. Data must therefore be triaged into distinct categories *before* the storage layer is provisioned.

### The 5V Characterization Model

| Dimension | Definition | Typical IoT Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | Total size of data generated | Terabytes to Petabytes per day |
| **Velocity** | Speed at which data is produced and must be processed | Real-time (ms) to Batch (hours) |
| **Variety** | Different formats and structures | Structured, semi-structured, unstructured |
| **Veracity** | Trustworthiness, noise, and quality of data | Sensor noise, missing values, outliers |
| **Value** | Business/analytical worth derived from data | Predictive insights, anomaly detection |

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Hospital Filing System**
> Imagine a large multi-specialty hospital receiving thousands of patients every hour. The reception desk does **not** file all patient records in one giant drawer. Instead:
> - **Emergency cases** (real-time vitals) → kept at the **ICU dashboard** (in-memory / edge storage).
> - **Routine X-rays and prescriptions** (structured) → filed in **labeled cabinets** (relational databases).
> - **Doctor's handwritten notes and voice recordings** (unstructured) → archived in a **document warehouse** (data lake / object storage).
> - **Patient history over years** (time-series) → moved to a **cold archive** that is queried rarely.
>
> Data Categorization for Storage works **identically**: IoT data is sorted by type, urgency, and use-case, then routed to the storage technology best suited for it.

### Core Categories of IoT Data

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Primary Structural Categories (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> 1. **Structured Data** — Tabular, schema-defined (e.g., sensor readings stored as rows and columns).
> 2. **Semi-Structured Data** — Partially organized with tags/markers but no rigid schema (e.g., JSON telemetry from devices).
> 3. **Unstructured Data** — No predefined schema; binary or free-form (e.g., images, audio, video from smart cameras).

### Functional Categories Based on Usage

Beyond structure, IoT data is also categorized by its **operational role**:
- **Time-Series Data** — Sequential readings indexed by timestamp (temperature, pressure).
- **Streaming Data** — Continuous, unbounded flow requiring real-time processing.
- **Batch Data** — Accumulated and processed in discrete chunks (daily aggregates).
- **Metadata** — Descriptive data about other data (device ID, firmware version, location).
- **Command/Control Data** — Bi-directional control packets sent *to* devices.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### Step 1: Why Categorization is Necessary
IoT ecosystems produce data at scales that violate the assumptions of traditional enterprise databases. A naive "store-everything-in-one-DB" approach fails on three fronts:
- **Latency Violation:** Querying petabytes of video footage to fetch a single temperature reading is computationally infeasible in real time.
- **Cost Inefficiency:** Storing ephemeral sensor data on expensive SSD-backed OLTP databases wastes resources.
- **Schema Rigidity:** SQL schemas cannot natively ingest highly variable JSON payloads from heterogeneous devices.

### Step 2: Classification by Structure

- **Structured Data**
  - Conforms to a fixed schema (rows, columns, data types).
  - Examples: CSV exports from temperature sensors, relational telemetry logs.
  - **Storage Fit:** RDBMS (MySQL, PostgreSQL), Time-Series DBs (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB).

- **Semi-Structured Data**
  - Self-describing via tags or hierarchical keys; no rigid schema enforcement.
  - Examples: JSON from REST APIs, XML from industrial gateways, YAML configs.
  - **Storage Fit:** Document stores (MongoDB, CouchDB), Wide-column stores (Cassandra, HBase).

- **Unstructured Data**
  - Binary or free-form; no inherent organization.
  - Examples: CCTV footage, voice commands, vibration spectrograms.
  - **Storage Fit:** Object storage (AWS S3, MinIO, Azure Blob), Data Lakes (HDFS, Delta Lake).

### Step 3: Classification by Time-Criticality

- **Hot Data** — Accessed frequently, must be available in milliseconds. → **In-memory caches (Redis, Memcached), Edge storage.**
- **Warm Data** — Accessed occasionally for analytics. → **Columnar warehouses (ClickHouse, Snowflake).**
- **Cold Data** — Rarely accessed, retained for compliance. → **Cold archival storage (AWS Glacier, tape backups).**

### Step 4: Classification by Motion (Static vs. In-Motion)

> [!NOTE]
> **Data in Motion** = streaming data, processed *before* persistence (e.g., Apache Kafka, Apache Flink).
> **Data at Rest** = already persisted, queried when needed (e.g., data lakes, warehouses).

### Step 5: Selection Matrix — Matching Category to Storage

| Data Category | Latency Requirement | Recommended Storage | Example Technology |
|---|---|---|---|
| Structured + Hot | <10 ms | In-memory RDBMS | Redis, MemSQL |
| Structured + Warm | 100 ms–1 s | RDBMS / Columnar | PostgreSQL, ClickHouse |
| Time-Series | 1 s–1 min | Time-Series DB | InfluxDB, TimescaleDB |
| Semi-Structured | 10 ms–1 s | Document Store | MongoDB, Couchbase |
| Unstructured (binary) | Async (minutes+) | Object Storage | AWS S3, MinIO |
| Streaming (in-motion) | <100 ms | Message Broker | Kafka, RabbitMQ, MQTT Broker |
| Cold/Archival | Hours | Cold Archive | AWS Glacier, Azure Archive |

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quantitative Metrics for Storage Sizing (High-Yield for Numerical Problems):**

| Metric | Formula | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Data Volume | $V_{total} = N \times R \times T$ | $N$ = number of devices, $R$ = sampling rate (samples/sec), $T$ = time horizon | Bytes |
| Storage with Replication | $V_{replicated} = V_{total} \times f$ | $f$ = replication factor (e.g., 3) | Bytes |
| Compression-Adjusted Size | $V_{compressed} = V_{total} \times C$ | $C$ = compression ratio (0 < C < 1) | Bytes |
| Effective Throughput | $\theta = \frac{V_{total}}{T_{window}}$ | $T_{window}$ = processing window | Bytes/sec |
| Query Latency Bound | $L_{max} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{QoS}}$ | $\lambda_{QoS}$ = QoS-constrained query rate | Seconds |
| Storage Tier Cost | $Cost = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (V_i \times P_i \times t_i)$ | $V_i$ = data in tier $i$, $P_i$ = price/GB/month, $t_i$ = retention months | Currency |

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** Soil moisture (time-series) → InfluxDB; drone imagery (unstructured) → S3; weather forecasts (structured batch) → PostgreSQL.
- **Smart Healthcare:** ECG streams (time-series) → Time-Series DB; MRI scans (unstructured) → Object Storage with HIPAA-compliant encryption.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Vibration signals (high-velocity time-series) → InfluxDB at the edge; aggregated daily reports → Data Warehouse.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Worked Example 1: Sizing Total Data Volume for a Smart Factory

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A smart factory deploys **500 sensors**, each sampling data at **2 samples per second**. Each sample is **128 bytes** (16-byte timestamp + 96-byte payload + 16-byte metadata). The factory operates **24 hours a day** for **30 days**. Compute:
1. Total uncompressed volume for one month.
2. Total volume with **replication factor f = 3**.
3. Total volume after applying **lossless compression with ratio C = 0.35**.
4. Effective average throughput in **MB/s**.

### Step 1: Compute the Sampling Rate Aggregate

The aggregate sampling rate is the number of devices multiplied by the per-device rate:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{agg} &= N \times R_{per\,device} \\
R_{agg} &= 500 \times 2 \\
R_{agg} &= 1000 \text{ samples/sec}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** Every second, all 500 devices contribute two readings each, yielding 1,000 total samples per second across the system.

### Step 2: Compute the Total Time Window in Seconds

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{sec} &= 24 \times 3600 \times 30 \\
T_{sec} &= 86400 \times 30 \\
T_{sec} &= 2{,}592{,}000 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** 24 hours = 86,400 seconds; multiplied by 30 days = 2.592 million seconds.

### Step 3: Compute the Total Uncompressed Volume

Using the formula $V_{total} = N \times R \times T \times S$ where $S$ = sample size:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{total} &= R_{agg} \times T_{sec} \times S \\
V_{total} &= 1000 \times 2{,}592{,}000 \times 128 \text{ bytes} \\
V_{total} &= 2.592 \times 10^{9} \times 128 \text{ bytes} \\
V_{total} &= 331.776 \times 10^{9} \text{ bytes} \\
V_{total} &= 331.776 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** Aggregate samples per second × total seconds × bytes per sample gives the raw storage requirement. Conversion: $10^9$ bytes ≈ 1 GB (using decimal SI convention common in storage marketing).

### Step 4: Apply Replication Factor

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{replicated} &= V_{total} \times f \\
V_{replicated} &= 331.776 \times 3 \\
V_{replicated} &= 995.328 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** Distributed systems (HDFS, Cassandra) replicate data across nodes for fault tolerance. With f = 3, three full copies are stored.

### Step 5: Apply Compression Ratio

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{compressed} &= V_{replicated} \times C \\
V_{compressed} &= 995.328 \times 0.35 \\
V_{compressed} &= 348.365 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** Lossless compression algorithms (e.g., Zstandard, LZ4) on time-series sensor data typically achieve 60–70% reduction, expressed as C ≈ 0.30–0.40.

### Step 6: Compute Effective Average Throughput

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \frac{V_{total}}{T_{sec}} \\
\theta &= \frac{331.776 \times 10^{9} \text{ bytes}}{2{,}592{,}000 \text{ s}} \\
\theta &= 128{,}000 \text{ bytes/sec} \\
\theta &= 128 \text{ KB/s} = 0.128 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** Throughput is bytes generated per unit time, independent of replication or compression (these affect storage, not generation rate).

---

## Worked Example 2: Tiered Storage Cost Optimization

**Problem Statement:**
A smart city's traffic system generates **20 TB/day** of mixed data. **70% is hot data (retained 7 days), 20% is warm data (retained 90 days), and 10% is cold data (retained 3 years).** Storage prices are:
- Hot tier: **$0.023 per GB/month**
- Warm tier: **$0.0125 per GB/month**
- Cold tier: **$0.004 per GB/month**

Compute the **monthly storage cost** for one month of operation.

### Step 1: Daily Volume Per Tier

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{hot} &= 20 \times 0.70 = 14 \text{ TB/day} \\
V_{warm} &= 20 \times 0.20 = 4 \text{ TB/day} \\
V_{cold} &= 20 \times 0.10 = 2 \text{ TB/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** Apply the percentage breakdown to the daily total of 20 TB.

### Step 2: Convert TB to GB (1 TB = 1024 GB)

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{hot}^{GB} &= 14 \times 1024 = 14{,}336 \text{ GB/day} \\
V_{warm}^{GB} &= 4 \times 1024 = 4096 \text{ GB/day} \\
V_{cold}^{GB} &= 2 \times 1024 = 2048 \text{ GB/day}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Compute Retention-Months Per Tier

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{hot} &= \frac{7}{30} = 0.2333 \text{ months} \\
t_{warm} &= 3 \text{ months} \\
t_{cold} &= 36 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** Hot data lives 7 days, warm data 90 days ≈ 3 months, cold data 3 years = 36 months.

### Step 4: Apply the Cost Formula

$$Cost = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (V_i^{GB/day} \times 30 \times t_i \times P_i)$$

(Note: We multiply by 30 to convert daily GB to monthly GB.)

**Hot Tier:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Cost_{hot} &= 14{,}336 \times 30 \times 0.2333 \times 0.023 \\
Cost_{hot} &= 14{,}336 \times 30 \times 0.005366 \\
Cost_{hot} &= \$552.96 \text{ (approx)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Warm Tier:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Cost_{warm} &= 4096 \times 30 \times 3 \times 0.0125 \\
Cost_{warm} &= 122{,}880 \times 0.0125 \\
Cost_{warm} &= \$1536.00
\end{aligned}$$

**Cold Tier:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Cost_{cold} &= 2048 \times 30 \times 36 \times 0.004 \\
Cost_{cold} &= 2{,}211{,}840 \times 0.004 \\
Cost_{cold} &= \$8847.36
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Sum the Total Monthly Cost

$$\begin{aligned}
Cost_{total} &= 552.96 + 1536.00 + 8847.36 \\
Cost_{total} &= \$10{,}936.32 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** Even though cold data is only 10% by volume, its long retention period makes it the dominant cost driver — a key insight for IoT storage architecture.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Data Categorization Taxonomy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[IoT Data Universe] --> B[By Structure]
    A --> C[By Time-Criticality]
    A --> D[By Motion State]
    A --> E[By Operational Role]

    B --> B1[Structured]
    B --> B2[Semi-Structured]
    B --> B3[Unstructured]

    C --> C1[Hot Data]
    C --> C2[Warm Data]
    C --> C3[Cold Data]

    D --> D1[Data in Motion]
    D --> D2[Data at Rest]

    E --> E1[Time Series]
    E --> E2[Streaming]
    E --> E3[Batch]
    E --> E4[Metadata]
    E --> E5[Command and Control]

    B1 --> F1[RDBMS Storage]
    B2 --> F2[Document Store]
    B3 --> F3[Object Storage]
    C1 --> F4[In Memory Cache]
    C2 --> F5[Columnar Warehouse]
    C3 --> F6[Cold Archive]
    D1 --> F7[Message Broker]
    D2 --> F8[Data Lake]
```

## Diagram 2: End-to-End IoT Storage Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    DEV[IoT Devices and Sensors] --> EDG[Edge Gateway]
    EDG --> F1{Data Classifier}
    F1 -->|Structured Hot| S1[Redis In Memory]
    F1 -->|Time Series| S2[InfluxDB]
    F1 -->|Semi Structured| S3[MongoDB]
    F1 -->|Unstructured Binary| S4[Object Storage S3]
    F1 -->|Cold Data| S5[Glacier Archive]

    S1 --> AN1[Real Time Analytics]
    S2 --> AN2[Time Series Analytics]
    S3 --> AN3[API Serving]
    S4 --> ML[Machine Learning Pipeline]
    S5 --> CP[Compliance Audits]

    AN1 --> DASH[Operational Dashboard]
    AN2 --> DASH
    AN3 --> DASH
    ML --> PRED[Predictive Insights]
    CP --> REP[Regulatory Reports]
```

## Diagram 3: Tiered Storage Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Incoming IoT Data] --> Q1{Latency Requirement}
    Q1 -->|< 10 ms| HOT[Hot Tier: In Memory]
    Q1 -->|10 ms to 1 s| WARM[Warm Tier: SSD RDBMS]
    Q1 -->|> 1 s| Q2{Access Frequency}
    Q2 -->|Daily| WARM
    Q2 -->|Monthly| COLD[Cold Tier: Object Storage]
    Q2 -->|Yearly| ARC[Archive Tier: Glacier]
    HOT --> RET[Set TTL: hours]
    WARM --> RET2[Set TTL: weeks]
    COLD --> RET3[Set TTL: months]
    ARC --> RET4[Set TTL: years]
```

## Diagram 4: Data Lifecycle Flow in an IoT System

```mermaid
graph TD
    GEN[Data Generation at Sensor] --> ING[Ingestion via MQTT]
    ING --> CLS[Classification Engine]
    CLS --> STR[Short Term Storage: Edge]
    STR --> ANA[Stream Analytics]
    ANA -->|Trigger Found| ACT[Actuation Command]
    ANA -->|Aggregate| AGG[Daily Aggregator]
    AGG --> MID[Medium Term: Cloud DB]
    MID --> LT[Long Term: Data Lake]
    LT --> MLT[ML Training Pipeline]
    LT --> ARP[Archive per Retention Policy]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 3-Mark Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between structured and unstructured data with one IoT example each.**

**Model Answer:**
Structured data conforms to a predefined schema with fixed fields and data types, typically stored in tabular form. **Example:** A temperature sensor reporting `{"timestamp": "2024-01-01T10:00:00Z", "value": 25.4}` ingested into a relational table with columns for time and value.

Unstructured data lacks a predefined schema and may be binary, textual, or multimedia. **Example:** Video footage from a smart surveillance camera stored as MP4 files in object storage.

Structured data enables fast SQL queries and analytics; unstructured data requires specialized processing (e.g., computer vision, NLP) and object storage systems.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is meant by "hot," "warm," and "cold" data in IoT storage? Why is tiered storage important?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Hot Data:** Frequently accessed, low-latency data stored in fast media (RAM, NVMe SSDs). E.g., last 24 hours of sensor readings used for real-time dashboards.
- **Warm Data:** Occasionally accessed, stored on standard SSDs or HDDs. E.g., last 30 days of telemetry for trend analysis.
- **Cold Data:** Rarely accessed, archived on cheap storage. E.g., years-old logs kept for regulatory compliance.

**Importance of Tiered Storage:** It optimizes **cost** (cheap storage for cold data), **performance** (fast storage for hot data), and **scalability** (automatic data movement between tiers), which is critical in IoT where data volumes are massive and access patterns vary widely.

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the 5V characteristics of IoT data. Discuss how each "V" influences the choice of storage technology. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A smart agriculture deployment has **2000 soil moisture sensors**, each generating **one reading every 5 seconds** of **64 bytes each**. The system uses **replication factor 3** and **compression ratio 0.4**. Compute:
  (i) Daily uncompressed data volume. **(3 marks)**
  (ii) Monthly compressed and replicated storage volume. **(4 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) The 5V Characteristics and Their Storage Influence:**

1. **Volume:** Refers to the sheer scale of data. Massive volumes (e.g., petabytes/day) demand **distributed storage** like HDFS, S3, or Cassandra rather than single-node databases.
2. **Velocity:** The speed of data generation and required processing. High velocity (millions of events/sec) requires **stream processing** (Kafka, Flink) and **in-memory databases** (Redis).
3. **Variety:** Diverse formats (text, JSON, video, binary). Heterogeneous formats require **polyglot persistence** — using multiple storage types (SQL + NoSQL + Object Storage) within one architecture.
4. **Veracity:** Data quality and trustworthiness. Noisy sensor data must be cleaned via **ETL pipelines** before storage; metadata is often stored in **RDBMS** with quality flags.
5. **Value:** The actionable insight extracted. High-value data is prioritized for **real-time analytics platforms** (e.g., Spark Streaming, dashboards); low-value raw data is archived.

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**(i) Daily Uncompressed Volume:**

Aggregate sampling rate:
$$\begin{aligned}
R_{agg} &= 2000 \times \frac{1}{5} = 400 \text{ samples/sec}
\end{aligned}$$

Seconds in a day:
$$\begin{aligned}
T_{sec} &= 86400 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

Total daily volume:
$$\begin{aligned}
V_{daily} &= 400 \times 86400 \times 64 \\
&= 34{,}560{,}000 \times 64 \\
&= 2{,}211{,}840{,}000 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 2.21 \text{ GB/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Monthly Compressed and Replicated Volume:**

Monthly uncompressed:
$$\begin{aligned}
V_{month} &= 2.21 \times 30 = 66.3 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}$$

After compression (C = 0.4):
$$\begin{aligned}
V_{compressed} &= 66.3 \times 0.4 = 26.52 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}$$

After replication (f = 3):
$$\begin{aligned}
V_{final} &= 26.52 \times 3 = 79.56 \text{ GB/month}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Compare structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data. Provide **two real-world IoT examples** for each category and identify the most suitable storage technology. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An IoT platform must store **time-series telemetry from 10,000 devices** for **5 years**. Each device sends **one record per minute** of **200 bytes**. The system uses **replication factor 2** and achieves **compression ratio 0.3**. Compute:
  (i) Total uncompressed data generated in 5 years. **(3 marks)**
  (ii) Final storage footprint after compression and replication. **(4 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Structured | Semi-Structured | Unstructured |
|---|---|---|---|
| Schema | Fixed, rigid | Flexible, self-describing | No schema |
| Format | Tables, rows, columns | JSON, XML, YAML | Images, video, audio, text |
| IoT Example 1 | Temperature logs in MySQL | MQTT payload in JSON from sensors | CCTV footage from smart camera |
| IoT Example 2 | GPS coordinates in PostgreSQL | Device configuration in YAML | Voice commands to smart speaker |
| Best Storage | RDBMS (PostgreSQL) | Document Store (MongoDB) | Object Storage (S3) |
| Query Method | SQL | API-based, NoSQL queries | File-based, ML pipelines |

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**(i) Total Uncompressed Data in 5 Years:**

Sampling rate:
$$\begin{aligned}
R_{agg} &= 10{,}000 \times \frac{1}{60} = 166.67 \text{ records/sec}
\end{aligned}$$

Seconds in 5 years:
$$\begin{aligned}
T_{sec} &= 5 \times 365 \times 86400 = 157{,}680{,}000 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

Total records:
$$\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= 166.67 \times 157{,}680{,}000 = 2.628 \times 10^{10} \text{ records}
\end{aligned}$$

Total volume:
$$\begin{aligned}
V_{5yr} &= 2.628 \times 10^{10} \times 200 \\
&= 5.256 \times 10^{12} \text{ bytes} \\
&= 5.256 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Final Storage After Compression and Replication:**

After compression (C = 0.3):
$$\begin{aligned}
V_{compressed} &= 5.256 \times 0.3 = 1.577 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}$$

After replication (f = 2):
$$\begin{aligned}
V_{final} &= 1.577 \times 2 = 3.154 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Students often forget to convert bytes → KB → MB → GB → TB. Always show the conversion explicitly. **1 TB = 1024 GB = 1,048,576 MB.**
> 2. **Confusing SI vs Binary:** Storage vendors use 1 TB = 10^12 bytes (SI), while OS uses 1 TiB = 2^40 bytes (binary). For KTU, use **1 TB = 1024 GB** unless explicitly stated.
> 3. **Order of Compression and Replication:** Apply replication FIRST (raw size × f), THEN compression (compressed). Reversing the order gives the wrong answer in some exam scenarios — read the question carefully.
> 4. **Missing Aggregate Sampling Rate:** A frequent error is multiplying 10,000 devices × 1 record/min × 200 bytes directly. You must convert minutes to seconds first.
> 5. **No Mention of Tiered Storage Rationale:** When asked "why categorize," many students list categories but fail to explain *why* the categorization matters (cost, latency, scalability). Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this.
> 6. **Forgetting MQTT/MQTT-Specific Note:** In IoT, the **MQTT broker** is itself a form of in-motion data storage. Mentioning this in answers impresses examiners.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Data Categorization for Storage:**

- **Three Structural Categories:** Structured (RDBMS), Semi-Structured (Document Store), Unstructured (Object Storage).
- **Three Time-Criticality Tiers:** Hot (in-memory, <10 ms), Warm (SSD, seconds), Cold (archive, minutes–hours).
- **Two Motion States:** Data in Motion (Kafka, MQTT brokers) vs. Data at Rest (Data Lakes, Warehouses).
- **5V Model:** Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value — every IoT storage decision maps to one or more Vs.
- **Core Volume Formula:** $V = N \times R \times T \times S$ (devices × rate × time × sample size).
- **Replication Formula:** $V_{replicated} = V \times f$.
- **Compression Formula:** $V_{compressed} = V \times C$, where $0 < C < 1$.
- **Tiered Storage Cost Formula:** $Cost = \sum V_i \times P_i \times t_i$.
- **Time-Series DBs** (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) are the *de facto* standard for IoT sensor data.
- **Polyglot Persistence** is the architectural norm — no single database handles all IoT data categories.
- **Edge Storage** (local on gateway) handles hot, latency-critical data; **Cloud Storage** handles warm/cold data.
- **Retention Policy** is critical: define TTL (Time To Live) for each tier to control costs and comply with regulations.
- **MQTT Brokers** (Mosquitto, HiveMQ, EMQX) act as both ingestion and short-term storage layers.
- **Data Lake vs. Data Warehouse:** Lake stores raw (structured + unstructured); Warehouse stores cleaned, structured, query-optimized data.
- **For numerical problems:** Always state assumptions (1 TB = 1024 GB, time in seconds, etc.) at the start.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembly Software for the Events
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCGT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS AND SMART COMPUTING. > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Assembly Software for the Events ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Event Assembly Software** in the context of the Internet of Things (IoT) refers to the specialized low-level firmware, middleware, and operating-system-level services responsible for **collecting, queuing, dispatching, and processing asynchronous events** generated by heterogeneous physical devices, sensors, actuators, and communication interfaces within an IoT node. In the KTU 2024 syllabus, this concept is studied under the umbrella of **Event-Driven Programming for IoT End Devices**, drawing heavily from the **Contiki Operating System** model (developed by Adam Dunkels) and the **Protothread** abstraction. The assembly software acts as the *event kernel* — a glue layer that binds hardware interrupts to software-defined event handlers. **KTU Exact Terminology** to remember: - **Event**: A software representation of a real-world occurrence (e.g., sensor value crossing a threshold, packet arrival on radio, timer expiry, button press). - **Event Dispatcher**: The core routine that polls the event queue and invokes the registered handler. - **Event Queue**: A circular buffer (`struct event`) where incoming events are stored before processing. - **Protothread**: A lightweight, stack-less, conditional-event-driven thread abstraction used in memory-constrained IoT nodes. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Triage Model > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuition:** Think of an IoT node as a **busy hospital emergency room**. | Hospital Analogy | IoT Event Assembly Software Equivalent | |---|---| | Ambulance arriving with a patient | Hardware interrupt from a sensor/peripheral | | Triage nurse logging the case | Event posting into the queue (`process_post`) | | Waiting room (priority order) | Circular event queue with FIFO/dispatch ordering | | Doctor called for a specific case | Event handler / process callback | | Patient's condition getting better/worse | Conditional blocking using `PROCESS_YIELD()` / `PROCESS_WAIT_EVENT_UNTIL()` | | Multiple specialists on call | Multiple registered protothreads / processes | The **assembly software is the triage nurse** — it never heals the patient, but **without it, the doctors (event handlers) would never know when to act**. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Typical event queue size** in Contiki: **64 events** by default. - **Event data type**: `unsigned char` (8-bit event identifier → supports up to **256 distinct event types** per node). - **Typical RAM footprint** of the Contiki event kernel: **< 2 KB**. - **Latency target** for hard-real-time event dispatch in IoT: **< 10 ms** for Class-A sensor motes. - **Process states** (Protothread state machine): `PROCESS_STATE_NONE`, `PROCESS_STATE_RUNNING`, `PROCESS_STATE_CALLED`, `PROCESS_STATE_YIELDED`, `PROCESS_STATE_EXITED`, `PROCESS_STATE_FINISHED`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 PCCGT602 Module-4 syllabus places this topic at the intersection of *Software Architecture for IoT* and *Smart Computing Paradigms*. The examiner expects familiarity with the Contiki event model and the way `process_post()` and `process_start()` work. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Event Assembly Software The assembly of events in an IoT node revolves around three tightly coupled architectural pillars: #### **Pillar 1 — The Event Data Structure** Every event in an IoT OS is modeled as a lightweight C `struct`: ```c struct event { process_event_t ev; // 8-bit event identifier struct process *p; // Pointer to the target process void *data; // Pointer to event payload (NULL if none) struct event *next; // Singly-linked-list pointer }; ``` **Why this design?** Memory is scarce on IoT motes (e.g., **MSP430** has only **2 KB RAM**, **AVR ATmega328** has **2 KB**). A `struct event` typically occupies only **8–10 bytes**, allowing hundreds of pending events in just a few kilobytes. #### **Pillar 2 — The Event Queue (Linked-List Ring Buffer)** The queue is **not a fixed array** but a **singly-linked list** of `struct event` nodes. This is deliberate: - **Dynamic allocation** avoids the boundary problem of a static array. - Events are enqueued via `process_post()` and dequeued by the dispatcher. - The dispatcher scans the list from `head` to `tail` at every scheduling tick. #### **Pillar 3 — The Process / Protothread Abstraction** Each task (e.g., reading a temperature sensor every 30 s, listening for radio packets) is a **protothread** — a C function that can be suspended and resumed via `PROCESS_YIELD()` macros that internally manipulate `lc_t` (local continuation variable). ### 2.2 The Event Lifecycle — Six Logical Steps 1. **Hardware Trigger** — A peripheral raises an interrupt (e.g., ADC ready, UART byte received, DIO edge detected). 2. **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** — Minimal code runs; it calls `process_post(p, ev, data)`. 3. **Event Insertion** — A `struct event` is allocated (often from a static pool) and appended to the global event queue. 4. **Event Dispatch** — The `process_run()` function is called (either from main loop or from the bottom-half ISR). It iterates through the queue. 5. **Handler Invocation** — The matching process's `PROCESS_THREAD()` is resumed from the saved line of continuation. 6. **Yield / Wait** — The handler either completes, yields voluntarily (`PROCESS_YIELD`), or blocks conditionally (`PROCESS_WAIT_EVENT_UNTIL(c)`), releasing the CPU until the next matching event. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below condenses every formula, structural constant, and scheduler rule you must memorize for the ESE. All symbols are KTU 2024 scheme standard. | # | Concept / Quantity | Mathematical / Structural Expression | Description & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Event identifier range | $0 \le \text{ev} < 2^{8} = 256$ | `process_event_t` is `unsigned char` → max **256 unique event types** per node | | 2 | RAM per `struct event` | $\text{sizeof}(\text{struct event}) \approx 8 \text{ to } 10 \text{ bytes}$ | On 8/16-bit MCUs (MSP430, AVR) | | 3 | Linked-list queue length | $L = \frac{\text{available RAM}}{\text{sizeof}(\text{struct event})}$ | For 2 KB RAM → $L \approx 200$ events max | | 4 | Dispatch time (worst case) | $T_d = N \cdot t_h$ | $N$ = number of registered processes, $t_h$ = handler entry overhead | | 5 | Conditional wait statement | `PROCESS_WAIT_EVENT_UNTIL(c)` | Suspends protothread until event `ev` arrives AND condition `c` holds | | 6 | Process states | $\{NONE, RUNNING, CALLED, YIELDED, EXITED, FINISHED\}$ | 6-state finite machine | | 7 | CPU utilization by dispatcher | $U_{disp} = \frac{T_{busy}}{T_{total}}$ | Where $T_{busy}$ is the time the CPU is inside `process_run()` | | 8 | Protothread stack size | $S_{pt} = 0 \text{ bytes}$ (stack-less) | All local state preserved in `lc_t` continuation variable | | 9 | Power-on event registration | `process_start(struct process *p, const char *name)` | Used inside `PROCESS_THREAD()` at boot | | 10 | Event posting cost | $O(1)$ amortized | Single `process_post()` call inserts event in constant time | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Smart Agriculture**: Soil-moisture sensor events drive irrigation actuators — assembly software guarantees ordering even when 50+ sensors fire per second. - **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: Vibration events on a motor are assembled and forwarded to a cloud gateway over **6LoWPAN/CoAP**. - **Wearable Health Monitors**: ECG events are timestamped, queued, and streamed over BLE — the assembly software ensures no sample is lost during BLE handshake gaps. - **Smart Home Hubs**: Zigbee/Z-Wave button-press events are de-multiplexed to the right automation rule via the event dispatcher. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivating the Maximum Number of Concurrent Events The most important quantitative result for KTU exam problems is: *"What is the maximum number of pending events an IoT mote can hold?"* **Step 1 — Identify the available memory budget.** A typical Class-0 IoT mote (e.g., **TI CC2538**, **Zolertia Z1**) has: $$M_{RAM} = 16 \text{ KB} = 16 \times 1024 = 16384 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 2 — Subtract the static kernel footprint.** The Contiki core (event kernel, process scheduler, timer library, link-layer drivers) consumes approximately: $$M_{kernel} \approx 4 \text{ KB} = 4096 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the memory available for the event queue.** $$M_{queue} = M_{RAM} - M_{kernel} = 16384 - 4096 = 12288 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 4 — Divide by the per-event overhead.** $$M_{event} = \text{sizeof}(\text{struct event})$$ On a 32-bit ARM Cortex-M: $$M_{event} = 4 \text{ (ev)} + 4 \text{ (p)} + 4 \text{ (data)} + 4 \text{ (next)} = 16 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the maximum queue length.** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{max} &= \frac{M_{queue}}{M_{event}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{12288}{16} \\[4pt] &= 768 \text{ events} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** A single ARM Cortex-M Class-1 IoT mote can buffer up to **768 pending events** even after subtracting the kernel. For 8-bit AVR motes the figure drops to **roughly 100–150 events**. ### 3.2 Derivating the Worst-Case Dispatch Latency When **N** processes are registered and the event dispatcher must scan all of them to find the owner of an event: **Step 1 — Define the handler invocation cost.** Each process-handler entry requires the following operations: 1. Save CPU registers: $t_1 = 4$ clock cycles. 2. Load `lc_t` continuation: $t_2 = 3$ cycles. 3. Compare event ID: $t_3 = 2$ cycles. 4. Branch decision: $t_4 = 1$ cycle. **Step 2 — Per-process handler overhead.** $$t_h = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4 = 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 10 \text{ cycles}$$ **Step 3 — For a clock of 8 MHz (typical of MSP430), cycle time is:** $$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{8 \times 10^6} = 0.125 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$$ **Step 4 — Per-process scan time.** $$t_{proc} = t_h \cdot T_{clk} = 10 \times 0.125 \text{ }\mu\text{s} = 1.25 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$$ **Step 5 — Worst-case dispatch latency (scanning all N processes to find a match).** $$ \begin{aligned} T_d^{worst} &= N \cdot t_{proc} \\[4pt] &= N \cdot 1.25 \text{ }\mu\text{s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Numerical example for N = 32 registered processes:** $$T_d^{worst} = 32 \times 1.25 \text{ }\mu\text{s} = 40 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$$ This **40 µs** is well within the **10 ms** hard-real-time budget — proving the event-assembly model is suitable for most IoT control loops. ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > This comparison is a high-yield KTU exam artifact. Examiners love asking *“Differentiate between …”* in 7-mark sub-questions. | Parameter | Contiki Event Kernel | Traditional RTOS (e.g., FreeRTOS) | Bare-Metal Superloop | |---|---|---|---| | Concurrency model | Event-driven + Protothread | Pre-emptive task scheduling | Sequential polling loop | | Stack per task | **0 bytes** (stackless) | Hundreds of bytes per task | 1 stack only | | RAM footprint | **< 2 KB** | 4–16 KB | < 1 KB | | Determinism | Soft real-time | Hard real-time | Best-effort | | Latency (worst case) | $O(N)$ process scan | Bounded by tick rate | Bounded by loop period | | Power efficiency | **High** (sleep between events) | Medium (idle hook needed) | High if well-designed | | Suitable for | Class-0/1 motes | Class-2 gateways | Single-purpose sensors | | KTU 2024 relevance | **Core syllabus item** | Reference only | Mentioned as baseline | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Event Assembly Software The diagram below maps the **complete data flow** from a hardware trigger up to the application-level event handler. This is the type of figure KTU expects in a 14-mark answer. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Hardware Peripheral
Sensor, Radio, Timer, GPIO] --> B[Interrupt Service Routine ISR
Minimal context save] B --> C[process_post function call
Arguments: process pointer event ID payload] C --> D[Event Allocator
Pulls struct event from static pool] D --> E[Global Event Queue
Singly linked list FIFO] E --> F[process_run Dispatcher
Called from main loop or bottom half] F --> G{Event Match?} G -- Yes --> H[Resume Target Protothread
Restore lc_t continuation] H --> I[Execute Event Handler Body
Inside PROCESS_THREAD macro] I --> J[PROCESS_YIELD or
PROCESS_WAIT_EVENT_UNTIL] J --> K[Suspended State
Wait for next event] K --> E G -- No --> L[Skip to next process in registry] L --> F I --> M[Handler Completes] M --> F ``` ### 4.2 Finite State Machine — Protothread Lifecycle ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> NONE : process declared NONE --> RUNNING : process_start called RUNNING --> CALLED : process_run selects process CALLED --> YIELDED : PROCESS_YIELD macro YIELDED --> RUNNING : awaited event arrives CALLED --> FINISHED : PROCESS_EXIT macro FINISHED --> [*] : cleanup RUNNING --> EXITED : fatal error EXITED --> [*] ``` ### 4.3 Event Posting and Dispatching — Sequence Diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Sensor as Sensor or Peripheral participant ISR as Interrupt Service Routine participant Post as process_post participant Queue as Event Queue Linked List participant Sched as process_run Scheduler participant Handler as Application Protothread Sensor->>ISR : Hardware interrupt line goes HIGH Note over ISR : Save minimal context ISR->>Post : Call process_post with event ID and data Post->>Queue : Allocate struct event and append to tail Post-->>ISR : Return immediately ISR-->>Sensor : Acknowledge interrupt Note over Sched : Main loop or bottom half polls Sched->>Queue : Dequeue head element Queue-->>Sched : struct event instance Sched->>Handler : Resume protothread from saved lc_t Handler->>Handler : Execute handler body Handler-->>Sched : PROCESS_YIELD or completion Sched->>Sched : Move to next registered process ``` ### 4.4 Event Queue as Linked-List Ring ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1[Head Event 1
ev equals 0x05] --> Q2[Event 2
ev equals 0x11] Q2 --> Q3[Event 3
ev equals 0x07] Q3 --> Q4[Tail Event 4
ev equals 0x22] Q4 --> Q5[NULL pointer end of list] Sched[process_run dispatcher] -. reads .-> Q1 NewEv[New ISR arrives] -. appends here .-> Q4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading tip for KTU 2024:** In the exam, the examiner often asks you to draw a similar flow diagram. Keep your nodes labeled with **plain uppercase text** (as shown above) — avoid italics, bold, or HTML tables inside node labels, as the rendering rules prohibit them. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2, Q1.a]** **Q1.a.** *Define an event in the context of the Contiki event-driven IoT model. What is the maximum number of unique events a single node can address and why?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **event** in the Contiki IoT model is a software construct representing a real-world occurrence such as a sensor threshold crossing, packet arrival, or timer expiry. The event is encoded as an **8-bit unsigned integer** (`process_event_t` in C). Therefore the maximum number of unique event types is: $$N_{ev} = 2^{8} = 256 \text{ events}$$ This limit arises from the memory-constrained design philosophy of Class-0/1 IoT motes, where 8 bits strike the optimal balance between expressive power and RAM economy (1 byte per event ID). --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q1.b]** **Q1.b.** *What is a protothread? Mention any two advantages.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **protothread** is a lightweight, **stack-less** concurrency primitive introduced by Adam Dunkels for memory-constrained IoT systems. Each protothread is a C function whose execution state is preserved in a single **`lc_t` (local continuation) variable**, allowing the function to suspend and resume at arbitrary points. **Two advantages:** 1. **Zero per-thread stack allocation** — multiple protothreads can coexist in a node with only **2 KB RAM**. 2. **Simpler than full threads** — the programmer can write linear-looking C code while still achieving event-driven concurrency. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently lose 3–4 marks by (i) confusing the event ID with the process pointer, (ii) forgetting that `process_post()` returns a Boolean indicating queue overflow, and (iii) drawing protothread state diagrams with the wrong number of states (should be 6, not 4). Memorize the 6 states listed in Section 2.3 above. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module 4 Choice Option 1** > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Q12.a]** **(a)** *Explain with a neat block diagram the architecture of event assembly software in a typical IoT node based on the Contiki event kernel. Discuss the roles of `process_post()`, `process_run()`, and the event queue. (7 marks)* **Model Solution (7 marks):** The **event assembly software architecture** in a Contiki-based IoT node is built around three core components: the **Event Queue**, the **Event Dispatcher**, and the **Process Registry**. A high-level block diagram is shown below (described in words for the exam script): 1. **Hardware Layer (Bottom):** Sensors, radio, timers, GPIOs raise interrupts. 2. **ISR Layer:** Minimal ISRs invoke `process_post(p, ev, data)`. 3. **Event Queue (Middle):** A singly-linked list of `struct event` nodes holds pending events in FIFO order. 4. **Dispatcher (`process_run()`):** Iterates the queue, identifies the target process, and resumes it from its saved `lc_t`. 5. **Protothread Layer (Top):** Each process executes its `PROCESS_THREAD()` body until it yields or blocks. **Roles in detail:** - **`process_post(struct process *p, process_event_t ev, void *data)`** — Allocates a `struct event` and appends it to the global event queue. Returns **1** on success, **0** if the queue is full. - **`process_run(void)`** — The scheduler entry point. Dequeues one event at a time, finds the matching process, and resumes it. - **Event Queue** — Decouples the interrupt context from the application context, enabling **asynchronous**, **non-blocking** operation — critical for battery-powered IoT motes. --- **(b)** *A Class-1 IoT mote has 32 KB of RAM. The Contiki kernel consumes 6 KB. Each `struct event` requires 12 bytes on this platform. Calculate: (i) the maximum number of pending events, and (ii) the worst-case dispatch latency if 48 processes are registered and the MCU runs at 16 MHz with a per-process scan cost of 8 cycles. (7 marks)* **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - $M_{RAM} = 32 \text{ KB} = 32768 \text{ bytes}$ - $M_{kernel} = 6 \text{ KB} = 6144 \text{ bytes}$ - $M_{event} = 12 \text{ bytes}$ - $N = 48$ registered processes - $f_{clk} = 16 \text{ MHz}$ - $t_h = 8$ cycles per process scan **(i) Maximum pending events:** **Step 1** — Available RAM after kernel: $$M_{queue} = 32768 - 6144 = 26624 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 2** — Max queue length: $$ \begin{aligned} L_{max} &= \frac{M_{queue}}{M_{event}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{26624}{12} \\[4pt] &= 2218.67 \\[4pt] &\approx 2218 \text{ events} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer (i):** $L_{max} \approx 2218$ events. **(ii) Worst-case dispatch latency:** **Step 1** — Clock period: $$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{16 \times 10^{6}} = 0.0625 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$$ **Step 2** — Per-process scan time: $$t_{proc} = 8 \times 0.0625 \text{ }\mu\text{s} = 0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$$ **Step 3** — Worst-case scan latency: $$ \begin{aligned} T_d^{worst} &= N \cdot t_{proc} \\[4pt] &= 48 \times 0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{s} \\[4pt] &= 24 \text{ }\mu\text{s} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer (ii):** $T_d^{worst} = 24 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$. **Conclusion:** This 24 µs latency is **vastly under the 10 ms** hard-real-time budget for IoT, validating the suitability of the event-assembly model. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module 4 Choice Option 2** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Q14.b]** **(a)** *With a neat diagram, explain the six states of a protothread in the Contiki event kernel. Why are protothreads preferred over traditional threads in IoT firmware? (7 marks)* **Model Solution (7 marks):** The **protothread state diagram** consists of **six states**, each represented as a node in the FSM: | State | Description | |---|---| | `PROCESS_STATE_NONE` | Initial — process declared but not yet started | | `PROCESS_STATE_RUNNING` | Process has been started and is eligible to be scheduled | | `PROCESS_STATE_CALLED` | Dispatcher has selected and resumed this process | | `PROCESS_STATE_YIELDED` | Process called `PROCESS_YIELD()` and is suspended | | `PROCESS_STATE_EXITED` | Process has terminated abnormally | | `PROCESS_STATE_FINISHED` | Process has completed and called `PROCESS_EXIT()` | **Why protothreads are preferred:** 1. **Stack-less execution** — All local state is encoded in a single `lc_t` continuation variable (typically 2 bytes), so hundreds of protothreads can coexist in < 2 KB RAM. 2. **Simpler programming model** — The C code looks like a normal sequential function, with `PROCESS_WAIT_EVENT_UNTIL()` providing clean event-driven flow. 3. **Deterministic** — No pre-emption, so the scheduler is fully cooperative and reproducible. 4. **Energy efficiency** — The CPU can be put to sleep between events, which is critical for battery-powered nodes lasting **years** on a single coin cell. --- **(b)** *Compare event-driven IoT firmware (Contiki model) with the traditional RTOS approach and the bare-metal superloop. Which is most suitable for a Class-0 mote with 4 KB RAM and why? (7 marks)* **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Comparison Table (to be drawn in the answer book):** | Parameter | Contiki Event Kernel | RTOS (FreeRTOS) | Bare-Metal Superloop | |---|---|---|---| | RAM footprint | **< 2 KB** | 4–16 KB | < 1 KB | | Concurrency | Cooperative (protothreads) | Pre-emptive (tasks) | Sequential | | Determinism | Soft real-time | Hard real-time | Best-effort | | Complexity | Low | High | Lowest | | Power efficiency | **High** (event-driven sleep) | Medium | High (if well-written) | **Suitability for a 4 KB RAM Class-0 mote:** The **Contiki event-driven model** is the **most suitable** for the following quantitative reasons: 1. **Memory fit:** With 4 KB RAM and ~2 KB kernel, only ~2 KB remains for application code — too small for an RTOS, but **ample for hundreds of protothreads**. 2. **Energy budget:** Class-0 motes sleep for > 99% of their lifetime. The event kernel wakes only on interrupts, whereas a bare-metal superloop must poll continuously (wasting energy) and an RTOS must tick its scheduler (waking the CPU). 3. **Concurrency without stacks:** A bare-metal superloop cannot block on multiple events; an RTOS would consume too much RAM. Protothreads provide **stack-less blocking**, the perfect middle ground. **Conclusion:** The **Contiki event assembly software** is the **optimal choice** for the described Class-0 mote. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4** - ⭐ **Event** = 8-bit unsigned identifier (`process_event_t`), max **256** per node. - ⭐ **`process_post()`** enqueues an event; **`process_run()`** dispatches it. - ⭐ **Protothread** = stack-less C function, state held in **`lc_t`** (2 bytes). - ⭐ **Six protothread states**: `NONE, RUNNING, CALLED, YIELDED, EXITED, FINISHED`. - ⭐ **Event queue** is a **singly-linked list**, not a static array. - ⭐ **Default queue capacity on Contiki** = **64 events** (configurable). - ⭐ **Worst-case dispatch latency** $T_d = N \cdot t_h$ — linear in number of processes. - ⭐ **`PROCESS_WAIT_EVENT_UNTIL(c)`** blocks until the event arrives **AND** condition `c` holds. - ⭐ **RAM budget for Class-0 motes** = **< 10 KB total**; event kernel is the only feasible scheduler. - ⭐ **Pre-empted?** No — Contiki is **cooperative**; processes yield voluntarily. - ⭐ **Power efficiency** comes from sleeping between events — the event kernel is the *enabler* of multi-year battery life. - ⭐ **Origin**: Contiki OS by **Adam Dunkels** (SICS, Sweden) — refer to the *Protothreads* paper if the examiner asks for references. - ⭐ **Exam tip**: Always draw the **3-block diagram** (Hardware → Queue → Dispatcher → Process) when asked about event architecture. - ⭐ **Pitfall**: Never claim that protothreads use a stack — that is a **common 1-mark deduction**. ---

topics.title: Data Store
topics.content:
# 📘 Module 4 — Data Store in IoT ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 🔹 Formal Academic Definition In the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)**, a **Data Store** is the persistent, queryable, and managed storage layer responsible for ingesting, retaining, indexing, and serving the vast streams of heterogeneous data generated by connected sensors, actuators, edge gateways, and smart devices. It acts as the **system of record** for IoT telemetry, configuration, and metadata, and forms the foundational layer of the IoT data pipeline that enables analytics, machine learning, and real-time actuation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCCGT602 – Module 4):** > *A Data Store in IoT refers to the database/storage infrastructure that supports the capture, retrieval, and management of sensor-generated data. It includes local, edge, and cloud storage models, time-series databases, NoSQL stores, and data lakes engineered for high-velocity, high-volume IoT workloads.* ### 🔹 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Refrigerator of Data" Think of an IoT system as a **massive, automated grocery supply chain**: - 🏭 **Sensors & Devices** are the **farms and factories** that constantly produce fresh produce (data). - 🚚 **Network Gateways** are the **delivery trucks** that carry this produce. - 🏬 **The Data Store** is the **refrigerated warehouse** — it doesn't just dump everything in one pile; it organizes, chills (indexes), labels (schemas), and shelves items (data) so they can be retrieved instantly when needed. - 🛒 **Analytics & Apps** are the **shopkeepers** who walk in, pick what they need, and put it on the shelf for the customer (end-user). Without a well-designed warehouse, the freshest produce rots before it reaches the customer — similarly, without a proper data store, IoT data becomes useless. ### 🔹 Core Characteristics of an IoT Data Store An IoT-grade data store must satisfy the **5V Big Data challenge**: | Characteristic | Meaning in IoT Context | |---|---| | **Velocity** | Handle millions of sensor readings per second | | **Volume** | Scale to petabytes/exabytes of stored telemetry | | **Variety** | Support structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data | | **Veracity** | Ensure data accuracy, validity, and trustworthiness | | **Value** | Enable extraction of actionable insights | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** The data store is **NOT** just a database. It is the *entire storage ecosystem* — including ingestion buffers, indexing engines, replication, partitioning, and retrieval APIs — designed to cope with IoT's unique traffic patterns. --- ### 🔹 Why IoT Cannot Use Traditional Databases Effectively | Parameter | Traditional RDBMS (e.g., MySQL) | IoT-Grade Data Store (e.g., InfluxDB, Cassandra) | |---|---|---| | Data Nature | Transactional, row-based | Time-stamped, append-heavy | | Scale | Vertical (single powerful server) | Horizontal (distributed cluster) | | Schema | Rigid, predefined | Flexible / schema-on-read | | Write Speed | Thousands/sec | Millions/sec | | Query Pattern | Ad-hoc SQL | Time-range & tag-based | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of IoT Data Stores IoT data stores are classified along **three orthogonal axes**: *location, structure, and access pattern*. ### 🅰️ By Location / Deployment | Type | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **Local Storage** | On-device flash/SD card storage | EEPROM, microSD on Arduino/RPi | | **Edge Storage** | On local gateway before cloud upload | Raspberry Pi with SQLite, local NAS | | **Cloud Storage** | Centralized, multi-tenant, elastic | AWS S3, Azure Blob, Google BigQuery | | **Hybrid Storage** | Tiered: hot data on edge, cold data in cloud | Edge caches + AWS S3 Glacier | ### 🅱️ By Data Structure | Type | Best For | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Relational (SQL)** | Structured metadata, device registry | MySQL, PostgreSQL | | **NoSQL – Document** | Semi-structured JSON payloads | MongoDB, CouchDB | | **NoSQL – Key-Value** | Fast lookups, session data | Redis, Amazon DynamoDB | | **NoSQL – Columnar** | Wide rows, high write throughput | Apache Cassandra, HBase | | **Time-Series Database (TSDB)** | Sensor time-stamped data | InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, OpenTSDB | | **Data Lake** | Raw, multi-format archival | AWS S3 + Hadoop, Azure Data Lake | | **Graph Database** | Device-to-device relationship mapping | Neo4j | ### 🅲️ By Access Pattern (CAP Triangle Application) In distributed IoT systems, only **two of three** properties can be guaranteed (Eric Brewer's **CAP Theorem**): $$ \text{CAP} = \underbrace{C}_{\text{Consistency}} + \underbrace{A}_{\text{Availability}} + \underbrace{P}_{\text{Partition Tolerance}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > In IoT, **network partitions are inevitable**, so the choice is between **CP** (consistency) and **AP** (availability). | Type | Use Case | Example | |---|---|---| | **CP Store** | Critical control systems (industrial IoT) | HBase, MongoDB (with config) | | **AP Store** | Telemetry dashboards, smart home | Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB | --- ## 2.2 Time-Series Databases (TSDB) — The Heart of IoT Most IoT data is **time-stamped** (sensor reading at time *t*). A Time-Series Database is purpose-built for this workload. ### 🕒 Anatomy of a TSDB Record A single record typically contains: $$ \text{Record} = \{\, t, \, \text{measurement}, \, \text{tag-set}, \, \text{field-set} \,\} $$ | Component | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---| | **Timestamp (t)** | When the reading was taken | `2024-11-15T10:32:14.221Z` | | **Measurement** | What is being measured | `temperature`, `humidity` | | **Tag-set** | Indexed metadata for fast filtering | `sensor_id=23, location=lab3` | | **Field-set** | Actual measured values | `value=27.4, unit=C` | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** In InfluxDB, **tags are indexed** (fast lookup) and **fields are not** (slow to query but efficient to store). This distinction is a favorite KTU short-answer question. ### 🕒 Storage Optimization in TSDB TSDBs use three core techniques to handle billions of points: 1. **Data Batching & Compression** - Records are buffered in memory and flushed in batches. - Compression algorithms like **Gorilla** (Facebook), **Delta-of-Delta**, and **Run-Length Encoding** reduce storage by up to **90%**. 2. **Downsampling & Retention Policies** - High-resolution data kept for short periods (e.g., raw data for **7 days**). - Aggregated data kept for long periods (e.g., hourly averages for **5 years**). 3. **Data Tiering** $$ \underbrace{\text{Hot (RAM + SSD)}}_{\text{Recent, queried often}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Warm (HDD)}}_{\text{Older, less frequent}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Cold (Object Storage)}}_{\text{Archive, rarely accessed}} $$ --- ## 2.3 Data Lake vs Data Warehouse vs Data Store > [!IMPORTANT] > A **Data Lake** stores *raw* data in native format. A **Data Warehouse** stores *processed, schema-applied* data. A **Data Store** is the umbrella term covering both, plus operational stores. | Property | Data Lake | Data Warehouse | Operational Data Store | |---|---|---|---| | Data Type | Raw, all formats | Cleansed, structured | Real-time, transactional | | Schema | Schema-on-read | Schema-on-write | Schema-on-write | | Users | Data scientists | Business analysts | Applications | | Cost | Low (object storage) | High (compute-heavy) | Medium | | Latency | High (batch) | Medium | Low (real-time) | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Description | |---|---|---| | **CAP Theorem** | $C \cap A \cap P \le 2$ | Only 2 of 3 guarantees possible | | **Write Throughput Estimate** | $W = \dfrac{N_{\text{sensors}} \cdot f_{\text{sampling}}}{T_{\text{flush}}}$ | Sensors × frequency ÷ flush interval | | **Storage Growth Rate** | $S(t) = S_0 + R \cdot t$ | $S_0$ = initial size, $R$ = rate, $t$ = time | | **Compression Ratio** | $C_r = \dfrac{\text{Uncompressed Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | Typical IoT: 5× to 10× | | **Gorilla Compression** | $\Delta t = t_{n+1} - t_n$ stored as variable-length integer | Saves up to 98% on timestamps | | **Retention Policy** | $R_p = \{R_{\text{raw}}, R_{\text{agg}}, T_{\text{downsample}}\}$ | Define what is kept and for how long | | **Sharding Key Choice** | $\text{shard}(\text{device\_id}) = \text{device\_id} \bmod N_{\text{shards}}$ | Distribute load evenly | > [!NOTE] > In **KTU exams**, you may be asked to compute storage requirements for an IoT deployment. The growth equation $S(t) = S_0 + R \cdot t$ is the most-tested formula. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Data Store Choice | Why | |---|---|---| | **Smart Agriculture** | InfluxDB at edge + S3 cloud | High-frequency sensor data, offline tolerance | | **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | Cassandra + Kafka | High write throughput, AP guarantees | | **Healthcare Wearables** | TimescaleDB (PostgreSQL) | HIPAA compliance, SQL familiarity | | **Smart Cities** | HBase + HDFS | Petabyte scale, batch analytics | | **Connected Vehicles** | Redis (hot) + Cassandra (warm) | Real-time alerts + historical telemetry | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Problem Framework: Sizing an IoT Data Store A KTU-style analytical question typically asks: *"Given N sensors, sampling at frequency f, how much storage is required over time T?"* ### 🧮 Given Parameters - Number of sensors: $N = 10{,}000$ - Sampling rate: $f = 1$ reading per second - Bytes per record: $B = 128$ bytes - Operational period: $T = 1$ year - Compression ratio: $C_r = 5$ ### 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Compute total readings per day** $$ N_{\text{day}} = N \times f \times 86{,}400 $$ $$ N_{\text{day}} = 10{,}000 \times 1 \times 86{,}400 $$ $$ N_{\text{day}} = 8.64 \times 10^{8} \;\text{readings/day} $$ **Step 2: Compute readings per year** $$ N_{\text{year}} = N_{\text{day}} \times 365 $$ $$ N_{\text{year}} = 8.64 \times 10^{8} \times 365 $$ $$ N_{\text{year}} = 3.1536 \times 10^{11} \;\text{readings/year} $$ **Step 3: Compute raw uncompressed storage** $$ S_{\text{raw}} = N_{\text{year}} \times B $$ $$ S_{\text{raw}} = 3.1536 \times 10^{11} \times 128 $$ $$ S_{\text{raw}} = 4.0366 \times 10^{13} \;\text{bytes} $$ Converting to terabytes: $$ S_{\text{raw}} = \dfrac{4.0366 \times 10^{13}}{1024^{4}} \approx \mathbf{36.71\;TB} $$ **Step 4: Apply compression ratio** $$ S_{\text{compressed}} = \dfrac{S_{\text{raw}}}{C_r} $$ $$ S_{\text{compressed}} = \dfrac{36.71}{5} \approx \mathbf{7.34\;TB} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** For 10,000 sensors sampling at 1 Hz, with 5× compression, a single year requires only **~7.34 TB** — a manageable size for modern cloud object storage. This is why **time-series compression is non-negotiable** in IoT. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Selecting the Right Data Store Use this decision matrix in KTU exam answers when asked *"Which data store would you choose for scenario X?"* | Requirement | Recommended Store | Justification | |---|---|---| | Sub-millisecond reads of current state | **Redis (Key-Value)** | In-memory, O(1) lookups | | Petabytes of historical sensor data | **Cassandra / HBase** | Linear horizontal scaling | | SQL-compatible analytics on time series | **TimescaleDB** | PostgreSQL extension, SQL support | | Storing raw binary image/sound from sensors | **S3 / Azure Blob (Data Lake)** | Cheap, schema-less, petabyte-scale | | Strict consistency for safety alarms | **MongoDB (CP config) / HBase** | Strong consistency, no stale reads | | Offline-first edge storage | **SQLite / LevelDB** | Embedded, zero-config, low-resource | | Relationship queries (device-to-device graph) | **Neo4j** | Native graph traversal, Cypher query | --- ## 3.3 Write-Throughput Bound Proof (Useful for KTU Numericals) Given a cluster of $N_{\text{shards}}$ nodes, each with maximum write rate $W_{\max}$ (writes/sec): $$ W_{\text{cluster}}^{\max} = N_{\text{shards}} \times W_{\max} $$ For the system to handle the IoT load without queuing: $$ W_{\text{cluster}}^{\max} \;\ge\; N_{\text{sensors}} \times f_{\text{sampling}} $$ Solving for minimum shards: $$ N_{\text{shards}}^{\min} = \left\lceil \dfrac{N_{\text{sensors}} \times f_{\text{sampling}}}{W_{\max}} \right\rceil $$ > [!EXAMPLE] > **Worked Example:** A smart factory has 50,000 sensors reporting at 10 Hz. Each Cassandra node can sustain 5,000 writes/sec. > > $$ > N_{\text{shards}}^{\min} = \left\lceil \dfrac{50{,}000 \times 10}{5{,}000} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 100 \right\rceil = \mathbf{100\;shards} > $$ --- ## 3.4 Retention Policy Derivation Define: - $R_{\text{raw}}$ = retention of raw high-resolution data - $R_{\text{agg}}$ = retention of aggregated data - $f_{\text{downsample}}$ = downsampling factor Total effective storage for one metric: $$ S_{\text{total}} = \underbrace{\dfrac{R_{\text{raw}} \cdot N \cdot B}{C_r}}_{\text{Hot Tier}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{R_{\text{agg}} \cdot N \cdot B}{C_r \cdot f_{\text{downsample}}}}_{\text{Cold Tier}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This equation is **not in any textbook by name** — it is a derived expression that examiners love because it combines all IoT data-store concepts. Use it in 14-mark questions for full marks. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 IoT Data Store Architecture (End-to-End Pipeline) ```mermaid flowchart TB sensors["IoT Sensors and Devices"] --> gateway["Edge Gateway"] gateway --> mqtt["MQTT or CoAP Broker"] mqtt --> streamproc["Stream Processor Kafka or Flink"] streamproc --> hotstore["Hot Store InfluxDB or Redis"] streamproc --> warmstore["Warm Store Cassandra or TimescaleDB"] streamproc --> lakestore["Data Lake S3 or HDFS"] hotstore --> dashboard["Real-time Dashboard"] warmstore --> analytics["Batch Analytics and ML"] lakestore --> ml["Machine Learning Pipelines"] analytics --> actsystem["Actuation and Control System"] dashboard --> userapp["User Mobile and Web Apps"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram in an exam:** Trace data flow from sensor → gateway → broker → stream processor → tiered storage → consumer applications. The **three-tier storage** (hot/warm/cold) is a frequent KTU sub-question. --- ## 4.2 Time-Series Data Store — Internal Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR ingest["Ingestion API"] --> buffer["Write Buffer WAL"] buffer --> cache["In-Memory Cache Shard Groups"] cache --> indexer["Tag Index TSI"] cache --> tsmergestore["Time-Structured Merge Tree TSM"] tsmergestore --> compactor["Compactor Service"] compactor --> diskstore["Persistent Disk Storage"] diskstore --> queryengine["Query Engine Flux or SQL"] queryengine --> clientapp["Client Application"] ``` **Component Roles:** | Component | Role | |---|---| | **Ingestion API** | Receives writes from MQTT/Kafka | | **Write Buffer / WAL** | Durability guarantee for uncommitted writes | | **In-Memory Cache** | Hot data for low-latency reads | | **Tag Index (TSI)** | Inverted index for fast tag-based filtering | | **TSM (Time-Structured Merge Tree)** | Sorted, compressed columnar storage | | **Compactor** | Merges small TSM files to reduce read amplification | | **Query Engine** | Translates Flux/SQL into physical operators | --- ## 4.3 CAP Theorem Selection Flow for IoT Data Store ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Choose IoT Data Store"] --> q1{"Is the workload
mission-critical
(safety alarms)?"} q1 -- Yes --> cp["Use CP Store
HBase or MongoDB"] q1 -- No --> q2{"Is real-time
read latency
sub-10ms required?"} q2 -- Yes --> ap_fast["Use AP Store with cache
Redis + Cassandra"] q2 -- No --> q3{"Is the data
strongly time-stamped?"} q3 -- Yes --> tsdb["Use TSDB
InfluxDB or TimescaleDB"] q3 -- No --> gen["Use Document Store
MongoDB or CouchDB"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > This CAP-selection flow is a **favorite 7-mark sub-question** in KTU 14-mark questions. Memorize the decision points. --- ## 4.4 Data Store Tiering Model ```mermaid flowchart LR edge["Edge Storage
Local Flash"] -->|periodic sync| fog["Fog Storage
Gateway NAS"] fog -->|streamed batch| cloudhot["Cloud Hot Tier
SSD-backed DB"] cloudhot -->|rollup| cloudwarm["Cloud Warm Tier
HDD-backed Columnar DB"] cloudwarm -->|aged data| cloudcold["Cloud Cold Tier
S3 Glacier or Tape"] ``` | Tier | Typical Latency | Typical Cost/GB | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Edge** | < 1 ms | High (flash) | Real-time control loops | | **Fog** | 1–10 ms | Medium | Local aggregation, alerts | | **Cloud Hot** | 10–50 ms | Medium | Recent telemetry (7–30 days) | | **Cloud Warm** | 100–500 ms | Low | Historical analytics (1 year) | | **Cloud Cold** | Seconds–minutes | Very low | Compliance archival (5–10 years) | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 🅐 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** *Define an IoT Data Store. List any two key requirements it must satisfy.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** An IoT Data Store is the persistent storage layer in an IoT architecture that ingests, retains, and serves the data produced by sensors, actuators, gateways, and smart devices for analytics, control, and decision-making. Two key requirements: 1. **High write throughput** — Must handle millions of concurrent sensor writes per second. 2. **Horizontal scalability** — Must scale by adding nodes, not by upgrading a single server. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two requirements)* --- ### **Q2.
