```mermaid
    cloud3 --> dash1
    cloud4 --> dash1

    cloud4 --> act1
    cloud4 --> act2
    cloud4 --> act3
    cloud4 --> act4
```

## 4.2 Closed-Loop Smart Irrigation Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1["Sense Soil Moisture"] --> a2["ADC Read on ESP32"]
    a2 --> a3["Compute VWC Percent"]
    a3 --> a4{"VWC less than SMT threshold"}
    a4 -- Yes --> a5["Publish MQTT On Command"]
    a4 -- No --> a6["No Action Log Status"]
    a5 --> a7["Gateway Receives Command"]
    a7 --> a8["Activate Relay Module"]
    a8 --> a9["Open Solenoid Valve"]
    a9 --> a10["Pump Runs for t seconds"]
    a10 --> a11["Re-Sense and Verify"]
    a11 --> a3
```

## 4.3 Smart Farming System Block Diagram (Hardware View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pwr["Solar Panel 6V 2W + Li-ion 18650 Battery 3.7V 3000mAh"]
    mcu["ESP32 DevKit V1 Dual Core 240 MHz WiFi Bluetooth"]
    s1["Capacitive Soil Sensor v1.2"]
    s2["DHT22 Air Sensor"]
    s3["Rain Drop Sensor"]
    s4["BH1750 Light Intensity"]

    pwr --> mcu
    s1 --> mcu
    s2 --> mcu
    s3 --> mcu
    s4 --> mcu

    mcu --> mqtt["MQTT Publish to HiveMQ Cloud"]
    mqtt --> nodeRed["Node-RED Flow on Raspberry Pi"]
    nodeRed --> influx["InfluxDB Time Series Store"]
    nodeRed --> graf["Grafana Visualization"]
    nodeRed --> rule["IFTTT Rule Engine"]
    rule --> relay["5V Relay Module"]
    relay --> pump["12V DC Water Pump"]
```

## 4.4 Layered Reference Model (KTU Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5["L5 Application Layer: Smart Farming App, Decision Support"]
    L4["L4 Middleware Layer: Cloud Platform, Database, ML Analytics"]
    L3["L3 Network Layer: MQTT, CoAP, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, 4G"]
    L2["L2 Edge Layer: Gateway, Edge Analytics, Local Storage"]
    L1["L1 Perception Layer: Sensors, Actuators, Cameras, Drones"]
    L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Smart Farming. List any FOUR IoT components used in a smart farming system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Smart Farming is an integrated approach to modern agriculture that uses **IoT sensors, communication networks, cloud/edge computing, and actuators** to monitor and automate farming operations for optimized resource use and yield.

Four components:
1. **Sensors** — soil moisture, DHT22 (temperature/humidity), pH, NPK.
2. **Communication module** — LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, or WiFi.
3. **Microcontroller/Gateway** — ESP32, Raspberry Pi.
4. **Actuators** — solenoid valves, pumps, fans, drones.
5. **Cloud platform** — AWS IoT, ThingSpeak, Blynk.

*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark each × 4 components = 3 marks)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of MQTT in smart farming? Why is LoRaWAN preferred over WiFi for rural deployment?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Role of MQTT (1.5 marks):**
MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) is a lightweight **publish-subscribe** messaging protocol used to transmit sensor data from field nodes to the cloud. It uses minimal bandwidth (2-byte header), runs over TCP, and supports QoS levels 0, 1, 2 — ideal for low-power, intermittent agricultural sensors.

**Why LoRaWAN over WiFi (1.5 marks):**
- **Range**: LoRaWAN covers up to **10–15 km** in rural areas vs WiFi's ~50 m.
- **Power**: LoRaWAN nodes can run on a battery for **5–10 years** vs WiFi's hours/days.
- **Cost**: No cellular subscription; license-free sub-GHz ISM band.
- **Penetration**: Sub-GHz signals penetrate foliage and walls better than 2.4 GHz WiFi.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **four-layer architecture** of a smart farming IoT system. Describe the perception and actuation layers in detail with examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A capacitive soil moisture sensor connected to an ESP32 (12-bit ADC, 3.3 V reference) produces digital counts of **1800** in dry soil and **3600** in saturated soil. Calibrate the sensor so that it directly outputs **Volumetric Water Content (%)** given that the dry and saturated VWC values are **0%** and **45%** respectively. Then determine the VWC when the sensor reads **2700**.

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The four-layer architecture of smart farming IoT:

1. **Perception Layer (Sensing) — 2 marks**
   - Consists of physical sensors that convert environmental quantities into electrical signals.
   - Examples: DHT22 (temperature & humidity), capacitive soil moisture sensor, DS18B20 (soil temperature), MQ-135 (gas/CO₂), NPK sensor, pH sensor, BH1750 (light), and UAV cameras.
   - Communicates with edge nodes via I²C, SPI, UART, or analog GPIO.

2. **Network/Communication Layer — 1.5 marks**
   - Transports sensor data to the cloud/edge using protocols like **MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, Zigbee, 6LoWPAN**.
   - Choice depends on **range, bandwidth, and power budget**.
   - Rural Kerala deployments favour **LoRaWAN** for long range; greenhouse deployments use **WiFi/Zigbee**.

3. **Middleware/Cloud Layer — 1.5 marks**
   - Handles data ingestion, storage (InfluxDB, MongoDB), analytics (ML, time-series forecasting), and visualization (Grafana, custom dashboards).
   - Runs **decision rule engines** that trigger actions.
   - Platforms: AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, ThingSpeak, Blynk, Kaa IoT.

4. **Application/Actuation Layer — 2 marks**
   - The action layer that interacts with the physical world via actuators.
   - Examples: **solenoid valves** (drip irrigation), **DC pumps**, **servo motors** (robotic greenhouses), **ventilation fans**, **drone sprayers**, and **greenhouse roof openers**.
   - Receives MQTT commands from the middleware and converts them to GPIO/relay control signals.

**Conclusion**: The four layers form a **closed-loop cyber-physical system** where physical state → digital decision → physical action.

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- ESP32 ADC: **12-bit** → $2^{12} = 4096$ levels
- Reference voltage: $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V
- Two calibration points: $(D_1, VWC_1) = (1800, 0\%)$ and $(D_2, VWC_2) = (3600, 45\%)$
- Unknown reading: $D_3 = 2700$

**Step 1: Linear equation form (1 mark)**

$$VWC = a \cdot D + b$$

**Step 2: Compute slope $a$ (2 marks)**

$$a = \frac{VWC_2 - VWC_1}{D_2 - D_1} = \frac{45 - 0}{3600 - 1800} = \frac{45}{1800} = 0.025 \; \%/\text{count}$$

**Step 3: Compute intercept $b$ (1 mark)**

$$b = VWC_1 - a \cdot D_1 = 0 - 0.025 \cdot 1800 = -45$$

**Step 4: Final calibration equation (1 mark)**

$$\boxed{VWC\% = 0.025 \cdot D - 45}$$

**Step 5: Compute VWC at D = 2700 (1 mark)**

$$VWC\% = 0.025 \cdot 2700 - 45 = 67.5 - 45 = 22.5\%$$

**Step 6: Interpretation (1 mark)**

A VWC of **22.5%** indicates moderate soil moisture — below the **field capacity (~30%)** but above the **wilting point (~10%)**. If this falls below the configured threshold (e.g., 20%), the smart irrigation system would trigger watering.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **closed-loop smart irrigation system** with a neat block diagram. Explain how **Sense → Decide → Actuate** cycle operates using a cloud-based MQTT rule engine.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 1-hectare paddy field in Thrissur has a current soil moisture deficit of **15% VWC**. The soil has root zone depth of **0.5 m**, field capacity = **35%**, and a 5 HP pump delivers **1500 L/min**. Calculate the **total water volume** required and the **pump run-time** to refill the root zone to field capacity. Comment on the **Water Use Efficiency** if the expected yield is 6 tonnes/hectare.

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Block diagram (3 marks):**
(Draw: Sensors → ESP32 → LoRaWAN Gateway → MQTT Broker → Cloud Rule Engine → Relay → Pump/Valve → Feedback to sensors)

**Closed-loop operation (4 marks):**

1. **Sense Stage**: Capacitive soil sensors at multiple locations (e.g., 5 nodes per hectare) capture real-time VWC. ESP32 reads ADC and publishes a JSON payload:
   `{"node_id":"N3","vwc":18.4,"ts":"2024-07-15T10:00:00Z"}` to MQTT topic `farm/z3/sensor`.

2. **Transmit Stage**: LoRaWAN gateway receives the packet, forwards to MQTT broker (e.g., HiveMQ) over 4G backhaul.

3. **Decide Stage**: A cloud-hosted **Node-RED / AWS IoT rule engine** evaluates:
   - IF `vwc < 20%` AND `rain_forecast == 0` THEN publish `{"cmd":"pump_on","duration_s":1800}` to topic `farm/z3/actuator`.

4. **Actuate Stage**: Edge gateway subscribes to actuator topic, triggers a **5 V relay → 12 V pump contactor → 1.5 kW pump** for the specified duration.

5. **Feedback**: Sensors re-measure after the cycle; loop continues. This forms a **negative feedback closed loop** stabilizing VWC near the **set-point (e.g., 25%)**.

**Conclusion**: Such systems reduce water use by 30–50% compared to flood irrigation.

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- Area $A = 1$ hectare $= 10{,}000$ m²
- Deficit $\Delta VWC = 15\%$
- Root depth $d_{root} = 0.5$ m
- Field capacity $FC = 35\%$ (so current $VWC = 35 - 15 = 20\%$)
- Pump flow $q = 1500$ L/min
- Expected yield $Y = 6000$ kg

**Step 1: Soil volume in root zone (1 mark)**

$$V_{soil} = A \cdot d_{root} = 10{,}000 \cdot 0.5 = 5{,}000 \;\text{m}^3$$

**Step 2: Water volume required (2 marks)**

Using 1% VWC = 10 L/m³ of soil:

$$V_{water} = \Delta VWC \cdot 10 \cdot V_{soil} = 15 \cdot 10 \cdot 5000 = 750{,}000 \;\text{L} = 750 \;\text{m}^3$$

**Step 3: Pump run-time (2 marks)**

$$t = \frac{V_{water}}{q} = \frac{750{,}000 \;\text{L}}{1500 \;\text{L/min}} = 500 \;\text{minutes} \approx 8.33 \;\text{hours}$$

**Step 4: Water Use Efficiency (WUE) (1.5 marks)**

$$WUE = \frac{Y}{W} = \frac{6000 \;\text{kg}}{750 \;\text{m}^3} = 8 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 5: Comment (0.5 mark)**

A WUE of **8 kg/m³** is excellent for paddy (typical range 3–10 kg/m³). Smart irrigation can push this further by avoiding over-watering, achieving yields above 10 kg/m³.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-deduction traps in Smart Farming questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Always write VWC **in %**, time in **seconds/minutes**, area in **m² or hectare**. A numerical answer without units → loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up ADC bits** — ESP32 ADC is **12-bit (0–4095)**, Arduino Uno is **10-bit (0–1023)**. Wrong $2^n$ → wrong calibration → 2 marks lost.
> 3. **Confusing VWC and gravimetric moisture** — VWC is volumetric (volume of water / volume of soil), gravimetric is mass-based. The formula $1\% VWC = 10\,L/m^3$ is for VWC only.
> 4. **Wrong MQTT topic hierarchy** — KTU expects structured topics like `farm/zone3/sensor/moisture`. Vague topics lose application-layer marks.
> 5. **Omitting the closed-loop justification** — When asked about irrigation, examiners expect the answer to mention **feedback** and **set-point** explicitly, not just one-way sensing.
> 6. **No block diagram in 7-mark questions** — A 7-mark "explain" question without a labelled diagram is capped at ~5 marks in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Smart Farming (Module 4)**
>
> ✅ **Definition**: Smart farming = IoT + data analytics + automation in agriculture.
>
> ✅ **Four layers**: Perception → Network → Middleware (Cloud) → Application/Actuation.
>
> ✅ **Sense–Decide–Actuate** is the core closed-loop paradigm.
>
> ✅ **Key sensors**: Soil moisture, DHT22, DS18B20, pH, NPK, BH1750, MQ-135.
>
> ✅ **Key actuators**: Solenoid valve, DC pump, fan, servo, drone.
>
> ✅ **Communication protocols**: MQTT (app layer), LoRaWAN/NB-IoT (long range), WiFi/Zigbee (short range).
>
> ✅ **Calibration equation**: $VWC\% = a \cdot ADC + b$ — must derive $a$ and $b$ from two reference points.
>
> ✅ **Irrigation volume**: $V_{water} = \Delta VWC \cdot 10 \cdot A \cdot d_{root}$ (in litres).
>
> ✅ **Evapotranspiration**: $ET_c = K_c \cdot ET_0$ (FAO-56 model).
>
> ✅ **Water Use Efficiency**: $WUE = \text{Yield (kg)} / \text{Water (m³)}$ — higher is better.
>
> ✅ **Smart farming benefits**: 30–50% water savings, 15–25% yield increase, lower chemical use.
>
> ✅ **Field capacity ≈ 30% VWC**; **Wilting point ≈ 10% VWC** for loam.
>
> ✅ **LoRaWAN range**: 10–15 km rural; **MQTT** is the de-facto IoT messaging protocol.
>
> ✅ **KTU-favourite diagram**: Always include a labelled block diagram for any "explain" question worth ≥ 7 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Home Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCGT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS AND SMART COMPUTING. > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Home Automation ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > **Home Automation (also called Smart Home or Domotics)** is the IoT-driven engineering discipline that uses sensors, actuators, embedded controllers, communication protocols, and cloud platforms to remotely monitor, control, and automate household appliances and environmental parameters (lighting, temperature, security, entertainment, energy) with minimal human intervention. Mathematically, a Home Automation System (HAS) can be modeled as a closed-loop cyber-physical system: $$HAS = \{S, A, C, N, D\}$$ Where: - $S$ = Set of Sensors (temperature, motion, gas, light, humidity) - $A$ = Set of Actuators (relays, motors, dimmers, smart locks) - $C$ = Central Controller (Microcontroller / SoC / Hub) - $N$ = Network Layer (Wi-Fi, Zigbee, Z-Wave, BLE, MQTT) - $D$ = Decision Engine (Rule-based logic + ML/AI) > [!NOTE] **Key Industry Standard Metrics** > - **Latency Target:** < 200 ms for control commands > - **Uptime SLA:** 99.9% for security subsystems > - **Power Budget:** < 5 W per node for battery-operated sensors > - **Encryption Standard:** AES-128 / TLS 1.3 mandatory > - **Data Sampling Rate:** 1 Hz typical for environmental sensors ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Butler" Model > [!TIP] **Intuitive Analogy for First-Time Learners** > Imagine hiring an invisible butler who lives inside your house walls. This butler: > - **Listens** to every room via tiny microphones and motion sensors (senses) > - **Remembers** your daily routines — when you wake up, leave, return, sleep (rule engine) > - **Acts** by turning on geysers, dimming lights, locking doors (actuators) > - **Talks** to you through your phone or voice assistant (interface) > - **Learns** your preferences over time and anticipates needs (AI/ML) > > That invisible butler is essentially a **Home Automation System** — a network of digital senses and reflexes distributed across your living space. ### 1.3 Why Home Automation Matters in KTU 2024 Curriculum | Engineering Driver | Real-World Impact | |---|---| | **Energy Efficiency** | Up to **30% reduction** in household electricity bills | | **Elderly Care** | Fall detection, medication reminders via ambient sensors | | **Security** | Real-time intrusion alerts with video verification | | **Convenience** | Single-tap control of 50+ appliances | | **Data Analytics** | Occupancy pattern mining for HVAC optimization | ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Layered Architecture of a Home Automation System A typical KTU-grade Home Automation architecture is structured into **5 functional layers**, often mapped to a simplified **OSI-inspired model**: #### Layer 1 — Perception / Sensing Layer - Physical sensors convert environmental parameters into electrical signals. - Common transducers: **DHT22** (temp/humidity), **PIR HC-SR501** (motion), **MQ-2** (gas), **LDR** (light). - Sensor output is typically analog → fed to **ADC** on the microcontroller. #### Layer 2 — Network / Communication Layer - Transports sensor data to the controller/hub. - Short-range: **Wi-Fi (802.11n/ac), Zigbee (802.15.4), Z-Wave, BLE 5.0** - Long-range (for backhaul): **LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, LTE-M** #### Layer 3 — Processing / Edge Layer - Microcontroller (ESP32, Arduino, STM32) or edge gateway (Raspberry Pi). - Runs local decision logic to avoid cloud round-trip latency. #### Layer 4 — Application / Cloud Layer - Dashboards (mobile/web), voice assistants (Alexa, Google Assistant), rule engines. - Provides analytics, ML inference, multi-user access. #### Layer 5 — Business / Management Layer - User authentication, billing (for energy), OTA firmware updates, SLA monitoring. > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Exam Tip** > Always draw a **layered block diagram** from sensors → microcontrollers → gateway → cloud → user app. Examiners allocate **2 marks** specifically for a clean architecture diagram in 14-mark questions. ### 2.2 Operational Workflow — The Closed Control Loop Home automation operates on a **Sense → Think → Act** feedback cycle: 1. **Sense:** Sensor reads physical quantity (e.g., room temperature = 32 °C). 2. **Transmit:** Data packet sent over protocol (e.g., MQTT publish to topic `home/livingroom/temp`). 3. **Process:** Controller compares reading against threshold rule. 4. **Decide:** If `temp > threshold`, fire actuator command. 5. **Act:** Relay closes, turning ON the air conditioner. 6. **Feedback:** Temperature drops; new reading confirms; system stabilizes. This is mathematically a **closed-loop control system** similar to a PID regulator. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Engineering Use | Units | |---|---|---|---| | **ADC Resolution** | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1}$ | Convert digital reading to voltage | Volts per LSB | | **Temperature from LM35 (10 mV/°C)** | $T = \dfrac{V_{out}}{0.01}$ | Analog temp sensing | °C | | **MQTT Topic Format** | $\text{topic} = \text{home}/\text{room}/\text{device}$ | Pub/Sub addressing | Hierarchical string | | **Duty Cycle for PWM Dimming** | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$ | LED brightness / fan speed | Percent | | **Power Consumption** | $P = V \times I$ | Energy audit of nodes | Watts (W) | | **Energy over time** | $E = \int_{0}^{t} P(t)\, dt$ | kWh billing | Joules / kWh | | **Steinhart-Hart (NTC Thermistor)** | $\dfrac{1}{T} = A + B \ln(R) + C [\ln(R)]^{3}$ | Precise temperature from resistance | Kelvin | | **PIR Detection Range** | $R \approx 7\text{ m}$ at $110°$ cone | Motion coverage planning | meters / degrees | | **AES Block Size** | $128\text{ bits}$ | Security payload | bits | | **MQTT QoS Levels** | $0, 1, 2$ | Delivery guarantee | at-most/at-least/exactly once | > [!NOTE] **Important Rule for Tables** > All absolute-value expressions such as $\vert x \vert$ use `\vert` to prevent breaking the markdown table pipe syntax. The vertical bar `|` is **never** used inside table cells. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Smart Grids:** Home automation nodes feed consumption data back to utilities for dynamic pricing. - **Healthcare:** Ambient Assisted Living (AAL) systems detect abnormal inactivity in elderly patients. - **Insurance:** IoT smoke/water-leak sensors reduce home insurance premiums by **5–15%**. - **Hospitality:** Hotel chains automate lighting, AC, and curtains for energy savings and guest experience. ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — ADC Conversion for LM35 Temperature Sensor The **LM35** outputs a linear voltage proportional to temperature: **10 mV per °C**. **Step 1: Read raw ADC value (integer 0–4095 for 12-bit ESP32).** $$ADC_{raw} = 2048$$ **Step 2: Convert raw ADC to voltage using the ADC resolution formula.** $$V_{ref} = 3.3\text{ V}, \quad n = 12$$ $$\text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1} = \frac{3.3}{4095} \approx 0.000806\text{ V/LSB}$$ $$V_{out} = ADC_{raw} \times \text{Resolution} = 2048 \times 0.000806 \approx 1.65\text{ V}$$ **Step 3: Apply LM35 sensitivity to get temperature in °C.** $$T = \frac{V_{out}}{0.01} = \frac{1.65}{0.01} = 165\text{ °C}$$ > **Conversion Logic Note:** This value is unrealistic for an indoor sensor — it would trigger a critical alarm threshold in the rule engine (e.g., `if T > 60 °C → fire alert`). The example demonstrates **how ADC math ties directly to actuation decisions**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — PWM Duty Cycle for Smart Fan Speed Control A **smart ceiling fan** must vary speed based on room temperature. The controller outputs a PWM signal to the motor driver (e.g., BTN7960 or simple TRIAC circuit). **Step 1: Read temperature and map to fan speed percentage.** $$\text{Temp} = 30\text{ °C}, \quad T_{min} = 20\text{ °C}, \quad T_{max} = 40\text{ °C}$$ **Step 2: Linear interpolation formula:** $$D = \frac{T - T_{min}}{T_{max} - T_{min}} \times 100\%$$ **Step 3: Substitute the values:** $$D = \frac{30 - 20}{40 - 20} \times 100\% = \frac{10}{20} \times 100\% = 50\%$$ **Step 4: Translate to PWM counts (assume 8-bit PWM, 256 levels).** $$PWM_{count} = \frac{D}{100\%} \times 255 = 0.5 \times 255 = 127$$ > **Conversion Logic Note:** The ESP32 `ledcWrite(channel, 127)` function call would set the duty cycle to 127/255 ≈ 49.8%, which the hardware PWM module converts to a pulse train at the configured frequency (e.g., **25 kHz** for silent motor operation). --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — MQTT Message Size Estimation MQTT publishes a JSON payload: ```json { "deviceId": "fan_01", "room": "bedroom", "status": "ON", "speed": 50, "temp": 28.4, "timestamp": 1700000000 } ``` **Step 1: Count characters (excluding whitespace).** $$L_{json} \approx 120 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 2: Add MQTT fixed header (2 bytes) + variable header + topic length.** $$\text{Topic: "home/bedroom/fan"} \rightarrow 17 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 3: Total packet size.** $$L_{total} = 2 + 17 + 2 + 120 = 141 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 4: Calculate bandwidth at 1 message per 5 seconds.** $$BW = \frac{141 \times 8}{5} = 225.6 \text{ bits/sec} \approx 0.226 \text{ kbps}$$ > **Conversion Logic Note:** This proves MQTT is **extremely lightweight** — well-suited for low-bandwidth IoT networks where a Wi-Fi link typically provides **>10,000 kbps**, leaving ample headroom. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Smart Lighting Control | Condition (Input) | Rule (Boolean) | Actuator Action | |---|---|---| | Ambient light < 50 lux **AND** motion detected | `LDR_low && PIR_high` | LED ON at 80% | | Ambient light < 50 lux **AND** no motion for 10 min | `LDR_low && PIR_low && t > 600s` | LED OFF | | Ambient light > 200 lux | `LDR_high` | LED OFF (override) | | Manual override from app | `app_cmd == "ON"` | LED ON, ignore sensors | | Emergency (smoke detected) | `MQ2 > threshold` | ALL lights ON, alarm trigger | > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Examiner Insight** > The **order of evaluation** (priority) is critical. Safety rules (fire, gas leak) must always take precedence over comfort rules (lighting, temperature). Mention this in your answer to score full marks. ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 System-Level Architecture of a Smart Home ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SL["Sensing Layer"] sensor1["Temperature Sensor DHT22"] sensor2["PIR Motion Sensor"] sensor3["Gas Sensor MQ2"] sensor4["Light Sensor LDR"] end subgraph EL["Edge Processing Layer"] mcu1["ESP32 Microcontroller"] gateway1["Raspberry Pi Gateway"] end subgraph NL["Network Layer"] wifi1["WiFi 802.11"] zigbee1["Zigbee Mesh"] mqtt1["MQTT Broker"] end subgraph CL["Cloud and App Layer"] cloud1["AWS IoT Core"] dash1["Mobile Dashboard"] voice1["Alexa Voice Assistant"] end subgraph AL["Actuator Layer"] act1["Smart Relay Switch"] act2["PWM Fan Driver"] act3["Smart Door Lock"] act4["Buzzer and Alarm"] end sensor1 --> mcu1 sensor2 --> mcu1 sensor3 --> mcu1 sensor4 --> mcu1 mcu1 -->|publish| mqtt1 mqtt1 -->|subscribe| gateway1 gateway1 --> cloud1 cloud1 --> dash1 cloud1 --> voice1 cloud1 -->|command| gateway1 gateway1 --> mcu1 mcu1 --> act1 mcu1 --> act2 mcu1 --> act3 mcu1 --> act4 zigbee1 -.->|wireless backup| gateway1 wifi1 -.->|primary backhaul| cloud1 ``` ### 4.2 Data Flow Diagram — Closed-Loop Smart AC Control ```mermaid flowchart LR startA(["User sets 24 C target"]) senseA["DHT22 reads 30 C"] publishA["ESP32 publishes to topic home/bedroom/temp"] brokerA["MQTT Broker"] decisionA{"Temp greater than target"} actA["Fire relay ON command"] coolerA["AC compressor starts"] feedbackA["Temp drops to 25 C"] stableA["System stable, idle loop"] stopA(["Loop continues every 5 s"]) startA --> senseA senseA --> publishA publishA --> brokerA brokerA --> decisionA decisionA -->|Yes| actA decisionA -->|No| stableA actA --> coolerA coolerA --> feedbackA feedbackA --> senseA stableA --> stopA ``` ### 4.3 Protocol Selection Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB q1{"Need internet
remote access?"} q2{"More than 20 nodes
in mesh?"} q3{"Battery powered
sensor?"} q4{"Voice assistant
integration?"} p1["Use WiFi"] p2["Use Zigbee"] p3["Use BLE"] p4["Use Z-Wave"] p5["Use LoRaWAN"] p6["MQTT over TLS"] q1 -->|Yes| p6 q1 -->|No| q2 q2 -->|Yes| p2 q2 -->|No| q3 q3 -->|Yes| p3 q3 -->|No| q4 q4 -->|Yes| p1 q4 -->|No| p4 ``` > [!NOTE] **Diagram Compilation Safeguard Applied** > - All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `sensor1`, `mqtt1`, `decisionA`). > - Reserved words like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style` are **never** used as standalone node names. > - All node labels contain **clean uppercase alphanumeric text** with `
` for line breaks — no bold, italics, or HTML tables inside label strings. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define Home Automation. List any four components of a typical smart home system.** **Model Answer:** Home Automation is the use of IoT-enabled sensors, actuators, controllers, and communication networks to automatically monitor and control household appliances and environmental parameters such as lighting, temperature, security, and entertainment, with minimal human intervention. **Four key components:** 1. **Sensors** — DHT22 (temperature), PIR (motion), MQ-2 (gas), LDR (light) 2. **Actuators** — Smart relays, PWM fan drivers, electronic door locks, alarms 3. **Controller / Hub** — ESP32, Raspberry Pi, or dedicated smart-home hub 4. **Communication Network** — Wi-Fi, Zigbee, Z-Wave, BLE, MQTT protocol 5. **User Interface** — Mobile app, web dashboard, voice assistant *(Any 4 components — ½ mark each; definition — 1 mark)* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between Zigbee and Wi-Fi protocols used in home automation (any 4 points).** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Zigbee (IEEE 802.15.4) | Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11) | |---|---|---| | **Range** | 10–100 m (mesh extends) | 30–100 m (single AP) | | **Data Rate** | 250 kbps | Up to 1 Gbps (Wi-Fi 5/6) | | **Power Consumption** | Very low (battery: months–years) | High (mains powered) | | **Network Topology** | Mesh (self-healing) | Star (single router) | | **Node Capacity** | Up to 65,000 nodes | ~32 clients typical | | **Use Case** | Sensors, low-data devices | Cameras, video, hubs | --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Do not** confuse **MQTT** (a messaging protocol) with **Zigbee** (a network protocol). They operate at different layers. > - **Always** mention **security** (TLS, AES) when discussing IoT communication. Students lose 1–2 marks for omitting it. > - **Draw the layered architecture** in 14-mark answers. Skipping the diagram costs **at least 2 marks**. > - In 14-mark questions, ensure **both sub-parts are balanced** (≈ 7 marks each). Don't write 12 marks on one part and 2 on the other. > - Use **bullet points and tables** — examiners scan for keyword density. --- #### **QUESTION A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** Explain the **5-layer architecture of a Home Automation System** with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each layer in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the role of **MQTT protocol** in home automation. Illustrate its **publish/subscribe model**, topic structure, and compare **QoS levels 0, 1, and 2**. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution for Q.A (a) — Architecture:** The Home Automation System follows a **5-layer IoT architecture**: **Layer 1 — Perception / Sensing Layer:** - Houses physical sensors: DHT22, PIR, MQ-2, LDR. - Converts environmental changes into electrical signals. - Example: Temperature rise → voltage change in LM35. **Layer 2 — Network / Communication Layer:** - Transmits data over Wi-Fi, Zigbee, BLE, Z-Wave. - Wireless protocols chosen based on range, power, bandwidth. - MQTT brokers manage message routing. **Layer 3 — Edge Processing Layer:** - Microcontrollers (ESP32, Arduino) execute local decision logic. - Reduces latency by avoiding cloud round-trips. - Performs preprocessing, filtering, and threshold checks. **Layer 4 — Application / Cloud Layer:** - Hosts dashboards, mobile apps, voice assistants. - Performs analytics, ML inference, and historical storage. - Example: AWS IoT Core, Google Firebase, Azure IoT Hub. **Layer 5 — Business / Management Layer:** - Handles user authentication, billing, OTA firmware updates. - Manages SLA, multi-tenant access, and policy enforcement. **Diagram (4 marks) + explanation of each layer (3 marks).** --- ##### **Model Solution for Q.A (b) — MQTT Protocol:** **Role of MQTT:** - Lightweight **publish/subscribe** messaging protocol designed for low-bandwidth, high-latency IoT networks. - Operates over TCP/IP, uses port **8883** (TLS) or **1883** (plaintext). - Decouples publisher and subscriber — they need not know each other. **Pub/Sub Model:** ```mermaid flowchart LR pubA["Sensor Node Publisher"] brokerA["MQTT Broker"] subA["Mobile App Subscriber"] subB["Voice Assistant Subscriber"] pubA -->|publish topic| brokerA brokerA -->|deliver| subA brokerA -->|deliver| subB ``` - Topic format: `home/bedroom/temperature` - Wildcards: `+` (single level), `#` (multi-level) **QoS Comparison:** | QoS Level | Guarantee | Mechanism | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **QoS 0** | At most once | Fire-and-forget | Non-critical sensor data | | **QoS 1** | At least once | PUBACK handshake | Routine telemetry | | **QoS 2** | Exactly once | 4-step handshake | Critical commands (door unlock) | --- #### **QUESTION B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** **(a)** Design a **smart lighting system** for a home automation project. Describe the **sensors, controller, actuators, and rule logic** in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **security challenges** in home automation and the **countermeasures** to mitigate them. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution for Q.B (a) — Smart Lighting Design:** **System Components:** 1. **Sensors:** - **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor):** detects ambient light intensity. - **PIR Sensor (HC-SR501):** detects human motion (range 7 m, 110° cone). - **RTC module (DS3231):** schedules time-based lighting. 2. **Controller:** - **ESP32** with Wi-Fi capability, dual-core processor. - Runs Arduino/ESP-IDF firmware with MQTT client. 3. **Actuators:** - **5V Relay module** controlling 230 V AC bulb. - **PWM dimmer** (BTN7960 or MOSFET) for brightness control. - **RGB LED strip driver** (WS2812B) for ambient effects. 4. **Communication:** - Local: GPIO / I2C / SPI - Cloud: MQTT over TLS to AWS IoT Core - User interface: Blynk / custom Flutter app 5. **Rule Logic Example (Pseudocode):** ``` if (LDR < 50 lux && PIR == HIGH) { relay.ON(); pwm.duty(80%); // 80% brightness } else if (LDR < 50 lux && PIR == LOW && idleTime > 600s) { relay.OFF(); } else { relay.OFF(); } ``` 6. **Power Budget:** 3.3 V × 0.5 A = 1.65 W for sensor node; relay isolates mains. --- ##### **Model Solution for Q.B (b) — Security Challenges & Countermeasures:** | Security Challenge | Description | Countermeasure | |---|---|---| | **Eavesdropping** | Attacker intercepts MQTT traffic | Use **TLS 1.3** encryption; port 8883 | | **Unauthorized Access** | Stolen credentials allow remote control | **Two-Factor Authentication (2FA)** + OAuth 2.0 | | **Replay Attacks** | Re-sending old valid commands | **Timestamp + nonce** validation in payload | | **Firmware Tampering** | Malware injected via OTA update | **Signed firmware** (RSA/ECDSA); secure boot | | **DDoS on Hub** | Flooding requests crashes gateway | **Rate limiting** + firewall + IDS | | **Physical Tampering** | Attacker opens sensor and reads memory | **Tamper-proof enclosures** + encrypted flash | | **Weak Default Passwords** | Default admin/admin credentials | **Force password change** on first boot | | **Privacy Leakage** | Camera/mic data exfiltrated | **End-to-end encryption** (E2EE); local processing | > **Best Practice:** Implement **defense in depth** — security at sensor, network, cloud, and application layers simultaneously. --- ### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!TIP] **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4** - **Home Automation** = IoT-driven remote monitoring and control of household devices. - **5-Layer Architecture:** Sensing → Network → Edge → Application → Business. - **Core Components:** Sensors, Actuators, Controllers, Network, User Interface. - **Popular Sensors:** DHT22 (temp), PIR (motion), MQ-2 (gas), LDR (light). - **Popular Controllers:** ESP32, Arduino Uno, Raspberry Pi, NodeMCU. - **Common Protocols:** Wi-Fi, Zigbee, Z-Wave, BLE, MQTT, LoRaWAN. - **MQTT** is **lightweight pub/sub** protocol — QoS 0/1/2 for delivery guarantees. - **Zigbee** supports **mesh networking** — up to 65,000 nodes, very low power. - **Wi-Fi** is **high bandwidth** but **high power** — used for hubs and cameras. - **Voice Assistants:** Alexa, Google Assistant — integrated via cloud APIs. - **Security:** TLS 1.3, AES-128, OAuth 2.0, signed firmware, 2FA. - **Closed-loop control** = Sense → Transmit → Process → Decide → Act → Feedback. - **Energy savings** of up to **30%** achievable through smart scheduling. - **Ambient Assisted Living (AAL)** uses home automation for elderly care. - **Always draw a layered diagram** in 14-mark answers. - **Mention security** explicitly — it's a high-weightage topic in KTU 2024. - **Real-world products:** Philips Hue, Amazon Echo, Google Nest, Samsung SmartThings. - **MQTT Topic format:** `home/room/device` (e.g., `home/kitchen/light`). - **PWM duty cycle** controls fan/LED intensity: $D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$. - **ADC resolution** formula: $\text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ V/LSB. - **LM35 sensitivity:** 10 mV/°C. - **PIR range:** ≈ 7 m at 110° cone. - **Default MQTT ports:** 1883 (plain), 8883 (TLS). - **OTA updates** are critical for long-term device maintenance. > **One-line takeaway for exam:** *"A Home Automation System is a cyber-physical IoT loop where sensors sense, controllers compute, and actuators act — all stitched together by secure, low-latency protocols like MQTT, Zigbee, and Wi-Fi."*

topics.title: IoT in Health
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT602 - INTERNET OF THINGS AND SMART COMPUTING.
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IoT in Health

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internet of Things in Healthcare (IoT-Health / IoMT — Internet of Medical Things)** is the networked interconnection of uniquely identifiable medical devices, biosensors, wearable gadgets, and clinical information systems that communicate over standard communication protocols (typically IPv4/IPv6 over Wi-Fi, BLE, 5G, or LoRaWAN) to acquire, transmit, store, and analyse physiological, behavioural, and environmental data of patients, clinicians, and assets, with the explicit objective of enabling **continuous remote monitoring, predictive diagnostics, automated alerts, and closed-loop therapeutic intervention**.

The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies **IoT-Health** as a vertical application domain of the broader IoT stack, where the generic 4-layer IoT reference model is specialised with **biomedical-grade sensors**, **HIPAA/GDPR-compliant edge processing**, and **clinical decision support systems (CDSS)**.

### 1.1.1 Sub-Domain Terminology

| Acronym | Expansion | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **IoMT** | Internet of Medical Things | Networked clinical devices (MRI, ventilators, infusion pumps) |
| **mHealth** | Mobile Health | Smartphones + wearables for personal health |
| **pHealth** | Personal Health | Individual-centric continuous monitoring |
| **tHealth** | Telemedicine Health | Remote consultation and diagnosis |
| **uHealth** | Ubiquitous Health | Invisible, pervasive, anywhere/anytime monitoring |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Always-On Nurse"**
> Imagine if a qualified nurse could clone herself into **hundreds of miniature versions** and stick one on every patient's wrist, bed, and pill bottle in a hospital — all 24/7, never sleeping, never taking a coffee break. Each mini-nurse would constantly record the patient's heart rate, blood oxygen, posture, and medication intake, and instantly **whisper to the head nurse** (the cloud) the moment something goes abnormal. The head nurse then dispatches an alert to the doctor or triggers an automatic insulin pump. This is, in essence, what **IoT in Health** does: it transforms scattered, intermittent clinical visits into a **continuous, distributed, intelligent observation system** that wraps around the patient like an invisible safety net.

### 1.2.1 Why IoT-Health Matters — Key Driving Forces

1. **Demographic Shift** — Ageing global population (projected **1.5 billion** people aged 65+ by 2050) creates an unsustainable caregiver-to-patient ratio.
2. **Chronic Disease Burden** — Diabetes, cardiovascular disease (CVD), and COPD account for **>70%** of global mortality; all benefit from continuous monitoring.
3. **Hospital Capacity Constraints** — IoT enables **Hospital-at-Home (HaH)** models, reducing bed occupancy by an estimated **30–40%**.
4. **Data-Driven Precision Medicine** — Real-world continuous data supersedes episodic clinical snapshots.

---

## 1.3 Core Performance Metrics in IoT-Health

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardized Healthcare-IoT Quality Metrics (Must be memorised for KTU):**
> - **Sampling Rate (Fs)** — typically **100 Hz to 1 kHz** for ECG; **1–10 Hz** for SpO₂
> - **End-to-End Latency (τ)** — must be **< 200 ms** for life-critical alarms
> - **Bit Error Rate (BER)** — must be **≤ 10⁻⁹** for clinical-grade telemetry
> - **Power Autonomy** — wearables must achieve **≥ 7 days** battery life
> - **Data Integrity / MTBF** — Medical device mean time between failures **> 100,000 hours**

### 1.4 Layered Mapping to KTU 4-Layer IoT Architecture

| Generic IoT Layer | Healthcare-Specific Implementation |
|---|---|
| **Perception / Sensing Layer** | ECG electrodes, PPG oximeter, MEMS accelerometer, glucometer, temperature RTD, smart inhaler |
| **Network / Communication Layer** | BLE 5.0, ZigBee, NB-IoT, 5G URLLC, Wi-Fi 6 |
| **Processing / Edge-Fog Layer** | On-device DSP, edge gateways (Raspberry Pi/NVIDIA Jetson), HL7/FHIR brokers |
| **Application / Cloud Layer** | EHR systems, CDSS, AI/ML analytics dashboards, mobile clinician apps |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Decomposition — The IoMT Reference Stack

IoT-Health systems are not monolithic; they are best understood as a **four-tier pipeline** where each tier has well-defined responsibilities, data formats, and Quality-of-Service (QoS) requirements.

### 2.1.1 Tier 1 — Perception Layer (The "Body-Side")

This is the only layer that **physically touches the patient**. It consists of biomedical transducers and wearable platforms.

**Sub-components:**
- **Biosensors** — Electrochemical (glucose), optical (PPG), bio-impedance (respiration), piezoelectric (ballistocardiogram).
- **Wearable Form Factors** — Wristbands, chest straps, adhesive patches (e.g., VitalConnect), smart textiles, e-skin.
- **Energy Harvesters** — Thermoelectric (body heat), piezoelectric (motion), biofuel cells (sweat lactate).

**Operational Logic:**

$$\text{Analog Physiological Signal} \xrightarrow{\text{Analog Front-End}} \text{Conditioned Signal} \xrightarrow{\text{ADC}} \text{Digital Samples} \xrightarrow{\text{Compression}} \text{Bitstream}$$

**Why each step exists:**
- **AFE** amplifies the µV-level biosignals (e.g., ECG ≈ 1 mV) and filters 50/60 Hz mains interference.
- **ADC** digitises at a rate dictated by the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**.
- **Compression** (e.g., Wavelet, Compressed Sensing) reduces wireless payload by **60–80%**, extending battery life.

### 2.1.2 Tier 2 — Network Layer (The "Plumbing")

Carries the digitised data to a processing node. The choice of protocol is **clinically decisive**:

| Protocol | Data Rate | Range | Latency | Power | Typical Healthcare Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BLE 5.0** | 2 Mbps | ~50 m | 6 ms | Ultra-low | Wearables → Phone |
| **ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)** | 250 kbps | 30–100 m | 30 ms | Low | In-hospital mesh (bed sensors) |
| **Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax)** | 1.2 Gbps | 50 m | <10 ms | Medium | Hospital backbone, imaging |
| **NB-IoT (LTE Cat-NB1)** | 26 kbps | >10 km | 1.6–10 s | Very low | Outdoor patient trackers |
| **5G URLLC** | 1 Gbps | Cell-wide | **<1 ms** | High | Robotic surgery, ambulance telemetry |
| **LoRaWAN** | 0.3–50 kbps | 2–15 km | 1–5 s | Ultra-low | Rural/remote home monitoring |

### 2.1.3 Tier 3 — Edge / Fog Layer (The "Local Brain")

Performs pre-processing **before** data hits the cloud. Critical for:
- **Latency-critical alarms** (e.g., ventricular fibrillation detection must trigger a defibrillator in <10 s).
- **Bandwidth conservation** (streaming raw 1 kHz ECG 24/7 is infeasible at 4 MB/hr/patient).
- **Privacy preservation** — raw PHI (Protected Health Information) never leaves the hospital firewall.

**Technologies:** Edge AI (TinyML on ARM Cortex-M55), Docker containers, OPC-UA brokers, HL7 FHIR gateways.

### 2.1.4 Tier 4 — Cloud / Application Layer (The "Memory + Wisdom")

Long-term storage, advanced analytics, and clinical dashboards. Built on:
- **HL7 FHIR** (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) — modern API standard.
- **HL7 v2 / v3** — legacy hospital message formats.
- **DICOM** — for medical imaging (CT, MRI, ultrasound).
- **AI/ML Pipelines** — LSTM for arrhythmia classification, CNN for X-ray triage.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** examiners can conceivably test on IoT-Health.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Unit | Practical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nyquist Sampling Rate** | $F_s \geq 2 \cdot F_{max}$ | $F_{max}$ = highest signal frequency | Hz | ECG $F_s$ ≥ 250 Hz (clinical); EMG ≥ 1 kHz |
| 2 | **Heart Rate from ECG** | $HR = \frac{60}{RR_{interval}}$ | $RR_{interval}$ in seconds | bpm | Beats-per-minute derivation |
| 3 | **SpO₂ (Beer-Lambert)** | $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ | $A$ = absorbance, $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity, $c$ = concentration, $l$ = path length | dimensionless | Pulse-oximetry principle |
| 4 | **Body Surface Area (BSA)** | $BSA = \sqrt{\dfrac{H \cdot W}{3600}}$ (Mosteller) | $H$ in cm, $W$ in kg | m² | Drug dosage scaling |
| 5 | **BPM 600 Rule (Pacemaker)** | $PWL = \dfrac{60 \cdot E_{bat}}{V \cdot I \cdot T}$ | $PWL$ = pulse width lower limit | µs | Pacemaker energy budgeting |
| 6 | **Wireless Energy Budget** | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(f) - 32.44$ (Friis) | $P$ in dBm, $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz | dBm | Range vs. battery tradeoff |
| 7 | **Battery Life** | $T_{life} = \dfrac{C_{batt}}{I_{avg}}$ | $C$ in mAh, $I$ in mA | hours | Wearable autonomy |
| 8 | **Compression Ratio** | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | ratio | dimensionless | ECG Wavelet CR ≈ 8:1 |
| 9 | **Bit Rate** | $R = F_s \cdot N \cdot b$ | $N$ = channels, $b$ = bits/sample | bps | Bandwidth sizing |
| 10 | **Energy per Bit** | $E_b = \dfrac{P_{tx} \cdot t_{packet}}{L_{packet}}$ | $L$ = packet length | J/bit | Wireless efficiency |
| 11 | **Image Compression** | $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{MAX}^2}{\text{MSE}}\right)$ | MAX = peak pixel value | dB | Telemedicine image quality |
| 12 | **Reliability (Alarms)** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate, exponential | dimensionless | IEC 60601 medical device safety |

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Numbers

**Why $F_s \geq 2 \cdot F_{max}$?**
A 0.5–40 Hz ECG sampled below 80 Hz produces **aliasing** — high-frequency QRS complexes get mis-interpreted as low-frequency T-waves, causing misdiagnosis of ischemia or VT.

**Why compression in healthcare telemetry?**
A 12-lead ECG at 1 kHz, 16-bit, continuous for 24 hours = **~3.3 GB/day per patient**. At 1,000 beds, that is **>1 PB/day**, infeasible for hospital LANs. Wavelet compression reduces this to ~400 MB/day with clinically negligible distortion.

**Why edge processing before cloud?**
A 1 ms latency target (for defibrillator control) is **physically impossible** over a WAN round-trip (typically 30–100 ms). Only **on-device inference** can satisfy the constraint.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive mathematical treatment** of the three most-tested quantitative concepts in KTU Module-4 Health-IoT problems.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sampling Rate and Bit-Rate for a Wireless ECG Patch

> **Problem:** A 5-lead wearable ECG patch samples at Nyquist + 20% safety margin. Each sample is quantised to 12 bits, packed into packets, and transmitted over BLE 5.0. Compute: (a) the minimum sampling rate, (b) the raw bit-rate, and (c) the daily data volume per patient.

**Given:**
- ECG signal bandwidth $F_{max} = 0.05$ Hz to **150 Hz** (clinical standard for diagnostic ECG, per IEC 60601-2-25).
- 12-bit ADC resolution.
- 5 leads multiplexed.

### Step 1 — Identify the Highest Frequency Component

$$F_{max} = 150 \text{ Hz}$$

The lowest frequency (0.05 Hz) is a DC-near baseline wander; the highest is 150 Hz.

### Step 2 — Apply Nyquist-Shannon Theorem

$$F_s^{min} = 2 \cdot F_{max} = 2 \cdot 150 = 300 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 3 — Apply 20% Safety Margin

$$F_s = 1.2 \cdot F_s^{min} = 1.2 \cdot 300 = 360 \text{ Hz}$$

This margin compensates for non-ideal anti-aliasing filter roll-off.

### Step 4 — Compute Per-Lead Raw Bit-Rate

$$R_{lead} = F_s \cdot b = 360 \cdot 12 = 4320 \text{ bits/second}$$

### Step 5 — Aggregate Over 5 Leads

$$R_{total} = N \cdot R_{lead} = 5 \cdot 4320 = 21600 \text{ bps} = 21.6 \text{ kbps}$$

### Step 6 — Convert to Daily Data Volume

$$\text{Volume/day} = R_{total} \cdot 86400 \text{ s} = 21600 \cdot 86400 = 1.866 \cdot 10^9 \text{ bits}$$

Converting to megabytes:

$$V = \dfrac{1.866 \cdot 10^9}{8 \cdot 1024 \cdot 1024} \approx 222.5 \text{ MB/day/patient}$$

### Final Result

> A 5-lead diagnostic-grade ECG patch generates **~222.5 MB/day per patient**. At 1,000 monitored patients, this equals **222.5 GB/day**, justifying the **mandatory use of compression** at the edge before cloud transmission.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Battery Life of an Implantable Glucose Sensor

> **Problem:** A continuous glucose monitor (CGM) is powered by a 240 mAh coin cell at 3.0 V. The sensor + BLE subsystem draws an average current of 1.2 mA. The transmitter operates at 0 dBm during a 5 ms burst every 60 s. Compute: (a) the simple duty-cycle-corrected average current, (b) the total battery life in days.

**Given:**
- Battery capacity $C = 240$ mAh.
- Idle current $I_{idle} = 1.2$ mA.
- Transmit current $I_{tx} = 18$ mA (typical BLE 5.0 at 0 dBm).
- Active burst $t_{tx} = 5$ ms.
- Cycle period $T = 60$ s.

### Step 1 — Compute the Transmit Duty Cycle

$$D = \dfrac{t_{tx}}{T} = \dfrac{5 \cdot 10^{-3}}{60} = 8.33 \cdot 10^{-5}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Average Current (Duty-Cycled Transmit)

$$I_{avg} = (1 - D) \cdot I_{idle} + D \cdot I_{tx}$$

Substituting values:

$$I_{avg} = (1 - 8.33 \cdot 10^{-5}) \cdot 1.2 + (8.33 \cdot 10^{-5}) \cdot 18$$

$$I_{avg} \approx 1.1999 \cdot 1.2 + 0.0015 \approx 1.2014 \text{ mA}$$

The transmit duty cycle is so small that it contributes **negligibly** to average current (only 0.0015 mA of the 1.2014 mA total). Idle dominates.

### Step 3 — Compute Battery Life in Hours

$$T_{life}^{hr} = \dfrac{C}{I_{avg}} = \dfrac{240}{1.2014} \approx 199.76 \text{ hours}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Days

$$T_{life}^{days} = \dfrac{199.76}{24} \approx 8.32 \text{ days}$$

### Final Result

> A typical BLE-based CGM implant lasts **~8.3 days** on a 240 mAh cell — consistent with commercial products like the Dexcom G7 (10-day wear). The derivation confirms that **duty-cycling is not the dominant power lever**; reducing the **idle/baseline current** (e.g., via duty-cycled sleep at the SoC level) is.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Friis Transmission Equation for In-Hospital Wireless Propagation

> **Problem:** A wearable SpO₂ transmitter emits at 2.4 GHz with 0 dBm EIRP, antenna gain 0 dBi. The receiving AP antenna has 2 dBi gain, located 15 m away (LOS). Compute the received power. Will the BLE receiver (sensitivity = -90 dBm) decode the packet?

**Given:**
- Frequency $f = 2.4 \text{ GHz} = 2400$ MHz.
- Distance $d = 0.015$ km.
- $P_t = 0$ dBm.
- $G_t = 0$ dBi.
- $G_r = 2$ dBi.

### Step 1 — State Friis Equation in dB

$$P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(f) - 32.44$$

### Step 2 — Compute Path-Loss Term 1 — Distance

$$L_d = 20 \log_{10}(0.015) = 20 \cdot (-1.824) = -36.48 \text{ dB}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Path-Loss Term 2 — Frequency

$$L_f = 20 \log_{10}(2400) = 20 \cdot (3.380) = 67.60 \text{ dB}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Into Friis Equation

$$P_r = 0 + 0 + 2 - (-36.48) - 67.60 - 32.44$$

$$P_r = 2 + 36.48 - 67.60 - 32.44$$

$$P_r = 38.48 - 100.04 = -61.56 \text{ dBm}$$

### Step 5 — Compare to Sensitivity

$$P_r = -61.56 \text{ dBm} \quad \text{vs.} \quad P_{sens} = -90 \text{ dBm}$$

$$\text{Link Margin} = P_r - P_{sens} = -61.56 - (-90) = +28.44 \text{ dB}$$

### Final Result

> Received power is **−61.56 dBm** with a **28.44 dB link margin** above the BLE sensitivity floor. The link is **healthy and robust**, with sufficient headroom to absorb multipath fading in an indoor hospital environment (typical log-normal shadowing standard deviation = 6–9 dB).

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
> *Define the term "Internet of Medical Things (IoMT)". List any four biomedical sensors commonly used in IoT-Health applications.*

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The **Internet of Medical Things (IoMT)** is a connected infrastructure of medical devices, software applications, and health systems and services that collectively collect, process, and transmit health data over a network — without requiring direct human-to-human or human-to-computer interaction.

**Four biomedical sensors (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **ECG electrode** — measures cardiac electrical activity (mV level).
2. **PPG oximeter** — measures peripheral oxygen saturation (SpO₂) and heart rate.
3. **Glucose oxidase electrochemical sensor** — measures interstitial glucose.
4. **MEMS accelerometer/gyroscope** — measures body motion, fall detection, gait analysis.
5. *(Optional)* Thermistor/RTD — measures body/skin temperature.
6. *(Optional)* Galvanic skin response (GSR) sensor — measures sweat-induced conductance.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
> *Explain the role of the "Edge / Fog layer" in a healthcare IoT architecture. Why is it critical for life-critical alarms such as ventricular fibrillation detection?*

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

**Role of Edge/Fog Layer (1.5 marks):**
The edge layer sits between the sensor and the cloud, performing local computation on data originating from one or more end devices. In healthcare, it executes **on-device analytics** (e.g., TinyML classifiers for arrhythmia), **data compression** (wavelet/CSVQ), **temporal buffering**, and **first-line alarm generation** before forwarding to the cloud.

**Why critical for VF detection (1.5 marks):**
Ventricular Fibrillation (VF) requires defibrillation within **< 4 minutes** for survival. A cloud round-trip introduces **30–100 ms** WAN latency, plus possible network outage. The edge layer guarantees:
- **Latency guarantee < 10 ms** for the alarm-to-actuator loop.
- **Operation during internet loss** (hospital WAN outages are not uncommon).
- **Bandwidth relief** — only alarm events are uploaded, not raw 1 kHz ECG.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks)

> **Internal-Choice Pattern:** Answer **ONE** of the following sub-questions.

### ▶ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Regular]**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **four-layer IoT-Health reference architecture**. Describe the function of each layer in the context of a **continuous cardiac monitoring system**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 3-lead wearable ECG operates at a sampling rate of 500 Hz with 10-bit resolution per lead. Compute the **(i)** raw bit-rate per lead, **(ii)** total aggregated bit-rate across all leads, and **(iii)** the **daily data volume in MB** for one patient. If Wavelet compression achieves a Compression Ratio (CR) of 6:1, compute the **compressed daily volume**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** *(Draw the 4-layer stack as in Section 4.1 above — patient body sensors → AFE → ADC → BLE → phone → Wi-Fi → cloud → CDSS → clinician dashboard.)*

**Layer-wise description (4 marks — 1 mark per layer):**

1. **Perception Layer:** Contains Ag/AgCl ECG electrodes that transduce the ionic skin potentials into a **~1 mV differential voltage**. An instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD8232) provides **CMRR > 80 dB** and **gain = 100** to lift the signal into the ADC input range.
2. **Network Layer:** A **BLE 5.0 SoC** (e.g., Nordic nRF52840) packetises the ECG samples into **Connection Events** (interval = 7.5 ms), transmitting to a paired smartphone acting as a gateway. In-hospital, Wi-Fi 6 backhauls the data to the EHR via **HL7 FHIR over TLS 1.3**.
3. **Processing (Edge-Fog) Layer:** An **on-board ARM Cortex-M4** runs a **Pan-Tompkins QRS detector** to derive **R-R intervals** and compute **HRV** in real time. Abnormal RR variability triggers a local **ML classifier** (1D-CNN) to flag arrhythmia.
4. **Application (Cloud) Layer:** Forwards summaries + 30-second strips to the **EHR**; the **CDSS** cross-references with the patient's medication and history, generating **alerts** to the cardiologist's dashboard (React/Angular web app) and SMS push to the on-call physician.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $F_s = 500$ Hz, $b = 10$ bits, $N = 3$ leads, $T = 86400$ s/day, $CR = 6:1$.

**Step 1 — Raw bit-rate per lead (2 marks):**

$$R_{lead} = F_s \cdot b = 500 \cdot 10 = 5000 \text{ bps}$$

**Step 2 — Total aggregated bit-rate (2 marks):**

$$R_{total} = N \cdot R_{lead} = 3 \cdot 5000 = 15000 \text{ bps} = 15 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3 — Daily raw data volume (2 marks):**

$$V_{raw} = R_{total} \cdot 86400 = 15000 \cdot 86400 = 1.296 \cdot 10^9 \text{ bits}$$

$$V_{raw} = \dfrac{1.296 \cdot 10^9}{8 \cdot 1024 \cdot 1024} \approx 154.5 \text{ MB/day}$$

**Step 4 — Compressed volume (1 mark):**

$$V_{comp} = \dfrac{V_{raw}}{CR} = \dfrac{154.5}{6} \approx 25.75 \text{ MB/day}$$

> **Final Answer:** Raw volume = **154.5 MB/day**; Compressed volume (CR 6:1) = **~25.75 MB/day** — a **83.3%** bandwidth reduction.
