List any four key features that distinguish it from the conventional power grid.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Smart Grid (SG)** is an *electricity network that integrates two-way digital communication, advanced sensing, and intelligent control* to deliver power efficiently, sustainably, and reliably. **Four distinguishing features (½ mark each = 2 marks):** 1. **Bidirectional power and information flow** — supports prosumers with rooftop solar feeding back to the grid. 2. **Self-healing capability** — automatically detects, isolates, and reconfigures around faults using FLISR. 3. **Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI)** — real-time 15-minute interval data from smart meters. 4. **Distributed Energy Resource (DER) integration** — seamless plug-and-play of solar, wind, and storage. *(Plus the introductory 1 mark for the definition.)* --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style] **Explain the role of a Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU) in a Smart Grid. Mention any two applications.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU)** is a high-speed sensor that synchronously samples voltage and current waveforms using **GPS-time-stamped** measurements at rates of up to **50 samples/second** (per IEEE C37.118). **Two applications (1½ marks):** 1. **Wide-Area Monitoring System (WAMS)** — provides real-time visualization of grid stability across substations. 2. **Real-time congestion management and oscillation detection** — prevents cascading blackouts by detecting inter-area modes early. *(Introductory role: 1 mark ; two applications: 2 marks.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks): Smart Grid Architecture, AMI, and Self-Healing** > **[KTU University Exam — 2024 ESE Pattern | Module 4 Internal Choice]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **NIST 3-layer conceptual model** of the Smart Grid. List the **seven logical domains** and explain the role of the **Communication Layer** in detail. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Explain the **Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI)** architecture. Discuss how AMI enables **(i) self-healing**, **(ii) Time-of-Day (TOD) pricing**, and **(iii) loss reduction** in a distribution utility. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):** The **NIST Smart Grid Conceptual Model** is the standard reference architecture that maps all stakeholders, actors, and information flows into a unified framework. It is the basis for ISGF (India Smart Grid Forum) implementation. **Step 2 — The 3 layers (1.5 marks):** - **Power & Energy Layer** — physical assets: generators, transformers, lines, switches. - **Communication Layer** — bidirectional data transport via RF Mesh, PLC, NB-IoT, 5G, OFC. - **Application / IT Layer** — SCADA, EMS, DMS, OMS, MDMS, billing, analytics. **Step 3 — The 7 domains (2.5 marks):** | # | Domain | KTU-Relevant Function | |---|---|---| | 1 | Bulk Generation | Conventional + utility-scale renewable plants | | 2 | Transmission | EHV (220/400 kV) interstate grid | | 3 | Distribution | 11/33 kV feeder-level IoT hotspot | | 4 | Customer | Smart homes, EVs, rooftop PV | | 5 | Operations | Utility control center with EMS | | 6 | Markets | Real-time electricity trading | | 7 | Service Provider | Third-party cloud / analytics / DR aggregators | **Step 4 — Role of Communication Layer (2 marks):** The **Communication Layer** is the *nervous system* of the smart grid. It provides: - **Latency-tiered transport** — GOOSE messages at < 4 ms for protection; SCADA at < 1 s; AMI at 1–15 s. - **Heterogeneous access** — fiber backhaul (substation), RF mesh (AMI), cellular (PMU), PLC (LV last-mile). - **Cybersecurity envelope** — IEC 62351, TLS, key management. - **Interoperability** — IEC 61850, DNP3, DLMS/COSEM, OpenADR. > **Note:** The 4-layer protocol stack (Application / Transport / Network / MAC-PHY) shown in *Figure D* of SECTION 4 is the most exam-friendly way to draw the Communication Layer. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Step 1 — AMI Definition (1 mark):** **Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI)** is the integrated system of *smart meters, communication networks, and data management systems* that enables **two-way communication** between utilities and consumers. **Step 2 — AMI 3-tier architecture (2 marks):** *(Reference Figure B from SECTION 4)* — Tier 1 (Smart Meters) → Tier 2 (Concentrator Gateway) → Tier 3 (HES + MDMS). **Step 3 — Self-healing enabled by AMI (1.5 marks):** - **Last-Gasp signal** from the meter identifies the exact outage zone within seconds. - **Ping-from-mesh** confirms restoration; **FLISR** algorithm in DMS automatically reconfigures feeders. - Result: **CAIDI** reduced from ~67 min to < 15 min. **Step 4 — TOD pricing enabled by AMI (1.5 marks):** - Smart meters record **15-minute interval kWh** consumption. - Utility publishes **Time-of-Day tariff** (Peak: ₹ 8.50/unit, Normal: ₹ 6.50, Off-peak: ₹ 4.50). - HEMS / smart app shifts loads to off-peak → consumer saves cost → grid flattens → **PAR reduces**. - *(Numerical example: 25 % PAR reduction, as derived in §3.2.)* **Step 5 — Loss reduction via AMI (1 mark):** - **Energy audit at feeder level** identifies non-technical losses (theft, faulty meters). - **Real-time tamper detection** flags meter-bypass events. - Combined with **VVO**, technical loss reduces by ~8.5 % (see §3.4). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice: Quantitative Smart Grid** > **[KTU University Exam — 2024 ESE Pattern | Module 4 Internal Choice]** **(a)** A distribution feeder serves **1000 customers**. During the year, three sustained interruptions occurred: | Event | Customers affected Nᵢ | Duration rᵢ (min) | |---:|---:|---:| | 1 | 250 | 90 | | 2 | 180 | 60 | | 3 | 120 | 45 | Compute **SAIDI, SAIFI, and CAIDI**, and explain how a smart-grid FLISR scheme would improve these indices. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** With reference to **Demand Response (DR)** and **Time-of-Day (TOD)** tariff, derive the **Peak-to-Average Ratio (PAR)** reduction for a feeder whose peak load is **15 kW** and average load is **7.5 kW**, after a DR program reduces the peak to **10 kW**. State the engineering benefits of lowering PAR. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Sum of customer-interruptions and customer-minutes (1 mark):** $$\sum N_i = 250 + 180 + 120 = 550 \text{ customer-interruptions}$$ $$\sum r_i \cdot N_i = (90 \times 250) + (60 \times 180) + (45 \times 120)$$ $$= 22{,}500 + 10{,}800 + 5{,}400 = 38{,}700 \text{ customer-minutes}$$ **Step 2 — SAIFI (1 mark):** $$SAIFI = \frac{\sum N_i}{N_{total}} = \frac{550}{1000} = 0.55 \text{ int/customer/yr}$$ **Step 3 — SAIDI (1 mark):** $$SAIDI = \frac{\sum r_i \cdot N_i}{N_{total}} = \frac{38{,}700}{1000} = 38.7 \text{ min/customer/yr}$$ **Step 4 — CAIDI (1 mark):** $$CAIDI = \frac{SAIDI}{SAIFI} = \frac{38.7}{0.55} \approx 70.36 \text{ min}$$ **Step 5 — FLISR improvement (3 marks):** - **Fault Location:** IEDs + smart switches pinpoint fault in < 1 s. - **Isolation:** Adjacent reclosers open to isolate the affected section. - **Service Restoration:** Tie-switch closes to back-feed from alternate feeder → healthy customers restored in **< 1 min**. - **Net Impact:** CAIDI drops to **< 15 min**; SAIDI drops by **60–80 %**; SAIFI remains roughly same (interruption still occurred, but duration collapses). --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Original PAR (1.5 marks):** $$PAR_{old} = \frac{P_{peak}}{P_{avg}} = \frac{15}{7.5} = 2.0$$ **Step 2 — New PAR after DR (1.5 marks):** $$PAR_{new} = \frac{P_{peak,new}}{P_{avg}} = \frac{10}{7.5} \approx 1.333$$ **Step 3 — PAR reduction percentage (1 mark):** $$\Delta PAR = \frac{2.0 - 1.333}{2.0} \times 100 \approx 33.33\%$$ **Step 4 — Engineering benefits of lowered PAR (3 marks):** 1. **Reduced line losses** — since $P_{loss} = I^2 R$, a 33 % peak reduction roughly halves peak current squared loss contribution. 2. **Improved transformer life** — less thermal cycling, lower hot-spot temperature, extended insulation life. 3. **Deferred capital expenditure** — utility avoids installing new substation capacity. 4. **Better voltage regulation** — flatter profile means smaller VR (Voltage Regulation) variation. 5. **Lower generation cost** — base-load plants run more, expensive peakers run less. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > 1. **Confusing "Demand (kW)" with "Energy (kWh)"** — In Part A, students often quote "100 units demand" instead of separating kW (power) and kWh (energy). Always state units explicitly. > 2. **Forgetting the √3 factor in 3-phase power** — A 7-mark numerical problem on $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$ is incomplete without $\sqrt{3}$. > 3. **Using $\div$ instead of correct KTU notation** — Use $\dfrac{\sum r_i N_i}{N_{total}}$, not ambiguous inline fractions in SAIDI/SAIFI derivations. > 4. **Writing "Smart Grid = IoT"** — IoT is an *enabler*; the smart grid is the *power system*; mark deduction if you ignore power engineering fundamentals. > 5. **Skipping the diagram** — Part (a) of Q-A explicitly asks for a "neat block diagram." A 1-mark loss is guaranteed if the diagram is missing. > 6. **Miscounting SAIDI vs SAIFI** — SAIDI uses **duration × customers** in numerator; SAIFI uses **only customers**. Mix-up is the #1 valuation error in distribution-engineering papers. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Smart Grid** = cyber-physical power grid + IoT + bidirectional flow. - ✅ **NIST Model** = 3 layers (Power / Comm / App) × 7 domains (Bulk Gen → Service Provider). - ✅ **AMI tiers**: Smart Meter → Concentrator → HES + MDMS. - ✅ **PMU** = GPS-synchronized high-speed sensor (50 samples/sec, IEEE C37.118). - ✅ **FLISR** = Fault Location, Isolation, Service Restoration → drives CAIDI down. - ✅ **SAIDI = Σ(rᵢNᵢ)/N_total** ; **SAIFI = ΣNᵢ/N_total** ; **CAIDI = SAIDI/SAIFI**. - ✅ **PAR = P_peak / P_avg** ; lower PAR = lower losses, longer asset life. - ✅ **Power identities:** $P = VI\cos\phi$ ; $Q = VI\sin\phi$ ; $S = \sqrt{P^2+Q^2}$. - ✅ **3-phase power:** $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$. - ✅ **Protocols:** DLMS/COSEM (meters), IEC 61850 (substation), OpenADR (DR), OCPP (EV), IEC 62351 (security). - ✅ **Communication techs:** RF Mesh, NB-IoT, LoRaWAN, PLC, 5G URLLC. - ✅ **Indian context:** ISGF, IS 16444 (meters), RDSS scheme, PM-Surya Ghar, 50 Hz grid. - ✅ **Kerala context:** KSEB smart prepaid meters, TOD tariff, KSERC reliability KPIs. - ✅ **DER hosting capacity** = Σ P_DER / P_feeder_rated ; safe up to ~40–60 %. - ✅ **VVO / CVR** raises voltage slightly within ±5 % band → reduces I²R loss by ~8–10 %. - ✅ **Self-healing time** target: < 1 minute for FLISR-enabled feeders. - ✅ **Cybersecurity standards:** IEC 62351, NERC-CIP, IS 16335. - ✅ **Smart Grid ≠ Smart Meter alone** — the meter is only the *tip* of the IoT iceberg. --- > **END OF MODULE 4 — SMART GRID NOTES | KTU 2024 SCHEME | PCCGT602**
